This mechanism is effective against any virus that uses a polymerase-driven replication — both DNA viruses (via DNA polymerase, e.g., aciclovir against HSV) and RNA viruses (via RNA polymerase or reverse transcriptase, e.g., ribavirin for HCV, AZT for HIV). Prions have no nucleic acid and therefore no polymerase — nucleoside analogues have no target. Why B/C are wrong: the mechanism applies to both virus types. Why E is wrong: chain termination applies broadly, not just to retroviruses.

applyIt.scenario: A 78-year-old immunocompromised patient presents with a painful vesicular rash affecting the right side of the face and palate, following the distribution of the maxillary branch (V2) of the trigeminal nerve. She reports the rash started with prodromal pain 3 days ago. Which ONE of the following diagnoses and management plans is MOST appropriate?
options: A. Primary herpes simplex — prescribe topical aciclovir cream and review in 5 days
B. Herpes zoster (shingles) of V2 — prescribe systemic aciclovir or valaciclovir within 72 hours of rash onset, manage pain, and refer for ophthalmological assessment
C. Herpes zoster (shingles) of V2 — give post-exposure prophylaxis with VZIG to prevent spread
D. Erythema multiforme — prescribe systemic corticosteroids and antihistamines
E. Hand, foot and mouth disease — reassure and prescribe supportive care only
applyIt.explanation: Unilateral dermatomal vesicular rash in the V2 distribution in an elderly immunocompromised patient = herpes zoster (shingles). VZV reactivates from the trigeminal ganglion. Systemic antivirals (aciclovir/valaciclovir) are most effective when started within 72 hours of rash onset — reduce viral shedding, shorten duration, and crucially reduce risk of post-herpetic neuralgia. V2 shingles does not directly threaten the eye (V1 does), but ophthalmological assessment is still wise given potential spread. Why A is wrong: primary HSV is typically bilateral gingivostomatitis, not dermatomal. Why C is wrong: VZIG is for contacts at risk (pregnant, immunocompromised) — not the patient herself. Why D/E: neither fits the clinical picture.

applyIt.scenario: A 34-year-old patient diagnosed with HIV 2 years ago has been on HAART with an undetectable viral load (VL <20 copies/mL) and a CD4 count of 620 cells/µL. He requires a lower molar extraction under local anaesthesia. Which ONE of the following BEST describes appropriate management?
options: A. Defer all dental treatment until CD4 count exceeds 800 cells/µL
B. Prescribe prophylactic antibiotics before the extraction due to HIV-positive status
C. Treat using standard universal precautions; no additional precautions are required beyond those for any patient
D. Refer to a specialist HIV dental unit as routine dental treatment cannot be performed in primary care
E. Ask the patient to self-isolate for 48 hours after the procedure due to high transmission risk
applyIt.explanation: A well-controlled HIV patient on effective HAART with undetectable VL and normal CD4 count has normal immune function and minimal transmission risk (U=U — undetectable = untransmittable). Standard universal precautions are applied to ALL patients, and no additional measures are required for this patient. Good oral health is especially important in HIV patients. Why A is wrong: CD4 of 620 is normal; deferral is inappropriate. Why B is wrong: NICE guidelines do not recommend routine antibiotic prophylaxis for dental procedures in HIV patients. Why D is wrong: uncomplicated dental treatment is entirely appropriate in primary care. Why E is wrong: with undetectable VL, transmission risk is negligible.

applyIt.scenario: A 52-year-old patient with known alcoholic cirrhosis requires a surgical tooth extraction. Pre-operative bloods show: ALT 180 U/L, albumin 28 g/L, PT 21 seconds (INR 1.9), platelets 85 ×10⁹/L. Which ONE of the following prescribing decisions is MOST appropriate?
options: A. Prescribe paracetamol 1g QDS for post-operative analgesia
B. Prescribe ibuprofen 400mg TDS for post-operative pain control
C. Prescribe diazepam 5mg for pre-operative anxiety management
D. Limit lidocaine to 2 cartridges maximum or use articaine as the local anaesthetic
E. Prescribe clarithromycin for prophylaxis as it has fewer drug interactions than penicillin
applyIt.explanation: In cirrhosis, hepatic drug metabolism is severely impaired. Lidocaine is an amide LA extensively metabolised by the liver — toxicity risk increases with dose. Limit to 2 cartridges (maximum). Articaine is partially metabolised by plasma pseudocholinesterases (as well as hepatic), making it safer. Why A is wrong: paracetamol is ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED in significant liver disease — even therapeutic doses overwhelm impaired hepatic detoxification (insufficient glutathione → NAPQI accumulation → hepatocyte necrosis). Why B is wrong: NSAIDs risk GI haemorrhage (varices) and renal impairment. Why C is wrong: benzodiazepines are not metabolised in liver disease — accumulate and cause coma. Why E is wrong: clarithromycin has many drug interactions and is avoided in liver disease.

applyIt.scenario: A patient's hepatitis B serology shows: HBsAg negative, HBsAb positive, HBcAb positive, HBeAg negative, HBeAb negative. Which ONE of the following MOST accurately describes this patient's status?
options: A. Active hepatitis B infection with high viral replication
B. Vaccinated against hepatitis B with full protection
C. Chronic hepatitis B carrier with low infectivity
D. Past hepatitis B infection with acquired natural immunity
E. Acute hepatitis B infection in the window period before seroconversion
applyIt.explanation: This pattern — HBsAg negative (virus cleared), HBsAb positive (immune), HBcAb positive (evidence of past infection) — indicates RESOLVED hepatitis B infection with natural immunity. The key distinction from vaccination: vaccinated people produce HBsAb but NOT HBcAb (because vaccination uses HBsAg only — the core antigen is never introduced). HBcAb persists for life and proves prior infection. Why B is wrong: vaccination never produces HBcAb. Why A/C: HBsAg is negative, so no active infection. Why E: window period = HBsAg fading but HBsAb not yet risen; HBcAb would be positive, but HBsAg would be transiently detectable before clearing.

applyIt.scenario: A dentist notices a 45-year-old patient with a 2 cm firm, non-tender swelling in the left submandibular region that has been present for 3 months. The patient has a history of IV drug use and a positive Mantoux test 10 years ago. Oral examination shows a painless irregular ulcer on the posterior tongue. Which ONE of the following management plans is MOST appropriate?
options: A. Prescribe a 2-week course of amoxicillin and review
B. Reassure — submandibular swellings in former IVDU are almost always reactive lymphadenopathy
C. Refer urgently for biopsy of the tongue ulcer and investigation of the neck swelling — TB must be excluded
D. Request a panoramic radiograph to investigate the submandibular swelling
E. Prescribe nystatin suspension — oral candidiasis is the most likely diagnosis in IVDU patients
applyIt.explanation: TB is strongly suggested: IVDU (risk factor), positive prior Mantoux, 3-month painless posterior tongue ulcer (characteristic of oral TB — secondary to pulmonary TB from swallowed sputum), and firm cervical lymphadenopathy (TB lymphadenitis). Management: urgent biopsy with histology and culture to confirm TB; refer to respiratory/ID team; treat with quadruple antituberculous therapy. Why A is wrong: amoxicillin does not treat TB. Why B is wrong: this clinical picture demands investigation. Why D: radiograph won't diagnose TB. Why E: the ulcer doesn't fit candidiasis clinically.

applyIt.scenario: A 35-year-old woman in her first trimester of pregnancy develops a spreading mandibular abscess. She has no drug allergies. She is taking no medications except folic acid. Which ONE of the following antibiotic choices is MOST appropriate?
options: A. Doxycycline 100mg once daily for 7 days — broad spectrum and well tolerated
B. Metronidazole 400mg three times daily — anaerobic cover is essential
C. Amoxicillin 500mg three times daily for 5 days — safe in all trimesters
D. Clarithromycin 250mg twice daily — well tolerated in pregnancy
E. Clindamycin 150mg four times daily — excellent bone penetration for mandibular infection
applyIt.explanation: Amoxicillin is the drug of choice for dental infections in pregnancy — it is safe in all trimesters with no teratogenic risk. Why A is wrong: doxycycline MUST be avoided in pregnancy (skeletal development in T1, dental discolouration in T2/T3, and bone growth impairment in newborn). Why B is wrong: metronidazole should be avoided in the first trimester due to concerns about mutagenicity (although evidence is limited). Why D is wrong: clarithromycin is avoided in pregnancy. Why E is wrong: clindamycin should be used with caution in the first trimester as it crosses the placenta readily.

applyIt.scenario: A 68-year-old patient on warfarin (target INR 2.5 for AF) attends with candidal stomatitis under an upper denture. He is also taking atorvastatin. You prescribe an antifungal. Which ONE of the following choices is ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED?
options: A. Nystatin oral suspension 100,000 units/mL — rinse and swallow 1mL QDS
B. Miconazole oromucosal gel 20mg/g — apply QDS
C. Fluconazole 50mg once daily for 7 days — with INR recheck
D. Amphotericin lozenges — dissolve slowly in mouth QDS
E. Improving denture hygiene and reviewing denture fit without antifungal
applyIt.explanation: Miconazole gel is absolutely contraindicated with warfarin. Miconazole is a potent CYP2C9 and CYP3A4 inhibitor — even when applied oromucosally, significant absorption occurs. It dramatically increases warfarin plasma levels causing INR to rise dangerously, risking major haemorrhage. This is a HIGH-YIELD interaction. Additionally, miconazole interacts with atorvastatin (CYP3A4 inhibition → rhabdomyolysis risk). Nystatin (A) is topical only, minimally absorbed, and SAFE with warfarin — it is the correct choice here. Fluconazole (C) also raises INR but less dramatically than miconazole; it should be used with caution and INR monitoring. Why D: amphotericin lozenges are essentially topical and safe.

applyIt.scenario: You extract a lower right molar from a medically complex patient. Three days later they return with a persistently painful socket with no clot and exposed bone. Examination shows no swelling, no lymphadenopathy, and no fever. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate management?
options: A. Prescribe amoxicillin 500mg TDS for 5 days — bacterial infection is likely
B. Prescribe metronidazole 400mg TDS — anaerobic bacteria are causing the dry socket
C. Perform local irrigation with chlorhexidine, apply a sedative dressing (e.g., Alvogyl), and provide adequate analgesia — no antibiotics needed
D. Refer to oral surgery urgently — dry socket following extraction always requires specialist management
E. Prescribe co-amoxiclav as a broad-spectrum cover for the exposed bone
applyIt.explanation: Dry socket (alveolar osteitis) is a localised inflammatory condition — NOT an infection. It results from failure of clot formation/retention in the socket. There is no evidence of spreading infection, systemic features, or abscess. Antibiotics are INAPPROPRIATE and provide no benefit for dry socket. Management: local irrigation to remove debris, Alvogyl dressing (local analgesic/antimicrobial), adequate systemic analgesia. Inappropriate antibiotic prescribing for dry socket drives resistance without clinical benefit — a common prescribing error.

applyIt.scenario: A patient on HAART for HIV-1 requires extraction of a broken down upper molar. On reviewing their drug chart you note they take tenofovir, emtricitabine, and ritonavir-boosted darunavir. Which ONE of the following is the MOST important prescribing consideration?
options: A. Standard lidocaine doses are safe and no dose adjustment is needed
B. Ritonavir is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor — it can significantly increase plasma levels of many co-administered drugs including certain local anaesthetics at higher doses
C. HAART patients require antibiotic prophylaxis before every invasive dental procedure
D. Tenofovir causes renal impairment, requiring dose reduction of amide local anaesthetics
E. Benzodiazepine sedation should be used for all HAART patients to minimise procedural stress
applyIt.explanation: Ritonavir is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor used as a pharmacokinetic booster. It inhibits metabolism of numerous drugs including articaine and some other amide anaesthetics metabolised by CYP3A4, potentially increasing their plasma levels. Always check the HIV drug interaction database (e.g., hiv-druginteractions.org) before prescribing any additional medication. Why C is wrong: NICE does not recommend routine prophylaxis for dental procedures in well-controlled HIV patients. Why D is wrong: lidocaine and articaine are hepatically metabolised, not primarily renal. Why E is wrong: benzodiazepines interact significantly with ritonavir.

applyIt.scenario: A 4-year-old child presents to the emergency department with high fever (39.5°C), widespread oral ulceration affecting the gingiva and hard palate, vesicular lesions around the mouth, and refusal to eat or drink. The mother reports he has not urinated in 12 hours. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate management?
options: A. Prescribe oral aciclovir 200mg five times daily and discharge — this is a self-limiting condition
B. Admit for IV fluids, IV aciclovir 250mg/m² three times daily, and supportive care due to the risk of dehydration from primary herpetic gingivostomatitis
C. Prescribe amoxicillin 125mg three times daily for bacterial tonsillitis
D. Reassure the mother — oral ulcers in children are normal aphthous stomatitis
E. Refer to dentistry for extraction of any mobile primary teeth under general anaesthesia
applyIt.explanation: This is a severe case of primary herpetic gingivostomatitis (PHG, HSV-1 primary infection) in a child with dehydration (no urine for 12 hours). When oral intake is severely compromised, hospital admission is warranted for IV fluid resuscitation and IV aciclovir to reduce disease severity and duration. The clinical presentation (fever, widespread vesicles/ulcers on both attached and non-attached mucosa including gingiva) is classic PHG. Why A is wrong: oral aciclovir requires swallowing, which may be impossible with severe oropharyngeal pain; IV route is required for severe disease. Why C is wrong: this is viral (HSV), not bacterial — amoxicillin is inappropriate. Why D is wrong: aphthous ulcers do not cause fever, do not affect attached gingiva, and do not cause perioral skin lesions. Why E is wrong: extraction is not indicated.

applyIt.scenario: A researcher is studying a new antiviral compound. The drug prevents viral entry into host cells by binding to and blocking the sialic acid receptor on respiratory epithelial cells. Which ONE of the following viruses would this drug MOST likely be effective against?
options: A. HIV — which uses CD4 and CCR5 as its entry receptors
B. Influenza A — which uses haemagglutinin to bind sialic acid receptors on respiratory epithelial cells
C. Hepatitis C — which enters hepatocytes via the CD81 receptor
D. Herpes simplex virus — which uses heparan sulphate proteoglycans for initial attachment
E. Rhinovirus — which uses ICAM-1 as its cellular receptor
applyIt.explanation: Influenza haemagglutinin (HA) binds sialic acid residues on the surface of respiratory epithelial cells — this is the essential first step in influenza infection. A drug that blocks sialic acid receptors would prevent influenza attachment and entry. This mirrors the mechanism of zanamivir (which targets neuraminidase rather than the receptor, but works on the same pathway). HIV (A) requires CD4 + CCR5/CXCR4 — not sialic acid. HCV (C) uses CD81, SR-B1, claudin-1, and occludin for hepatocyte entry. HSV (D) uses heparan sulphate for initial binding, then specific herpesvirus entry mediator (HVEM) or nectin for fusion. Rhinovirus (E) uses ICAM-1 (intercellular adhesion molecule 1) on upper respiratory tract epithelium.

applyIt.scenario: A 55-year-old immunocompetent woman presents with a 3-day history of unilateral vesicular rash on the right cheek and chin, following a dermatomal distribution. She has severe pain in the area, which preceded the rash by 2 days. She denies any oral symptoms. Which ONE of the following is the MOST likely diagnosis and the MOST appropriate treatment?
options: A. Herpes labialis (cold sore) — apply topical aciclovir cream for 5 days
B. Herpes zoster (V3 distribution, mandibular branch) — prescribe oral aciclovir 800mg five times daily for 7 days, or valaciclovir 1g three times daily for 7 days
C. Impetigo — prescribe flucloxacillin 500mg four times daily for 7 days
D. Contact dermatitis from dental materials — prescribe topical hydrocortisone cream
E. Bell's palsy (HSV-1 reactivation) — prescribe aciclovir 200mg five times daily for 5 days
applyIt.explanation: Unilateral vesicular rash following a dermatomal distribution preceded by pain (prodrome) in a 55-year-old = herpes zoster (shingles). The V3 (mandibular) branch distribution affects the cheek/chin/lower lip skin. Shingles is VZV reactivation from the trigeminal ganglion. Treatment must be started within 72 hours for maximum benefit: aciclovir 800mg 5× daily for 7 days OR valaciclovir 1g TDS for 7 days (fewer tablets, better compliance). Benefits: reduces pain severity, duration of rash, viral shedding, and risk of post-herpetic neuralgia (especially important in patients >50). Why A is wrong: herpes labialis occurs at the vermilion border/perioral area, is less severe, and does not follow a dermatomal pattern. Why C is wrong: impetigo (Staphylococcus/Streptococcus) does not cause dermatomal distribution or prodromal pain. Why D is wrong: no contact allergen history; dermatomal distribution rules out contact dermatitis. Why E is wrong: Bell's palsy causes facial nerve palsy, not vesicular skin rash.

applyIt.scenario: A 32-year-old patient known to be HIV-positive with a CD4 count of 450 cells/μL and undetectable viral load on HAART presents for a routine scale and polish. The dental receptionist informs you that the practice has a policy of not treating HIV-positive patients. Which ONE of the following BEST describes the appropriate response?
options: A. Comply with the practice policy — it is within the practice's rights to decline HIV-positive patients
B. Treat the patient with full PPE including double gloves, face shield, and gown
C. Advise the patient that practice policy means they must be referred to a specialist HIV dental clinic for all treatment
D. Treat the patient using standard universal infection control precautions — refusal constitutes unlawful discrimination under the Equality Act 2010 and violates GDC standards
E. Defer treatment until the CD4 count exceeds 500 cells/μL
applyIt.explanation: This question tests knowledge of both clinical and professional ethics. HIV infection is a protected characteristic under the Equality Act 2010 (disability). Refusing dental treatment to an HIV-positive patient who can be managed safely with standard infection control precautions is unlawful discrimination. The GDC's 'Standards for the Dental Team' explicitly prohibits discrimination against patients on grounds of protected characteristics. A patient with CD4 450 and undetectable viral load is well-controlled — the U=U principle applies, and standard universal precautions (which apply to ALL patients regardless of known infection status) are entirely adequate. Why A is wrong: no dental practice has the right to operate discriminatory policies. Why B is wrong: double gloves and additional PPE are not evidence-based requirements for well-controlled HIV. Why C is wrong: specialist referral is not appropriate for routine primary dental care. Why E is wrong: CD4 count does not create a treatment threshold in this context.

applyIt.scenario: A 40-year-old patient with known chronic HCV infection (genotype 1, on sofosbuvir/velpatasvir) presents for extraction of a heavily decayed lower molar. His recent blood results show: INR 1.3, platelets 135 × 10⁹/L, ALT 85 U/L. Which ONE of the following BEST describes the haemostatic management?
options: A. Postpone extraction until sofosbuvir/velpatasvir treatment is completed — antiviral therapy causes bleeding
B. Standard extraction with local haemostatic measures (tranexamic acid mouthwash post-operatively, pressure packing) — INR 1.3 and platelets 135 are within acceptable limits for routine extraction
C. Refer to haematology for fresh frozen plasma infusion before the extraction
D. Prescribe NSAIDs for post-operative analgesia to reduce inflammation
E. Defer extraction as ALT of 85 indicates acute liver failure
applyIt.explanation: Interpreting these bloods: INR 1.3 (mildly elevated but <2.5 — acceptable for routine extraction), platelets 135 × 10⁹/L (low normal but >80 × 10⁹/L — adequate for routine extraction), ALT 85 (mildly elevated ×2 ULN — indicates some hepatocellular inflammation but NOT acute liver failure). This patient can have a straightforward extraction with standard local haemostatic measures: tranexamic acid 5% mouthwash (10mL, hold 5 minutes, QDS for 2 days), pressure packing with gauze, consider resorbable haemostatic material, suturing if appropriate. Why A is wrong: sofosbuvir/velpatasvir (DAAs) do not cause bleeding — they treat HCV. Why C is wrong: FFP is not indicated for INR 1.3. Why D is wrong: NSAIDs impair platelet function and can cause GI bleeding — especially risky in liver disease. Why E is wrong: ALT 85 is mildly elevated, not indicative of acute liver failure (which requires INR >1.5 + encephalopathy).

applyIt.scenario: A 65-year-old patient presents with jaundice, right upper quadrant pain, and weight loss. Investigations reveal: elevated CA 19-9, obstructive jaundice pattern (ALP 450 U/L, bilirubin 180 μmol/L, ALT 95 U/L), dilated bile ducts on ultrasound. No history of HBV, HCV, or alcohol excess. Which ONE of the following is the MOST likely underlying cause of the dental team's concern if this patient presents for an extraction?
options: A. HCV cirrhosis — prescribe sofosbuvir-based DAA therapy before proceeding
B. Pancreatic carcinoma causing obstructive jaundice — the primary dental concern is coagulopathy from cholestatic liver disease (impaired vitamin K absorption) and potential underlying malignancy
C. Autoimmune hepatitis — prescribe prednisolone before extraction
D. Alcoholic hepatitis — advise alcohol cessation before dental treatment
E. Primary biliary cholangitis — request AMA antibody test before proceeding
applyIt.explanation: The clinical picture (obstructive jaundice pattern, weight loss, elevated CA 19-9, dilated ducts, elderly patient) strongly suggests pancreatic head carcinoma or cholangiocarcinoma causing extrahepatic biliary obstruction. The dental concern: obstructive jaundice → bile cannot enter the small intestine → vitamin K is fat-soluble and requires bile salts for absorption → vitamin K deficiency → impaired synthesis of clotting factors II, VII, IX, X → elevated PT/INR → bleeding risk. Management: check INR before any invasive procedure. If elevated, consider parenteral vitamin K (10mg IV/IM) to partially correct the coagulopathy, liaise with the patient's medical team. Additionally, consider the patient's oncological status, prognosis, and treatment plan — major dental work may be deprioritised. Why A, C, D, E are wrong: the clinical picture does not fit these diagnoses (no viral hepatitis markers, no alcohol history, obstructive pattern, high CA 19-9).

applyIt.scenario: A 28-year-old university student presents with a 5-day history of sore throat, profound fatigue, fever, and bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy. On examination, exudative tonsillitis is present. A Monospot test is positive. The GP prescribed amoxicillin 500mg TDS before the test result returned. The patient now has a widespread maculopapular rash. Which ONE of the following BEST explains the rash?
options: A. The patient has developed penicillin allergy — administer IM adrenaline immediately

B. The maculopapular rash is an immune-mediated reaction specific to the combination of aminopenicillin (amoxicillin) administration during EBV infectious mononucleosis — occurs in 80–90% of EBV patients given amoxicillin/ampicillin
C. The rash indicates secondary bacterial infection requiring a different antibiotic
D. EBV causes a direct viral exanthem — the rash is from the virus, not the antibiotic
E. This is a drug-induced Stevens-Johnson syndrome — the patient needs hospital admission and systemic corticosteroids
applyIt.explanation: This is the classic EBV mononucleosis + amoxicillin rash combination — a high-yield examination topic. When aminopenicillins (amoxicillin, ampicillin) are given to patients with active EBV infection, 80–90% develop a widespread erythematous maculopapular rash, typically on the trunk, appearing 5–10 days into treatment. The mechanism: EBV infection causes a transient polyclonal B cell activation that generates heterophile antibodies and IgM antibodies against the amino-penicilloyl moiety — these form immune complexes triggering the rash. It is NOT a true IgE-mediated penicillin allergy (the patient can usually receive penicillin safely in future, without EBV infection). Why A is wrong: this is not anaphylaxis — there is no urticaria, angioedema, bronchospasm, or hypotension. Why C is wrong: a different antibiotic is not needed — EBV is viral. Why D is wrong: EBV itself can cause a faint rash but not the florid 80–90% rash described. Why E is wrong: SJS is severe mucocutaneous blistering — this is a maculopapular rash.

applyIt.scenario: A 45-year-old patient presents with a 3-week history of a painful, swollen lower jaw. The swelling has a firm, woody consistency and is not fluctuant. There is a discharging sinus tract on the left side of the chin discharging small yellow granules. He had a lower left molar extracted 4 weeks ago. Which ONE of the following is the MOST likely diagnosis?
options: A. Dry socket — treat with alvogyl dressing and analgesia
B. Ludwig's angina — emergency airway management required immediately
C. Cervicofacial actinomycosis — prolonged penicillin therapy (6–12 months) is required
D. Squamous cell carcinoma — refer urgently for biopsy under the 2-week cancer wait pathway
E. Osteosarcoma of the mandible — CT scan and oncology referral required
applyIt.explanation: Slow-onset (weeks), woody induration, sinus tracts discharging 'sulphur granules' (yellow aggregates of Actinomyces filaments), following a dental procedure = classic cervicofacial actinomycosis. Actinomyces israelii is a Gram-positive, filamentous, anaerobic commensal of the oral flora that becomes pathogenic after mucosal disruption (extraction, trauma). The dense granulomatous fibrotic reaction explains the woody consistency and slow progression. Treatment: prolonged amoxicillin (500mg TDS for 6–12 months) — the extended duration is needed to penetrate the dense fibrous tissue and sterilise all colonies. Surgical drainage for established abscesses. Why A is wrong: dry socket is early post-extraction pain, not a 3-week woody swelling with sinus tracts. Why B is wrong: Ludwig's angina is acute, bilateral, and airway-threatening — not 3 weeks of slowly evolving woody swelling. Why D is wrong: SCC typically presents as an ulcer or exophytic mass, not as a sinus tract with sulphur granules. Why E is wrong: osteosarcoma is rare and does not produce sinus tracts.

applyIt.scenario: A 70-year-old man takes warfarin for atrial fibrillation (target INR 2.0–3.0) and simvastatin 40mg daily for hypercholesterolaemia. He develops an acute dentoalveolar abscess with trismus, fever 38.5°C, and spreading submandibular swelling. Which ONE of the following antibiotic prescribing decisions is MOST appropriate?
options: A. Prescribe clarithromycin 500mg twice daily for 7 days — safe for patients on warfarin who are penicillin-intolerant
B. Prescribe amoxicillin 500mg TDS + metronidazole 400mg TDS for 5 days — warfarin dose should be held for the duration of the course
C. Prescribe amoxicillin 500mg TDS + metronidazole 400mg TDS for 5 days — warn patient about potential INR rise from metronidazole and arrange INR check in 3–5 days
D. Prescribe co-amoxiclav 625mg TDS as first-line — it eliminates the need for metronidazole
E. No antibiotic needed — treat with drainage alone
applyIt.explanation: This is a spreading dental infection with systemic features (fever, trismus) — antibiotics are clearly indicated as an adjunct to surgical source control. First-line choice: amoxicillin + metronidazole (covers streptococci and anaerobes). The warfarin interaction to be aware of: metronidazole inhibits CYP2C9 → raises S-warfarin levels → INR elevation (potentially significant). Management: prescribe the standard therapeutic dose of metronidazole (do not reduce — reduces efficacy), warn the patient about signs of bleeding (unusual bruising, prolonged bleeding from gums), and arrange INR check in 3–5 days so warfarin dose can be adjusted if needed. Why A is wrong: clarithromycin is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor that raises warfarin levels AND inhibits simvastatin metabolism (rhabdomyolysis risk) — two dangerous interactions. Why B is wrong: holding warfarin without checking INR first risks thromboembolism. Why D is wrong: co-amoxiclav is second-line (failed amoxicillin); also interacts with warfarin (hepatotoxicity risk). Why E is wrong: systemic features mandate antibiotic therapy alongside drainage.

applyIt.scenario: A 35-year-old patient presents with a 2-day history of throbbing pain and swelling from a lower right second molar with a periapical abscess visible on X-ray. Temperature is 36.8°C, there is no trismus, no lymphadenopathy, and the swelling is localised to the vestibule. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate management?
options: A. Prescribe amoxicillin 500mg TDS for 5 days and review in 5 days — antibiotics will resolve the abscess
B. Extract or perform emergency pulpotomy/drainage of the tooth as the primary treatment — antibiotics are NOT indicated without systemic features
C. Prescribe co-amoxiclav 625mg TDS for 7 days for broad-spectrum coverage
D. Prescribe both amoxicillin and metronidazole for 5 days as a precautionary measure
E. Refer to oral surgery — all periapical abscesses require specialist management
applyIt.explanation: This is a textbook scenario for appropriate antibiotic stewardship. The patient has a localised dentoalveolar abscess WITHOUT systemic features (afebrile at 36.8°C, no trismus, no lymphadenopathy, localised swelling). According to SDCEP and NICE guidance: antibiotics are NOT indicated for localised dental abscess without systemic features. The correct treatment is surgical source control: (1) Extract the tooth, OR (2) Emergency pulpotomy/pulpectomy to drain the abscess via the root canal. Antibiotics do not penetrate avascular abscess cavities and provide no benefit when source control is performed in an immunocompetent patient without systemic features. Why A is wrong: prescribing antibiotics without source control is inappropriate prescribing — the abscess will not resolve with antibiotics alone. Why C and D are wrong: broad-spectrum antibiotics for a localised abscess = inappropriate antibiotic stewardship. Why E is wrong: primary dental care is appropriate for this presentation.

applyIt.scenario: A 55-year-old patient with long-standing rheumatoid arthritis is on methotrexate 15mg weekly and adalimumab (anti-TNF-α biologic) monthly. She develops a slowly expanding painful ulcer on the left buccal mucosa that has not resolved over 3 weeks. On examination, the ulcer has indurated edges and central necrosis. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate action?
options: A. Prescribe metronidazole — biologic therapy causes anaerobic oral infections
B. Prescribe topical triamcinolone — likely an aphthous ulcer exacerbated by immunosuppression
C. Urgent 2-week-wait referral to oral and maxillofacial surgery for biopsy — persistent indurated oral ulcer in an immunosuppressed patient requires malignancy and opportunistic infection exclusion
D. Reassure and review in 6 weeks — biologic therapy commonly causes oral aphthous ulcers that self-resolve
E. Stop adalimumab and review — the biologic is causing the ulcer
applyIt.explanation: An indurated, non-healing oral ulcer of >3 weeks duration in an immunosuppressed patient requires urgent investigation. Differential diagnosis includes: (1) Oral squamous cell carcinoma (most feared — immunosuppression increases malignancy risk); (2) Opportunistic infection (deep fungal infection — Histoplasma, Mucor; atypical mycobacterial infection; viral — CMV ulcer in severe immunocompromise); (3) Lymphoma (non-Hodgkin's lymphoma risk increased with anti-TNF-α therapy); (4) Aphthous-like ulcers from methotrexate. The indurated edges and central necrosis are features requiring biopsy — topical steroids or antibiotics should not be prescribed without histological diagnosis. Why D is wrong: 3 weeks of induration warrants biopsy, not further watching. Why E is wrong: stopping a biologic is a specialist decision, not a dental decision, and should not be done to resolve a dental presentation without oncological exclusion.

applyIt.scenario: A dental nurse reports that a patient treated in the afternoon had active herpes labialis visible on their lip. The dental nurse, who has no history of cold sores, notices a cluster of painful vesicles on the pulp of their right index finger the following week. Which ONE of the following BEST describes the diagnosis, how it occurred, and appropriate management?
options: A. Paronychia — bacterial infection from poor hand hygiene; treat with flucloxacillin

B. Herpetic whitlow — primary HSV-1 infection of the finger from direct contact with the patient's infected oral secretions during an ungloved procedure; treat with aciclovir 200mg five times daily for 5 days and do NOT incise
C. Herpes zoster — VZV reactivation from childhood infection; treat with aciclovir 800mg five times daily for 7 days
D. Contact dermatitis from latex gloves — switch to vinyl gloves
E. Dyshidrotic eczema — refer to dermatology for steroid treatment
applyIt.explanation: Herpetic whitlow is primary HSV-1 (or HSV-2) infection of a finger, acquired through direct inoculation of infected oral or genital secretions. It is a recognised occupational hazard in dental and medical professionals. Presentation: severe burning/throbbing pain, cluster of vesicles on the finger pulp, regional lymphadenopathy, possible fever. It mimics bacterial felon (deep pulp space infection) — the critical distinction: DO NOT incise herpetic whitlow (incision can cause viral spread and secondary bacterial superinfection, and leads to complications). Management: aciclovir 200mg 5× daily for 5 days, wound care, avoid direct patient contact until healed, report to occupational health. Prevention: always wear gloves for all intraoral procedures. Why C is wrong: VZV causes dermatomal distribution, not finger-tip vesicles with an occupational exposure history. Why D is wrong: contact dermatitis does not cause grouped vesicles. Why E is wrong: dyshidrotic eczema is bilateral and symmetric.

applyIt.scenario: A 38-year-old patient is newly diagnosed with HIV with a CD4 count of 12 cells/μL. He is commenced on co-trimoxazole prophylaxis and HAART. One week after starting HAART, he develops worsening headache, fever, and neck stiffness. CSF analysis reveals India ink staining positive for encapsulated yeast. Which ONE of the following BEST describes this clinical scenario?
options: A. Bacterial meningitis from dental infection — prescribe penicillin G IV
B. Cryptococcal meningitis — a pre-existing opportunistic infection unmasked by immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) after starting HAART
C. HSV encephalitis from trigeminal ganglion reactivation — prescribe IV aciclovir
D. Tuberculous meningitis — prescribe RIPE chemotherapy
E. PCP pneumonia with CNS dissemination — increase co-trimoxazole dose
applyIt.explanation: India ink stain positive for encapsulated yeast in CSF = Cryptococcus neoformans. This patient has cryptococcal meningitis, which occurs in advanced AIDS (CD4 <100). The clinical scenario illustrates immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS): when HAART is initiated in a patient with pre-existing subclinical cryptococcal infection, the recovering immune system mounts a vigorous inflammatory response against the fungus → paradoxical worsening of cryptococcal meningitis symptoms. Management: (1) Treat the cryptococcal infection aggressively — IV amphotericin B + flucytosine for 2 weeks (induction), then fluconazole for 8 weeks (consolidation); (2) HAART may need to be temporarily held in severe IRIS. This is why clinicians screen for cryptococcal antigen before starting HAART in patients with CD4 <100 — to treat pre-existing infection first. Why A is wrong: bacterial meningitis would not show India ink yeast. Why C and D are wrong: CSF features distinguish cryptococcal from HSV/TB meningitis.

applyIt.scenario: A 22-year-old male presents to the dental emergency service with a 4-hour history of rapidly worsening bilateral neck swelling, floor of mouth elevation, and difficulty swallowing. His temperature is 39.2°C. He had a lower wisdom tooth extracted 48 hours ago at a different practice. On examination, his mouth opening is severely restricted (8mm). Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate immediate action?
options: A. Prescribe co-amoxiclav 625mg TDS and review in 24 hours
B. Perform intraoral incision and drainage of the submandibular space in the dental chair
C. Immediately call 999, prepare for emergency airway management, and transfer to hospital by emergency ambulance — this is Ludwig's angina with imminent airway compromise
D. Prescribe IV amoxicillin and metronidazole and review in 4 hours
E. Arrange urgent CT scan before initiating any treatment
applyIt.explanation: This is a life-threatening emergency — Ludwig's angina with airway compromise. Bilateral neck swelling + floor of mouth elevation + dysphagia + restricted mouth opening (8mm) + fever following extraction = bilateral submandibular space infection with potential for imminent airway obstruction. The tongue can be progressively displaced superiorly and posteriorly, obstructing the airway. Mortality from untreated airway obstruction is certain. Immediate action: (1) Call 999 — emergency ambulance with paramedics; (2) Keep patient sitting upright; (3) Prepare for emergency surgical airway if complete obstruction occurs; (4) Do NOT attempt intraoral procedures in the dental chair — impossible with 8mm mouth opening and risk of precipitating complete obstruction. Hospital management: awake fibre-optic intubation or surgical tracheostomy to secure airway, IV antibiotics, surgical drainage in theatre. Why A: oral antibiotics are completely inadequate for this severity. Why B: intraoral drainage is impossible and dangerous with 8mm opening. Why E: CT is useful but must NOT delay emergency treatment.

applyIt.scenario: A 52-year-old patient takes phenytoin 300mg daily for epilepsy and is prescribed fluconazole 50mg daily for 2 weeks for oral candidiasis. Two days later she calls the practice complaining of double vision, unsteady gait, and slurred speech. Which ONE of the following BEST explains these symptoms?
options: A. Fluconazole is causing fungal meningitis — refer to neurology immediately
B. Fluconazole inhibits CYP2C9, reducing phenytoin metabolism, causing phenytoin accumulation and toxicity — nystagmus, ataxia, and dysarthria are classic features of phenytoin toxicity
C. Phenytoin is causing fluconazole toxicity by inducing its metabolism
D. The patient has developed an allergic reaction to fluconazole — prescribe antihistamine
E. This is an idiosyncratic reaction requiring immediate fluconazole cessation and emergency admission
applyIt.explanation: Fluconazole inhibits CYP2C9 (and CYP3A4), which is the main enzyme metabolising phenytoin. With fluconazole inhibiting CYP2C9, phenytoin clearance is reduced → phenytoin plasma levels rise above the therapeutic range. Phenytoin has a narrow therapeutic index — toxicity features follow a predictable dose-dependent sequence: nystagmus (first), ataxia (next), dysarthria and diplopia, then confusion, and at very high levels, paradoxical seizures and cardiac arrhythmias. Management: (1) Stop fluconazole; (2) Check plasma phenytoin level urgently; (3) Depending on level, may need hospital admission; (4) Resume fluconazole only with close phenytoin monitoring and possible dose reduction, or use nystatin instead (no drug interactions). For patients on phenytoin (or other enzyme-sensitive anticonvulsants), azithromycin is the safer macrolide/antifungal alternative — nystatin avoids systemic interactions entirely.

applyIt.scenario: A 58-year-old man with cirrhosis secondary to HBV (Child-Pugh B, INR 1.8, platelets 65 × 10⁹/L, albumin 28 g/L) requires extraction of an unrestorable upper right first premolar. Which ONE of the following represents the MOST appropriate pre-operative preparation?
options: A. Proceed without special preparation — INR <2.0 is always safe for dental extraction

B. Refer to hospital oral surgery with haematology liaison — pre-operative tranexamic acid rinse, consider platelet transfusion if <50 × 10⁹/L, no NSAIDs post-operatively, use paracetamol (max 2g/day) for pain
C. Prescribe oral vitamin K 10mg for 3 days to normalise the INR before extraction
D. Proceed using intraosseous local anaesthesia only — conventional LA is contraindicated in liver disease
E. Prescribe ibuprofen 400mg TDS for post-operative pain as anti-inflammatory cover
applyIt.explanation: Child-Pugh B cirrhosis with INR 1.8 and platelets 65 × 10⁹/L represents significant bleeding risk from multiple haemostatic defects: reduced clotting factor synthesis (INR elevated), thrombocytopaenia (splenomegaly-related sequestration), and low albumin (hypoalbuminaemia affecting drug distribution). Hospital-based oral surgery is appropriate for this complexity: (1) Pre-operative tranexamic acid mouthwash (10mg/mL); (2) Platelet transfusion if <50 × 10⁹/L (platelets here are 65 — borderline; liaise with haematology); (3) Resorbable haemostats (gelfoam/surgicel), suturing; (4) Avoid NSAIDs completely (D-isomer and all COX inhibitors worsen platelet function and risk GI bleeding in cirrhosis); (5) Paracetamol max 2g/day is the safe analgesic. Why A is wrong: INR and platelet count must both be considered together. Why C is wrong: oral vitamin K helps if vitamin K deficiency contributes (obstructive jaundice) but cannot reverse coagulation defects of synthetic failure in cirrhosis. Why E is wrong: NSAIDs are absolutely contraindicated in liver disease.

applyIt.scenario: A dental student asks why the same patient can contract influenza every year despite having been infected before. Which ONE of the following BEST explains this phenomenon?
options: A. Influenza destroys all memory B cells during infection, so immunity is never established

B. Antigenic drift — gradual point mutations in haemagglutinin and neuraminidase genes mean each year's circulating strains have different surface antigens that are not well-recognised by antibodies from previous infection
C. The immune response to influenza is primarily T cell-mediated, and T cell memory wanes within 6 months
D. Influenza undergoes antigenic shift every year, generating a completely new pandemic strain
E. Antibodies to influenza are IgM only — they cannot provide long-term protection as IgG class switching does not occur
applyIt.explanation: Annual influenza reinfection is explained by antigenic drift: influenza RNA polymerase lacks proofreading activity → high error rate → point mutations accumulate in the H (haemagglutinin) and N (neuraminidase) genes. The new antigenic variants are sufficiently different from prior year strains that existing antibodies bind with lower affinity and provide inadequate neutralisation. This is why the WHO annually updates influenza vaccine composition to match predicted circulating strains. Memory B cells and T cells are maintained (A is wrong). T cell immunity to influenza (especially CD8+ T cells targeting conserved internal proteins) is relatively durable — but does not prevent infection (C is partially wrong). Antigenic SHIFT (D) produces pandemics — not annual epidemics. Influenza immunity does involve IgG (E is wrong) — class switching does occur.

applyIt.scenario: A 45-year-old patient has received a kidney transplant 6 months ago and is on tacrolimus, mycophenolate, and prednisolone. She presents with persistent white patches on her tongue that wipe off, revealing a raw red surface, despite 14 days of nystatin. She has a CD4 count of 280 (checked by the transplant team). Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate next step?
options: A. Continue nystatin for another 14 days — the course was too short
B. Prescribe miconazole gel — safe second-line topical antifungal for all transplant patients
C. Prescribe systemic fluconazole 100–200mg daily for 14 days after checking for drug interactions with tacrolimus
D. Prescribe amphotericin B lozenges as first-line escalation
E. Refer to oral medicine for biopsy — these lesions cannot be candidiasis in a transplant patient
applyIt.explanation: Pseudomembranous candidiasis unresponsive to topical nystatin in a significantly immunocompromised transplant patient (triple immunosuppression, CD4 280) requires escalation to systemic antifungal therapy. Fluconazole 100–200mg daily for 14 days is appropriate. CRITICAL INTERACTION: tacrolimus is metabolised by CYP3A4. Fluconazole inhibits CYP3A4 → tacrolimus levels can rise dramatically → nephrotoxicity and toxicity (tremor, headache, nephrotoxicity). The transplant team must be informed, tacrolimus levels must be monitored closely, and tacrolimus dose may need reduction. Miconazole (B) also inhibits CYP3A4 and has similar interactions with tacrolimus — not automatically safer. Continuing nystatin alone (A) is insufficient for 14-day treatment failure in an immunocompromised patient. Amphotericin lozenges (D) are an alternative but systemic fluconazole is more evidence-based. These lesions should be treated clinically before biopsy (E) — diagnostic features are classic candidiasis.

applyIt.scenario: A dentist prescribes metronidazole 400mg TDS for 5 days for pericoronitis in a healthy 24-year-old patient. The patient returns 3 days later with no improvement. On examination, the pericoronitis is no worse, but the patient has mild trismus (25mm mouth opening) and a temperature of 37.5°C. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate management?
options: A. Increase metronidazole dose to 500mg TDS for a further 7 days
B. Switch to co-amoxiclav 625mg TDS as second-line monotherapy — metronidazole has failed
C. Irrigate the pericoronal space under the operculum, add amoxicillin 500mg TDS to the existing metronidazole to provide streptococcal coverage, and review in 2–3 days
D. Refer immediately to A&E — trismus indicates Ludwig's angina
E. Prescribe clindamycin 300mg QDS and stop metronidazole
applyIt.explanation: This case illustrates why metronidazole monotherapy for pericoronitis is insufficient. Metronidazole covers only anaerobes — it has NO activity against aerobic/microaerophilic streptococci (S. anginosus group), which are key pathogens in pericoronitis. The mild trismus (25mm) and low-grade temperature indicate incomplete treatment — the infection is not worsening dramatically but is not resolving either. The correct escalation: add amoxicillin 500mg TDS (streptococcal cover) to the existing metronidazole (anaerobic cover) — this completes the polymicrobial antibiotic coverage that should have been prescribed from the outset. Continue combined therapy for 5 days from the escalation date, with local irrigation and review. Why B is wrong: co-amoxiclav is reasonable second-line, but adding amoxicillin (a step more logical than switching entirely) while maintaining anaerobic cover is preferred. Why D is wrong: grade I-II trismus with 37.5°C temperature does not indicate Ludwig's angina requiring emergency transfer. Why E is wrong: clindamycin is appropriate for penicillin-allergic patients, not first switch in a penicillin-tolerant patient.

applyIt.scenario: A 7-year-old child who recently received a measles, mumps, and rubella (MMR) vaccine develops fever and a widespread maculopapular rash 8 days after vaccination. The mother is concerned about measles. Which ONE of the following BEST explains this presentation?
options: A. The child has contracted natural measles — the MMR vaccine has failed
B. This is a vaccine-strain measles-like reaction — mild, self-limiting, occurring in ~5% of MMR recipients 7–12 days post-vaccination; not contagious
C. The child has developed anaphylaxis to MMR — administer adrenaline immediately
D. The MMR has caused permanent immune suppression — the child needs immunoglobulin
E. This is rubella from the MMR vaccine — no specific treatment needed but the child is highly contagious
applyIt.explanation: Fever and maculopapular rash 7–12 days after MMR vaccination is a recognised, mild, self-limiting post-vaccine reaction occurring in approximately 5% of vaccinees. It is caused by low-level replication of the vaccine-strain attenuated measles virus — the immune response to this replication causes transient fever and a mild measles-like rash. Key points: (1) Not natural measles — vaccine-strain virus is attenuated (weakened) and does not cause the full disease; (2) NOT contagious — vaccine-strain measles is not transmitted to contacts; (3) Self-limiting — resolves within 1–3 days; (4) Does NOT indicate vaccine failure — the child will still be protected. Natural measles (A) has higher fever, koplik spots, and the rash begins behind the ears and spreads downward — timing is also different (not 8 days post-vaccine). Anaphylaxis (C) occurs within minutes of injection. Contagiousness (E) applies to natural rubella but not vaccine-strain virus.

applyIt.scenario: A 30-year-old nurse pricks her finger with a needle used on an HBsAg-positive, HBeAg-positive patient. She received the full 3-dose HBV vaccination course 2 years ago; anti-HBs measured 6 weeks after the third dose was 12 mIU/mL. What is her immune status and what management is required?
options: A. Anti-HBs 12 mIU/mL confirms adequate immunity — no action needed beyond incident reporting
B. Anti-HBs 12 mIU/mL indicates a low responder — give a single HBV booster dose and re-check anti-HBs in 4–6 weeks; administer HBIG immediately as protection is uncertain
C. No action needed — HBeAg-positive patients are low infectivity
D. Start a 28-day antiviral course (tenofovir) as HBV post-exposure prophylaxis
E. Administer HBIG only — no vaccine booster is needed for low responders
applyIt.explanation: Anti-HBs thresholds: >100 mIU/mL = good responder (adequate protection — no HBIG needed post-exposure). 10–100 mIU/mL = low responder (some immunity, uncertain protection). <10 mIU/mL = non-responder (not immune — needs repeat course and HBIG post-exposure). This nurse has anti-HBs of 12 mIU/mL = low responder. Post-needlestick from a high-infectivity source (HBsAg+, HBeAg+): (1) Immediate HBIG (hepatitis B immunoglobulin) — provides passive protection while vaccine booster takes effect; (2) Booster vaccine dose; (3) Re-check anti-HBs in 4–6 weeks to confirm adequate response. HBeAg-positive (C) actually indicates HIGH infectivity — opposite of the assumption in C. Antivirals (D) are for HIV post-exposure prophylaxis, not HBV. HBIG alone without a booster (E) provides only short-term passive protection without establishing durable immunity.

applyIt.scenario: A 45-year-old patient with rheumatoid arthritis is on methotrexate 20mg weekly and is prescribed co-amoxiclav 625mg TDS for a dental infection. Two weeks later she presents with profound fatigue, mouth ulcers, and her FBC shows WBC 1.2 × 10⁹/L (neutrophils 0.4), Hb 85g/L, platelets 60 × 10⁹/L. Which ONE of the following BEST explains this presentation?
options: A. Co-amoxiclav has caused aplastic anaemia — a well-recognised class effect of penicillins
B. Co-amoxiclav inhibits renal tubular secretion of methotrexate, causing methotrexate accumulation and bone marrow toxicity (pancytopaenia)
C. The patient's rheumatoid arthritis is causing Felty syndrome — splenomegaly with neutropaenia
D. The dental infection has spread to the bone marrow causing aplastic anaemia
E. Methotrexate toxicity has occurred spontaneously — the antibiotic has not contributed
applyIt.explanation: This is a critical and potentially fatal drug interaction. Methotrexate is a folate antagonist that inhibits DHFR — it has a narrow therapeutic index. Methotrexate is primarily renally eliminated. Co-amoxiclav (and other penicillins, NSAIDs) inhibit renal tubular secretion of methotrexate via organic acid transporters (OAT) → methotrexate accumulates to toxic levels → bone marrow suppression (pancytopaenia: severe neutropaenia + anaemia + thrombocytopaenia), mucositis (mouth ulcers), hepatotoxicity, nephrotoxicity. This combination is EXTREMELY DANGEROUS — penicillins should be prescribed with great caution to patients on methotrexate and the oncology/rheumatology team should be consulted. If methotrexate toxicity is confirmed: administer folinic acid (leucovorin) rescue, provide supportive care for bone marrow failure. This interaction applies to all penicillins, not just co-amoxiclav. Prescribers must check drug interactions in all patients on methotrexate before prescribing antibiotics.

applyIt.scenario: A 26-year-old patient discloses to the dentist that she was diagnosed with HIV 6 months ago but has not told her live-in partner, who attends the same dental practice. The partner is also your patient. She asks you to keep her diagnosis confidential and not to inform her partner. Which ONE of the following BEST describes the ethical and legal framework for this situation?
options: A. You must immediately inform the partner — they are at risk and have a right to know
B. Maintain confidentiality — HIV status is not transmissible via normal dental care; the partner's risk is not caused by the dental team

C. Maintain strict confidentiality with the patient; encourage her to disclose to her partner and consider referral to GUM clinic for partner notification support — breach of confidentiality is only considered in exceptional risk situations after ethical guidance is sought
D. Inform the partner without the patient's consent — the duty of care to the partner overrides confidentiality
E. Report the patient's HIV status to the NHS so that partner notification can be arranged automatically
applyIt.explanation: This scenario tests the ethical principles of confidentiality vs. duty of care. The fundamental approach: (1) Maintain patient confidentiality — this is the default. (2) Encourage and support the patient to disclose to her partner — this is the most appropriate and respectful approach. (3) Refer to GUM clinic — GUM services have specialist partner notification programmes that can facilitate disclosure with patient support. (4) Breach of confidentiality is only ethically permissible in EXCEPTIONAL circumstances: when there is a serious, imminent, identifiable risk to a third party AND all other options have been exhausted AND the risk cannot be averted any other way — even then, seeking ethical guidance (GMC/GDC guidance, ethics committee, legal advice) is required before breaching. HIV transmission from dental procedures (not the partner's concern here) is separate from sexual transmission risk. The dentist does not automatically know the partner's HIV risk status. Immediate disclosure without consent (A, D) is not appropriate.

applyIt.scenario: A 55-year-old immunocompetent patient presents with a 3-week history of a painless, progressive swelling in the right submandibular region. On examination, there is a firm, non-tender, 3cm lymph node. He has no recent dental infection, no fever, and no oral ulcers. He is a 30-pack-year smoker. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate immediate referral?
options: A. Prescribe amoxicillin for 14 days — reactive lymphadenopathy from dental infection
B. Refer to the dentist's own practice for an OPG X-ray
C. Urgent 2-week-wait head and neck cancer referral — persistent, non-tender, firm cervical lymphadenopathy of >3 weeks in a smoker requires exclusion of malignancy
D. Refer to ENT for adenoidectomy
E. Refer to oral medicine for a salivary gland biopsy — this is a salivary gland calculus
applyIt.explanation: Persistent (>3 weeks), painless, firm cervical lymphadenopathy in a smoker (significant head and neck cancer risk factor) meets the 2-week-wait urgent cancer referral criteria under NICE NG12 (Suspected Cancer: Recognition and Referral). The differential diagnosis includes: metastatic squamous cell carcinoma from an oropharyngeal/laryngeal primary (HPV and tobacco-related), lymphoma (Hodgkin's or non-Hodgkin's), thyroid carcinoma, salivary gland malignancy. This patient has not had dental infection (ruling out reactive lymphadenopathy), the node is firm and non-tender (features of malignancy), and it has persisted for 3 weeks. Antibiotic prescription without investigation (A) delays diagnosis. An OPG alone (B) is insufficient. Adenoidectomy (D) is for enlarged adenoids in children. Salivary gland calculus (E) causes painful swelling at mealtimes — not this presentation.

applyIt.scenario: A 19-year-old female patient who is 8 weeks pregnant presents with an acute periapical abscess of the upper right canine with spreading buccal cellulitis, fever of 38.8°C, and lymphadenopathy. She has a documented penicillin allergy (rash with amoxicillin 5 years ago, no systemic features). Which ONE of the following antibiotic regimens is MOST appropriate?
options: A. Doxycycline 100mg twice daily for 7 days — broad-spectrum cover in penicillin allergy
B. Clindamycin 300mg four times daily for 5 days — appropriate for penicillin allergy in pregnancy
C. Ciprofloxacin 500mg twice daily for 5 days — fluoroquinolones are safe in the first trimester
D. Clarithromycin 500mg twice daily for 5 days — the first-line macrolide for dental infections
E. Erythromycin 500mg four times daily for 5 days — the preferred macrolide in pregnancy
applyIt.explanation: Managing a spreading dental infection in a pregnant patient with penicillin allergy requires careful antibiotic selection. Safe antibiotics in pregnancy: amoxicillin (first-line, not suitable here), erythromycin (preferred macrolide in pregnancy — well-established safety record), cefalexin (cephalosporin, generally safe but cross-reactivity concern with penicillin allergy — though the allergy was a non-serious rash). Contraindicated in pregnancy: doxycycline/tetracyclines (A) — fetal dental staining and bone growth inhibition; ciprofloxacin/fluoroquinolones (C) — fetal cartilage damage; clarithromycin (D) — potential teratogenicity in animal studies, not recommended. Clindamycin (B) is not absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy but erythromycin is preferred where it provides adequate coverage. Note: The non-serious rash with amoxicillin 5 years ago may indicate the patient could potentially tolerate amoxicillin (85% of patients reporting penicillin allergy can tolerate penicillin) — but caution is appropriate especially in pregnancy.

applyIt.scenario: A 34-year-old patient presents with a 2-week history of painful oral ulcers following a course of clindamycin for a dental infection. She describes profuse watery diarrhoea with 6–8 bowel movements per day, abdominal cramping, and low-grade fever. Stool test is positive for C. difficile toxin. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate management?
options: A. Continue clindamycin and add metronidazole 400mg TDS — C. difficile responds to metronidazole combined with the existing antibiotic
B. Stop clindamycin immediately, prescribe oral vancomycin 125mg four times daily for 10 days, advise soap-and-water handwashing (not alcohol gel), and refer to GP/gastroenterology
C. Continue clindamycin — oral ulcers and diarrhoea are common side effects that do not require dose modification
D. Prescribe high-dose probiotics — the only evidence-based treatment for antibiotic-associated CDI
E. Advise the patient to take clindamycin with food and increase fluid intake — GI symptoms will resolve
applyIt.explanation: Confirmed C. difficile infection (CDI) following clindamycin is a medical emergency. Management: (1) STOP the precipitating antibiotic (clindamycin) immediately — this is the most critical step; (2) Oral vancomycin 125mg QDS for 10 days is the current first-line UK treatment for a first episode of CDI (NICE NG199, 2021 — replacing oral metronidazole as first-line); (3) Handwashing with soap and water — critical because C. difficile forms hardy spores that are NOT killed by alcohol hand gel; (4) Infection control precautions (isolate if hospitalised, gloves, apron); (5) Refer to GP/gastroenterology for monitoring and management. The oral ulcers here are likely from the CDI-related nutritional disruption or direct colonic inflammation effects. CDI is not treated by continuing the antibiotic (A, C). Probiotics (D) have some evidence for prevention but are not first-line treatment. Dietary modification (E) is wholly inadequate.

applyIt.scenario: A 22-year-old dental student reports that her 6-month-old nephew has been diagnosed with congenital CMV infection — he has sensorineural hearing loss and developmental delay. The mother (the student's sister) had a flu-like illness in the second trimester of pregnancy. Which ONE of the following BEST explains the mechanism of fetal harm?
options: A. CMV is transmitted to the fetus via breast milk during the neonatal period

B. Primary maternal CMV infection during pregnancy leads to viraemia, transplacental transmission of CMV to the fetus, where the developing cochlea and CNS are particularly vulnerable to CMV replication and inflammation
C. The flu-like illness represents maternal immune activation that causes autoimmune fetal cochlear damage
D. CMV is a DNA virus that integrates into fetal cochlear cell chromosomes causing permanent deletion of hearing genes
E. Congenital CMV only causes harm if the mother had previous CMV exposure — primary infection during pregnancy is less dangerous
applyIt.explanation: Congenital CMV (cCMV) occurs when primary CMV infection during pregnancy (most dangerous in first trimester, but any trimester can cause harm) leads to CMV viraemia in the mother. CMV crosses the placenta and infects the developing fetus. The developing cochlea and CNS are particularly susceptible to CMV cytopathic effects (viral replication, local inflammation, cell death). Sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL) is the most common long-term consequence of cCMV — affecting up to 50% of symptomatic cCMV infants and can be delayed or progressive (developing after birth). CMV is the leading non-genetic cause of congenital SNHL. Other consequences: microcephaly, psychomotor retardation, chorioretinitis, hepatosplenomegaly. Breast milk transmission (A) occurs but causes different postnatal infection. The mechanism is direct viral harm (B), not autoimmune (C). CMV does not integrate into chromosomes like retroviruses (D). PRIMARY maternal infection is the most dangerous (E is reversed — prior immunity is partially protective).

applyIt.scenario: A 60-year-old patient on prednisolone 15mg daily for polymyalgia rheumatica presents with persistent dull throbbing pain in the upper anterior region. Examination reveals a small fluctuant swelling palatally adjacent to the upper left lateral incisor. The tooth has a periapical lesion on X-ray. Which ONE of the following is the MOST important additional precaution compared to treating the same abscess in a patient NOT on steroids?
options: A. Use articaine instead of lidocaine — steroids reduce lidocaine effectiveness
B. Prescribe double the normal antibiotic dose — immunosuppression reduces antibiotic efficacy

C. Assess for the need for steroid supplementation (sick day rules / steroid cover) if the procedure is stressful — long-term steroids suppress the HPA axis and may impair the stress response during surgical procedures
D. Prescribe prophylactic antibiotics before extraction as IE prophylaxis is required for steroid-treated patients
E. Schedule the extraction at a higher adrenaline concentration LA to compensate for reduced vasodilation from steroids
applyIt.explanation: The key additional concern for patients on long-term systemic corticosteroids is adrenal suppression (hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis suppression). The HPA axis responds to physiological stress (infection, surgery, illness) by producing cortisol — which is essential for maintaining cardiovascular stability during stress. Patients taking ≥5mg prednisolone daily for >4 weeks may have inadequate HPA response to surgical stress → risk of adrenal crisis (hypotension, collapse, severe hypoglycaemia). Sick day rules/steroid cover: for minor dental procedures under LA, many patients can continue their normal steroid dose (minor procedures are generally not sufficiently stressful). For more invasive/prolonged procedures: consider doubling the steroid dose on the day or supplementing with additional hydrocortisone (consult guidelines and patient's specialist). This patient's dose of 15mg prednisolone daily is above the threshold for potential HPA suppression. LA type (A) and adrenaline concentration (E) are unaffected by steroid use. Double antibiotic dose (B) is not evidence-based. NICE NG178 does not indicate IE prophylaxis for steroid users (D).

applyIt.scenario: A clinical microbiology laboratory reports that a pus specimen from a dental abscess grew Streptococcus constellatus (S. milleri group). Sensitivity testing shows: sensitive to penicillin, clindamycin, metronidazole — resistant to amoxicillin. Which ONE of the following should the clinician be aware of?
options: A. S. constellatus is sensitive to metronidazole alone — this is adequate treatment

B. S. constellatus is an aerobic/microaerophilic streptococcus — metronidazole has NO activity against it; the sensitivity report is likely erroneous. S. constellatus is reliably sensitive to penicillin/amoxicillin — resistance is extremely rare and should prompt repeat testing
C. S. constellatus resistance to amoxicillin is common and expected — switch to metronidazole alone
D. S. constellatus is an obligate anaerobe fully covered by metronidazole
E. S. constellatus producing abscesses only requires drainage — no antibiotics are needed
applyIt.explanation: This question tests understanding of microbiology laboratory results in their clinical context. S. constellatus (S. milleri/S. anginosus group) is an aerobic/microaerophilic streptococcus. Critical microbiology fact: metronidazole has NO activity against aerobic or microaerophilic organisms — it requires anaerobic conditions to be activated. A sensitivity report showing S. constellatus 'sensitive to metronidazole' is a laboratory artefact and should not guide clinical prescribing. Additionally, S. constellatus is almost universally sensitive to amoxicillin — resistance to amoxicillin in streptococci is extremely rare and warrants repeat testing to exclude contamination or identification error. The clinician should: (1) Query the sensitivity results with the microbiologist; (2) Continue amoxicillin (or phenoxymethylpenicillin) as the appropriate antibiotic; (3) Add metronidazole only for any anaerobic co-pathogens present. Treating with metronidazole alone (A, C) would be pharmacologically inappropriate and potentially dangerous.

applyIt.scenario: A 68-year-old patient presents with a 2-week history of right-sided facial pain and pressure. He thinks it might be a dental problem. On examination, there are no teeth on the upper right that are tender to percussion or have any periapical pathology on X-ray. On further examination, small, non-vesicular grouped lesions are noticed on the hard palate and along the gingiva of the upper right quadrant. The pain precedes the lesions by approximately 1 week. Which ONE of the following is the MOST likely diagnosis?
options: A. Herpes labialis — prescribe topical aciclovir cream
B. Herpes zoster affecting the maxillary branch of the trigeminal nerve (V2) — prescribe aciclovir 800mg five times daily for 7 days
C. Trigeminal neuralgia — prescribe carbamazepine 100mg twice daily
D. Dentoalveolar abscess — refer for panoramic radiograph
E. Herpangina from Coxsackievirus — no specific treatment required
applyIt.explanation: The key diagnostic clues: (1) Pre-rash pain (prodrome of 1 week) preceding the skin/mucosal lesions — characteristic of herpes zoster, where VZV replication in the nerve causes pain before the characteristic rash appears; (2) Unilateral distribution confined to the maxillary (V2) territory (hard palate, upper right gingiva); (3) No dental cause identified; (4) Elderly patient (shingles is more common and more severe with age). Herpes zoster can mimic dental pain in the prodromal phase, leading to unnecessary dental investigations. The palatal and gingival lesions represent mucosal zoster. Treatment: aciclovir 800mg 5× daily for 7 days (or valaciclovir 1g TDS for 7 days), started within 72 hours of rash. Herpes labialis (A) is perioral/vermilion border — not palatal. Trigeminal neuralgia (C) causes episodic electric-shock pain, not a continuous aching prodrome with mucosal lesions. Dentoalveolar abscess (D) was excluded by examination. Herpangina (E) affects posterior oropharynx in children.

applyIt.scenario: A patient takes simvastatin 40mg daily. She develops an acute dental infection and the dentist correctly prescribes amoxicillin 500mg TDS. She also requires metronidazole 400mg TDS. A dental nurse colleague suggests adding clarithromycin for broader cover. Which ONE of the following BEST explains why clarithromycin should be avoided in this patient?
options: A. Clarithromycin is absolutely contraindicated with amoxicillin — severe beta-lactam interaction
B. Clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4, the enzyme that metabolises simvastatin — statin levels rise dramatically, risking skeletal muscle damage (myopathy, rhabdomyolysis)
C. Clarithromycin is not active against the organisms causing dental infections
D. Clarithromycin combined with metronidazole causes a disulfiram reaction if the patient drinks alcohol
E. Clarithromycin is contraindicated only if the patient also has renal failure
applyIt.explanation: This tests knowledge of a critical drug-drug interaction. Clarithromycin is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor. Simvastatin (and lovastatin, atorvastatin) are primarily metabolised by CYP3A4. Co-administration with a CYP3A4 inhibitor → statin plasma levels increase dramatically (up to 10-fold) → myopathy risk (muscle pain, weakness) → rhabdomyolysis (severe skeletal muscle breakdown) → myoglobinuria → acute kidney injury — can be life-threatening. Safe alternatives for this patient: (1) Azithromycin (minimal CYP3A4 inhibition — no significant statin interaction); (2) Erythromycin is also a CYP3A4 inhibitor but less potent than clarithromycin (still caution warranted). Statin-sparing antibiotics: clindamycin (no CYP3A4 interaction), doxycycline (no significant statin interaction), fluoroquinolones. No amoxicillin-clarithromycin interaction (A). Clarithromycin does cover dental organisms (C). The disulfiram reaction (D) is metronidazole + alcohol, not clarithromycin. Renal failure (E) is not the primary concern here.

applyIt.scenario: A 50-year-old patient presents for a routine check-up. During the consultation, she mentions she has been on hydroxychloroquine and low-dose prednisolone 5mg daily for systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) for the past 8 years. Which oral conditions should the dentist be specifically vigilant for?
options: A. Denture stomatitis only — corticosteroids cause excess saliva production promoting candidal growth
B. Periodontal disease, oral candidiasis (from prednisolone-induced immunosuppression), oral ulcers (SLE-specific oral lesions), and osteonecrosis of the jaw (from long-term hydroxychloroquine)

C. Oral candidiasis (from immunosuppression), SLE-associated oral ulcers and lichenoid lesions, salivary gland hypofunction (xerostomia from SLE or hydroxychloroquine), and increased caries risk from xerostomia
D. No specific oral conditions — SLE only affects the skin and joints
E. Oral hairy leukoplakia from EBV reactivation — all patients on prednisolone develop this
applyIt.explanation: SLE with hydroxychloroquine and prednisolone presents multiple oral health considerations: (1) SLE oral manifestations: painless oral ulcers (especially on the palate — a recognised diagnostic criterion for SLE), discoid lupus lesions in the mouth (lichenoid appearance), angular cheilitis; (2) Prednisolone-induced immunosuppression: increased susceptibility to oral candidiasis (candida overgrowth), impaired wound healing; (3) Xerostomia: occurs in ~30% of SLE patients from salivary gland involvement (secondary Sjögren's syndrome is common) — hydroxychloroquine can also cause xerostomia; (4) Xerostomia consequences: increased caries risk, periodontal disease, difficulty wearing dentures; (5) Bisphosphonate MRONJ: hydroxychloroquine does NOT cause MRONJ — that is bisphosphonates (B is wrong about hydroxychloroquine/osteonecrosis). OHL (E) is associated with severe immunosuppression (AIDS) — not typical at prednisolone 5mg. Prednisolone actually REDUCES saliva production via immunosuppression, not increases it (A).

applyIt.scenario: A 72-year-old man returns from a 3-week trip to rural Southeast Asia. Within 2 weeks of returning, he develops high fever (40°C), rigors, severe headache, and myalgia. Peripheral blood film shows Plasmodium falciparum with >10% parasitaemia. Which ONE of the following MOST accurately identifies the infection type and the dental team's responsibility?
options: A. Malaria is a bacterial infection — prescribe ciprofloxacin immediately

B. This is severe P. falciparum malaria — a medical emergency. Dental treatment should be deferred, the patient needs emergency hospital admission for IV artesunate. Any planned dental procedures should wait until the patient has fully recovered
C. The dental team should perform extraction as planned — the malaria does not affect the oral cavity
D. Prescribe chloroquine for the malaria alongside any dental treatment — chloroquine is the first-line treatment
E. Reassure the patient — P. falciparum malaria is self-limiting and does not require specific treatment
applyIt.explanation: Severe P. falciparum malaria is a life-threatening emergency. With >10% parasitaemia, this patient has severe malaria with risk of: cerebral malaria, acute respiratory distress syndrome, acute kidney injury, DIC, hypoglycaemia, and death (case fatality rate 10–30% even with treatment). Medical treatment: IV artesunate is the WHO/UK recommended first-line treatment for severe falciparum malaria (replacing IV quinine). Dental considerations: (1) Defer ALL non-emergency dental treatment — patient is critically unwell and would likely deteriorate with any additional physiological stress; (2) Thrombocytopaenia (very common in malaria — platelet count often <50 × 10⁹/L in severe cases) increases bleeding risk; (3) Patient needs immediate emergency referral/hospital admission — the dental team's role is to recognise this as a medical emergency and not proceed with dental treatment. Malaria is a parasitic infection (Plasmodium), not bacterial (A). Chloroquine (D) has widespread resistance in P. falciparum — not first-line. It is not self-limiting (E).

applyIt.scenario: A 48-year-old patient requires extraction of a root-treated upper molar. He discloses he was diagnosed with bisphosphonate-related osteonecrosis of the jaw (BRONJ/MRONJ) 2 years ago, following extraction of a lower molar while on IV zoledronic acid for prostate cancer metastases. The upper molar is causing recurrent infection. Which ONE of the following represents the MOST appropriate management?
options: A. Extract the tooth immediately in the practice — root-treated teeth are low MRONJ risk

B. Refer to the maxillofacial unit for assessment — this patient is at very high risk of further MRONJ and the extraction must be planned with specialist input, antibiotic cover, and primary wound closure
C. Prescribe long-term antibiotics to manage the infection conservatively — extraction is absolutely contraindicated in this patient for life
D. Stop the zoledronic acid for 6 months before extraction — drug holiday eliminates MRONJ risk
E. Extract the tooth and prescribe hyperbaric oxygen therapy immediately post-operatively
applyIt.explanation: This is one of the highest-risk scenarios for MRONJ in dentistry. The patient: (1) Has a history of established MRONJ following previous extraction — the highest risk factor; (2) Is/was on IV zoledronic acid for oncological indication (highest MRONJ risk drug — approximately 1% risk per procedure, vs 0.001% with oral bisphosphonates for osteoporosis); (3) Requires extraction of an infected tooth — extraction is the commonest MRONJ trigger. Management: specialist maxillofacial unit referral is mandatory. If extraction cannot be avoided: (1) Antibiotic prophylaxis (amoxicillin 3g or clindamycin 600mg pre-operatively + prolonged post-operative course); (2) Atraumatic extraction technique; (3) Primary wound closure (suturing flap over the socket); (4) Chlorhexidine mouthwash; (5) Close follow-up for 8 weeks. A drug holiday (D) from zoledronic acid is NOT recommended — bisphosphonates remain in bone for years regardless of cessation. Extraction is not absolutely contraindicated (C) but requires specialist assessment. Immediate practice extraction (A) is inappropriate for this risk profile.

writeIt.prompt: Describe the replication cycle of HIV in a CD4+ T-cell. Include the role of each of the three essential viral enzymes, and explain how antiretroviral drugs can target these steps.

markScheme.point: HIV attaches to CD4 receptor and co-receptor (CCR5 or CXCR4) via surface glycoproteins GP120 and GP41
Membrane fusion occurs; viral nucleocapsid enters host cell and releases two RNA strands and three enzymes
Reverse transcriptase converts single-stranded RNA into double-stranded DNA (via RNA-DNA hybrid intermediate, RNA degraded by ribonuclease H domain)
Integrase cleaves dinucleotides from each 3' end of viral DNA and facilitates integration into host cell genome (provirus); this is permanent
Proviral DNA is transcribed into mRNA; viral proteins synthesised; protease cleaves longer polyprotein into functional structural and enzymatic components
New virions assemble; bud through cell membrane acquiring new envelope; mature into infectious particles

Antiretroviral targeting: entry — fusion inhibitors (block GP41); reverse transcriptase — NRTIs (nucleoside analogues incorporated into DNA, halt elongation) or NNRTIs (bind and alter reverse transcriptase directly)
Antiretroviral targeting: integrase inhibitors (prevent integration into host genome); protease inhibitors (prevent protein processing — immature non-infectious virions produced)
