D. IgA — produced exclusively in response to bloodborne infections
E. IgD — the main antibody in the late phase of primary immune response
mockBank.explanation: IgM is produced first (days 5–10) and is detectable early in primary infection. By week 2–3, class switching occurs and IgG becomes predominant. IgG has higher affinity and specificity, longer half-life, and provides long-term protection. The IgM-to-IgG switch is diagnostically useful — detectable IgM indicates recent infection.

mockBank.question: A patient is infected with a bacterium that replicates intracellularly. Which immune effector mechanism is MOST important for clearing this infection?
options: A. Antibody-mediated neutralisation (humoral immunity)
B. Complement membrane attack complex
C. CD8+ cytotoxic T lymphocytes eliminating infected cells, supported by Th1 CD4+ T cells
D. NK cells independently clearing all intracellular infections without T cell help
E. IgE-mediated eosinophil activation
mockBank.explanation: Intracellular pathogens (M. tuberculosis, Listeria, viruses) present antigen via MHC Class I to CD8+ CTLs, which kill infected cells by perforin/granzyme-mediated apoptosis. Th1 CD4+ T cells provide cytokine help (IFN-γ activates macrophages). Antibodies cannot reach intracellular pathogens — humoral immunity (A/B) is primarily effective against extracellular organisms.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following viruses is classified as a single-stranded DNA virus in the Baltimore classification?
options: A. Influenza
B. Hepatitis B virus
C. Parvovirus
D. Rotavirus
E. HIV
mockBank.explanation: Parvovirus B19 is a single-stranded DNA virus — causes fifth disease (erythema infectiosum) in children and aplastic crisis in patients with sickle cell disease. Influenza is ssRNA; HBV is unusual (partially dsDNA, replicates via RNA intermediate); rotavirus is dsRNA; HIV is a retrovirus (ssRNA).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following statements about viral envelopes is CORRECT?
options: A. All viruses have an envelope derived from the host cell nucleus
B. Enveloped viruses are more resistant to drying and disinfection than non-enveloped viruses
C. The viral envelope is derived from the host cell membrane and contains viral glycoproteins needed for cell entry
D. Non-enveloped viruses are always more virulent than enveloped viruses
E. The envelope contains viral nucleic acid directly, without a capsid
mockBank.explanation: The viral envelope is a lipid bilayer acquired from the host cell membrane during viral budding. It is studded with viral glycoproteins essential for binding to host cell surface receptors and mediating membrane fusion during entry. Why B is wrong: enveloped viruses are MORE fragile — the lipid membrane is disrupted by drying, heat, detergents, and disinfectants. Non-enveloped viruses survive in the environment much longer.

mockBank.question: A patient develops measles after exposure at a party. The rash resolves and she recovers fully. She is then re-exposed to measles 5 years later but does not become infected. Which ONE of the following BEST explains her immunity?
options: A. She had a persistent infection with measles virus providing ongoing immunity
B. Her innate immune system became permanently sensitised to measles antigens
C. IgG memory B cells from her primary infection rapidly produce high-affinity antibodies preventing re-infection
D. Her CD4 count rose to supranormal levels following infection, preventing viral entry
E. Measles only infects patients once due to viral latency
mockBank.explanation: Measles causes an acute infection that is cleared by the immune system (not latent). Long-term immunity is provided by memory B cells and memory T cells from the primary infection. Upon re-exposure, memory B cells rapidly differentiate into plasma cells producing high-affinity IgG — the antibody response is faster, stronger, and more specific than the primary response, neutralising virus before symptoms develop.

mockBank.question: A patient presents 2 days after onset of vesicular oral lesions. Which diagnostic technique would BEST confirm HSV infection at this stage?
options: A. Serology for HSV IgG — confirms prior infection
B. Serology for HSV IgM — indicates recent infection
C. PCR of a swab from the lesion — detects viral DNA directly within 24 hours
D. Complement fixation test — traditional gold standard for viral diagnosis
E. Viral culture — allows sensitivity testing of the isolated virus
mockBank.explanation: In acute HSV infection (days 1–3), PCR from the lesion swab is the gold standard — it detects viral DNA rapidly and accurately before an antibody response has developed. IgM (B) takes several days to appear; IgG (A) is positive with prior infection, not specific for current active disease. Culture (E) takes too long and requires a special viral transport swab.

mockBank.question: A 5-year-old child develops extensive painful intraoral vesicles and ulcers, refuses to eat, and has a temperature of 38.8°C. This is her first such episode. Which ONE of the following is the MOST likely diagnosis?
options: A. Recurrent herpes labialis (cold sore)
B. Varicella (chickenpox) — oral stage
C. Primary herpetic gingivostomatitis (HSV-1)
D. Herpangina (Coxsackie A)
E. Hand, foot and mouth disease
mockBank.explanation: Herpetic gingivostomatitis — primary HSV-1 — classically presents in children under 6 with widespread oral ulceration, fever, and difficulty eating. It is the most severe manifestation of primary HSV infection. Recurrent herpes labialis (A) occurs at the lip border in adults. Herpangina (D) affects the posterior soft palate/tonsillar pillars. HFMD (E) has palmar/plantar lesions.

mockBank.question: Aciclovir has poor oral bioavailability, typically requiring 5 doses daily. Which drug solves this problem and by what mechanism?
options: A. Ganciclovir — more potent than aciclovir and therefore requires less frequent dosing
B. Valaciclovir — a prodrug of aciclovir that is better absorbed orally and converted to aciclovir after absorption
C. Zidovudine — an alternative thymidine analogue with better bioavailability
D. Idoxuridine — converted to aciclovir after oral absorption
E. Foscarnet — bypasses the need for thymidine kinase activation
mockBank.explanation: Valaciclovir is the L-valyl ester prodrug of aciclovir. After oral absorption, it is rapidly hydrolysed to aciclovir by intestinal wall and hepatic esterases. Oral bioavailability ~55% (vs ~15–30% for aciclovir), allowing twice-daily dosing for most indications. This improves patient compliance significantly.

mockBank.question: A 28-year-old pregnant woman (32 weeks) has never had chickenpox. She is exposed to a child with active varicella at a family gathering (face-to-face contact for 20 minutes). She has no detectable VZV antibodies on testing. What is the MOST appropriate post-exposure management?
options: A. Live attenuated varicella vaccine within 3 days
B. Aciclovir prophylaxis for 10 days
C. No action required — VZV is not serious in pregnancy
D. Zoster immunoglobulin (VZIG) IM as soon as possible
E. Advise isolation and give oral aciclovir if symptoms develop
mockBank.explanation: Non-immune pregnant women are high-risk contacts for VZV — varicella in pregnancy risks maternal pneumonitis (up to 10% mortality) and neonatal varicella. VZIG (zoster immunoglobulin) provides passive immunity and must be given as soon as possible after significant exposure (face-to-face contact in VZV-non-immune contact). The live vaccine (A) is CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy.

mockBank.question: Which herpes virus reactivates to cause shingles, and from which anatomical location does reactivation occur?
options: A. HSV-1 reactivates from the trigeminal ganglion
B. VZV reactivates from the dorsal root ganglia or cranial nerve ganglia
C. EBV reactivates from memory T lymphocytes
D. CMV reactivates from hepatocytes
E. HHV-8 reactivates from the epidermis
mockBank.explanation: After primary chickenpox, VZV becomes latent in dorsal root ganglia (spinal cord) and cranial nerve ganglia (including the trigeminal ganglion). Reactivation causes shingles — a dermatomal rash corresponding to the nerve territory where VZV was latent. Trigeminal ganglion reactivation causes facial/oral shingles.

mockBank.question: A 35-year-old dental nurse develops painful vesicular lesions on her right index finger 3 days after treating a patient with active herpes labialis while not wearing gloves. Which ONE of the following is the MOST likely diagnosis?
options: A. Paronychia (bacterial infection of the nail fold)
B. Herpetic whitlow (HSV-1 infection of the finger)
C. Shingles (VZV) involving the digit
D. Varicella (chickenpox) — localised to the finger only
E. Tinea manuum (dermatophyte infection)
mockBank.explanation: Herpetic whitlow is HSV-1 (or HSV-2) infection of the finger, acquired by direct contact with oral/genital HSV lesions. Classic in healthcare workers who examine patients without gloves. It presents with an intensely painful vesicular lesion at the fingertip. Treatment: systemic aciclovir. Prevention: gloves at all times when examining oral lesions.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following is TRUE about Epstein-Barr virus (EBV)?
options: A. EBV exclusively infects T lymphocytes and establishes latency there
B. EBV causes glandular fever in children but is typically asymptomatic in adolescents
C. EBV is associated with Burkitt's lymphoma, nasopharyngeal carcinoma, and oral hairy leukoplakia
D. EBV is transmitted primarily by the blood-borne route
E. EBV encephalitis is the most common complication of primary EBV infection
mockBank.explanation: EBV is associated with Burkitt's lymphoma (endemic form in Africa), nasopharyngeal carcinoma, Hodgkin's lymphoma, and oral hairy leukoplakia (in HIV/immunosuppression). Why A is wrong: EBV infects B lymphocytes (and epithelial cells), not T cells. Why B is wrong: EBV is typically asymptomatic in young children but causes glandular fever in adolescents/young adults.

mockBank.question: What is the role of CCR5 co-receptor in HIV entry and what is its clinical relevance?
options: A. CCR5 is expressed by all cells and enables HIV to infect any cell type
B. CCR5 is used as a co-receptor by macrophage-tropic (R5) HIV strains; CCR5 antagonists block entry as a therapeutic strategy
C. CCR5 is the primary HIV receptor; CD4 is only a co-receptor
D. CCR5 mutations increase HIV susceptibility — patients with CCR5 mutations always progress faster to AIDS
E. CCR5 is only present on NK cells and is not relevant to T-cell infection
mockBank.explanation: Most transmitted HIV strains (R5 strains) use CCR5 alongside CD4 for entry into macrophages and T cells. Individuals with homozygous CCR5-Δ32 mutation have no functional CCR5 and are highly resistant to HIV infection. Maraviroc, a CCR5 antagonist, is an antiretroviral drug that blocks this co-receptor, preventing viral entry.

mockBank.question: A patient newly diagnosed with HIV has a CD4 count of 450 cells/µL and undetectable viral load after 6 months of HAART. The patient asks when their partner can consider them 'non-infectious' for HIV. Which ONE of the following is the MOST accurate response?
options: A. They remain infectious for life regardless of viral load
B. Infectious risk is negligible when viral load is consistently undetectable — 'Undetectable = Untransmittable' (U=U)
C. They need a CD4 count above 500 before being considered non-infectious
D. They are not infectious only if their CD4 count and viral load have been normal for 2 consecutive years
E. HIV transmission is only prevented by condom use — viral load status is irrelevant
mockBank.explanation: PARTNER 2 study (2019) and HPTN 052 study confirmed that people living with HIV with consistently undetectable viral load on HAART do not transmit HIV sexually — U=U (Treatment as Prevention, TasP). This is now WHO-endorsed. Viral suppression is the most effective HIV prevention strategy available.

mockBank.question: A patient with untreated HIV presents with white corrugated lesions on the lateral borders of the tongue that cannot be wiped off. Which ONE of the following BEST describes this lesion?
options: A. Pseudomembranous candidiasis — treat with nystatin
B. Leukoplakia — refer under 2-week wait for biopsy to exclude malignancy
C. Oral hairy leukoplakia — EBV-associated, an HIV indicator condition, generally requires no specific treatment
D. Lichen planus — treat with topical corticosteroids
E. Frictional keratosis — reassure and review
mockBank.explanation: Oral hairy leukoplakia (OHL) is caused by EBV reactivation in the lateral tongue epithelium. Characteristic corrugated 'hairy' appearance, cannot be wiped off, and is usually asymptomatic. It is an HIV indicator condition — any patient with OHL and unknown HIV status should be offered testing. OHL generally resolves with HAART and does not require specific antiviral treatment.

mockBank.question: A dental nurse sustains a deep needlestick injury from a needle used on a patient with HIV whose viral load is detectable (50,000 copies/mL). She is HIV-negative. What is the CORRECT first step in her management?
options: A. Immediately take a blood sample from both patient and nurse to confirm HIV status
B. Encourage bleeding, wash the wound with soap and water, and report immediately — start PEP within 72 hours
C. Apply tourniquet to the injured finger and refer to A&E
D. PEP is only required if the source patient is confirmed HIV-positive by Western Blot
E. A single dose of antiretroviral therapy is sufficient for prevention
mockBank.explanation: Immediate management: encourage bleeding (do NOT suck), wash wound with soap and water, report immediately to the occupational health or senior team. PEP (28-day antiretroviral course) must be started within 72 hours (ideally <1 hour) for maximum efficacy. The source is known HIV-positive with detectable VL — PEP is clearly indicated. A tourniquet (C) is contraindicated.

mockBank.question: A 42-year-old intravenous drug user is found to be anti-HCV antibody positive on routine screening. His ALT is elevated at 85 U/L. He is asymptomatic. Which ONE of the following statements is MOST accurate?
options: A. A positive anti-HCV antibody confirms he has active hepatitis C infection
B. A positive anti-HCV antibody requires confirmatory HCV RNA PCR to distinguish active infection from past resolved infection
C. He should be treated with interferon and ribavirin as soon as possible
D. Hepatitis C is a DNA virus that integrates into the genome, making it difficult to cure
E. He has protective immunity and cannot be re-infected with HCV
mockBank.explanation: Anti-HCV antibody confirms exposure to HCV but cannot distinguish active infection from resolved past infection. HCV RNA PCR is required to confirm active viraemia. If PCR is positive, direct-acting antivirals (protease + polymerase inhibitors) should be offered — cure rates >90%. Why D is wrong: HCV is an RNA virus that does NOT integrate into the genome. Why E is wrong: previous HCV infection does not provide protective immunity — re-infection is possible.

mockBank.question: A 55-year-old with alcoholic cirrhosis and known oesophageal varices requires a tooth extraction. Pre-operatively, his INR is 2.1 and platelet count is 75 ×10⁹/L. Which ONE of the following statements about haemostasis is MOST accurate?
options: A. His bleeding risk is normal because the INR of 2.1 reflects warfarin use, not liver disease
B. His elevated INR reflects impaired hepatic synthesis of coagulation factors (especially II, VII, IX, X); low platelets from portal hypertension — both increase bleeding risk
C. His platelets are elevated due to portal hypertension, reducing bleeding risk
D. Only the INR matters for dental extractions — platelet count is irrelevant below 100
E. Vitamin K supplementation will fully correct his coagulopathy
mockBank.explanation: Liver disease causes a dual haemostatic defect: (1) Reduced synthesis of vitamin K-dependent clotting factors (II, VII, IX, X) → prolonged PT/INR; (2) Thrombocytopenia from portal hypertension → splenic sequestration of platelets. Both increase post-extraction bleeding risk. Vitamin K supplementation (E) may partially correct INR but cannot restore coagulation factor synthesis in severe cirrhosis — fresh frozen plasma or clotting factors are needed.

mockBank.question: A patient with decompensated liver cirrhosis presents for urgent dental treatment. Their medication list includes: spironolactone, propranolol, lactulose. Which dental drug combination is MOST appropriate?
options: A. Diazepam 5mg oral premedication, ibuprofen 400mg post-op analgesia, fluconazole for oral thrush
B. Articaine 4% for LA, codeine 30mg for post-op analgesia, nystatin for oral thrush
C. Lidocaine 2% standard dose, paracetamol 1g post-op, metronidazole if infection present
D. Midazolam IV sedation, aspirin 300mg for analgesia, erythromycin for antibiotic cover
E. Prilocaine 3% for LA, diclofenac 50mg post-op, clindamycin for antibiotic cover
mockBank.explanation: B is safest: articaine (partially plasma-metabolised, safer in liver disease), codeine (use cautiously — hepatically metabolised but at lower risk than paracetamol or NSAIDs), nystatin (topical, no systemic absorption, safe). Why A is wrong: diazepam (→ coma), ibuprofen (→ GI bleed/renal failure). Why C is wrong: paracetamol is ABSOLUTELY contraindicated in significant liver disease. Why D is wrong: midazolam IV + cirrhosis = encephalopathy/coma; aspirin → GI bleed. Why E is wrong: diclofenac (NSAID) and propranolol interaction risk.

mockBank.question: What is the dental significance of a patient being HBsAg positive?
options: A. The patient cannot receive local anaesthetic safely
B. Dental treatment must be deferred until HBV is fully treated
C. Standard universal infection control precautions are used; there are no additional restrictions on dental treatment
D. The patient requires general anaesthesia for all dental procedures to reduce exposure risk
E. Antibiotic prophylaxis is mandatory before every dental procedure
mockBank.explanation: HBsAg-positive patients are treated with standard universal precautions — gloves, mask, protective eyewear, safe sharps management. There is no restriction on dental treatment and no need for additional precautions beyond those applied to ALL patients. Healthcare workers should ensure their HBV vaccination status is confirmed (HBsAb titre ≥10 mIU/mL). All dental staff should be vaccinated.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following best explains why serology is preferred for diagnosing chronic HIV infection, while PCR is preferred for acute HIV?
options: A. PCR can only detect RNA viruses; HIV becomes DNA after integration so serology is needed later
B. In acute infection, virus is detectable before antibodies develop (PCR detects RNA); in chronic infection, viral load is suppressed but antibodies persist
C. Serology is preferred in acute infection because it is faster; PCR is only used for monitoring
D. PCR is too expensive for routine chronic disease monitoring; serology provides cost-effective long-term surveillance
E. Serology and PCR provide identical information — choice is purely based on laboratory availability
mockBank.explanation: During acute HIV: high viral replication, virus detectable by PCR before IgM/IgG develop (window period — ~10 days for PCR vs ~30–45 days for antibody tests). Fourth-generation tests combine p24 antigen + antibody to shorten the window. In established chronic HIV: antibody tests confirm diagnosis; PCR is used to measure viral load and monitor treatment response.

mockBank.question: A patient presents with painful trismus, fluctuant swelling in the submandibular space, and difficulty swallowing, 5 days after a lower molar extraction. Temperature 38.7°C. Which ONE of the following is the MOST serious concern?
options: A. Developing dental caries in adjacent teeth
B. Ludwig's angina — a potentially life-threatening spreading cellulitis involving the floor of mouth with risk of airway compromise
C. A localised submandibular abscess that will resolve with oral antibiotics
D. Dry socket — treat with Alvogyl dressing and analgesia
E. Pericoronitis — prescribe metronidazole 400mg TDS
mockBank.explanation: Ludwig's angina is a bilateral spreading cellulitis of the submandibular, submental, and sublingual spaces. Features: trismus, floor of mouth swelling, brawny induration (not fluctuant), dysphagia, drooling. Life-threatening: oedema can displace the tongue posteriorly causing fatal airway obstruction. Requires urgent hospital admission, IV antibiotics, surgical drainage, and anaesthetic standby for airway management.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following conditions does a dentist have a LEGAL DUTY to report to the local Public Health Authority?
options: A. Oral candidiasis
B. Herpes labialis
C. Mumps
D. Dental abscess
E. Angular cheilitis
mockBank.explanation: Mumps is a notifiable disease in the UK — dentists encountering swollen salivary glands suggestive of mumps must report to the local health protection team. Other notifiable diseases relevant to dental practice: tuberculosis, scarlet fever, measles, whooping cough, gonorrhoea, hepatitis B. Oral opportunistic infections and dental pathology are not notifiable.

mockBank.question: On Gram staining, Gram-positive bacteria appear purple because:
options: A. Their outer membrane absorbs more of the safranin counterstain
B. Their thick peptidoglycan cell wall retains crystal violet dye and resists decolourisation by alcohol
C. They contain more lipopolysaccharide than Gram-negative bacteria
D. They are larger than Gram-negative bacteria, trapping more dye non-specifically
E. Their inner cell membrane binds crystal violet irreversibly
mockBank.explanation: Gram-positive bacteria have a single, thick (20–80 nm) peptidoglycan cell wall with no outer membrane. After crystal violet staining and iodine fixing, alcohol decolourisation cannot penetrate the thick peptidoglycan — the dye is retained and cells appear purple. Gram-negative bacteria have a thin peptidoglycan layer plus an outer lipopolysaccharide membrane; alcohol dissolves this outer membrane allowing dye washout — cells stain pink with safranin counterstain.

mockBank.question: Rifampicin is used as part of the quadruple therapy for tuberculosis. Which ONE of the following correctly describes its mechanism of action?
options: A. Inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase, preventing DNA replication
B. Inhibits bacterial RNA polymerase, blocking transcription of mRNA from DNA
C. Inhibits bacterial translation at the 50S ribosomal subunit
D. Blocks peptidoglycan cross-linkage by binding penicillin-binding proteins
E. Causes toxic metabolite production that directly damages bacterial DNA
mockBank.explanation: Rifampicin inhibits bacterial DNA-dependent RNA polymerase (the β-subunit), blocking the initiation of mRNA transcription. Human RNA polymerase is structurally different and not affected. This is highly selective. Important interaction: rifampicin induces hepatic CYP450 enzymes, drastically reducing plasma levels of many drugs including oral contraceptives, warfarin, and antiretrovirals.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following antibiotics works by disrupting bacterial cell membrane integrity, causing leakage of cell contents?
options: A. Vancomycin
B. Ciprofloxacin
C. Polymyxin B
D. Tetracycline
E. Erythromycin
mockBank.explanation: Polymyxins (polymyxin B and colistin) are lipopeptide antibiotics that bind to lipopolysaccharide in the outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria, disrupting membrane integrity and causing leakage of cytoplasmic contents. They are used as a last resort for MDR Gram-negative infections. Vancomycin (A) disrupts peptidoglycan; ciprofloxacin (B) targets topoisomerase; tetracycline (D) inhibits 30S ribosome; erythromycin (E) inhibits 50S.

mockBank.question: A patient develops C. difficile colitis after treatment with clindamycin. Which ONE of the following BEST explains why clindamycin causes this?
options: A. Clindamycin directly stimulates C. difficile toxin production
B. Clindamycin selectively kills C. difficile's natural competitors in the colon, allowing overgrowth and toxin-mediated colitis
C. Clindamycin is metabolised to a compound that is structurally similar to C. difficile toxin A
D. C. difficile is always present in clindamycin preparations as a contaminant
E. Clindamycin is an aminoglycoside that damages colonic mucosa directly
mockBank.explanation: Clindamycin has broad anaerobic and aerobic Gram-positive coverage, killing the protective anaerobic commensals (Bacteroides, Clostridium species) that normally suppress C. difficile. In their absence, C. difficile (spores of which are ubiquitous) germinates, proliferates, and produces toxins A and B causing pseudomembranous colitis. Any broad-spectrum antibiotic can cause this, but clindamycin has the highest risk.

mockBank.question: A 45-year-old patient takes atorvastatin 40mg daily for hyperlipidaemia. He requires antibiotic treatment for an acute dentoalveolar abscess. Which ONE of the following antibiotic choices carries the GREATEST risk of an adverse interaction?
options: A. Penicillin V 500mg QDS — narrow spectrum, minimal interactions
B. Metronidazole 400mg TDS — interacts with warfarin and alcohol but not statins
C. Clarithromycin 250mg BD — CYP3A4 inhibitor causing atorvastatin accumulation with risk of rhabdomyolysis
D. Amoxicillin 500mg TDS — broad spectrum but minimal statin interaction
E. Doxycycline 100mg OD — tetracycline with no statin interaction
mockBank.explanation: Clarithromycin is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor. Atorvastatin is metabolised by CYP3A4 — clarithromycin inhibits this metabolism, causing accumulation of atorvastatin and increased risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis (muscle breakdown → renal failure). This interaction can be life-threatening. In this patient, use penicillin V, metronidazole, or amoxicillin instead.

mockBank.question: For antibiotic prophylaxis before a dental extraction in an immunocompromised patient (not penicillin-allergic), what is the correct drug, dose, and timing?
options: A. Amoxicillin 1g one hour before the procedure
B. Amoxicillin 3g one hour before the procedure
C. Penicillin V 500mg QDS starting 2 days before the procedure
D. Metronidazole 400mg one hour before the procedure
E. Amoxicillin 500mg given immediately after the procedure
mockBank.explanation: Antibiotic prophylaxis for immunocompromised patients (or patients with previous head and neck radiotherapy): amoxicillin 3g orally, 1 hour before the procedure. This achieves peak plasma levels at the time of the surgical wound formation. The clot becomes impenetrable to antibiotics within 3 hours of formation — timing is critical. Penicillin-allergic alternative: clindamycin 600mg 1 hour before.

mockBank.question: A patient taking methotrexate for rheumatoid arthritis requires antibiotic treatment for a dental infection. Which ONE of the following antibiotics should be AVOIDED due to increased methotrexate toxicity risk?
options: A. Metronidazole — causes methotrexate accumulation in the CNS
B. Penicillin V — reduces methotrexate renal excretion, raising plasma levels
C. Clindamycin — directly increases methotrexate hepatotoxicity
D. Nystatin — systemic absorption competes with methotrexate for plasma protein binding
E. Fluconazole — induces methotrexate metabolism
mockBank.explanation: Penicillins (and tetracyclines) can reduce the renal tubular secretion of methotrexate, leading to its accumulation and toxicity (bone marrow suppression, mucositis, renal failure). The interaction is most significant with higher doses of methotrexate used for cancer, but should still be noted even at lower rheumatology doses. Contact the prescribing physician before prescribing penicillin in a methotrexate patient.

mockBank.question: A breastfeeding mother requires antibiotic treatment. Which ONE of the following antibiotics is MOST appropriate, with the LEAST risk to the infant?
options: A. Metronidazole — concentrated in breast milk; single dose less risk than prolonged course
B. Doxycycline — safe in breastfeeding despite pregnancy restrictions
C. Amoxicillin — present in breast milk at low concentrations; safe for the infant
D. Clarithromycin — safe in breastfeeding; no restrictions
E. Clindamycin — high breast milk concentration makes it safe for infant
mockBank.explanation: Amoxicillin transfers into breast milk at low concentrations — risk to infant is low. Monitor infant for diarrhoea/candidal nappy rash. Why A is wrong: metronidazole is present in breast milk at significant concentrations; single-dose use is less of a concern but prolonged courses should be avoided or pumped-and-discarded. Why B is wrong: doxycycline binds calcium in teeth/bones of newborn. Why D is wrong: clarithromycin should be avoided in lactation.

mockBank.question: A patient on warfarin is found to have oral candidiasis. The dentist prescribes nystatin suspension. Which ONE of the following best describes the interaction between nystatin and warfarin?
options: A. Nystatin markedly increases warfarin effect — INR recheck essential
B. Nystatin slightly decreases warfarin absorption if taken simultaneously
C. There is no clinically significant interaction — nystatin is topically active with negligible systemic absorption
D. Nystatin inhibits CYP2C9 moderately, requiring INR monitoring every 2 weeks
E. Nystatin is contraindicated with warfarin due to risk of haemorrhage
mockBank.explanation: Nystatin is a polyene antifungal that acts exclusively topically — it is not absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract. It has no systemic distribution and therefore no pharmacokinetic interactions with warfarin. Nystatin is the SAFE antifungal choice for patients on warfarin or anticoagulants. Contrast with miconazole gel (absolute contraindication) and fluconazole (moderate interaction requiring INR monitoring).

mockBank.question: A patient takes amoxicillin for a dental infection and develops a widespread maculopapular rash 5 days later. He is also taking allopurinol for gout. Which ONE of the following is the MOST likely explanation?
options: A. True penicillin allergy — the patient has had a classic IgE-mediated anaphylactic reaction
B. The rash is from the dental infection resolving — no drug reaction has occurred
C. Amoxicillin + allopurinol interaction causing an increased rate of ampicillin-type rash
D. The patient has developed Steven-Johnson syndrome requiring hospitalisation
E. Amoxicillin is contraindicated in patients taking gout medications
mockBank.explanation: The combination of amoxicillin (or ampicillin) with allopurinol significantly increases the incidence of maculopapular skin rash — approximately 3× more common than with amoxicillin alone. The mechanism is not fully understood. This rash is NOT a true IgE-mediated penicillin allergy and does not indicate future anaphylaxis risk from penicillin. However, if in doubt, referral for allergy testing is appropriate.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following statements about the commensal flora of the oral cavity is CORRECT?
options: A. Commensal oral bacteria are harmless and cannot cause disease under any circumstances
B. Commensal bacteria help prevent infection by competitive exclusion of pathogenic organisms
C. Antibiotics improve oral health by eliminating all oral bacteria including harmful ones
D. The oral commensal flora is identical in all healthy adults regardless of diet
E. Commensal oral bacteria have no virulence factors and cannot form biofilm
mockBank.explanation: Commensal oral bacteria occupy ecological niches, produce bacteriocins, and compete for nutrients — preventing colonisation by more pathogenic organisms. Disrupting this balance (e.g., with broad-spectrum antibiotics) allows opportunistic organisms (Candida, C. difficile) to flourish. Commensal bacteria CAN become pathogenic when host defences are compromised (e.g., oral streptococci causing endocarditis).

mockBank.question: The shingles vaccine is offered to adults aged 70 in the UK. Which ONE of the following BEST explains the rationale for this age group?
options: A. VZV first infects people aged 70, so vaccination at this age prevents primary infection
B. Most people aged 70 have never been exposed to VZV and require vaccination
C. VZV-specific T cell immunity wanes with age (immunosenescence); boosting at 70 prevents reactivation
D. The shingles vaccine contains a novel VZV strain that provides broader protection than childhood chickenpox immunity
E. The shingles vaccine replaces the need for antiviral treatment in this age group
mockBank.explanation: By age 70, most UK adults have had chickenpox (>95% seropositive). VZV is latent in their ganglia. However, VZV-specific T cell immunity (which normally suppresses reactivation) declines with age (immunosenescence). The shingles vaccine boosts VZV-specific cellular immunity, reducing the risk of reactivation to shingles and significantly reducing post-herpetic neuralgia. It does not prevent primary chickenpox.

mockBank.question: A 28-year-old dental student shows: HBsAg−, HBsAb+ (titre 15 mIU/mL), HBcAb−. Which ONE of the following BEST describes her hepatitis B immune status?
options: A. Past resolved hepatitis B infection with natural immunity
B. Active hepatitis B infection with low infectivity
C. Vaccine-induced immunity — she has been vaccinated and is protected
D. Non-immune to hepatitis B — the titre of 15 mIU/mL is below the protective threshold
E. Chronic hepatitis B carrier — HBcAb negative is common in carriers
mockBank.explanation: HBsAb+ (indicating immunity) + HBcAb− (no core antibody, meaning she has never been naturally infected) = vaccine-induced immunity. A titre of 15 mIU/mL exceeds the protective threshold of 10 mIU/mL — she is protected against HBV. Healthcare workers require confirmation of protective titres as part of occupational health. If titre were <10 mIU/mL, she would need a booster dose.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following orofacial infections is caused by an anaerobic actinomyces-like organism, presents as indurated 'woody' swelling with sinuses draining sulfur granules, and typically follows tooth extraction or jaw fracture?
options: A. Ludwig's angina
B. Pericoronitis
C. Actinomycosis
D. Osteomyelitis
E. Submandibular salivary gland obstruction
mockBank.explanation: Actinomycosis is caused by Actinomyces israelii — a filamentous, anaerobic, Gram-positive bacterium that is part of normal oral flora. After mucosal disruption (extraction, fracture), it causes a chronic, indurated, woody swelling with discharging sinuses. The pus contains characteristic yellow 'sulfur granules' (bacterial colonies). Treat with prolonged amoxicillin (6–12 months).

mockBank.question: A patient with HIV on HAART (undetectable viral load) requires extraction of an impacted lower wisdom tooth under local anaesthesia. He has no other medical conditions. The MOST appropriate management is:
options: A. Defer all elective dental treatment until CD4 count is >1000
B. Carry out extraction with standard universal precautions; no special modification needed
C. Prescribe prophylactic amoxicillin 3g 1 hour prior as HIV-positive status constitutes immune compromise
D. Refer to hospital oral surgery unit as HIV patients require specialist management for all surgical procedures
E. Check CD4 count before every dental procedure and cancel if below 350
mockBank.explanation: A well-controlled HIV patient with undetectable viral load effectively has normal immune function for dental purposes. Standard universal precautions are used for all patients and provide adequate protection. NICE does not recommend prophylactic antibiotics for dental procedures in HIV patients. Routine surgical procedures can be performed in primary care. The CD4 count does not need to be checked before routine dental procedures.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following viruses is classified as a double-stranded RNA virus?
options: A. Adenovirus
B. Hepatitis B virus
C. Rotavirus
D. Influenza virus
E. HIV
mockBank.explanation: Rotavirus is a double-stranded RNA virus (Baltimore Group III) — the most common cause of severe diarrhoeal disease in children worldwide. Adenovirus is dsDNA; HBV is partially dsDNA (unusual); influenza is ssRNA (negative-sense); HIV is ssRNA (retrovirus).

mockBank.question: Gentamicin is an aminoglycoside antibiotic. Which ONE of the following correctly describes its mechanism of action?
options: A. Binds to the 50S ribosomal subunit and inhibits translocation
B. Binds to the 30S ribosomal subunit and prevents correct reading of mRNA, causing misreading and premature termination
C. Inhibits peptidoglycan cross-linkage by binding to penicillin-binding proteins
D. Binds directly to peptidoglycan monomers, disrupting cell wall assembly
E. Inhibits bacterial topoisomerase II, causing double-strand DNA breaks
mockBank.explanation: Gentamicin binds irreversibly to the 30S ribosomal subunit at the 16S rRNA. It causes misreading of the genetic code and prevents correct reading of mRNA, leading to incorporation of incorrect amino acids and premature termination of protein synthesis. The result is production of aberrant proteins that insert into the bacterial membrane, creating channels for gentamicin to enter — amplifying the effect.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following is correct regarding infective endocarditis (IE) prophylaxis for dental procedures under current NICE guidelines?
options: A. Antibiotic prophylaxis is recommended for all dental procedures in patients with prosthetic heart valves
B. Antibiotic prophylaxis is recommended for high-risk cardiac patients undergoing extractions only
C. Antibiotic prophylaxis is NOT recommended routinely before dental procedures, even in high-risk cardiac patients
D. Antibiotic prophylaxis with clindamycin is mandatory for all patients with congenital heart disease
E. Antibiotic prophylaxis is recommended for all patients with a history of rheumatic fever
mockBank.explanation: NICE guideline CG64 (2008, reaffirmed 2016): antibiotic prophylaxis against infective endocarditis is NOT recommended routinely before dental procedures, even in patients with prosthetic valves, previous IE, or structural heart disease. The risk-benefit analysis determined that the risk of anaphylaxis and resistance promotion outweighs the marginal benefit. Good oral hygiene is the most important preventive measure.

mockBank.question: A patient with HSV-1 encephalitis is admitted to the neurological ICU. Which ONE of the following antiviral agents and routes of administration is MOST appropriate?
options: A. Topical aciclovir cream applied to any visible oral lesions
B. Oral valaciclovir 500mg twice daily for 5 days
C. Intravenous aciclovir at high dose (10–15 mg/kg TDS for 14–21 days)
D. Oral aciclovir 200mg five times daily for 7 days
E. Oral ganciclovir — more potent than aciclovir and able to cross the blood-brain barrier
mockBank.explanation: HSV encephalitis has 70% mortality if untreated — a neurological emergency. IV aciclovir (not oral) at high dose (10–15 mg/kg every 8 hours for 14–21 days) is the gold standard. IV route achieves adequate CSF levels (oral aciclovir has poor bioavailability). Aciclovir crosses the blood-brain barrier when given intravenously. Treatment should be started empirically on clinical suspicion before PCR results.

mockBank.question: What is the most appropriate management for a newborn baby born to a mother who is HBsAg-positive?
options: A. Monitor the infant for jaundice and treat if it develops
B. Give HBV vaccine only at birth, no other intervention needed
C. Give HBIG (hepatitis B immunoglobulin) AND HBV vaccine within 24 hours of birth
D. Test the infant for HBsAg at birth and treat with antivirals if positive
E. Advise the mother to bottle-feed to prevent HBV transmission; no other intervention needed
mockBank.explanation: Perinatal HBV transmission has a 50% risk of chronic carriage in vaginally-delivered babies. Combined passive-active immunoprophylaxis (HBIG + vaccine) within 24 hours of birth reduces this risk to <5%. HBIG provides immediate passive antibody protection while the vaccine stimulates active immunity. The full vaccine course (3 doses) must be completed.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following is the correct antibiotic treatment for odontogenic sinusitis of dental origin?
options: A. Penicillin V 500mg QDS for 5 days — same as dentoalveolar abscess
B. Amoxicillin or penicillin V for 7 days — sinusitis requires a longer course
C. Metronidazole 400mg TDS for 3 days — anaerobes are the primary pathogens
D. No antibiotics needed — sinusitis always resolves spontaneously
E. Clarithromycin 500mg BD for 14 days — macrolides penetrate sinus mucosa best
mockBank.explanation: Dental sinusitis (sinusitis of odontogenic origin) typically involves the maxillary sinus following upper molar extraction or periapical infection. When antibiotic treatment is indicated (severe, prolonged, or with systemic features), amoxicillin or penicillin V for 7 days is recommended — a longer course than for dentoalveolar abscess, reflecting the more chronic nature of sinus infection.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following characteristics distinguishes fungi from bacteria?
options: A. Fungi are prokaryotic organisms, while bacteria are eukaryotic
B. Fungi are eukaryotic organisms with cell walls containing chitin, while bacteria have peptidoglycan cell walls
C. Fungi replicate exclusively by sexual reproduction, while bacteria replicate asexually
D. Fungi lack a nucleus, while bacteria have a true nucleus
E. Fungi cannot cause disease in humans, while bacteria are the primary fungal pathogens
mockBank.explanation: Fungi are eukaryotes (have a nucleus, membrane-bound organelles) — unlike bacteria which are prokaryotes. Fungal cell walls contain chitin (a polysaccharide) rather than peptidoglycan. This structural difference is the basis for antifungal drugs that target ergosterol (in fungal membranes) rather than peptidoglycan. Fungi include yeasts (Candida, replicate by budding/mitosis) and moulds (Aspergillus, replicate via spores/meiosis).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following diseases is transmitted via the faecal-oral route?
options: A. HIV
B. Hepatitis B
C. Hepatitis A
D. Herpes simplex type 2
E. Hepatitis C
mockBank.explanation: Hepatitis A is transmitted faecal-orally — via contaminated food (particularly shellfish), water, or close contact with infected individuals. Hepatitis E is also faecal-oral (undercooked pork). HIV (A), HBV (B), and HCV (E) are blood-borne (parenteral). HSV-2 (D) is sexually transmitted.

mockBank.question: Tetanus toxin (tetanospasmin) causes spastic paralysis. Which ONE of the following mechanisms BEST explains this?
options: A. It inhibits acetylcholine synthesis at the neuromuscular junction
B. It cleaves SNARE proteins at inhibitory spinal cord interneurones, preventing glycine release and causing uncontrolled motor neurone firing
C. It blocks nicotinic acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction
D. It causes demyelination of spinal cord motor tracts
E. It induces apoptosis of spinal cord motor neurones
mockBank.explanation: Tetanospasmin (produced by C. tetani) travels via retrograde axonal transport to the spinal cord. It cleaves SNARE proteins (specifically synaptobrevin/VAMP) at inhibitory interneurones, preventing vesicle fusion and release of inhibitory neurotransmitters (glycine, GABA). Without inhibition, motor neurones fire continuously → spastic paralysis (lockjaw, risus sardonicus, opisthotonus).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following statements about the viral capsid is CORRECT?
options: A. The capsid is a lipid structure that fuses with the host cell membrane
B. The capsid is a protein coat that protects viral nucleic acid and mediates cell attachment in non-enveloped viruses
C. The capsid is found only in RNA viruses and not DNA viruses
D. The capsid is destroyed when a virus enters a host cell and is never rebuilt
E. The capsid is identical in all viruses — its structure does not vary between species
mockBank.explanation: The viral capsid is a proteinaceous coat that protects viral nucleic acid from nucleases during transmission. In non-enveloped viruses, capsid proteins also mediate attachment to host cell receptors. The capsid is assembled from repeating protein subunits (capsomers) in either icosahedral or helical arrangements. After cellular entry, the capsid is disassembled (uncoating) to release the nucleic acid.

mockBank.question: The measles virus is classified as a single-stranded, negative-sense RNA virus. Which ONE of the following correctly describes negative-sense RNA viruses?
options: A. Their RNA can be directly used as mRNA by host ribosomes for protein synthesis
B. Their RNA is complementary to mRNA and must be transcribed into positive-sense RNA by viral RNA polymerase before translation
C. Their RNA is double-stranded and requires unwinding before replication
D. Their RNA integrates into the host genome and is transcribed by host RNA polymerase
E. Their RNA is identical to that of retroviruses and requires reverse transcription
mockBank.explanation: Negative-sense (−) RNA is the antisense complement of mRNA — it cannot be directly translated. Negative-sense RNA viruses (influenza, measles, mumps, RSV) package their own RNA-dependent RNA polymerase to transcribe (−)RNA into (+)mRNA first. Positive-sense (+) RNA viruses (poliovirus, HCV) have RNA that acts directly as mRNA.

mockBank.question: A 15-year-old presents with severe sore throat, tonsillar exudate, cervical lymphadenopathy, and splenomegaly. Monospot test is positive. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate management?
options: A. Prescribe amoxicillin immediately to cover the bacterial component
B. Reassure, advise rest, analgesia (paracetamol/ibuprofen), and avoid contact sports until splenomegaly resolves
C. Prescribe aciclovir to eliminate EBV and prevent complications
D. Refer urgently for tonsillectomy as bacterial tonsillitis is more likely
E. Prescribe prednisolone to reduce the inflammatory response immediately
mockBank.explanation: Glandular fever (infectious mononucleosis, EBV) is a self-limiting viral illness managed supportively. Amoxicillin (A) is CONTRAINDICATED — in EBV infection, it causes a widespread maculopapular rash in ~95% of patients. Avoid contact sports due to risk of splenic rupture (a rare but life-threatening complication). Aciclovir (C) is not effective for EBV. Corticosteroids (E) may be used for severe complications (airway obstruction) but not routine use.

mockBank.question: Post-herpetic neuralgia is a recognised complication of shingles. Which ONE of the following is TRUE?
options: A. Post-herpetic neuralgia is caused by VZV reactivating again in the same dermatome
B. It refers to persistent neuropathic pain in the affected dermatome lasting more than 3 months after the rash has healed
C. It is prevented by antiviral treatment started after complete rash resolution
D. Post-herpetic neuralgia only occurs when shingles affects a limb dermatome
E. It is treated with aciclovir as VZV persists in the peripheral nerve
mockBank.explanation: Post-herpetic neuralgia (PHN) is defined as pain persisting for >3 months after shingles rash resolution. It is caused by nerve damage from VZV during reactivation. It is more common in elderly and immunosuppressed patients. Antivirals (valaciclovir, aciclovir) started within 72h of rash onset reduce VZV replication and nerve damage, thereby reducing PHN risk. Established PHN is treated with gabapentin, pregabalin, or TCAs — not antivirals.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly describes how HIV integrase inhibitors work?
options: A. They block GP120 binding to the CD4 receptor, preventing viral entry
B. They competitively inhibit reverse transcriptase, preventing RNA-to-DNA conversion
C. They prevent the integration of viral double-stranded DNA into the host cell genome
D. They block viral protease, preventing polyprotein processing into functional proteins
E. They inhibit the HIV budding process, trapping virions inside the cell
mockBank.explanation: Integrase strand transfer inhibitors (INSTIs — e.g., raltegravir, dolutegravir, bictegravir) block HIV integrase from inserting the viral double-stranded DNA into the host chromosome. Without integration, no provirus is formed and the viral DNA degrades. INSTIs are now the preferred component of first-line HAART due to excellent tolerability and high genetic barrier to resistance.

mockBank.question: An HIV-positive patient presents with erythematous candidiasis. Which ONE of the following BEST describes the clinical appearance of this condition?
options: A. White plaques on the buccal mucosa that can be wiped off leaving a bleeding surface
B. Red, flat, atrophic patches most commonly on the palate and dorsal tongue
C. White corrugated lesions on the lateral border of the tongue that cannot be wiped off
D. Painful vesicular lesions at the lip border
E. Purple-red lesions on the palate that are non-tender
mockBank.explanation: Erythematous candidiasis presents as red, flat atrophic patches — most commonly on the hard palate and dorsal tongue. It is distinct from pseudomembranous candidiasis (A — white patches that wipe off) and oral hairy leukoplakia (C — EBV-associated, not Candida). It causes burning sensation. Treat with antifungal (nystatin suspension or fluconazole) and address underlying immunosuppression.

mockBank.question: Hepatitis D virus (HDV) is unusual among hepatitis viruses because it can only infect individuals who are simultaneously infected with which other virus?
options: A. Hepatitis A
B. Hepatitis B
C. Hepatitis C
D. Hepatitis E
E. HIV
mockBank.explanation: HDV (delta virus) is a defective RNA virus that requires HBsAg (hepatitis B surface antigen) as its envelope protein — it cannot replicate or cause infection without HBV co-infection. HDV can occur as co-infection (simultaneous HBV + HDV) or superinfection (HDV in a chronic HBV carrier). Superinfection causes severe acute hepatitis with higher risk of fulminant liver failure.

mockBank.question: A patient with hepatic cirrhosis undergoes general anaesthesia for an unrelated procedure and develops hepatorenal syndrome post-operatively. Which ONE of the following MOST likely contributed to this?
options: A. The patient drank alcohol post-operatively
B. Severe jaundice under GA causes renal vasoconstriction and acute kidney injury
C. The LA used intra-operatively was nephrotoxic
D. Post-operative paracetamol doses caused direct renal toxicity
E. General anaesthesia always causes hepatorenal syndrome in cirrhotic patients
mockBank.explanation: Hepatorenal syndrome is a life-threatening complication where severe jaundice (high bilirubin) in the setting of surgery causes marked splanchnic vasodilation and renal vasoconstriction. General anaesthesia further reduces renal perfusion. The kidneys fail secondary to the liver's pathological haemodynamics — not from intrinsic renal disease. This is why patients with severe jaundice requiring GA need specialist pre-operative optimisation.

mockBank.question: A dentist wants to rule out periodontal pathogens as the cause of a patient's rapidly progressing periodontitis. What is the MOST appropriate sample for microbiological culture?
options: A. Standard cotton swab from the gingival margin
B. Charcoal swab from the deepest periodontal pocket
C. Aspirate of gingival crevicular fluid or subgingival curette sample in transport medium
D. Saliva culture — all periodontal pathogens are found in saliva
E. Blood culture — severe periodontitis always produces bacteraemia
mockBank.explanation: Periodontal pathogens are predominantly obligate anaerobes. The correct approach: subgingival sample using a curette or paper point placed at the base of the deepest pocket, placed immediately in anaerobic transport medium (to prevent anaerobe death). Standard swabs (A/B) are inadequate. Charcoal swabs (B) would inactivate PCR if molecular testing is used.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly describes a peritonsillar abscess (quinsy)?
options: A. It is exclusively caused by Group A Streptococcus in 100% of cases
B. It is a collection of pus in the space between the tonsil and the pharyngeal wall, most commonly with mixed flora; GAS is found in ~30% of cases
C. It is treated with topical antibiotics and mouthwash alone
D. It presents as bilateral, painless swelling of the tonsillar pillars
E. It only occurs in patients who have previously had their tonsils removed
mockBank.explanation: Quinsy (peritonsillar abscess) is a collection of pus in the peritonsillar space. It is polymicrobial (mixed flora) in ~90% of cases; GAS (S. pyogenes) is isolated in approximately 30%. Presents with severe unilateral sore throat, trismus, 'hot potato' voice, uvular deviation, and a fluctuant peritonsillar bulge. Management: surgical drainage + systemic antibiotics (IV amoxicillin ± metronidazole), not topical treatment.

mockBank.question: Sulfonamides and trimethoprim both inhibit folate synthesis but at different steps. Why does folate inhibition kill bacteria but not human cells?
options: A. Human cells absorb preformed folate from the diet and do not synthesise it de novo
B. Human cells use a different enzyme (dihydropteroate reductase) that is resistant to sulfonamides
C. Sulfonamides are activated by bacterial thymidine kinase, similar to aciclovir
D. Human cells have a nuclear membrane that prevents sulfonamide entry
E. Human cells preferentially use uracil rather than thymine, making them insensitive to folate inhibition
mockBank.explanation: Bacteria must synthesise folate de novo — they cannot absorb preformed folate from the environment. Sulfonamides (PABA analogues) and trimethoprim block bacterial folate synthesis. Human cells cannot synthesise folate — they take up dietary folate (as humans lack the enzyme dihydropteroate synthetase). Therefore, these drugs selectively kill bacteria without harming human cells.

mockBank.question: A patient being treated with metronidazole for a dental abscess is also on warfarin. What action is MOST appropriate?
options: A. Substitute metronidazole with amoxicillin to avoid the interaction
B. Prescribe metronidazole but advise the patient to double their warfarin dose temporarily
C. Prescribe metronidazole, warn the patient about increased bleeding risk, and arrange an INR check within 2–3 days of starting treatment
D. Metronidazole is absolutely contraindicated with warfarin — use clindamycin instead
E. The interaction is only relevant if the patient drinks alcohol — advise abstinence and no INR check needed
mockBank.explanation: Metronidazole remains first-line for pericoronitis even in warfarin patients — it is NOT absolutely contraindicated. It potentiates warfarin by CYP2C9 inhibition and protein binding displacement. Management: prescribe metronidazole (pericoronitis requires anaerobic cover), warn patient about increased bruising/bleeding risk, arrange INR check within 2–3 days, and inform the anticoagulation clinic so they can adjust warfarin as needed.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following antibiotic choices represents the MOST appropriate use of antibiotics according to antimicrobial stewardship principles?
options: A. Co-amoxiclav as first-line for all dental infections to provide the broadest possible cover
B. Penicillin V as first-line for dentoalveolar abscess — narrow spectrum, effective against likely organisms
C. Clarithromycin for all patients due to its excellent spectrum and bioavailability
D. Routine prophylactic antibiotics after every extraction to prevent dry socket
E. Amoxicillin for 14 days to ensure complete eradication of all bacteria
mockBank.explanation: Antimicrobial stewardship principles: use the narrowest effective spectrum, appropriate dose, and shortest effective duration. Penicillin V is narrow-spectrum, effective against the streptococci and anaerobes of dentoalveolar infections, and preserves gut flora. Co-amoxiclav (A) and clarithromycin (C) should be reserved for second-line use. Routine prophylaxis (D) promotes resistance. Prolonged courses (E) promote resistance and increase adverse effects.

mockBank.question: A public health outbreak occurs with a highly contagious viral illness causing fever, rash, and respiratory symptoms. Genome sequencing identifies the causative virus as having a segmented, negative-sense RNA genome. Which ONE of the following viruses BEST fits this description?
options: A. Hepatitis C virus
B. Measles virus
C. Influenza virus
D. Varicella zoster virus
E. Cytomegalovirus
mockBank.explanation: Influenza virus has a segmented (8 segments), negative-sense, single-stranded RNA genome — the segmented nature allows genetic reassortment between strains, enabling antigenic shifts and the emergence of pandemic strains. VZV and CMV are dsDNA viruses. Measles is ssRNA but non-segmented. HCV is ssRNA positive-sense.

mockBank.question: An 85-year-old patient with multiple myeloma on chemotherapy develops bilateral oral candidiasis and widespread herpes zoster involving V1. In addition to appropriate antifungal and antiviral treatment, what additional consideration is MOST important?
options: A. Prescribe prophylactic penicillin V to prevent superimposed bacterial infection
B. Ensure the dose of aciclovir is increased to IV administration given immunosuppression
C. Defer treatment until chemotherapy is complete
D. Only treat the herpes zoster — candidiasis resolves spontaneously with antiviral treatment
E. Apply topical corticosteroids to the oral ulcers to speed healing
mockBank.explanation: In immunocompromised patients, herpes zoster (shingles) is more severe, more prolonged, and more likely to disseminate (affecting multiple dermatomes or internal organs). IV aciclovir (not oral) is recommended for immunocompromised patients with VZV to achieve adequate plasma and tissue levels. V1 involvement also warrants urgent ophthalmological review. Simultaneous treatment of both candidiasis and VZV is required.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following best describes the 'window period' in HIV testing?
options: A. The period after HIV treatment is stopped during which viral load rises
B. The time between HIV exposure and when the immune system is completely destroyed
C. The time between HIV exposure and when the test becomes reliably positive — during this time a recently infected person may test negative but be infectious
D. The period during which HIV can only be transmitted sexually and not via blood
E. The 24-hour window after exposure during which PEP is most effective
mockBank.explanation: The window period is the time between HIV exposure and when a test reliably detects infection. 4th-generation tests (p24 antigen + antibody): window period ~10–45 days. During this time, the person may have very high viral load (acute HIV) and be highly infectious, but HIV tests may be negative. Point-of-care tests may have longer window periods. This is why repeat testing at 45–90 days is recommended after a potential exposure.

mockBank.question: Raised gamma-glutamyl transpeptidase (GGT) in liver function tests is most suggestive of:
options: A. Viral hepatitis (hepatitis A or B)
B. Obstructive jaundice (gallstones or pancreatic cancer)
C. Alcohol-induced liver disease
D. Primary biliary cholangitis
E. Haemochromatosis
mockBank.explanation: GGT is raised in alcohol-related liver disease (alcohol induces hepatic GGT synthesis) and in obstructive jaundice. However, a markedly raised GGT disproportionate to other liver enzymes, particularly in the context of a patient's history, strongly suggests alcohol-induced hepatic damage. It is a sensitive (though non-specific) marker for alcohol misuse.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly describes salivary gland bacterial infection (bacterial sialadenitis)?
options: A. Always caused by a single pathogen: Staphylococcus aureus
B. Most commonly caused by paramyxovirus (mumps virus)
C. Typically polymicrobial (mixed flora), associated with reduced salivary flow or anatomical obstruction
D. Usually viral and self-limiting; antibiotics are never required
E. Only occurs after salivary gland surgery
mockBank.explanation: Bacterial sialadenitis (acute suppurative parotitis or submandibular sialadenitis) is typically polymicrobial — caused by mixed oral flora (oral streptococci, S. aureus, anaerobes) that ascend from the oral cavity. It is associated with: reduced salivary flow (dehydration, anticholinergics, Sjögren's), salivary duct obstruction (calculi), or poor oral hygiene. Treat with rehydration, salivary stimulation, and systemic antibiotics (co-amoxiclav or IV antibiotics for severe cases).

mockBank.question: A patient with no known drug allergies has been prescribed penicillin V for a dental abscess but is not improving after 48 hours. What is the MOST appropriate next step?
options: A. Stop antibiotics — if not improving, surgery is the only option
B. Double the penicillin V dose to overcome bacterial resistance
C. Review clinically: consider whether drainage is required; if adequate drainage has been achieved, switch to co-amoxiclav or add metronidazole
D. Switch immediately to vancomycin IV — MRSA is likely
E. Prescribe clindamycin IM for better bioavailability
mockBank.explanation: If a patient is not improving on first-line antibiotics after 48 hours: (1) Review whether adequate surgical drainage has been achieved — antibiotics cannot penetrate a closed abscess adequately; (2) Consider whether inadequate spectrum is the issue — if polymicrobial infection suspected, adding metronidazole (anaerobic cover) or switching to co-amoxiclav (broader spectrum with beta-lactamase inhibitor) is appropriate. MRSA (D) is uncommon in community dental infections. Vancomycin IV (D) is disproportionate.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following best explains why patients with splenectomy have increased susceptibility to encapsulated bacteria such as Streptococcus pneumoniae?
options: A. The spleen produces IgA — loss of the spleen eliminates mucosal immunity
B. The spleen filters encapsulated bacteria from blood and produces opsonising antibodies (IgM and IgG) against polysaccharide capsules; without it, clearance is impaired
C. The spleen is the only site of complement production; splenectomy eliminates complement
D. The spleen produces all circulating neutrophils; its removal depletes phagocytic capacity
E. Splenectomy reduces CD4+ T cell count below 200, causing AIDS-like immunodeficiency
mockBank.explanation: The spleen is the primary site for filtering blood-borne bacteria and for producing IgM antibodies against T-independent antigens like bacterial polysaccharide capsules (important for S. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis, H. influenzae type b). After splenectomy, opsonisation of encapsulated bacteria is impaired. Management: lifelong prophylactic penicillin, vaccination (pneumococcal, meningococcal, Hib).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following clinical scenarios is BEST managed with serological testing (antibody detection) rather than PCR?
options: A. A patient 2 days after onset of vesicular oral lesions — confirm active HSV
B. Diagnosis of VZV encephalitis in an immunocompromised child
C. Screening for HIV in a patient tested 6 weeks after a potential exposure event
D. Confirming active HSV infection in a CSF sample from a patient with suspected encephalitis
E. Detecting SARS-CoV-2 in a patient with acute respiratory symptoms and positive lateral flow test
mockBank.explanation: Serology (antibody detection) is most appropriate for: (1) Established/chronic infections where antibodies have had time to develop; (2) Screening in low-prevalence populations where specificity matters. At 6 weeks post-exposure, 4th-generation HIV tests (p24 antigen + antibody) are reliably positive. PCR is preferred for all acute presentations (A, B, D, E) where viral nucleic acid is detectable before/during peak illness.

mockBank.question: Primary varicella (chickenpox) in a healthy adult is DIFFERENT from varicella in a child in that:
options: A. Adults develop fewer lesions and milder disease than children
B. Adults are more susceptible to severe varicella pneumonitis and have higher complication rates
C. Chickenpox in adults is always caused by HHV-6 rather than VZV
D. Adults cannot develop post-infectious complications from varicella
E. Adults respond better to VZIG prophylaxis and rarely develop chickenpox even when non-immune
mockBank.explanation: Chickenpox is generally a mild self-limiting illness in young children. In adults and immunocompromised patients, primary varicella carries significantly higher complication rates: varicella pneumonitis (especially in smokers and pregnant women), encephalitis, and secondary bacterial infection. Pregnant women and immunocompromised adults who are non-immune are given VZIG post-exposure and may need aciclovir treatment.

mockBank.question: A 26-year-old gay man attends for HIV testing. He is HIV-negative. He reports regular unprotected anal sex with a partner of unknown HIV status. Which ONE of the following preventive strategy would MOST effectively reduce his HIV acquisition risk?
options: A. Post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) taken daily for protection
B. Pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) — daily antiretroviral taken before and after potential exposure
C. HIV vaccination — now available and highly effective
D. Monthly HIV testing — early detection allows immediate treatment before onward spread
E. Vitamin D supplementation — strengthens innate immune barriers
mockBank.explanation: PrEP (pre-exposure prophylaxis — typically tenofovir/emtricitabine) reduces HIV acquisition by ~99% when taken correctly. It is recommended for people at ongoing high risk of HIV. PEP (A) is taken AFTER potential exposure within 72 hours — not suitable for ongoing prevention. There is no licensed HIV vaccine (C). Monthly testing (D) does not prevent acquisition. Vitamin D (E) has no established HIV prevention benefit.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following is a clinical feature of Dupuytren's contracture as it relates to liver disease?
options: A. Yellowing of the fingertips due to bilirubin deposition in the skin
B. A sign of increased oestrogen: bilateral breast enlargement in males
C. Progressive flexion contracture of the ring and little fingers, associated with chronic liver disease
D. Tremor of the outstretched hand (asterixis) due to ammonia accumulation
E. Enlargement of the fingernails due to chronic hypoxia from liver disease
mockBank.explanation: Dupuytren's contracture is a fibroproliferative disorder causing palmar fascia thickening and progressive flexion contracture of the ring and little fingers. It is associated with chronic liver disease (especially alcoholic cirrhosis), diabetes, epilepsy, and manual work. The mechanism in liver disease is unclear but related to altered fibroblast function and growth factor dysregulation. Asterixis (D) is 'liver flap' from ammonia. Gynaecomastia (B) is from oestrogen excess.

mockBank.question: A patient with chronic hepatitis C has recently completed a 12-week course of direct-acting antivirals and has a sustained virological response (undetectable HCV RNA 12 weeks post-treatment). What does this mean for their dental care?
options: A. They are cured — no infection control precautions are needed
B. They should be treated as potentially infectious until yearly HCV testing confirms absence
C. SVR12 represents cure in >99% of cases; standard universal precautions are applied as for all patients
D. They require lifelong antiviral prophylaxis before dental procedures
E. HCV persists in dental pulp and standard precautions are insufficient
mockBank.explanation: Sustained virological response at 12 weeks (SVR12) after direct-acting antiviral therapy represents cure in >99% of patients — HCV RNA is undetectable and does not return. However, standard universal precautions (gloves, mask, eye protection, safe sharps disposal) are applied to ALL patients regardless of known infection status, because unknown infections also exist. There is no need for additional precautions beyond the standard.

mockBank.question: A microbiology report states that a cultured organism is 'beta-haemolytic on blood agar'. Which ONE of the following BEST describes what this means?
options: A. The organism is Gram-negative and produces a brown pigment around colonies
B. The organism produces a toxin that causes partial breakdown of red blood cells, giving a green halo around colonies
C. The organism produces complete lysis of red blood cells around colonies, creating a clear zone on blood agar
D. The organism causes no haemolysis — neither red blood cells nor surrounding tissue are affected
E. The organism causes haemolysis only when cultured in anaerobic conditions
mockBank.explanation: On blood agar: beta-haemolysis = complete RBC lysis → clear/transparent zone around colonies (e.g., Group A Streptococcus, Group B Streptococcus, S. pyogenes). Alpha-haemolysis = partial RBC lysis → green discolouration (e.g., S. pneumoniae, viridans streptococci). Gamma-haemolysis = no haemolysis (e.g., Enterococcus). Beta-haemolytic streptococci (GAS) are always treated with systemic antibiotics.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following is NOT a notifiable disease in the UK?
options: A. Tuberculosis
B. Gonorrhoea
C. Oral candidiasis
D. Measles
E. Scarlet fever
mockBank.explanation: Oral candidiasis is not a notifiable disease — it is a very common opportunistic infection with no public health reporting requirement. Notifiable diseases relevant to dentistry include: TB (A), gonorrhoea (B), measles (D), scarlet fever (E), mumps, hepatitis B, whooping cough, and meningococcal disease. These require formal notification to the Local Health Protection Team/UKHSA.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly pairs an antibiotic with its site of protein synthesis inhibition?
options: A. Vancomycin — 30S ribosomal subunit
B. Erythromycin — 30S ribosomal subunit
C. Doxycycline — 50S ribosomal subunit
D. Gentamicin — 30S ribosomal subunit
E. Chloramphenicol — 30S ribosomal subunit
mockBank.explanation: Gentamicin (aminoglycoside) inhibits the 30S ribosomal subunit — preventing correct mRNA reading. 30S inhibitors: aminoglycosides and tetracyclines. 50S inhibitors: macrolides (erythromycin, clarithromycin), clindamycin, and chloramphenicol. Vancomycin does not inhibit ribosomes — it disrupts cell wall synthesis. Doxycycline (a tetracycline) inhibits the 30S, not 50S.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following immune cells is the primary effector of the innate immune response against bacterial infection?
options: A. CD4+ T helper cells
B. Memory B cells
C. NK cells and neutrophils
D. Plasma cells
E. Regulatory T cells
mockBank.explanation: The innate immune response is immediate and non-specific. Neutrophils are the first phagocytes to arrive at a bacterial infection site; NK (natural killer) cells kill virus-infected and tumour cells without prior sensitisation. CD4+ T cells (A), memory B cells (B), and plasma cells (D) are all components of the adaptive immune response. Regulatory T cells (E) modulate immune responses to prevent autoimmunity.

mockBank.question: HHV-6 and HHV-7 cause which ONE of the following conditions?
options: A. Kaposi's sarcoma
B. Glandular fever (infectious mononucleosis)
C. Roseola infantum (exanthem subitum) — high fever then rash in infants
D. Chickenpox and shingles
E. Oral hairy leukoplakia
mockBank.explanation: HHV-6 and HHV-7 cause roseola infantum (sixth disease/exanthem subitum) — a common viral illness in infants characterised by 3–4 days of high fever that resolves suddenly, followed by a maculopapular rash starting on the trunk. Usually benign and self-limiting. HHV-8 (A) causes Kaposi's sarcoma; EBV (B) causes glandular fever; VZV (D) causes chickenpox/shingles; EBV (E) causes OHL.

mockBank.question: A dental surgeon performs an extraction and a small amount of blood splashes into their mouth. The patient is known to have HIV with a viral load of 200 copies/mL. What is the MOST appropriate immediate action?
options: A. Spit out blood, rinse mouth with water; risk is negligible — no further action needed
B. Spit out blood, rinse mouth thoroughly with water, do NOT use antiseptic mouthwash, report immediately and seek PEP advice
C. Gargle with chlorhexidine mouthwash for 5 minutes to inactivate HIV
D. Swallow the water to neutralise the virus with stomach acid
E. Wait 24 hours to assess symptoms before contacting occupational health
mockBank.explanation: Oral mucous membrane exposure to HIV-positive blood: spit out and rinse mouth with water (do NOT use antiseptics — they damage mucosa and may increase absorption). Report IMMEDIATELY to occupational health/A&E. PEP should be considered despite the low viral load (200 copies/mL still has transmission potential — not undetectable). PEP must be started within 72 hours; ideally within 1 hour for maximum efficacy.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following viral features determines how long the virus can survive on environmental surfaces (fomites)?
options: A. Whether the virus is DNA or RNA
B. The size of the viral genome
C. Whether the virus has a lipid envelope (enveloped vs non-enveloped)
D. The number of different cell types the virus can infect
E. Whether the virus causes acute or latent infection
mockBank.explanation: Enveloped viruses (HIV, HSV, influenza, VZV) have a lipid bilayer that is easily disrupted by drying, heat, detergents, and alcohol-based disinfectants — they survive poorly on surfaces. Non-enveloped viruses (norovirus, adenovirus, parvovirus, poliovirus) lack this lipid layer and are much more resistant to environmental conditions, surviving on surfaces for days to weeks. This explains why norovirus spreads so readily on surfaces.

mockBank.question: A patient with alcoholic cirrhosis and portal hypertension has low platelet count (60 ×10⁹/L). What is the mechanism of thrombocytopenia in this patient?
options: A. The liver produces fewer megakaryocytes in cirrhosis
B. Portal hypertension causes splenomegaly; platelets are sequestered in the enlarged spleen
C. Alcohol directly destroys circulating platelets
D. The liver produces a platelet-destroying antibody in cirrhosis
E. Cirrhosis causes vitamin K deficiency, which reduces platelet production
mockBank.explanation: Portal hypertension (elevated pressure in the portal venous system from fibrotic liver) causes splenomegaly (enlarged spleen). The enlarged spleen sequesters (traps) a much larger proportion of circulating platelets — up to 90% of the platelet pool can be held in the spleen (normal ~30%). This reduces circulating platelet count. Additionally, thrombopoietin (platelet growth factor) production by the liver is reduced.

mockBank.question: CPE (carbapenemase-producing Enterobacteriaceae) are a major concern in healthcare settings. Which ONE of the following correctly describes why carbapenems are a 'last resort' antibiotic?
options: A. Carbapenems are always more toxic than other antibiotics
B. Carbapenems are beta-lactam antibiotics with the broadest spectrum, covering MDR Gram-negative bacteria that have failed all other beta-lactams
C. Carbapenems are only effective against Gram-positive organisms
D. Carbapenems are oral medications that require complex dosing schedules
E. Carbapenems cannot penetrate bacterial biofilms
mockBank.explanation: Carbapenems (meropenem, imipenem, ertapenem) are broad-spectrum beta-lactams that resist most beta-lactamases. They are reserved as 'last resort' for multi-drug-resistant (MDR) Gram-negative infections (e.g., ESBL-producing E. coli, P. aeruginosa, Klebsiella) where all other options have failed. CPE produce carbapenemases that destroy even carbapenems — this creates pan-resistant organisms with very few treatment options.

mockBank.question: A patient presents with cervical lymphadenopathy and an indurated swelling in the right neck. Fine needle aspiration of the node reveals granulomas with caseating necrosis and acid-fast bacilli. Which ONE of the following is the MOST likely diagnosis?
options: A. Reactive lymphadenopathy secondary to dental abscess
B. Squamous cell carcinoma metastasis in cervical lymph nodes
C. Tuberculous lymphadenitis (scrofula)
D. EBV-associated lymphoma
E. Sarcoidosis with cervical node involvement
mockBank.explanation: Caseating granulomas with acid-fast bacilli (Ziehl-Neelson positive) in a cervical lymph node = tuberculous lymphadenitis (historically called scrofula). This is secondary TB — often from primary pulmonary TB. Management: refer to respiratory/infectious diseases team; treat with quadruple antituberculous therapy (rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol for 2 months, then rifampicin + isoniazid for 4 months). TB is a notifiable disease.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following best describes 'immunological memory' and its clinical significance?
options: A. The ability of dendritic cells to recognise multiple pathogen types simultaneously
B. The persistence of long-lived memory B and T cells after primary infection, enabling faster, stronger responses to re-exposure — the basis for vaccination
C. The permanent presence of antibodies that never wane after vaccination
D. The storage of pathogens in lymph nodes for future reference by the immune system
E. The reflex response of the complement system when previously encountered pathogens return
mockBank.explanation: Immunological memory is mediated by long-lived memory B cells and memory T cells that persist for years after infection or vaccination. Upon re-exposure to the same antigen, memory cells proliferate rapidly, producing a secondary immune response that is faster (starts within 24 hours vs 7–10 days), greater in magnitude (higher antibody titres), more specific (class-switched IgG), and of longer duration. Vaccination exploits this memory without natural infection.

mockBank.question: Prion diseases are relevant to dentistry because prions can survive in dental pulp tissue. Which ONE of the following sterilisation/decontamination methods is EFFECTIVE against prions?
options: A. Standard autoclave sterilisation at 134°C for 3 minutes
B. 2% glutaraldehyde (Cidex) immersion for 10 minutes
C. Ultraviolet irradiation for 30 minutes
D. Sodium hypochlorite 20,000 ppm for 1 hour followed by autoclaving at 134°C
E. Dry heat oven at 180°C for 30 minutes
mockBank.explanation: Prions are extraordinarily resistant to all standard decontamination methods. The most effective decontamination combines: sodium hypochlorite (20,000 ppm, 1 hour) to expose and denature prion protein, followed by porous load autoclaving (134°C for 18 minutes). Standard autoclaving alone (A) does not reliably inactivate prions. In practice, the solution in dentistry is single-use disposable endodontic instruments — eliminating the need for decontamination.

mockBank.question: A dentist examines a patient and finds white corrugated lesions on the buccal mucosa that CAN be wiped off to reveal a red, raw, bleeding base. Which ONE of the following is the MOST likely diagnosis?
options: A. Oral hairy leukoplakia — EBV-associated
B. Lichen planus — characteristic white lace-like pattern
C. Pseudomembranous candidiasis — white plaques that wipe off leaving erythematous mucosa
