D. Frictional keratosis from a sharp tooth
E. Squamous cell carcinoma — warrants urgent 2-week referral
mockBank.explanation: White plaques that CAN be wiped off to reveal a red, raw, bleeding base = pseudomembranous candidiasis (oral thrush). Caused by Candida albicans. Treat with antifungal (nystatin or fluconazole) and investigate underlying cause (immunosuppression, diabetes, inhaled corticosteroids, xerostomia). OHL (A) cannot be wiped off. Lichen planus (B) shows Wickham's striae/reticular pattern. SCC (E) is non-removable and firm/indurated.

mockBank.question: When prescribing doxycycline for a dental infection, which ONE of the following monitoring consideration is MOST important?
options: A. Blood glucose monitoring — doxycycline causes hyperglycaemia
B. Renal function monitoring — doxycycline is primarily excreted by the kidneys
C. Contraindication in children under 12 and in pregnancy from the second trimester due to tooth discolouration and skeletal effects
D. Liver function tests — doxycycline causes hepatocellular necrosis in all patients
E. Cardiac monitoring — doxycycline prolongs the QT interval
mockBank.explanation: Doxycycline chelates calcium in developing mineralised tissues, causing permanent brown/yellow tooth discolouration and enamel hypoplasia. Contraindicated: children under 12 (developing permanent teeth), pregnancy from T2 (foetal dental/skeletal development). Also causes SJS, photosensitivity, and hepatotoxicity with alcohol. Unlike other antibiotics, doxycycline is actually safer in renal failure as it is excreted mainly via the GI tract.

mockBank.question: A patient develops tetanus after gardening without gloves and cutting their hand on a rusty nail. Which transmission route is responsible?
options: A. Droplet (respiratory) transmission from soil aerosols
B. Vector-borne transmission via soil mites
C. Direct inoculation — C. tetani spores from soil contaminate the wound (environmental/inoculation route)
D. Faecal-oral transmission from C. tetani in gut flora
E. Blood-borne transmission from a previous blood transfusion
mockBank.explanation: C. tetani forms heat-resistant spores that survive in soil for decades. Infection occurs when spores contaminate wounds (especially deep, dirty, or puncture wounds). The anaerobic wound environment allows spore germination and toxin production. Prevention: tetanus vaccination (part of childhood schedule) + wound debridement + tetanus prophylaxis (vaccine ± immunoglobulin) for contaminated wounds.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following is the CORRECT sequence of events during viral cell entry via receptor-mediated endocytosis?
options: A. Attachment to receptor → fusion with nuclear membrane → replication → assembly
B. Attachment to surface receptor → endocytosis → endosomal acidification causes viral release into cytoplasm → replication
C. Viral capsid directly penetrates the host cell membrane without prior receptor binding
D. Virus replicates extracellularly first, then enters the cell fully formed
E. Attachment to receptor → viral nucleic acid injected directly through cell wall (as in bacteriophage)
mockBank.explanation: Receptor-mediated endocytosis: (1) Viral surface proteins bind specific host cell receptors; (2) The virus is engulfed in a clathrin-coated pit → endosome; (3) Endosomal acidification (pH drop) triggers conformational change in viral proteins → viral escape into cytoplasm (uncoating); (4) Nucleic acid replication begins. Influenza uses this pathway — HA protein undergoes pH-triggered fusion. This pathway is also used by adenovirus and some retroviruses.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly describes why antivirals are most effective when given early in infection?
options: A. Antiviral drugs work by boosting the immune system — the immune system is strongest in early infection
B. Viral replication is exponential in the early phase — early treatment catches the virus before peak replication and minimises tissue damage from viral cytopathic effects
C. Early antiviral treatment prevents viral latency, as viruses become latent only after 72 hours
D. Antivirals need a minimum of 72 hours to reach therapeutic plasma concentrations
E. The blood-brain barrier is more permeable in early infection, allowing antivirals to reach viral reservoirs
mockBank.explanation: Viral replication is exponential in the early phase of infection — viral titres can double every few hours. Early antiviral treatment (e.g., aciclovir within 72 hours of shingles rash onset) reduces peak viral load, limits tissue damage (nerve damage from VZV), reduces symptom duration and severity, and decreases the risk of complications like post-herpetic neuralgia. Once maximum viral replication has occurred and tissue damage is done, antivirals have less impact.

mockBank.question: A patient with untreated HIV has a CD4 count of 80 cells/µL. Which ONE of the following conditions is he at HIGHEST risk of developing?
options: A. Herpes labialis — requires CD4 <500 to develop
B. Periodontal disease — only occurs when CD4 <200
C. Toxoplasma gondii encephalitis — most common at CD4 <100
D. Oral candidiasis — only occurs when CD4 <50
E. Lymphadenopathy — only occurs when CD4 <100
mockBank.explanation: CD4 thresholds for AIDS-defining conditions: >200: oral candidiasis, PCP risk begins; <200: PCP, OHL; <100: Toxoplasma encephalitis, CMV disease, MAC (Mycobacterium avium complex), cryptococcal meningitis; <50: CMV retinitis. At CD4 80, this patient is at high risk for Toxoplasma encephalitis (most common cause of intracranial mass lesion in AIDS). OHL and oral candidiasis can occur at higher CD4 counts.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following statements about hepatitis E infection is CORRECT?
options: A. Hepatitis E is a DNA virus transmitted blood-borne (parenterally)
B. Hepatitis E is particularly dangerous in pregnant women, with up to 20% mortality
C. Hepatitis E has a carrier state analogous to hepatitis B
D. An effective vaccine against hepatitis E is widely available in the UK
E. Hepatitis E cannot become chronic under any circumstances
mockBank.explanation: Hepatitis E (ssRNA virus, faecal-oral transmission from undercooked pork or contaminated water) is particularly severe in pregnant women — mortality up to 20% in the third trimester (mechanism unclear but possibly due to altered immune tolerance in pregnancy). It can become chronic in immunosuppressed patients. No vaccine is widely available in the UK. There is no carrier state (unlike HBV). Why A is wrong: HEV is RNA, not DNA, and faecal-oral, not blood-borne.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following organisms is Gram-negative AND a diplococcus?
options: A. Streptococcus pneumoniae
B. Staphylococcus aureus
C. Neisseria gonorrhoeae
D. Streptococcus pyogenes
E. Enterococcus faecalis
mockBank.explanation: Neisseria gonorrhoeae (gonococcus) is a Gram-negative diplococcus — a sexually transmitted pathogen that can also cause pharyngeal infection via orogenital contact. The characteristic intracellular diplococci on Gram stain within neutrophils (in urethral discharge) is diagnostic but requires culture on selective media (e.g., Thayer-Martin) or NAAT for confirmation. N. meningitidis is also Gram-negative diplococci. S. pneumoniae is Gram-positive alpha-haemolytic diplococci.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following antibiotics works by INHIBITING NUCLEIC ACID SYNTHESIS rather than cell wall synthesis or protein synthesis?
options: A. Amoxicillin
B. Erythromycin
C. Doxycycline
D. Ciprofloxacin
E. Vancomycin
mockBank.explanation: Ciprofloxacin (a fluoroquinolone) inhibits bacterial DNA topoisomerases (DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV), preventing DNA supercoiling and replication — leading to irreparable DNA strand breaks and bacterial death. Amoxicillin (A) and vancomycin (E) inhibit cell wall synthesis; erythromycin (B) inhibits 50S ribosome; doxycycline (C) inhibits 30S ribosome. Metronidazole and rifampicin also target nucleic acids.

mockBank.question: Mycobacterium tuberculosis survives within macrophages by preventing phagolysosomal fusion. This is an example of which type of immune evasion?
options: A. Aggressive immune evasion — M. tuberculosis kills macrophages directly
B. Active immune evasion — M. tuberculosis produces proteins that destroy lysosomes
C. Passive immune evasion — M. tuberculosis resists destruction by inhibiting phagosome-lysosome fusion, surviving intracellularly
D. Adaptive immune evasion — M. tuberculosis evolves rapidly to evade host antibodies
E. Antigenic variation — M. tuberculosis changes its surface antigens to prevent recognition
mockBank.explanation: M. tuberculosis secretes proteins (e.g., ESAT-6, LpqH) that block phagosome-lysosome fusion within macrophages, preventing acidification and enzyme-mediated killing. This is passive immune evasion — the bacterium survives without actively attacking the immune system. It then replicates within macrophages (a sanctuary site). This is why TB is treated with multiple antibiotics for 6 months — intracellular bacteria are harder to reach.

mockBank.question: A patient presents with grouped vesicular lesions on the right lip border following a period of intense stress. She has had 3 similar episodes in the past year. Diagnosis is herpes labialis. Which ONE of the following statements about management is CORRECT?
options: A. Prescribe systemic aciclovir 200mg 5× daily for 5 days for every episode
B. Topical aciclovir 5% cream applied at the first sign of prodrome can reduce severity and duration
C. The condition cannot be treated — antivirals are only effective in primary infection
D. Prescribe valaciclovir 1g daily indefinitely as suppressive therapy for all patients
E. Refer for urgent biopsy to exclude squamous cell carcinoma
mockBank.explanation: Herpes labialis (recurrent cold sores) is usually mild and self-limiting (5–12 days). Topical aciclovir 5% cream applied at the very first sign (prodromal tingling/itching) can reduce severity and duration. Systemic antivirals (A) are reserved for severe/frequent episodes. Long-term suppressive therapy (D — oral aciclovir or valaciclovir) is appropriate only for patients with very frequent (>6/year) or severe recurrences. Biopsy (E) is inappropriate for typical cold sores.

mockBank.question: Kaposi's sarcoma in the oral cavity most commonly affects which ONE of the following anatomical locations?
options: A. The anterior tongue
B. The mandibular alveolar ridge
C. The hard palate and gingiva
D. The soft palate exclusively
E. The lips only
mockBank.explanation: Oral Kaposi's sarcoma (HHV-8 associated, seen in HIV/immunosuppression) most commonly affects the hard palate and gingiva. It presents as reddish-purple macules, papules, or nodules that may ulcerate or bleed. The hard palate is the most common site, followed by the gingiva. Oral KS may be the first manifestation of AIDS in some patients — any such lesion in an at-risk patient warrants HIV testing.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following describes the 'super-added infection' concept in viral hepatitis?
options: A. A second strain of the same hepatitis virus infecting an already-infected patient
B. A patient with pre-existing liver disease (e.g. alcoholic cirrhosis) who develops acute hepatitis A on top of already-compromised liver function — dramatically worsening prognosis
C. A healthcare worker who contracts hepatitis from a patient in addition to their existing chronic HBV
D. The simultaneous infection with two different hepatitis viruses (e.g. HBV + HCV co-infection)
E. Re-infection with hepatitis B after apparent recovery
mockBank.explanation: Super-added infection describes an acute viral hepatitis (typically HAV or HEV) occurring in a patient whose liver function is already compromised from another cause (alcoholic cirrhosis, chronic HBV, NAFLD). The acute infection on top of already-reduced hepatic reserve can precipitate acute-on-chronic liver failure, which has high mortality. This is why HAV vaccination is recommended for patients with chronic liver disease.

mockBank.question: A patient develops a severe skin reaction with mucosal involvement (blistering affecting >30% of body surface area) after starting doxycycline. Which ONE of the following diagnoses is MOST likely?
options: A. Contact dermatitis from topical antibiotic cream
B. Erythema multiforme minor
C. Toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN) — the most severe form of Stevens-Johnson spectrum
D. Urticarial IgE-mediated reaction to penicillin
E. Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms (DRESS) caused by metronidazole
mockBank.explanation: Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) and toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN) are the same disease spectrum. <10% BSA involvement = SJS; >30% BSA = TEN. Both are life-threatening dermatological emergencies with mucosal involvement (oral, ocular, genital). TEN has ~30% mortality. Doxycycline (and many other drugs including allopurinol, sulfonamides, phenytoin) can cause SJS/TEN. Immediate drug cessation and specialised burn unit care are required.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following is the MOST common cause of acute bacterial parotitis (infectious parotitis) in hospitalised, dehydrated patients?
options: A. Streptococcus pneumoniae
B. Paramyxovirus
C. Staphylococcus aureus ascending from the oral cavity due to reduced salivary flow
D. Actinomyces israelii
E. Mycobacterium tuberculosis
mockBank.explanation: Acute bacterial parotitis classically occurs in elderly, dehydrated, or postoperative patients with reduced salivary flow (dehydration, anticholinergics, diuretics). Reduced saliva allows ascending infection from the oral cavity — S. aureus is the most common pathogen (gram-positive, skin flora that colonises the oropharynx). Presents with unilateral parotid swelling, tenderness, and pus expressible from Stenson's duct. Treat with rehydration and IV antibiotics (flucloxacillin or co-amoxiclav). Paramyxovirus causes mumps (bilateral).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly describes how hepatitis B virus differs from hepatitis C virus in replication?
options: A. HBV is an RNA virus while HCV is a DNA virus
B. HBV integrates into the host genome (via its DNA form) potentially causing chromosomal mutations and HCC; HCV is an RNA virus that does not integrate
C. HCV integrates into the genome as a provirus; HBV replicates only in the cytoplasm
D. Both HBV and HCV integrate into the host genome, leading to similar chronic disease patterns
E. HBV replicates using reverse transcriptase; HCV uses DNA polymerase
mockBank.explanation: HBV is a DNA virus that integrates its genome into the host hepatocyte DNA (via its unique partial dsDNA structure and reverse transcription of pgRNA). Integration near oncogenes contributes to HCC risk. HCV is an ssRNA virus that does not integrate — it replicates in the cytoplasm and can be completely eradicated with effective treatment. This explains why HBV cannot be cured (cccDNA reservoir in hepatocytes persists) while HCV can be cured with DAA therapy.

mockBank.question: A patient with alcoholic liver disease has a serum albumin of 24 g/L (normal 35–50 g/L). Which ONE of the following best explains a clinically relevant consequence for dental prescribing?
options: A. Low albumin increases the risk of allergic reactions to penicillin
B. Many drugs are heavily protein-bound; low albumin increases free (active) drug fraction, potentiating drug effects and toxicity
C. Low albumin reduces gastric acid, impairing oral drug absorption
D. Low albumin causes peripheral oedema that prevents local anaesthetic penetration
E. Low albumin is only significant for intravenous medications, not orally administered drugs
mockBank.explanation: Albumin is the major plasma protein for drug binding. Many drugs (including lidocaine, warfarin, diazepam, phenytoin) are highly protein-bound — only the 'free' unbound fraction is pharmacologically active. In hypoalbuminaemia, less drug binds to albumin → higher free drug fraction → enhanced pharmacological effects and increased toxicity risk. This is clinically relevant for all drugs with high protein binding in patients with significant liver disease.

mockBank.question: A patient taking prednisolone 40mg daily for 6 weeks for IBD develops oral candidiasis. Which ONE of the following immune cells is MOST directly impaired by corticosteroids, predisposing to Candida infection?
options: A. B cells — corticosteroids deplete circulating B lymphocytes
B. Neutrophils and T cells — corticosteroids impair neutrophil recruitment and reduce T-cell cytokine production
C. NK cells — corticosteroids selectively eliminate NK cells from circulation
D. Complement — corticosteroids inhibit complement synthesis in the liver
E. Eosinophils — the loss of eosinophils allows Candida to proliferate
mockBank.explanation: Corticosteroids cause broad but selective immunosuppression: they impair neutrophil migration to infection sites (chemotaxis), reduce phagocytic efficiency, and suppress T-cell cytokine production (especially IL-2 and IFN-γ). Together, these effects impair cell-mediated immunity and mucosal defence — creating conditions that allow Candida overgrowth. Candida is normally controlled by a combination of neutrophils and T-cell-mediated immune surveillance.

mockBank.question: A 40-year-old immunocompromised patient (post-renal transplant) is found to have CMV viraemia on routine surveillance. Which ONE of the following antivirals is used to treat CMV disease (NOT HSV)?
options: A. Aciclovir — the standard antiviral for all herpes viruses
B. Valaciclovir — the prodrug of aciclovir with better bioavailability
C. Ganciclovir (or its prodrug valganciclovir) — requires CMV-encoded phosphokinase, more active against CMV than HSV
D. Oseltamivir — the broad-spectrum antiviral effective against CMV and influenza
E. Interferon alpha — the first-line treatment for all CMV infections
mockBank.explanation: Ganciclovir (IV) and valganciclovir (oral prodrug) are the drugs of choice for CMV disease. Like aciclovir, ganciclovir is a nucleoside analogue — but it is activated by CMV UL97 protein kinase (not thymidine kinase), giving it much greater activity against CMV. CMV phosphokinase is more efficient at phosphorylating ganciclovir than the TK used by HSV. Aciclovir has minimal CMV activity at therapeutic doses.

mockBank.question: A patient taking the anticoagulant apixaban (a direct oral anticoagulant/DOAC) requires metronidazole for pericoronitis. Which ONE of the following statements is MOST accurate regarding drug interaction?
options: A. There is no interaction — DOACs do not interact with antibiotics
B. Metronidazole significantly inhibits CYP3A4 metabolism of apixaban, substantially increasing bleeding risk — switch to amoxicillin
C. Metronidazole may modestly increase apixaban levels — prescribe with caution and warn about increased bruising/bleeding risk
D. Apixaban must be stopped before prescribing any dental antibiotic
E. Metronidazole should be avoided with DOACs; clarithromycin is a safe alternative
mockBank.explanation: Apixaban is metabolised by CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein. Metronidazole can modestly inhibit these pathways, potentially increasing apixaban exposure. The interaction is less dramatic than metronidazole + warfarin (because DOACs have more predictable pharmacokinetics and don't require INR monitoring). However, caution is advised: prescribe metronidazole (it remains the appropriate antibiotic for pericoronitis), warn about increased bleeding, and keep the course short (3 days).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly describes the Centor criteria used for the assessment of bacterial pharyngitis?
options: A. Four clinical criteria (tonsillar exudate, tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, absence of cough, fever >38°C) — a higher score indicates higher likelihood of GAS infection
B. Blood test criteria measuring ESR, CRP, and white cell count to determine antibiotic need
C. Radiographic criteria to assess peritonsillar abscess formation
D. Swab culture criteria used only in secondary care settings
E. Age-only criteria — patients under 10 years always require antibiotics for sore throat
mockBank.explanation: Centor criteria assess likelihood of Group A Streptococcal pharyngitis: (1) Tonsillar exudate; (2) Swollen, tender anterior cervical lymph nodes; (3) History of fever; (4) Absence of cough. Each criterion scores 1 point. Score ≥3: consider antibiotics or throat swab. Score 0–1: viral cause likely, no antibiotics. Modified McIsaac criteria add age-adjustment. In primary care dentistry, this helps guide antibiotic prescribing for pharyngeal infections.

mockBank.question: A patient with HIV who has been on HAART for 10 years asks about screening for other health conditions. Which ONE of the following cancers is MOST associated with long-term HIV infection even with controlled viral load?
options: A. Breast cancer — increased risk due to immune dysregulation
B. Prostate cancer — HIV causes direct prostate cell transformation
C. Non-Hodgkin's lymphoma (EBV-associated) and anal carcinoma (HPV-associated)
D. Oral squamous cell carcinoma — due to direct HSV-1 transformation
E. Thyroid cancer — due to altered pituitary-thyroid axis in HIV
mockBank.explanation: Even with well-controlled HIV on HAART, patients have higher rates of AIDS-defining cancers (Kaposi's sarcoma, NHL, invasive cervical cancer) and non-AIDS-defining cancers (anal cancer from HPV, oropharyngeal cancer, lung cancer, liver cancer). NHL is EBV-associated and occurs due to chronic B-cell stimulation and reduced immune surveillance. HPV-related anal and oropharyngeal cancers are increased due to impaired HPV-specific immune responses.

mockBank.question: The liver normally synthesises which ONE of the following groups of coagulation factors that would be deficient in a patient with severe hepatic failure?
options: A. Von Willebrand factor and platelet glycoproteins
B. Factor VIII and Factor XIII only
C. Factors II, VII, IX, and X (vitamin K-dependent factors), as well as Factors V, XI, XII
D. Platelet-activating factor and thromboxane A2 only
E. Only fibrinogen and Factor VIII are synthesised in the liver
mockBank.explanation: The liver synthesises all coagulation factors EXCEPT Factor VIII (which is produced by endothelial cells and other tissues). In hepatic failure, all liver-synthesised factors are reduced. The most clinically significant are the vitamin K-dependent factors II (prothrombin), VII, IX, and X — deficiency of these prolongs PT/INR. Factor VII has the shortest half-life (~6 hours), so is the first to fall in acute liver failure.

mockBank.question: MDR-TB (multidrug-resistant tuberculosis) is defined as resistance to which TWO first-line anti-tuberculous drugs?
options: A. Rifampicin and ethambutol
B. Isoniazid and pyrazinamide
C. Rifampicin and isoniazid
D. Ethambutol and pyrazinamide
E. Streptomycin and isoniazid
mockBank.explanation: MDR-TB is defined as M. tuberculosis resistant to at least rifampicin AND isoniazid — the two most potent first-line anti-TB drugs. MDR-TB requires 18–20 months of second-line drugs. XDR-TB (extensively drug-resistant) adds resistance to fluoroquinolones and injectable agents. MDR-TB is a global public health emergency, with high prevalence in Eastern Europe, Russia, and South-East Asia.

mockBank.question: Koch's postulates were originally developed using the germ theory of disease. Which scientist first proposed that microorganisms cause specific diseases (the 'germ theory')?
options: A. Alexander Fleming
B. Louis Pasteur
C. Robert Koch
D. Joseph Lister
E. Ignaz Semmelweis
mockBank.explanation: Louis Pasteur developed the germ theory of disease in the 1850s–1860s, demonstrating that microorganisms cause fermentation, putrefaction, and infectious disease. Robert Koch later formalised the criteria for proving microbial causation (Koch's postulates). Joseph Lister applied germ theory to antiseptic surgery. Ignaz Semmelweis demonstrated hand-washing reduced puerperal fever. Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following clinical scenarios describes virus-causing IMMUNE-MEDIATED tissue damage rather than direct cytopathic effect?
options: A. HSV-1 causing oral ulcers by direct lysis of oral epithelial cells
B. HIV directly killing CD4+ T cells through membrane disruption after budding
C. Hepatitis B causing hepatocyte death through cytotoxic T lymphocyte attack on infected hepatocytes
D. Rotavirus causing diarrhoea by directly destroying enterocytes
E. Poliovirus lysing motor neurones in the anterior horn of the spinal cord
mockBank.explanation: HBV itself is not directly cytopathic to hepatocytes — liver damage is caused by the host immune response (CTLs recognising HBV antigens on hepatocyte surface via MHC Class I). The CTL attack clears HBV but damages hepatocytes. This is why immunosuppressed patients can be chronic HBV carriers with minimal liver damage (their immune response is diminished). HSV (A), rotavirus (D), and poliovirus (E) cause direct cytopathic effects.

mockBank.question: A 78-year-old patient with renal impairment (eGFR 25 mL/min) requires antibiotic treatment for a dental abscess. Which ONE of the following antibiotics would require the GREATEST dose modification?
options: A. Metronidazole — primarily renal excretion requires major dose reduction
B. Amoxicillin — renally excreted; dose reduction needed at eGFR <30
C. Doxycycline — primarily renal clearance; severe accumulation in renal failure
D. Clindamycin — only renally excreted and requires dose reduction
E. Penicillin V — no dose adjustment ever needed in renal failure
mockBank.explanation: Amoxicillin is primarily excreted unchanged by the kidneys. At eGFR <30 mL/min, dose reduction is recommended to prevent accumulation and neurotoxicity. Doxycycline (C) is an exception — it is NOT primarily renally excreted (eliminated via the GI tract) and is one of the few antibiotics safe at normal doses in renal failure. Metronidazole (A) is hepatically metabolised; dose adjustment is needed only in severe renal failure. Clindamycin (D) is hepatically metabolised.

mockBank.question: A 30-year-old patient presents with acute onset of unilateral facial palsy, vesicular eruption in the ear canal and on the pinna, and ipsilateral sensorineural hearing loss. Which ONE of the following is the MOST likely diagnosis?
options: A. Bell's palsy — idiopathic facial nerve palsy
B. VZV reactivation in the geniculate ganglion (Ramsay Hunt syndrome)
C. Ophthalmic shingles affecting the V1 branch
D. HSV-1 encephalitis with facial nerve involvement
E. Middle ear infection (otitis media) spreading to the facial nerve
mockBank.explanation: Ramsay Hunt syndrome = VZV reactivation in the geniculate ganglion of the facial nerve (CN VII). Classic triad: (1) Painful vesicular eruption in the ear canal/pinna (auricular shingles); (2) Ipsilateral lower motor neurone facial palsy; (3) Sensorineural hearing loss/vertigo. Treat with aciclovir/valaciclovir + prednisolone. Prognosis is worse than Bell's palsy — only 50-60% full recovery.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following is the most appropriate management for a patient with active tuberculosis presenting to a dental practice for a routine dental appointment?
options: A. Carry out routine dental treatment as TB is not transmissible in a dental setting
B. Defer all non-urgent dental treatment and refer the patient to their TB team; when treatment is essential, use full respiratory precautions (FFP3 mask, negative-pressure room)
C. Prescribe rifampicin prophylaxis for all dental staff after the appointment
D. Treat with standard surgical mask and gloves — PPE prevents all TB transmission
E. Treat the patient last in the day so any TB aerosols dissipate overnight
mockBank.explanation: Active pulmonary TB is transmitted via airborne droplet nuclei (tiny particles that remain suspended for hours). Dental procedures generate aerosols — high risk of transmission in a standard dental setting. Management: defer non-urgent dental treatment until TB is confirmed as non-infectious (2 weeks of appropriate treatment + clinical assessment). For urgent/emergency care: FFP3 respirator for all staff, N95/FFP3 mask for patient (if tolerated), ventilated negative-pressure room ideally. Standard surgical masks do not protect against airborne TB.

mockBank.question: A patient with a history of anaphylaxis to amoxicillin (documented immediate hypersensitivity reaction with urticaria, angioedema, and hypotension) requires antibiotic treatment for a periapical abscess. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate choice?
options: A. Cefalexin 500mg QDS — cephalosporins have minimal cross-reactivity with penicillins
B. Amoxicillin at a lower dose — the reaction was likely dose-related
C. Metronidazole 400mg TDS — appropriate coverage and no penicillin cross-reactivity
D. Co-amoxiclav — contains amoxicillin but clavulanate prevents the allergic reaction
E. Erythromycin 500mg QDS — effective and safe alternative
mockBank.explanation: Documented anaphylaxis to amoxicillin is an absolute contraindication to all penicillins and contraindication to cephalosporins (5–10% cross-reactivity due to shared beta-lactam ring). Co-amoxiclav (D) contains amoxicillin — absolutely contraindicated. Cefalexin (A) carries 5–10% cross-reactivity risk — should be avoided in documented anaphylaxis. Metronidazole (C) has no beta-lactam structure and no cross-reactivity with penicillins. Erythromycin (E) is an alternative but clarithromycin has more interactions; erythromycin is less preferred in current dental guidelines.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following statements about hepatitis A vaccination is CORRECT?
options: A. The hepatitis A vaccine contains live attenuated virus and is contraindicated in immunosuppressed patients
B. The hepatitis A vaccine is an inactivated vaccine; two doses provide lifelong immunity
C. The hepatitis A vaccine is NOT available for adults — only children receive it
D. The hepatitis A vaccine protects against hepatitis E as the viruses are closely related
E. The hepatitis A vaccine should not be given to patients with chronic liver disease
mockBank.explanation: The hepatitis A vaccine is an inactivated (killed) vaccine — safe in immunosuppressed patients (unlike live vaccines). Two doses (0 and 6–12 months) provide very long-lasting (possibly lifelong) immunity. Recommended for: travellers to endemic areas, men who have sex with men, chronic liver disease patients (to prevent super-added infection), and laboratory workers. It does NOT protect against HEV (different virus family).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following statements about natural killer (NK) cells is CORRECT?
options: A. NK cells are part of the adaptive immune system and require antigen-specific priming
B. NK cells only kill bacteria; they cannot target virally-infected cells
C. NK cells are innate immune cells that kill virus-infected and tumour cells without prior sensitisation — they recognise cells with reduced MHC Class I expression
D. NK cells produce IgG antibodies against intracellular pathogens
E. NK cells are identical to CD8+ cytotoxic T lymphocytes and perform the same functions
mockBank.explanation: NK cells are innate immune cells — they do not require antigen-specific priming. They kill cells with REDUCED MHC Class I expression (viruses and tumours often downregulate MHC I to evade CTLs — NK cells fill this gap). Kill mechanism: perforin and granzyme B (same as CTLs). They also produce IFN-γ, activating macrophages. They are regulated by a balance of activating and inhibitory receptors on their surface.

mockBank.question: Bacitracin is an antibiotic that disrupts cell wall synthesis by a mechanism different from penicillin. Which step does bacitracin target?
options: A. Inhibits PBPs, preventing peptidoglycan cross-linkage
B. Binds D-Ala-D-Ala peptidoglycan monomers to prevent cross-linkage
C. Disrupts the lipid carrier (bactoprenol) that transports peptidoglycan precursors across the cell membrane
D. Inhibits the MurA enzyme in early peptidoglycan biosynthesis
E. Disrupts the outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria
mockBank.explanation: Bacitracin targets the lipid carrier (undecaprenyl pyrophosphate / bactoprenol) that transports peptidoglycan monomers across the cell membrane to the exterior. By interfering with this carrier's recycling, bacitracin prevents monomer export and halts cell wall synthesis. It is used topically (too nephrotoxic for systemic use) for Gram-positive infections. This is a different target to penicillins (PBPs) and vancomycin (D-Ala-D-Ala).

mockBank.question: A mother living with HIV in the UK is considering breastfeeding her newborn. Which ONE of the following gives the MOST accurate advice?
options: A. Breastfeeding is safe if the mother has an undetectable viral load on HAART
B. Breastfeeding is recommended for all HIV-positive mothers as the benefits outweigh the risks in the UK
C. In high-income countries with access to safe formula milk and HAART, breastfeeding is avoided to eliminate the residual vertical transmission risk
D. All infants born to HIV-positive mothers are given antiretrovirals for life after birth
E. Breastfeeding only poses risk during mastitis episodes — it is safe otherwise
mockBank.explanation: In the UK (high-income setting with safe formula milk available), breastfeeding is advised AGAINST for HIV-positive mothers — even with undetectable viral load, there remains a small but real residual risk of vertical transmission via breastmilk. The recommendation differs in low-income settings where formula feeding risks (contaminated water, cost, stigma) may outweigh the breastfeeding transmission risk. UK guidelines: avoid breastfeeding + give infant PEP for 4 weeks.

mockBank.question: Aciclovir resistance in herpes simplex virus most commonly develops through which ONE of the following mechanisms?
options: A. Mutation in viral DNA polymerase preventing aciclovir triphosphate incorporation
B. Upregulation of efflux pumps that expel aciclovir from infected cells
C. Mutation or deletion in the viral thymidine kinase gene — virus cannot phosphorylate aciclovir
D. Overproduction of deoxythymidine by the infected cell, competing with aciclovir
E. Mutation in the viral GP120 protein preventing aciclovir binding
mockBank.explanation: Aciclovir resistance is rare but occurs primarily in immunocompromised patients on long-term aciclovir prophylaxis. Most commonly, mutations or deletions in the viral thymidine kinase (TK) gene render TK nonfunctional — the virus cannot phosphorylate aciclovir to its active form. Less commonly, mutations in viral DNA polymerase prevent incorporation of aciclovir triphosphate. Resistant HSV is treated with foscarnet (which bypasses the need for TK activation).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following infections would require isolation of the patient in a dental practice due to risk of airborne transmission?
options: A. HIV infection with detectable viral load
B. Hepatitis C — highly transmissible by aerosol in dental settings
C. Active pulmonary tuberculosis
D. Syphilis — primary stage genital ulcer
E. Herpes simplex labialis in the blister phase
mockBank.explanation: Pulmonary TB is the only common infection in this list transmitted by the airborne route (droplet nuclei — tiny particles that remain airborne for hours). Dental procedures generate aerosols, creating high transmission risk. HIV (A) and HCV (B) are blood-borne — standard precautions prevent transmission. Syphilis (D) is transmitted by direct contact with chancre. HSV labialis (E) is contact-transmitted and poses low risk to staff with universal precautions and avoidance of touching lesions.

mockBank.question: A dental patient on long-term phenytoin for epilepsy requires antibiotic treatment for a severe dentoalveolar abscess. Which ONE of the following antibiotics has a potentially significant pharmacokinetic interaction with phenytoin?
options: A. Penicillin V — no pharmacokinetic interaction
B. Metronidazole — inhibits CYP2C9, the primary enzyme metabolising phenytoin, risking phenytoin toxicity
C. Amoxicillin — directly inhibits phenytoin hepatic clearance
D. Nystatin — systemic absorption competes with phenytoin for CYP450 binding
E. Doxycycline — induces CYP450 enzymes reducing phenytoin levels
mockBank.explanation: Metronidazole inhibits CYP2C9 (and CYP3A4), the primary hepatic enzyme responsible for phenytoin metabolism. Concurrent use can reduce phenytoin clearance, causing phenytoin accumulation and toxicity (nystagmus, ataxia, diplopia, dysarthria). In a patient on phenytoin who requires antibiotics for a dental abscess, consider using amoxicillin (no CYP interaction) instead of metronidazole if clinically appropriate. If metronidazole is essential, warn the patient and monitor phenytoin levels.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following mechanisms allows influenza virus to undergo antigenic SHIFT — the process responsible for pandemic influenza?
options: A. Rapid point mutations in the haemagglutinin (HA) gene during replication
B. Reassortment of RNA gene segments when two different influenza strains infect the same host cell simultaneously
C. Integration of influenza RNA into the host genome where it acquires new mutations
D. Recombination of influenza DNA with host cell DNA in the cell nucleus
E. Horizontal gene transfer via conjugation between influenza and other ssRNA viruses
mockBank.explanation: Influenza has a segmented genome (8 RNA segments). Antigenic SHIFT = major genomic reassortment: two different influenza strains (e.g., human H3N2 + avian H5N1) co-infect the same cell → their gene segments mix randomly during replication → a novel virus emerges with a completely new combination of HA/NA antigens that no human population has immunity to → pandemic potential. Antigenic DRIFT = minor point mutations (yearly vaccine updates).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following liver function tests is MOST specific for alcoholic liver disease when isolated and disproportionately elevated?
options: A. Alanine transaminase (ALT)
B. Aspartate transaminase (AST)
C. Gamma-glutamyl transpeptidase (GGT)
D. Alkaline phosphatase (ALP)
E. Bilirubin
mockBank.explanation: GGT is the most sensitive marker for alcoholic liver disease — alcohol induces hepatic GGT synthesis. A disproportionately elevated GGT (particularly when ALT and AST are only mildly raised) in the clinical context of alcohol misuse is highly suggestive. ALP is predominantly elevated in cholestatic/obstructive liver disease (gallstones, PBC). ALT is more specific for hepatocellular damage. Bilirubin is non-specific, elevated in many hepatic and non-hepatic conditions.

mockBank.question: The term 'antigenic variation' describes a mechanism used by certain pathogens to evade the immune response. Which ONE of the following pathogens is a classic example of this?
options: A. Streptococcus pyogenes — produces a different toxin every year
B. Mycobacterium tuberculosis — changes its surface antigens during macrophage residence
C. Influenza virus — undergoes antigenic drift (point mutations in HA/NA) annually, requiring annual vaccine reformulation
D. Herpes simplex virus — changes GP120 annually to evade immunity
E. Staphylococcus aureus — produces a different capsular polysaccharide with each division
mockBank.explanation: Influenza undergoes antigenic drift (gradual accumulation of point mutations in haemagglutinin and neuraminidase genes during replication). The RNA polymerase of influenza has no proofreading activity — it introduces errors at a rate ~1 million times higher than DNA polymerases. These mutations cause subtle changes in HA/NA epitopes, allowing the virus to evade existing antibodies. This necessitates annual reformulation of the seasonal influenza vaccine.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following is a recognised appropriate indication for antibiotic prophylaxis in dentistry?
options: A. A routine extraction in a healthy patient with no medical history
B. An extraction in a patient who previously received radiation to the jaws for head and neck cancer
C. A patient with a hip replacement — routine antibiotic prophylaxis for all dental procedures
D. A patient with atrial fibrillation on warfarin before a simple scale and polish
E. Filling a cavity in an immunocompetent adult — prevention of post-operative infection
mockBank.explanation: Antibiotic prophylaxis is indicated before extractions (and other oral surgery) in patients with previous jaw radiation to reduce the risk of osteoradionecrosis (ORN) — radiation impairs bone vascular supply and healing. Amoxicillin 3g (or clindamycin 600mg if penicillin-allergic) 1 hour before the procedure is recommended. Joint replacement patients (C) do NOT require routine dental antibiotic prophylaxis per current UK guidelines (NICE/BSOS consensus). IE prophylaxis (for AF + warfarin patients) is also no longer recommended routinely.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the role of C3b in the complement cascade?
options: A. C3b acts as a chemotactic factor, recruiting neutrophils to the site of infection
B. C3b coats bacterial surfaces to mark them for phagocytosis (opsonisation)
C. C3b initiates the membrane attack complex by binding to C6
D. C3b is an anaphylatoxin that triggers mast cell degranulation
E. C3b activates T lymphocytes via MHC Class II presentation
mockBank.explanation: C3b is the primary opsonin of the complement system. It coats (opsonises) bacterial surfaces, making them recognisable to phagocytes that express complement receptors. This greatly enhances phagocytosis efficiency. C3a and C5a (not C3b) are the chemotactic anaphylatoxins that recruit neutrophils (A) and trigger mast cell degranulation (D). C5b (not C3b) initiates MAC formation by recruiting C6-C9 (C). T lymphocyte activation is antigen-specific and not complement-mediated (E).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST distinguishes innate immunity from adaptive immunity?
options: A. Innate immunity involves B cells producing IgM; adaptive immunity involves T cells
B. Innate immunity is antigen-specific; adaptive immunity is non-specific
C. Innate immunity acts immediately and non-specifically; adaptive immunity is antigen-specific and develops over days
D. Innate immunity produces immunological memory; adaptive immunity does not
E. Innate immunity is only effective against extracellular pathogens; adaptive immunity handles intracellular pathogens
mockBank.explanation: Innate immunity is the immediate (within hours), non-specific first line of defence — it uses pattern recognition receptors to detect common pathogen patterns. Adaptive immunity is antigen-specific and takes days to weeks to develop (clonal expansion and differentiation required). A is wrong: B cells and IgM are part of adaptive immunity. B is wrong: it's the reverse — innate is non-specific, adaptive is antigen-specific. D is wrong: immunological memory is a hallmark of adaptive, not innate immunity. E is wrong: both arms of immunity can respond to both intracellular and extracellular pathogens.

mockBank.question: A dentist treating an HIV-positive patient wants to understand why the patient is susceptible to intracellular pathogens. CD4+ T helper cells recognise antigen presented by which ONE of the following?
options: A. MHC Class I molecules on any nucleated cell
B. MHC Class II molecules on antigen-presenting cells
C. CD8 co-receptor on cytotoxic T lymphocytes
D. Natural killer cells via Fc receptor binding
E. IgG opsonins on macrophage surfaces
mockBank.explanation: CD4+ T helper cells recognise exogenous antigens presented by MHC Class II molecules on antigen-presenting cells (dendritic cells, macrophages, B cells). HIV destroys CD4+ T cells, impairing this recognition and therefore impairs all downstream adaptive immune responses. MHC Class I (A) presents endogenous antigens to CD8+ cytotoxic T cells. CD8 (C) is the co-receptor for cytotoxic T cells, not helper T cells. Natural killer cells (D) are innate immune cells that do not rely on antigen presentation. IgG opsonins (E) facilitate phagocytosis by macrophages via Fc receptors, not T cell activation.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes a superantigen and its clinical consequence?
options: A. A toxin encoded on bacteriophages that directly kills host cells by membrane lysis
B. A bacterial protein that bypasses normal antigen presentation and activates up to 25% of all T cells, causing massive cytokine release
C. An opsonin produced by the host that enhances phagocytosis of toxin-producing bacteria
D. A plasmid-encoded adhesin that enhances bacterial colonisation of mucosal surfaces
E. A complement-activating protein that triggers the membrane attack complex against host cells
mockBank.explanation: Superantigens (e.g., staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome toxin-1, TSST-1) cross-link MHC Class II molecules with T cell receptors non-specifically, activating 20–25% of all T cells simultaneously (vs. the 0.001% in normal antigen-specific responses). This causes massive, unregulated cytokine release ('cytokine storm'), leading to toxic shock syndrome with hypotension, fever, multi-organ failure, and 20% mortality. A describes bacteriophage-encoded membrane-lytic toxins. C describes opsonins (complement C3b or IgG). D describes adhesins. E describes complement-activating mechanisms.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following represents the MOST important non-immunological host defence mechanism in the oral cavity?
options: A. Production of IgG antibodies against oral pathogens
B. Phagocytosis of bacteria by circulating neutrophils
C. Salivary flow and its content of lysozyme, lactoferrin, and secretory IgA
D. Complement C3b opsonisation of dental plaque bacteria
E. CD8+ T cell destruction of virally infected oral epithelial cells
mockBank.explanation: Saliva provides the most important non-immunological oral defence. Salivary flow mechanically clears bacteria, food debris, and pathogens. Lysozyme cleaves bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan, lactoferrin sequesters iron (essential for bacterial growth), and secretory IgA (sIgA) prevents microbial adherence to mucosal surfaces. Reduced salivary flow (xerostomia from Sjögren's, radiotherapy, or medications) dramatically increases oral infection susceptibility. IgG (A) and CD8+ T cells (E) are adaptive immune responses. Neutrophil phagocytosis (B) and C3b opsonisation (D) are systemic innate responses.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following viral structural components is MOST important for determining host tropism (the ability of a virus to infect specific cell types)?
options: A. The viral capsid protein
B. The viral envelope glycoproteins that bind to specific host cell surface receptors
C. The viral RNA polymerase that determines replication fidelity
D. The nucleocapsid that protects the viral genome from host nucleases
E. The viral protease that cleaves host cell proteins during replication
mockBank.explanation: Viral envelope glycoproteins (e.g., HIV gp120, influenza haemagglutinin) determine host tropism by binding to specific receptors on target cells. HIV gp120 binds CD4 and co-receptors (CCR5/CXCR4) — explaining why HIV only infects CD4+ T cells. Influenza haemagglutinin binds sialic acid receptors on respiratory epithelium. Capsid proteins (A) protect genome and aid in cell entry for non-enveloped viruses. RNA polymerase (C) determines mutation rate, not tropism. Nucleocapsid (D) protects genomic material. Protease (E) is important in viral maturation but not tropism.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the eclipse period during viral replication?
options: A. The time from initial exposure to onset of symptoms
B. The interval after cell entry during which intact virions cannot be detected inside or outside the cell
C. The period when the immune system suppresses viral replication below detectable levels
D. The time between viral shedding peaks in a primary infection
E. The latency period in herpes simplex virus between primary and secondary infections
mockBank.explanation: The eclipse period is the window after viral entry into a host cell during which intact infectious virions cannot be detected — the virus has disassembled (uncoated) its genome, and new virions have not yet been assembled. It is distinct from the incubation period (A, which is time from exposure to symptoms). Immune suppression of viral load (C) is not the eclipse period. Shedding peaks (D) relate to the full replication cycle. HSV latency (E) is a distinct mechanism of viral persistence, not the eclipse period.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the Baltimore classification system for viruses?
options: A. Classifies viruses by their mode of transmission (droplet, blood-borne, faecal-oral)
B. Classifies viruses by genome type (DNA/RNA), strandedness, and relationship of genome to mRNA
C. Classifies viruses by the type of disease they cause (respiratory, gastrointestinal, CNS)
D. Classifies viruses by their geographic distribution and endemic regions
E. Classifies viruses by whether they are enveloped or non-enveloped
mockBank.explanation: The Baltimore classification groups viruses into 7 classes based on their genome type (DNA or RNA), strandedness (double or single stranded), and whether the genomic sense strand directly serves as mRNA (positive-sense) or must be transcribed first (negative-sense), or whether they use reverse transcriptase (retroviruses). This reflects how viruses replicate and has functional implications. Transmission routes (A), disease type (C), geography (D), and envelope status (E) are used in other classification systems.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes a latent viral infection?
options: A. A chronic infection where virus replicates continuously at low levels
B. A slow infection where viral replication proceeds very slowly over many years
C. A state where viral genetic material persists in host cells but infectious virus is not produced unless reactivation occurs
D. An abortive infection where the virus fails to replicate and is cleared by the immune system
E. A persistent infection where the virus integrates into every host cell chromosome simultaneously
mockBank.explanation: In a latent infection, viral DNA (or RNA in the case of retroviruses) persists in host cells without producing infectious virions. The virus is not actively replicating during latency but can reactivate under triggers (stress, immunosuppression, illness) to cause recurrent disease. Examples: HSV-1 latent in trigeminal ganglia (reactivates as cold sores), VZV latent in dorsal root ganglia (reactivates as shingles). Chronic infection (A) implies ongoing low-level replication. Slow infections (B) such as prion diseases are distinct. Abortive infection (D) implies viral clearance. Integration into every cell (E) occurs only with specific retroviruses.

mockBank.question: A patient presents with a vesicular lesion on the lip. The clinician suspects herpes simplex virus. Which ONE of the following diagnostic methods provides the MOST rapid confirmation?
options: A. Viral culture — growing virus from a swab specimen
B. Serology — measuring IgG antibodies to HSV
C. PCR — detecting viral DNA directly from a lesion swab
D. Tzanck smear — examining cells scraped from the base of the vesicle
E. Electron microscopy — visualising viral particles in vesicle fluid
mockBank.explanation: PCR is the gold standard for rapid, sensitive HSV diagnosis — it detects viral DNA directly from a lesion swab, giving results within hours. It is both more sensitive and faster than culture. Viral culture (A) takes days and is less sensitive for recurrent disease. IgG serology (B) detects past exposure, not active infection — IgM would be more useful for primary infection but has poor sensitivity. Tzanck smear (D) shows multinucleated giant cells but cannot distinguish between herpesvirus types (HSV vs VZV). Electron microscopy (E) can identify virion morphology but is slow and requires specialist equipment.

mockBank.question: A 6-year-old child presents with high fever, extensive painful oral ulceration on the gingiva and hard palate, and vesicular lesions on the lips. The MOST likely diagnosis is which ONE of the following?
options: A. Recurrent herpes labialis (cold sores)
B. Primary herpetic gingivostomatitis caused by HSV-1
C. Aphthous stomatitis (recurrent mouth ulcers)
D. Herpangina caused by Coxsackievirus A
E. Oral candidiasis secondary to antibiotic use
mockBank.explanation: Primary herpetic gingivostomatitis is the classic presentation of first-time HSV-1 infection, typically in children aged 1–5. Features: fever, malaise, extensive painful gingivitis with vesicles and ulcers throughout the oral mucosa (gingiva, hard palate, tongue). It is self-limiting (10–14 days) but can require hospitalisation if oral intake is reduced. Recurrent herpes labialis (A) is milder and localised to the vermilion border — it occurs in adults with prior HSV exposure. Aphthous ulcers (C) do not cause fever and do not affect the attached gingiva or skin. Herpangina (D) affects the soft palate/tonsils and is associated with hand, foot, and mouth disease. Candidiasis (E) causes white plaques that can be wiped off, not vesicles.

mockBank.question: A 65-year-old patient presents with unilateral facial pain and vesicular rash along the distribution of the maxillary division of the trigeminal nerve. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate management?
options: A. Prescribe aciclovir 200mg five times daily for 5 days
B. Prescribe aciclovir 800mg five times daily for 7 days, starting within 72 hours
C. Reassure and review — shingles always resolves without antiviral treatment
D. Prescribe valaciclovir 500mg twice daily for 5 days
E. Refer immediately to ophthalmology without prescribing antivirals
mockBank.explanation: Shingles (herpes zoster reactivation) in the trigeminal nerve distribution requires antiviral therapy started within 72 hours of rash onset to reduce severity and the risk of post-herpetic neuralgia (PHN). The correct dose is aciclovir 800mg five times daily for 7 days (much higher than the 200mg dose used for recurrent herpes labialis). Aciclovir 200mg × 5 daily (A) is the dose for recurrent herpes labialis — too low for shingles. Reassurance alone (C) is incorrect — untreated shingles has 20–40% risk of PHN. Valaciclovir 500mg (D) is the recurrent herpes labialis dose — shingles requires valaciclovir 1g three times daily. If ophthalmic branch (V1) is involved, urgent ophthalmology referral is needed but antivirals should be prescribed simultaneously (not delayed).

mockBank.question: A 19-year-old student presents with sore throat, fever, cervical lymphadenopathy, and fatigue. A Monospot test is positive. Which ONE of the following is the MOST important dental consideration for this patient?
options: A. Prescribe amoxicillin for the sore throat to prevent secondary bacterial infection
B. Avoid contact sports for 6–8 weeks due to the risk of splenic rupture
C. Schedule regular dental check-ups as EBV causes chronic periodontitis
D. Prescribe aciclovir 800mg five times daily as EBV is a herpesvirus
E. Refer to haematology immediately as lymphadenopathy indicates lymphoma
mockBank.explanation: Infectious mononucleosis (EBV) causes splenomegaly in up to 50% of cases. Splenic rupture is a life-threatening emergency that can occur with minimal trauma. Patients must avoid contact sports and heavy exercise for 6–8 weeks. This is critical if the patient is an athlete or has a physically demanding lifestyle. Amoxicillin/ampicillin (A) must be AVOIDED in EBV mononucleosis — it causes a characteristic maculopapular rash in 80–90% of patients. Aciclovir (D) has no proven benefit in EBV mononucleosis and is not routinely prescribed. EBV does not cause chronic periodontitis (C). Lymphadenopathy in mononucleosis is expected and does not warrant immediate haematology referral unless the presentation is atypical (persistent > 6 weeks, B symptoms, very high lymphocyte counts).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following groups of patients is at HIGHEST risk of developing severe disseminated CMV disease?
options: A. Healthy immunocompetent adults with primary CMV infection
B. Elderly patients with controlled type 2 diabetes
C. Patients following solid organ transplantation on immunosuppressive therapy
D. Pregnant women in the third trimester
E. Patients with controlled HIV infection on antiretroviral therapy with CD4 count > 500
mockBank.explanation: Solid organ transplant recipients on immunosuppressive therapy (tacrolimus, ciclosporin, azathioprine, corticosteroids) are at the highest risk of severe CMV disease because their impaired T cell immunity cannot control CMV reactivation or primary infection from the donor organ. CMV can cause pneumonitis, retinitis, colitis, and encephalitis in this group. CMV in immunocompetent adults (A) causes a mild mononucleosis-like illness. Controlled diabetics (B) are not significantly immunocompromised for CMV. Primary CMV infection in pregnancy (D) is dangerous to the fetus (congenital CMV) but the mother rarely develops severe disease. Controlled HIV with CD4 > 500 (E) is not significantly immunocompromised — CMV retinitis typically occurs when CD4 < 50.

mockBank.question: Aciclovir is selectively toxic to herpesvirus-infected cells compared to uninfected host cells. Which ONE of the following BEST explains this selectivity?
options: A. Aciclovir is only taken up by cells that express viral envelope glycoproteins
B. Aciclovir is phosphorylated by viral thymidine kinase in infected cells, activating it to its active triphosphate form
C. Aciclovir directly cleaves viral DNA by acting as a nuclease
D. Aciclovir inhibits viral protease, preventing cleavage of viral polyproteins
E. Aciclovir acts as a viral envelope glycoprotein antagonist, preventing cell entry
mockBank.explanation: Aciclovir's selectivity is based on its activation mechanism. It is a prodrug that requires phosphorylation by viral thymidine kinase (TK), an enzyme present only in herpesvirus-infected cells. Viral TK phosphorylates aciclovir to aciclovir monophosphate; host cell kinases then convert it to the active aciclovir triphosphate, which inhibits viral DNA polymerase and acts as a chain terminator. Uninfected cells lack viral TK and therefore cannot activate aciclovir — this is the basis of its selectivity and safety. It does not act as a nuclease (C), protease inhibitor (D), or glycoprotein antagonist (E). Cell uptake (A) is not the selectivity mechanism.

mockBank.question: HIV uses which ONE of the following combinations of receptors to enter CD4+ T cells?
options: A. CD4 alone is sufficient for HIV entry into T cells
B. CD4 as the primary receptor and either CCR5 or CXCR4 as co-receptors
C. CD8 and CCR5 as dual co-receptors
D. CXCR4 alone is sufficient for HIV entry into macrophages
E. CD4 and CD8 as dual primary receptors with CCR5 as co-receptor
mockBank.explanation: HIV entry requires two sequential receptor interactions: first, gp120 binds CD4 (the primary receptor), causing a conformational change that exposes a co-receptor binding site. The co-receptor is either CCR5 (used by macrophage-tropic R5 strains, predominant in early infection) or CXCR4 (used by T cell-tropic X4 strains, predominant in late AIDS). This is clinically important: individuals homozygous for the CCR5Δ32 deletion are highly resistant to HIV infection. CD4 alone (A) is insufficient — co-receptor binding is required for membrane fusion. CD8 (C, E) is expressed on cytotoxic T cells and is not an HIV receptor. CXCR4 alone (D) cannot mediate entry without the CD4 primary binding step.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the typical course of untreated HIV infection over time?
options: A. Acute seroconversion illness → prolonged clinical latency → AIDS-defining illness
B. Immediate progression to AIDS-defining illness within months of infection
C. Prolonged asymptomatic period → acute seroconversion illness → AIDS
D. Cyclical episodes of acute illness separated by complete immune recovery
E. Slow but continuous decline in viral load with preservation of CD4 count
mockBank.explanation: Untreated HIV follows a predictable natural history: (1) Acute retroviral syndrome (seroconversion illness) 2–4 weeks after infection — fever, lymphadenopathy, rash, pharyngitis; (2) Clinical latency (asymptomatic period) lasting 10 years on average — CD4 count slowly declines, viral load relatively stable; (3) AIDS when CD4 < 200 cells/μL — AIDS-defining illnesses (PCP, CMV retinitis, Kaposi's sarcoma) develop. Immediate AIDS progression (B) occurs only in rare 'rapid progressors'. The sequence C describes is incorrect — seroconversion comes first. Cyclical illness with recovery (D) is not characteristic. Viral load progressively increases (not decreases) without treatment (E).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following combinations represents a HAART (highly active antiretroviral therapy) regimen that includes three different drug classes?
options: A. Zidovudine + lamivudine (both NRTIs) + efavirenz (NNRTI)
B. Two NRTIs only — zidovudine + lamivudine
C. A single protease inhibitor with no backbone NRTIs
D. Integrase inhibitor alone at high dose
E. Two nucleoside analogues only — tenofovir + emtricitabine
mockBank.explanation: HAART typically combines: 2 NRTIs (the 'backbone') + 1 drug from another class (NNRTI, protease inhibitor, or integrase inhibitor). Zidovudine (AZT) + lamivudine (3TC) are NRTIs; efavirenz is an NNRTI — together these target two different stages of HIV replication (reverse transcription). The triple combination dramatically reduces the risk of resistance emergence (resistance to all three simultaneously would require multiple simultaneous mutations). Two NRTIs alone (B, E) are inadequate monotherapy for HAART. A single protease inhibitor (C) or integrase inhibitor alone (D) would rapidly generate resistance.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following oral conditions is considered an AIDS-defining illness and is caused by reactivation of a herpesvirus in severely immunocompromised patients?
options: A. Oral candidiasis (pseudomembranous)
B. Hairy leukoplakia (EBV-associated white corrugated lesions on lateral tongue)
C. Linear gingival erythema
D. Necrotising ulcerative periodontitis
E. Oral Kaposi's sarcoma (HHV-8 associated)
mockBank.explanation: Oral Kaposi's sarcoma is an AIDS-defining illness caused by HHV-8 (Kaposi's sarcoma herpesvirus) reactivation in severely immunocompromised patients (CD4 < 100). It presents as purple/red macular or nodular lesions on the palate or gingiva. Oral candidiasis (A) is common in HIV but not an AIDS-defining illness — it is an early indicator of immunocompromise. Hairy leukoplakia (B) is EBV-associated and indicates significant immunocompromise but is not classified as AIDS-defining. Linear gingival erythema (C) and necrotising ulcerative periodontitis (D) are HIV-associated periodontal conditions but not AIDS-defining illnesses. The AIDS-defining oral lesions are Kaposi's sarcoma and, in some classifications, severe oesophageal candidiasis.

mockBank.question: A healthcare worker suffers a needlestick injury from a needle used on an HIV-positive patient. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate immediate management?
options: A. Immediate HIV testing of the healthcare worker; if positive, start HAART
B. Wash the wound thoroughly, encourage bleeding, report to occupational health, and start post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) within 1 hour
C. Do nothing if the healthcare worker has a detectable HIV antibody titre from previous vaccination
D. Wait 48 hours for baseline viral load result before deciding on PEP
E. Prescribe a single dose of zidovudine as prophylaxis
mockBank.explanation: Immediate management of HIV needlestick: wash wound under running water (do NOT suck), encourage bleeding, report immediately to occupational health. PEP must be started within 1 hour of exposure (ideally) and no later than 72 hours — it reduces seroconversion risk by ~80%. PEP is a 28-day course of 2–3 antiretrovirals. HIV testing of the healthcare worker (A) is done as a baseline but PEP should start before results return. There is no HIV vaccine (C) — anti-HIV antibodies indicate infection not protection. Waiting 48 hours (D) is too late for optimal PEP efficacy. A single NRTI dose (E) is inadequate — combination therapy is required.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the epidemiology and prevention of Hepatitis A virus (HAV)?
options: A. HAV is blood-borne and prevented by safe sex practices and needle exchange programmes
B. HAV is transmitted faecal-orally, causes no chronic infection, and is preventable by vaccination
C. HAV causes chronic infection in 30% of cases and is treated with interferon alpha
D. HAV is endemic only in tropical countries and requires no prevention in the UK
E. HAV is transmitted sexually and by vertical mother-to-child transmission
mockBank.explanation: HAV is transmitted faecal-orally (contaminated water, food, shellfish, person-to-person contact). It causes acute self-limiting hepatitis with no chronic carrier state — all patients recover and clear the virus. Prevention is by vaccination (2 doses give lifelong protection), hand hygiene, and safe food/water. HAV is blood-borne (A) — it is not blood-borne, unlike HBV and HCV. HAV does not cause chronic infection (C). HAV is globally distributed including developed countries (D) — travellers to endemic areas are at risk. Sexual transmission is not the primary route (E) — vertical transmission does not occur.

mockBank.question: A patient has the following serology: HBsAg negative, anti-HBs positive, anti-HBc negative. Which ONE of the following BEST interprets this result?
options: A. The patient has chronic HBV infection
B. The patient has resolved previous HBV infection with natural immunity
C. The patient is a non-immune individual with no previous exposure or vaccination
D. The patient has received HBV vaccination and developed vaccine-induced immunity
E. The patient is in the window period after acute HBV infection
mockBank.explanation: The serological pattern HBsAg(-), anti-HBs(+), anti-HBc(-) = vaccine-induced immunity. Vaccination contains only HBsAg (not whole virus), so only anti-HBs develops — there is no anti-HBc because the patient was never infected with the whole virus. Contrast with natural immunity (B): HBsAg(-), anti-HBs(+), anti-HBc(+) — both anti-HBs and anti-HBc are positive after natural infection and recovery. Chronic HBV (A): HBsAg persistent >6 months + anti-HBc. Non-immune with no exposure (C): all three negative. Window period (E): HBsAg falls below detection but anti-HBs not yet risen — anti-HBc IgM would be the only positive marker.

mockBank.question: A patient is HBsAg positive and HBeAg positive. Which ONE of the following BEST describes this patient's infectivity status?
options: A. Low infectivity — HBeAg indicates early resolved infection
B. High infectivity — HBeAg indicates active viral replication and high viral load
C. No infectivity — HBsAg positivity without HBeAg antibodies indicates cleared infection
D. Moderate infectivity — only the healthcare worker's vaccination status matters
E. Low infectivity — HBsAg in isolation does not indicate replication
mockBank.explanation: HBeAg (hepatitis B e antigen) is a marker of active viral replication and high infectivity. HBeAg-positive patients typically have viral loads >1 million copies/mL and carry the highest transmission risk. This is critical in dental practice — an HBeAg-positive patient should be treated with full standard infection control precautions, and the dental team's vaccination and antibody status must be confirmed. Anti-HBe positivity (not HBeAg) indicates declining replication and lower (but not absent) infectivity. HBsAg positivity alone (E) does not distinguish replication levels — HBeAg and viral load testing are required. HBeAg does not indicate cleared infection (A).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes Hepatitis C virus (HCV) compared to HBV?
options: A. HCV has an effective vaccine; HBV does not
B. HCV rarely progresses to chronic infection; HBV chronicity rate is 90% in adults
C. HCV is an RNA virus with a high mutation rate, making vaccine development difficult, and causes chronic infection in approximately 80% of cases
D. HCV is predominantly transmitted faecal-orally, unlike HBV which is blood-borne
E. HCV is effectively treated with interferon alpha monotherapy in >95% of patients
mockBank.explanation: HCV is a positive-sense single-stranded RNA virus with a highly error-prone RNA polymerase, generating enormous genetic diversity (6 major genotypes, many subtypes). This diversity has prevented vaccine development. HCV causes chronic infection in ~80% of acutely infected individuals (vs. 5–10% in immunocompetent adults for HBV), with 20–30% progressing to cirrhosis over 20 years. HBV has an effective vaccine (A); no HCV vaccine exists. HCV chronicity is high, not low (B). Both HBV and HCV are blood-borne/sexual (D). Direct-acting antivirals (DAAs, not interferon) now cure HCV in >95% of patients (E).

mockBank.question: A patient with liver cirrhosis presents for extraction of a lower molar. Clotting investigations show: PT 21 seconds (normal <13), INR 1.9, platelet count 68 × 10⁹/L. Which ONE of the following is the MOST important pre-operative consideration?
options: A. Prescribe vitamin K 10mg oral for 3 days before extraction
B. Refer to haematology for a coagulation factor infusion and platelet transfusion before extraction
C. Liaise with the patient's hepatologist, ensure haemostatic measures are prepared, and consider hospital-based dental care
D. Proceed with extraction using a dry socket packing only
E. Prescribe NSAIDs post-operatively for pain control
mockBank.explanation: Cirrhotic patients have multiple haemostatic defects: reduced synthesis of clotting factors (II, VII, IX, X all made in liver), thrombocytopaenia (from portal hypertension → splenic sequestration), and impaired fibrinolysis. An INR of 1.9 and platelets of 68 × 10⁹/L represent significant bleeding risk. The correct approach is to liaise with the hepatologist, plan haemostatic measures (tranexamic acid rinse, resorbable haemostats, suturing, gelfoam), and for high-risk cases, refer to secondary care for hospital-based dentistry. Oral vitamin K (A) may help if the patient is vitamin K deficient but does not reverse all coagulation defects in cirrhosis. Infusion/transfusion (B) may be needed but requires haematology input, not as a first-line independent decision. Proceeding without planning (D) risks life-threatening haemorrhage. NSAIDs (E) are contraindicated in liver disease — they increase bleeding risk and can precipitate hepatic failure.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the relationship between Hepatitis D virus (HDV) and Hepatitis B virus (HBV)?
options: A. HDV and HBV are completely independent viruses that both cause chronic hepatitis
B. HDV is a defective RNA virus that requires HBsAg for its envelope and can only infect patients who already have HBV
C. HDV causes acute hepatitis only and requires HBV for transmission but not for replication
D. HDV can superinfect any patient regardless of their HBV status
E. HBV vaccination provides protection against HAV but not HDV
mockBank.explanation: HDV is a unique defective RNA virusoid that cannot replicate independently. It requires HBsAg (Hepatitis B surface antigen) to form its own envelope for packaging and transmission. Therefore, HDV can only infect individuals who are already HBV-infected (co-infection or superinfection). HBV vaccination protects against both HBV and HDV (because without HBV infection, HDV cannot replicate or cause disease). Co-infection (HDV + HBV simultaneously) causes severe acute hepatitis. Superinfection (HDV acquired later by a chronic HBV carrier) dramatically accelerates cirrhosis. The two are not independent (A). HDV can cause chronic infection in superinfection (C). HDV requires concurrent HBV infection (D).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following patients with Hepatitis E infection carries the HIGHEST risk of fulminant hepatic failure and death?
options: A. A healthy immunocompetent 25-year-old male
B. A 35-year-old woman in her third trimester of pregnancy
C. A 60-year-old man with controlled type 2 diabetes
D. A 45-year-old healthcare worker with prior HEV vaccination
E. A child aged 10 years with no underlying medical conditions
mockBank.explanation: HEV infection during pregnancy, particularly in the third trimester, carries a mortality rate of 20–25% due to fulminant hepatic failure. The mechanism is thought to involve immune modulation during pregnancy and altered HEV immune response. This is one of the most important high-yield facts about HEV. In the general population (A, C, E), HEV causes self-limiting acute hepatitis with <1% mortality. There is no licensed HEV vaccine in the UK (D — a vaccine exists in China but not widely available). HEV is transmitted faecal-orally (contaminated water, especially in endemic regions), and also zoonotically via undercooked pork.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST explains why Gram-positive bacteria retain the crystal violet stain during the Gram staining procedure?
options: A. Gram-positive bacteria have an outer membrane that traps the crystal violet-iodine complex
B. Gram-positive bacteria have a thick peptidoglycan layer that retains the crystal violet-iodine complex after decolourisation
C. Gram-positive bacteria produce more lipopolysaccharide, which binds crystal violet
D. Gram-positive bacteria lack a capsule, allowing crystal violet to penetrate more easily
E. Gram-positive bacteria have penicillin-binding proteins that bind crystal violet specifically
mockBank.explanation: Gram-positive bacteria have a thick peptidoglycan cell wall (20–80 nm) that forms a tight barrier. When decolourised with acetone-alcohol, this thick wall dehydrates and contracts, trapping the crystal violet-iodine complex inside the cell. Gram-negative bacteria have a thin peptidoglycan layer (2–7 nm) and an outer membrane containing lipopolysaccharide (LPS) — the decolourant dissolves the outer membrane lipids, washing out the crystal violet. They then take up the safranin counterstain and appear pink. Gram-negatives have the outer membrane (A), not Gram-positives. LPS (C) is characteristic of Gram-negatives. Capsules (D) can be found in both Gram types. Penicillin-binding proteins (E) are targets for penicillin action, not staining.

mockBank.question: A 45-year-old patient presents to the emergency department with rapidly spreading neck swelling, trismus, and difficulty breathing following a lower second molar extraction 48 hours ago. Examination reveals bilateral submandibular swelling, woody induration, and floor of mouth elevation. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate immediate management?
options: A. IV amoxicillin and metronidazole only, with review at 24 hours
B. Urgent surgical airway management, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, and emergency surgical drainage
C. Oral amoxicillin 500mg TDS and hot saltwater rinses, review in 48 hours
D. Drain the submandibular space intraorally and prescribe metronidazole
E. CT scan first, then antibiotic therapy, with surgery deferred for 72 hours
mockBank.explanation: This presentation describes Ludwig's angina — a rapidly progressive bilateral cellulitis of the submandibular, sublingual, and submental spaces. It is a life-threatening emergency. The most feared complication is airway obstruction (due to floor of mouth elevation and tongue displacement). Management priorities: (1) Secure the airway IMMEDIATELY (awake fibre-optic intubation or surgical airway if oedema is severe — do not delay for CT); (2) IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (co-amoxiclav + metronidazole) to cover mixed aerobic/anaerobic flora; (3) Emergency surgical drainage in theatre. Antibiotics alone (A) are inadequate for established deep space infection with airway threat. Oral antibiotics (C) are completely inappropriate — IV route is required. Intraoral drainage (D) is inadequate for bilateral space infection. Deferring surgery for 72 hours (E) risks fatal airway obstruction.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the characteristic clinical features and treatment of cervicofacial actinomycosis?
options: A. Rapidly progressive cellulitis of the submandibular space, treated with emergency drainage
B. Chronic low-grade granulomatous infection with 'sulphur granules', draining sinuses, and treated with prolonged high-dose penicillin
C. Acute painful lymph node enlargement in a child following a cat scratch, treated with azithromycin
D. Fungal infection of the jaw bone causing periapical radiolucency, treated with fluconazole
E. Viral infection of the salivary glands causing facial swelling, treated with antivirals
mockBank.explanation: Cervicofacial actinomycosis is caused by Actinomyces israelii (a Gram-positive, filamentous, anaerobic commensal of the oral flora). Classic features: chronic (weeks to months), painless/mildly painful jaw swelling, sinus tracts discharging 'sulphur granules' (yellow aggregates of bacterial filaments), woody induration, crossing anatomical tissue planes. Treatment: prolonged penicillin (6–12 months) to penetrate the dense fibrotic granuloma. Ludwig's angina (A) is a different condition — acute and rapidly progressive. Cat scratch disease (C) is caused by Bartonella henselae. Fungal periapical infection (D) is extremely rare. Viral salivary gland infection (E) describes mumps.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following factors MOST increases the risk of developing osteomyelitis of the jaw following dental extraction?
options: A. History of well-controlled type 1 diabetes with HbA1c of 48 mmol/mol
B. Patient age under 30 with healthy bone marrow
C. Previous therapeutic radiation to the jaws (osteoradionecrosis predisposition)
D. Use of ibuprofen for post-operative pain control
E. Extraction of a maxillary central incisor in a systemically healthy patient
mockBank.explanation: Therapeutic radiation to the jaw (typically ≥ 54 Gy for head and neck cancer) causes obliterative endarteritis — blood vessel destruction reducing bone vascularity and healing capacity. This creates a risk of osteoradionecrosis (ORN) — infected avascular bone that fails to heal. Dental extraction is the most common trigger. Management includes antibiotic prophylaxis (amoxicillin 3g pre-operatively) and, ideally, completing all extractions before radiation therapy. Well-controlled diabetes (A) does not significantly increase osteomyelitis risk — poorly controlled diabetes would. Young healthy patients (B) have good immune function and bone healing capacity. NSAIDs (D) do not predispose to osteomyelitis. Maxillary anterior extractions (E) are low-risk due to good blood supply.

mockBank.question: A pus sample from a dental abscess is sent to the microbiology laboratory. Which ONE of the following processes provides the MOST clinically actionable information for antibiotic prescribing?
options: A. Microscopy alone — Gram stain to identify organism morphology
