Prions ARE found in lymphoid tissue and potentially in dental pulp (B). Glutaraldehyde (D) does not inactivate prions. The UK HAS strict restrictions (E).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the role of Toll-Like Receptors (TLRs) in innate immunity?
options: A. TLRs are expressed on T lymphocytes and recognise MHC-peptide complexes on antigen-presenting cells
B. TLRs are pattern recognition receptors that detect conserved microbial structures (PAMPs) and activate innate immune responses via NF-κB signalling
C. TLRs are complement receptors that bind C3b on opsonised bacteria
D. TLRs are found exclusively in the nucleus and detect viral double-stranded RNA
E. TLRs produce cytokines directly — they act as both receptor and cytokine factory
mockBank.explanation: Toll-Like Receptors (TLRs) are the prototypical pattern recognition receptors (PRRs) of innate immunity. They are expressed on macrophages, dendritic cells, neutrophils, and epithelial cells. TLRs recognise pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) — conserved microbial structures not present in human cells: LPS (TLR4), flagellin (TLR5), bacterial DNA (TLR9), viral dsRNA (TLR3), viral ssRNA (TLR7/8). TLR activation triggers NF-κB nuclear translocation → gene transcription → pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6, IL-12) and type I interferons. TLRs do NOT recognise MHC-peptide complexes (A) — that is the T cell receptor. TLRs do not bind C3b (C) — that is complement receptor 1 (CR1). TLRs are found on cell surfaces and in endosomes (TLR3, 7, 8, 9), not exclusively in the nucleus (D). TLRs signal for cytokine production but are not themselves cytokine-producing units (E).

mockBank.question: A patient with chronic liver disease secondary to HCV cirrhosis requires dental treatment and is in pain. Which ONE of the following analgesics is SAFEST for short-term pain control?
options: A. Aspirin 600mg QDS — a well-established analgesic with proven efficacy
B. Ibuprofen 400mg TDS — provides anti-inflammatory benefit
C. Paracetamol 500mg (reduced dose) with close monitoring — safest analgesic in liver disease at recommended doses
D. Dihydrocodeine 30mg TDS — opioids are preferred in liver disease as they avoid hepatic metabolism
E. Naproxen 500mg BD — a long-acting NSAID with superior analgesic effect
mockBank.explanation: Paracetamol is the analgesic of choice in liver disease at standard therapeutic doses (max 2g/day in severe cirrhosis, vs 4g/day in healthy adults). Although paracetamol is hepatically metabolised, at therapeutic doses it is safe — the concern is overdose (which depletes glutathione and accumulates the toxic metabolite NAPQI). NSAIDs (aspirin, ibuprofen, naproxen) are CONTRAINDICATED in liver disease: (1) Impair prostaglandin-mediated renal blood flow → acute kidney injury (the liver-kidney axis is already vulnerable in cirrhosis); (2) Increase bleeding risk (already impaired clotting in cirrhosis); (3) Can precipitate hepatic decompensation. Opioids (dihydrocodeine, D) are hepatically metabolised and accumulate in liver disease → risk of hepatic encephalopathy and respiratory depression — use with great caution if at all. Aspirin (A) also impairs platelet function — especially dangerous in cirrhosis with thrombocytopaenia.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following statements about Candida species in the oral cavity is MOST accurate?
options: A. Candida albicans is exclusively pathogenic — it is never found in the oral flora of healthy individuals
B. Candida albicans is a commensal yeast found in the oral flora of 50–60% of healthy adults, only causing disease when host defences are compromised
C. Oral candidiasis always requires systemic antifungal therapy with fluconazole
D. Candida glabrata is the most common cause of oral candidiasis and is more sensitive to azoles than C. albicans
E. All Candida species are equally sensitive to fluconazole — species identification is clinically irrelevant
mockBank.explanation: Candida albicans is a dimorphic commensal fungus found in the oral cavity of 40–60% of healthy adults without causing disease — it exists as non-pathogenic blastospores (yeast form) in a healthy host. Pathogenesis requires predisposing factors: immunosuppression, antibiotics (disrupting competing bacteria), corticosteroids (inhaled or systemic), diabetes, dentures, xerostomia, extremes of age. When conditions favour overgrowth, C. albicans switches to the pathogenic hyphal (filamentous) form that invades epithelial cells. Local treatment with nystatin or miconazole is first-line for most oral candidiasis (C is wrong). Candida glabrata (D) is increasingly important as it has reduced azole sensitivity — species identification matters clinically. Not all Candida species respond to fluconazole (E) — C. krusei is inherently resistant, C. glabrata shows reduced sensitivity.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following scenarios represents appropriate antibiotic prescribing according to UK antibiotic stewardship principles for dentistry?
options: A. Prescribing amoxicillin for every patient with dental pain to prevent infection progression
B. Prescribing antibiotics following all extractions to prevent dry socket and post-operative infection
C. Reserving antibiotics for spreading infections with systemic features (fever, trismus, lymphadenopathy) and always treating the cause surgically
D. Prescribing broad-spectrum antibiotics (co-amoxiclav) as first-line for all dental infections to avoid treatment failure
E. Prescribing 10-day antibiotic courses to ensure complete eradication of oral pathogens
mockBank.explanation: UK antibiotic stewardship in dentistry (SDCEP, NICE, PHE guidance) is built on the principle: treat the cause first, use antibiotics only when clinically indicated. Antibiotics are indicated for: spreading infections with systemic features (fever >38°C, trismus, dysphagia, lymphadenopathy, cellulitis), medically compromised patients, or when surgical source control is insufficient/delayed. The narrow-spectrum first-line antibiotic is amoxicillin (or phenoxymethylpenicillin) ± metronidazole, for 3–5 days. Prophylactic prescribing for pain prevention (A), post-extraction prophylaxis (B), and broad-spectrum first-line (D) are all examples of inappropriate antibiotic overuse. Ten-day courses (E) far exceed recommended duration. The key message: every antibiotic prescription without clear indication contributes to antimicrobial resistance — a global health emergency.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the clinical significance of CD4 count and viral load in monitoring HIV treatment response?
options: A. CD4 count reflects transmission risk; viral load reflects immune function
B. CD4 count reflects current immune function and predicts risk of opportunistic infections; viral load reflects treatment efficacy and transmission risk
C. Both CD4 count and viral load are useful for staging HIV but viral load is not monitored after starting HAART
D. A rising CD4 count always indicates HAART failure requiring treatment change
E. Viral load and CD4 count move in the same direction — both fall as HIV progresses
mockBank.explanation: CD4 count (normal 500–1500 cells/μL) reflects immune competence: it determines risk of specific opportunistic infections (PCP: CD4 <200; toxoplasma <100; CMV retinitis <50; MAC <50) and guides prophylaxis decisions. Viral load (HIV RNA copies/mL) measures active viral replication: an undetectable viral load (<20–40 copies/mL) on HAART indicates treatment success, negligible transmission risk (U=U), and predicted CD4 recovery. In untreated HIV, viral load and CD4 count move inversely (A, E are wrong) — high viral load → falling CD4. After starting HAART: viral load falls to undetectable within 3–6 months; CD4 count rises. Viral load IS monitored long-term on HAART to detect treatment failure (C is wrong). A rising CD4 on HAART (D) is a GOOD sign — it indicates immune reconstitution.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the antiviral role of type I interferons (IFN-α and IFN-β)?
options: A. Type I interferons directly kill virally infected cells by activating the complement membrane attack complex
B. Type I interferons are produced by virally infected cells and signal neighbouring cells to upregulate antiviral defence mechanisms, limiting viral spread
C. Type I interferons enhance B cell production of IgM antibodies specific to the infecting virus
D. Type I interferons are produced exclusively by natural killer cells after recognising MHC Class I downregulation
E. Type I interferons block viral entry by binding to viral surface glycoproteins
mockBank.explanation: Type I interferons (IFN-α from plasmacytoid dendritic cells/leukocytes; IFN-β from fibroblasts and most nucleated cells) are the body's rapid antiviral alarm system. When a cell detects intracellular viral dsRNA (via TLR3, RIG-I, MDA5), it secretes type I interferons. These act on neighbouring cells via the JAK-STAT pathway to: (1) Upregulate PKR (double-stranded RNA-activated protein kinase) — inhibits protein synthesis; (2) Upregulate 2'-5'OAS — activates RNase L to degrade viral RNA; (3) Enhance MHC Class I expression — facilitating CD8+ T cell recognition. This creates an antiviral state in cells before they are infected. Interferons do not directly kill cells via complement (A). IgM production (C) is an adaptive immune response. NK cells respond to interferons but are not the primary producers (D). Interferons do not bind viral glycoproteins (E).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate management for a non-immune healthcare worker who has had significant exposure to a child with active chickenpox?
options: A. No intervention required — chickenpox is mild in adults and healthcare workers are at no greater risk
B. Administer varicella-zoster immunoglobulin (VZIG) within 96 hours of exposure, exclude from work from days 8–21 post-exposure
C. Prescribe aciclovir prophylactically for 14 days
D. Administer live attenuated varicella vaccine immediately after exposure
E. Test for VZV IgG first; if positive, no action needed; if negative, give VZIG
mockBank.explanation: The correct approach for a healthcare worker with possible VZV exposure is: first confirm serostatus. Many adults who report no history of chickenpox are actually VZV IgG seropositive (due to subclinical childhood infection). If VZV IgG is POSITIVE → already immune → no action needed. If VZV IgG is NEGATIVE (truly non-immune) → give VZIG within 96 hours of exposure AND exclude from patient contact from days 8–21 post-exposure (the incubation period range when chickenpox could develop). VZV is serious in adults and immunocompromised patients (A is wrong). Aciclovir prophylaxis (C) is not standard UK practice. Live varicella vaccine (D) should not be given post-exposure as it takes weeks to generate immunity and could cause vaccine-strain VZV infection. E correctly describes the testing-first approach.

mockBank.question: A patient with known cirrhosis presents for a surgical procedure. Clotting tests show: INR 2.1, platelet count 55 × 10⁹/L, fibrinogen 0.8 g/L. Which ONE of the following BEST explains the mechanism of the coagulation abnormality?
options: A. The patient is taking warfarin — the INR elevation is entirely drug-induced

B. The liver produces all clotting factors except Factor VIII; cirrhosis reduces synthesis of factors II, V, VII, IX, X, and XI, causing coagulopathy. Thrombocytopaenia results from splenic sequestration due to portal hypertension
C. Thrombocytopaenia in cirrhosis is caused by bone marrow failure secondary to alcohol toxicity
D. The elevated INR indicates only a Vitamin K deficiency — supplement with oral Vitamin K only
E. Fibrinogen is elevated in liver disease due to acute phase reaction
mockBank.explanation: Cirrhotic coagulopathy is multifactorial: (1) Reduced clotting factor synthesis — the liver produces all factors except Factor VIII (made by endothelium); severe cirrhosis reduces Factors II (prothrombin), V, VII, IX, X, XI, fibrinogen → elevated PT/INR and APTT; (2) Thrombocytopaenia — portal hypertension causes splenomegaly → splenic sequestration of platelets + reduced thrombopoietin production by the damaged liver; (3) Hypofibrinogenaemia — fibrinogen synthesis reduced; (4) Dysfibrinogenaemia — abnormal fibrinogen produced. This is not warfarin (A) — the patient has no indication for anticoagulation. Bone marrow failure (C) occurs with severe chronic alcohol disease but is not the primary mechanism. Vitamin K supplementation (D) helps if deficiency contributes but does not reverse all coagulopathy in cirrhosis. Fibrinogen is LOW in liver disease, not elevated (E).

mockBank.question: Vancomycin resistance in Enterococcus (VRE) is most commonly mediated by which ONE of the following mechanisms?
options: A. Production of vancomycin-inactivating enzymes (vancomycinase) that hydrolyse the drug
B. Efflux pumps that actively expel vancomycin from the bacterial cell
C. Alteration of the peptidoglycan precursor terminal D-Ala-D-Ala to D-Ala-D-Lac, reducing vancomycin binding affinity 1000-fold
D. Thickening of the cell wall to prevent vancomycin from penetrating to its target
E. Mutation in the vancomycin binding protein on the outer membrane
mockBank.explanation: Vancomycin binds the D-Ala-D-Ala terminus of peptidoglycan precursors (lipid II) to block cell wall synthesis. VRE (vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus) encodes van gene clusters (vanA, vanB) that reprogram peptidoglycan synthesis to use D-Ala-D-Lac (depsipeptide) instead of D-Ala-D-Ala. This single change reduces vancomycin binding affinity by approximately 1000-fold, conferring high-level resistance. This resistance can transfer via conjugative plasmids to other organisms including S. aureus (→ VRSA). Cell wall thickening (D) is the mechanism of intermediate vancomycin resistance in S. aureus (VISA — vancomycin-intermediate S. aureus), not VRE. There is no vancomycin-inactivating enzyme (A). Efflux (B) is the mechanism for macrolide and tetracycline resistance. There is no outer membrane binding protein target (E).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly describes the role of TNF-α (tumour necrosis factor-alpha) in infection?
options: A. TNF-α is exclusively produced by T lymphocytes and drives B cell antibody class switching
B. TNF-α is a pro-inflammatory cytokine produced by macrophages that promotes neutrophil recruitment, fever, and acute phase response, but causes septic shock when produced in excess
C. TNF-α directly kills bacteria by activating complement via the lectin pathway
D. TNF-α suppresses inflammation to prevent tissue damage during infection
E. TNF-α is the primary cytokine responsible for antibody production in the germinal centre
mockBank.explanation: TNF-α is a pleiotropic pro-inflammatory cytokine, primarily produced by macrophages, dendritic cells, and T cells in response to LPS and other PAMPs. Its local actions: endothelial activation (upregulates adhesion molecules → neutrophil extravasation), fever induction (acts as an endogenous pyrogen via prostaglandin E2), stimulation of acute phase protein synthesis, and direct cytotoxicity against tumour cells. Excess systemic TNF-α (e.g., Gram-negative septicaemia) causes septic shock: vasodilation → hypotension → multi-organ failure. This is why anti-TNF-α biologics (adalimumab, infliximab, etanercept) used for rheumatoid arthritis and Crohn's disease significantly increase infection risk — patients on these drugs must be assessed carefully before dental procedures. TNF-α is not exclusively from T cells (A). It does not activate complement directly (C). It promotes, not suppresses, inflammation (D). Germinal centre reactions are driven by IL-4, IL-21, and CD40L (E).

mockBank.question: Zidovudine (AZT) was the first antiretroviral drug used to treat HIV. Which ONE of the following BEST describes its mechanism of action?
options: A. Zidovudine inhibits HIV protease, preventing cleavage of viral polyproteins into functional components
B. Zidovudine is a nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI) — a thymidine analogue that is incorporated into viral DNA by reverse transcriptase, acting as a chain terminator
C. Zidovudine blocks HIV integrase, preventing integration of viral DNA into the host chromosome
D. Zidovudine binds to the CD4 receptor, blocking HIV gp120 attachment
E. Zidovudine is a non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NNRTI) that binds directly to the reverse transcriptase enzyme
mockBank.explanation: Zidovudine (ZDV, AZT) is a nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI). It is a thymidine analogue that, after intracellular phosphorylation to AZT-triphosphate, is incorporated into the growing viral DNA chain by reverse transcriptase in preference to natural thymidine. However, AZT-triphosphate lacks the 3'-OH group needed to attach the next nucleotide → chain termination → no complete viral DNA → no integration → no new virus. NRTIs as a class all act as chain terminators (false substrates). Protease inhibitors (A) prevent maturation of new virions by blocking polyprotein cleavage. Integrase inhibitors (C) (raltegravir, dolutegravir) block chromosomal integration. CCR5 antagonists (D) (maraviroc) block co-receptor binding. NNRTIs (E) (efavirenz, nevirapine) bind allosterically to reverse transcriptase — distinct from NRTIs.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the role of Streptococcus mutans in dental caries?
options: A. S. mutans is a primary early coloniser of clean tooth surfaces that establishes the biofilm for later cariogenic bacteria
B. S. mutans produces glucosyltransferases that synthesise insoluble glucan polymers facilitating biofilm adhesion, and ferments dietary sugars to lactic acid causing enamel demineralisation
C. S. mutans is exclusively responsible for root caries in elderly patients — it plays no role in coronal caries
D. S. mutans is effective only in alkaline pH environments — acid environments inhibit its growth
E. S. mutans is a Gram-negative anaerobe that requires oxygen-free environments in deep dentine for caries progression
mockBank.explanation: Streptococcus mutans is the primary cariogenic bacterium. Its virulence factors for dental caries: (1) Glucosyltransferases (GTFs) synthesise insoluble glucan from sucrose → mediates irreversible binding to tooth surfaces and forms the scaffold of dental plaque biofilm; (2) Acidogenicity — ferments dietary carbohydrates (especially sucrose) to lactic acid, lowering plaque pH to <5.5 → enamel demineralisation; (3) Aciduricity — survives and replicates in acidic environments (pH 4.5) that inhibit most competitors; (4) Can also produce fructan polymers that serve as a carbohydrate reserve. S. mutans is not the early coloniser (A) — pioneers are S. oralis and S. sanguinis. It causes both coronal and root caries (C). It thrives in acidic conditions (D is wrong). S. mutans is Gram-positive, not Gram-negative (E).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the current treatment landscape for Hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection?
options: A. HCV treatment still requires 48 weeks of interferon-alpha plus ribavirin — cure rates are approximately 50%
B. Direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) targeting HCV NS3/NS4A, NS5A, and NS5B achieve cure rates >95% in 8–12 weeks, with minimal side effects
C. HCV has no effective treatment — only supportive care and liver transplantation for end-stage disease
D. HCV treatment requires lifelong HAART similar to HIV management
E. DAAs cure HCV but do not prevent re-infection — treated patients have lifelong immunity
mockBank.explanation: The development of direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) has revolutionised HCV treatment. Current DAA regimens (e.g., sofosbuvir/velpatasvir; glecaprevir/pibrentasvir) are: (1) Highly effective — >95% sustained virological response (SVR, i.e., cure) across all genotypes; (2) Short — 8–12 weeks; (3) Oral, well-tolerated with minimal side effects; (4) Pangenotypic — work across all 6 HCV genotypes. This compares to the old interferon + ribavirin regimen (A) which had 50% cure rates, required 48 weeks, and caused significant side effects (fatigue, depression, haematological toxicity). HCV IS curable (C). Treatment is finite, not lifelong (D). DAAs cure HCV but do NOT confer immunity — re-infection is possible after cure (E is wrong — treated patients have NO immunity).

mockBank.question: A patient reports 'penicillin allergy' — hives after taking penicillin 20 years ago. Before prescribing amoxicillin for a dental infection, which ONE of the following represents BEST practice?
options: A. Never prescribe any beta-lactam antibiotic — use clindamycin for all dental infections

B. Take a detailed allergy history: what happened, how long after taking the drug, when, and whether the patient has since tolerated penicillin — many reported penicillin allergies are not true allergies
C. Refer immediately to allergy clinic for formal desensitisation before prescribing any antibiotic
D. Prescribe co-amoxiclav as it contains clavulanic acid which reduces allergic reactions to the amoxicillin component
E. Test for penicillin allergy by prescribing a 100mg test dose and observing for 30 minutes
mockBank.explanation: Up to 10% of patients report penicillin allergy, but studies show approximately 90% can safely receive penicillin when formally evaluated. Common reasons for over-reporting: (1) The reaction was a viral rash coinciding with antibiotic use (especially in EBV mononucleosis — amoxicillin causes characteristic rash); (2) Side effects misclassified as allergy (e.g., GI upset); (3) The reaction was mild and 20 years ago (IgE-mediated sensitivity wanes over time in many patients). Best practice: elicit a detailed allergy history (nature of reaction, timing, treatment required, subsequent penicillin use). If the history suggests low risk (e.g., rash >1 hour after dose, no systemic features), the risk-benefit of prescribing may favour amoxicillin — or use clindamycin. Blanket avoidance (A) is inappropriate and contributes to clindamycin overuse and C. difficile. Immediate specialist referral (C) is not required for routine dental infections. Co-amoxiclav (D) still contains amoxicillin. Informal test dosing (E) must never be done outside an allergy clinic with resuscitation facilities.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes a live attenuated vaccine and its key advantage over inactivated vaccines?
options: A. A live attenuated vaccine contains killed pathogens that stimulate a stronger immune response than natural infection

B. A live attenuated vaccine contains weakened but replicating pathogen that closely mimics natural infection, typically generating stronger and more durable cellular and humoral immunity with fewer doses
C. A live attenuated vaccine is safer than inactivated vaccines in all patient groups including immunocompromised patients
D. A live attenuated vaccine works by stimulating innate immunity only — no adaptive immune response is generated
E. A live attenuated vaccine contains pathogen DNA that integrates into host chromosomes to generate long-term memory
mockBank.explanation: Live attenuated vaccines (MMR, varicella, yellow fever, BCG, oral polio) contain living but weakened (attenuated) pathogens that replicate in the host. Because they replicate, they more closely mimic natural infection, eliciting strong T cell (CD4+ and CD8+) and B cell responses with long-lasting immunological memory, often after just 1–2 doses. Key disadvantage: CONTRAINDICATED in immunocompromised patients (risk of disease from the vaccine strain), pregnant women, and those receiving immunosuppressants. Inactivated vaccines (influenza, HBV, polio IPV) contain killed/subunit antigens — safer for immunocompromised but generally require adjuvants and booster doses. Killed pathogens (A) describe inactivated vaccines. Live vaccines ARE contraindicated in immunocompromised patients (C). All vaccines trigger adaptive immunity (D). mRNA vaccines encode antigen but do not integrate into chromosomes (E).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly identifies a virus and the cancer it is associated with?
options: A. HBV — Kaposi's sarcoma
B. EBV — Burkitt's lymphoma and nasopharyngeal carcinoma
C. HCV — cervical carcinoma
D. HPV types 16 and 18 — hepatocellular carcinoma
E. HTLV-1 — chronic lymphocytic leukaemia (CLL)
mockBank.explanation: EBV is associated with multiple malignancies: Burkitt's lymphoma (endemic form in African children, c-myc/IgH translocation), nasopharyngeal carcinoma (particularly in Southeast Asian populations), Hodgkin's lymphoma (Reed-Sternberg cells), primary CNS lymphoma (in AIDS), and post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder. HBV (A) is associated with hepatocellular carcinoma — KS is caused by HHV-8. HCV (C) is associated with hepatocellular carcinoma and B cell non-Hodgkin's lymphoma — not cervical cancer. HPV 16/18 (D) cause cervical carcinoma, oropharyngeal SCC, anal carcinoma — not hepatocellular carcinoma. HTLV-1 (E) causes adult T cell leukaemia/lymphoma (ATLL) — not CLL (which has no known viral aetiology).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following statements about Bell's palsy and herpesvirus reactivation is MOST accurate?
options: A. Bell's palsy is caused exclusively by HSV-2 reactivation in the geniculate ganglion
B. VZV reactivation in the geniculate ganglion causes Ramsay Hunt syndrome, and HSV-1 reactivation is implicated in Bell's palsy (idiopathic facial nerve palsy)
C. Both Bell's palsy and Ramsay Hunt syndrome are caused by the same herpesvirus — VZV
D. Bell's palsy is a Lyme disease manifestation and is not virally mediated
E. HSV-1 causes bilateral facial palsy in 80% of cases
mockBank.explanation: Ramsay Hunt syndrome (herpes zoster oticus) is caused by VZV reactivation in the geniculate ganglion of the facial nerve (CN VII). Classic features: ipsilateral facial nerve palsy + vesicular rash in the external auditory meatus and/or hard palate + possible hearing loss and vertigo. Bell's palsy (idiopathic facial nerve palsy) is believed to be caused by HSV-1 reactivation in the facial nerve, causing oedematous nerve compression within the bony facial canal. Antiviral treatment (prednisolone ± aciclovir) improves outcomes if started within 72 hours. Bell's palsy is not exclusively HSV-2 (A). Bell's palsy and Ramsay Hunt are caused by DIFFERENT herpesviruses — HSV-1 and VZV respectively (C). Lyme disease (D) can cause facial palsy but is not the primary aetiology. Bell's palsy is almost always unilateral (E).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following clinical features is MOST characteristic of the acute HIV seroconversion illness?
options: A. Painless lymphadenopathy confined to the cervical region, with no systemic symptoms
B. A glandular fever-like illness with fever, sore throat, widespread lymphadenopathy, rash, and myalgia occurring 2–4 weeks after initial infection
C. Opportunistic infections such as PCP and CMV retinitis, occurring within 1 month of infection
D. Gradual weight loss and night sweats developing over several years
E. Thrush and oral hairy leukoplakia as the presenting features of primary HIV infection
mockBank.explanation: Acute HIV retroviral syndrome (primary HIV infection, seroconversion illness) occurs 2–4 weeks after initial infection, during the period of peak viral replication. Presentation mimics infectious mononucleosis: fever (90%), sore throat, widespread lymphadenopathy, maculopapular rash (70%, often truncal), myalgia, arthralgia, and headache. It is self-limiting (1–2 weeks). During this phase: viral load is very high (infectious peak), CD4 count transiently drops, and HBsAg-equivalent is p24 antigen (detectable before antibodies). Diagnosis: 4th generation HIV test (detects p24 + antibodies). Opportunistic infections (C) occur only in AIDS (CD4 <200) — not in acute infection. Weight loss and night sweats (D) characterise symptomatic chronic HIV. Thrush/OHL (E) occur in symptomatic HIV with declining CD4, not at primary infection.

mockBank.question: A dental nurse who is not vaccinated against HAV has been closely exposed to a patient who subsequently tests positive for Hepatitis A. The exposure occurred 48 hours ago. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate management?
options: A. Administer the first dose of HAV vaccination only — immunoglobulin is no longer available in the UK
B. Administer normal human immunoglobulin (HNIG) within 14 days of exposure AND commence HAV vaccination
C. No action required — HAV is mild and self-limiting in immunocompetent adults
D. Prescribe ribavirin for 14 days as post-exposure prophylaxis for HAV
E. Test the nurse's HAV IgG first; if positive, no action needed; if negative, give HNIG only (no vaccine)
mockBank.explanation: For non-immune individuals exposed to HAV within the previous 14 days, post-exposure prophylaxis consists of: (1) Normal human immunoglobulin (HNIG) — provides passive immediate protection (pre-formed antibodies); (2) First dose of HAV vaccine — begins active immunisation. The combination provides both immediate (HNIG) and long-term (vaccine) protection. HNIG is most effective if given within 7 days. UK guidance (UKHSA/PHE): contacts of confirmed HAV cases should receive PEP within 2 weeks of exposure. HAV in adults can cause prolonged morbidity (jaundice, fatigue for weeks) — it is not trivial (C). Ribavirin (D) has no role in HAV post-exposure prophylaxis. Testing IgG first (E) is appropriate but if negative, both HNIG and vaccine should be given (not HNIG alone).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following teeth is MOST likely to cause a submandibular space infection when the infection spreads beyond the apex?
options: A. Upper second premolar — apices lie above the buccal fat pad
B. Lower second and third molars — roots extend below the mylohyoid muscle insertion
C. Upper first molar — palatal root may perforate the palate causing palatal abscess
D. Lower canine — apex lies above the attachment of the mentalis muscle
E. Upper central incisor — apex may perforate nasal floor causing nasal infection
mockBank.explanation: The mylohyoid muscle forms the floor of the mouth and divides the sublingual space (above) from the submandibular space (below). The key anatomical principle: tooth apices that lie BELOW the mylohyoid attachment drain into the submandibular space. The lower second and third molar roots typically extend below the mylohyoid ridge — apical infection spreads inferiorly into the submandibular space. The lower first molar apex is often at or slightly above the attachment (can go either way). Lower premolars and anteriors have apices above the mylohyoid → drain into the sublingual or buccal spaces. Upper second premolar (A) drains buccally or into the maxillary sinus region. Upper first molar palatal root (C) perforates the palate → palatal abscess. Lower canine (D) apex is above the mentalis → vestibular/buccal abscess. Upper central incisor (E) can occasionally drain into nasal floor in very long-rooted cases.

mockBank.question: For a patient with no drug allergies requiring antibiotic treatment for a spreading odontogenic infection with systemic features, which ONE of the following represents the CORRECT first-line regimen?
options: A. Amoxicillin 250mg three times daily for 3 days
B. Amoxicillin 500mg three times daily for 5 days (with metronidazole 400mg three times daily for 5 days if anaerobic cover required)
C. Co-amoxiclav 375mg twice daily for 7 days
D. Doxycycline 100mg once daily for 7 days as first-line
E. Erythromycin 250mg four times daily for 5 days
mockBank.explanation: SDCEP/FGDP antibiotic prescribing guidelines for adults with spreading odontogenic infections: first-line is amoxicillin 500mg three times daily for 5 days. If anaerobic cover is needed (swelling with anaerobic features — foul odour, fluctuant, severe), add metronidazole 400mg three times daily for 5 days. Together they cover the mixed aerobic/anaerobic polymicrobial flora of dental infections. Amoxicillin 250mg (A) is underdosed for a spreading infection — 500mg is the appropriate therapeutic dose. Co-amoxiclav (C) is second-line (if first-line fails) — also the dose is incorrect (should be 625mg TDS). Doxycycline (D) has high resistance rates in oral streptococci and poor anaerobic cover — not first-line. Erythromycin (E) has high resistance rates in the UK — not recommended as first-line for dental infections.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the association between Human Papillomavirus (HPV) and oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma?
options: A. HPV causes oropharyngeal SCC through direct carcinogenic effect of its surface glycoproteins
B. HPV types 16 and 18 encode E6 and E7 oncoproteins that degrade p53 and Rb tumour suppressor proteins respectively, driving cell cycle dysregulation and carcinogenesis
C. HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC has a worse prognosis than HPV-negative tumours
D. HPV causes oropharyngeal SCC only in immunocompromised patients
E. The HPV vaccine is not effective against oropharyngeal carcinoma — it only protects against cervical cancer
mockBank.explanation: HPV types 16 and 18 are the high-risk oncogenic strains responsible for HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC (particularly base of tongue and tonsil — the oropharyngeal squamocolumnar junction). The oncogenic mechanism: E6 protein binds and degrades p53 tumour suppressor → failure to arrest cell cycle at G1/S checkpoint after DNA damage; E7 protein inactivates Rb (retinoblastoma protein) → releases E2F transcription factors → uncontrolled cell proliferation. HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC has a BETTER prognosis (C) than HPV-negative (alcohol/tobacco-related) tumours — better response to chemoradiotherapy, younger patients. HPV oropharyngeal SCC affects immunocompetent adults (D) — it is not an immunocompromised-exclusive condition. The HPV vaccine (Gardasil 9) DOES provide protection against HPV 16/18 in multiple anatomical sites (E is wrong).

mockBank.question: A dental nurse develops a painful cluster of vesicles on the pulp of her index finger 1 week after treating a patient with active herpes labialis. She has no history of cold sores herself. Which ONE of the following is the MOST likely diagnosis and appropriate management?
options: A. Paronychia from Staphylococcus aureus — treat with flucloxacillin 500mg QDS for 5 days
B. Herpetic whitlow (primary HSV-1 infection of the finger) — aciclovir 200mg five times daily for 5 days and wound care
C. Allergic contact dermatitis from latex gloves — patch testing and glove change
D. Felon (deep pulp space infection) — immediate incision and drainage
E. Dyshidrotic eczema — topical corticosteroid cream
mockBank.explanation: Herpetic whitlow is primary HSV-1 (or HSV-2) infection of the finger, typically acquired through direct contact with infected oral secretions. It is a recognised occupational hazard for healthcare workers without adequate protection. Clinical features: painful vesicular eruption on the finger pulp or periungual area, associated with intense burning/throbbing pain, fever, and local lymphadenopathy — mimics bacterial infection. Management: aciclovir 200mg five times daily for 5 days; wound care; do NOT incise and drain (this can cause viral spread and secondary bacterial superinfection — often performed in error). Prevention: proper glove use for all intraoral procedures. Staphylococcal paronychia (A) presents with purulent infection at the nail fold — no vesicles. Latex allergy (C) presents with urticaria/eczema, not vesicles. A felon (D) is a deep bacterial pulp space infection requiring drainage — no vesicles. Dyshidrotic eczema (E) causes bilateral palmar/plantar vesicles, not unilateral finger involvement after occupational exposure.

mockBank.question: For a patient with a confirmed penicillin allergy requiring an antibiotic for a mild odontogenic infection, which macrolide should be AVOIDED as first choice if the patient is also taking clarithromycin-sensitive medications such as simvastatin?
options: A. Azithromycin — it is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor and elevates simvastatin levels
B. Clarithromycin — it is the most potent CYP3A4 inhibitor among macrolides and can cause rhabdomyolysis when combined with statins
C. Erythromycin — it has no significant CYP interactions and is the safest macrolide choice
D. All macrolides are equally potent CYP3A4 inhibitors — there is no preferred option
E. Clindamycin — it is a macrolide that is safe with simvastatin
mockBank.explanation: Clarithromycin is the MOST potent CYP3A4 inhibitor among macrolides and must be avoided with simvastatin (and other statins metabolised by CYP3A4 — lovastatin, atorvastatin). The interaction can cause profound statin accumulation → myopathy and rhabdomyolysis (muscle breakdown releasing myoglobin → renal failure). Clinical approach for penicillin-allergic patients on simvastatin: choose azithromycin (minimal CYP3A4 inhibition) or clindamycin (not a CYP3A4 inhibitor). Azithromycin (A) has minimal CYP3A4 inhibition — it is a CYP3A4 SUBSTRATE (not inhibitor) — it is the safer macrolide choice. Erythromycin (C) IS a moderate CYP3A4 inhibitor — not the safest option. All macrolides are NOT equally potent (D). Clindamycin (E) is not a macrolide — it is a lincosamide — but it is correct that it does not significantly inhibit CYP3A4.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the process of phagocytosis and phagolysosomal killing?
options: A. Phagocytosis requires complement activation first — neutrophils cannot ingest bacteria without opsonisation

B. Neutrophils and macrophages engulf pathogens into phagosomes, which fuse with lysosomes to form phagolysosomes containing bactericidal enzymes (elastase, MPO), reactive oxygen species, and defensins
C. Phagocytes kill bacteria exclusively through the respiratory burst — no enzymatic killing occurs
D. Macrophages phagocytose bacteria and present antigens via MHC Class I to CD8+ T cells
E. Phagocytosis eliminates all pathogens — pathogens cannot survive inside phagolysosomes
mockBank.explanation: Phagocytosis: pathogens are engulfed by neutrophils or macrophages via receptor-mediated ingestion (Fc receptors bind IgG-opsonised bacteria; complement receptors bind C3b-opsonised bacteria). The pathogen is enclosed in a phagosome, which fuses with lysosomes containing: hydrolytic enzymes (elastase, cathepsins, defensins), myeloperoxidase (MPO — generates hypochlorous acid), and toxic reactive oxygen species (superoxide, hydrogen peroxide) from the respiratory burst (NADPH oxidase activation). Phagocytosis can occur without opsonisation via PRRs, though it is less efficient (A). Enzymatic killing is critical alongside the respiratory burst — chronic granulomatous disease shows the result of deficient ROS alone (C). Macrophages present endogenous antigens via MHC Class I and exogenous via MHC Class II (D is half-wrong). Some pathogens (Mycobacterium, Legionella) survive inside phagolysosomes by inhibiting phagolysosomal fusion (E is wrong).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the oral manifestations and dental considerations in a patient with active pulmonary tuberculosis?
options: A. Active pulmonary TB has no oral manifestations — dental treatment can proceed normally

B. Oral TB ulcers are common presenting features; active TB is a notifiable disease; dental treatment should be deferred until the patient is non-infectious (usually after 2 weeks of effective chemotherapy) and treated in negative pressure rooms
C. Oral TB causes white plaques on the tongue similar to oral candidiasis
D. TB treatment with rifampicin accelerates the metabolism of no dental medications
E. TB is no longer a public health concern in the UK — routine dental treatment is not affected
mockBank.explanation: Oral TB: primary oral TB ulcers are rare but can occur (typically on the tongue or gingivae — chronic painful irregular ulcers). Secondary oral TB (haematogenous spread) also causes ulceration. More commonly, dentists may unknowingly treat a patient with undiagnosed active pulmonary TB — which can be transmitted by aerosol during dental procedures. Key dental considerations: (1) Active pulmonary TB is a notifiable disease (report to Health Protection Team); (2) Patients are infectious until 2 weeks of effective chemotherapy (rifampicin + isoniazid + pyrazinamide + ethambutol — RIPE); (3) Elective dental treatment should be deferred until non-infectious; (4) If treatment is urgent, use appropriate infection control (negative pressure room, N95 respirator). Rifampicin (D) IS a potent CYP inducer — it significantly accelerates metabolism of many drugs including corticosteroids, anticoagulants, and oral contraceptives. TB remains significant in the UK (E) — particularly in certain urban populations.

mockBank.question: A dental student performs a procedure on an HBsAg-positive patient without wearing gloves. The student has completed the primary HBV vaccination course and anti-HBs is 280 mIU/mL. What is the MOST appropriate action?
options: A. Immediate HBIG injection and re-vaccination
B. No specific post-exposure action required beyond completing an incident report — the student is adequately vaccinated
C. Immediate HIV PEP as blood-borne virus exposure occurred
D. Blood test for HBsAg in 6 weeks to detect infection
E. Quarantine the student from clinical duties for 6 months
mockBank.explanation: An anti-HBs of 280 mIU/mL is well above the 100 mIU/mL threshold for full protection (some guidelines use >10 mIU/mL as the minimum). A fully vaccinated responder with adequate anti-HBs is protected against HBV infection — no HBIG and no additional vaccination doses are required after exposure. Actions: complete an incident report (documenting the exposure, nature of contact, source patient HBV status, and student's vaccination/immunity status), counsel the student, reinforce PPE importance. HBIG (A) is for non-immune or non-responder individuals. HIV PEP (C) is only indicated for HIV exposure risk — not for HBV in a vaccinated, immune individual. HBsAg testing (D) is not indicated for immune individuals. Quarantine (E) is completely inappropriate.

mockBank.question: Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) carries the mecA gene. Which ONE of the following BEST describes how mecA confers resistance to all beta-lactam antibiotics?
options: A. mecA encodes a beta-lactamase enzyme that destroys the beta-lactam ring

B. mecA encodes PBP2a (penicillin-binding protein 2a), an alternative transpeptidase with very low affinity for all beta-lactams, allowing cell wall synthesis to continue in the presence of beta-lactam antibiotics
C. mecA encodes an efflux pump that actively removes beta-lactam antibiotics from the bacterial cell
D. mecA encodes a modified outer membrane protein that prevents beta-lactam entry into the periplasmic space
E. mecA encodes a regulatory protein that downregulates expression of all PBPs, reducing beta-lactam binding sites
mockBank.explanation: mecA (located on the mobile SCCmec element) encodes PBP2a (also called PBP2'), an alternative penicillin-binding protein that functions as a transpeptidase for cell wall synthesis but has extremely low affinity for all beta-lactam antibiotics (penicillins, cephalosporins, carbapenems). Because PBP2a has such low beta-lactam affinity, MRSA can continue cross-linking peptidoglycan even in high concentrations of beta-lactam antibiotics, conferring broad resistance. This resistance mechanism is distinct from beta-lactamase production (A), which destroys the drug but is overcome by beta-lactamase inhibitors (clavulanic acid) — mecA resistance is NOT overcome by these inhibitors. Efflux (C) is not the MRSA mechanism. Gram-positives don't have outer membranes (D). PBP downregulation (E) is not the mecA mechanism.

mockBank.question: A 45-year-old patient presents to hospital with acute confusion, fever, and temporal lobe seizures. Brain MRI shows haemorrhagic necrosis of the temporal lobes. HSV encephalitis is suspected. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate immediate treatment?
options: A. Oral aciclovir 800mg five times daily — the same dose as for herpes zoster
B. IV aciclovir 10mg/kg every 8 hours for 14–21 days
C. IV ganciclovir — the treatment of choice for all herpesvirus CNS infections
D. Lumbar puncture first — treatment should be withheld until CSF culture confirms HSV
E. Oral valaciclovir 1g three times daily — equivalent to IV aciclovir for CNS infections
mockBank.explanation: HSV encephalitis is a neurosurgical and infectious disease emergency with mortality of 70% if untreated and 20–30% with treatment. IV aciclovir 10mg/kg every 8 hours (total 30mg/kg/day) for 14–21 days is the standard treatment — MUST be started empirically as soon as HSV encephalitis is clinically suspected (do NOT wait for culture results). CSF PCR for HSV DNA (via lumbar puncture) confirms diagnosis but treatment should begin immediately. Oral aciclovir (A) achieves insufficient CNS concentrations. Ganciclovir (C) is used for CMV CNS disease. Withholding treatment pending culture (D) would be catastrophic given the mortality of untreated disease. Oral valaciclovir (E) achieves higher bioavailability than oral aciclovir but still cannot reliably reach therapeutic CNS concentrations for encephalitis.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following is the MOST common primary immunodeficiency in humans and its characteristic dental/oral manifestation?
options: A. DiGeorge syndrome (T cell deficiency) — recurrent severe oral fungal infections
B. Selective IgA deficiency — recurrent sinopulmonary infections; may also have increased susceptibility to oral infections, and anti-IgA antibodies can cause anaphylaxis to blood products
C. Severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID) — the most common immunodeficiency, presenting at birth
D. Chronic granulomatous disease — recurrent staphylococcal infections due to absent oxidative burst
E. X-linked agammaglobulinaemia — recurrent viral infections due to absent B cells
mockBank.explanation: Selective IgA deficiency is the most common primary immunodeficiency, occurring in approximately 1 in 500 individuals (Europeans). Most are asymptomatic but susceptible to recurrent sinopulmonary infections, gastrointestinal infections, and some autoimmune conditions. Crucially: patients with selective IgA deficiency can develop anti-IgA antibodies — if given blood products containing IgA (most blood products), anaphylactic reactions can occur. Oral manifestations: increased susceptibility to oral infections and periodontal disease (sIgA is the major mucosal defence). DiGeorge syndrome (A) presents with T cell deficiency, recurrent viral/fungal infections, and cardiac defects — not the most common immunodeficiency. SCID (C) is severe but rare. Chronic granulomatous disease (D) is an innate immune defect but not the most common overall immunodeficiency. XLA (E) causes recurrent bacterial infections (not viral — T cells intact).

mockBank.question: A 22-year-old patient presents with a painful, swollen, red operculum overlying a partially erupted lower right wisdom tooth. Temperature is 37.8°C, trismus grade II (mouth opening 20mm). Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate management?
options: A. Irrigate under the operculum, prescribe ibuprofen, and discharge without antibiotics
B. Immediate extraction of the wisdom tooth under local anaesthesia in the surgery
C. Irrigate under the operculum with chlorhexidine, prescribe amoxicillin 500mg TDS + metronidazole 400mg TDS for 5 days (due to systemic features and trismus), and arrange review in 2–3 days
D. Prescribe amoxicillin only and review at 1 week — trismus does not warrant dual antibiotic therapy
E. Refer immediately to A&E for IV antibiotics
mockBank.explanation: Pericoronitis (infection of the operculum around a partially erupted wisdom tooth) with systemic features (low-grade fever 37.8°C) and trismus (limited mouth opening due to inflammation of the muscles of mastication) warrants: (1) Local treatment — irrigation under the operculum with warm saline/chlorhexidine; (2) Antibiotic therapy — the combination of amoxicillin (streptococcal cover) + metronidazole (anaerobic cover) is appropriate for systemic features or trismus; (3) Review in 2–3 days; (4) Once acute episode resolved — assess for wisdom tooth extraction. Simple pericoronitis without systemic features (A) can be managed with local measures + analgesics only. Extraction during acute infection (B) risks spreading infection and is technically difficult with trismus. IV antibiotics/A&E (E) would be appropriate for severe cellulitis or airway threat — not grade II trismus at 37.8°C.

mockBank.question: Viruses cannot be grown on standard bacteriological culture media. Which ONE of the following is the MOST important reason for this limitation?
options: A. Viruses are destroyed by the temperature required for bacterial culture (37°C)
B. Viruses are obligate intracellular parasites that require living host cells for replication — they have no independent metabolic machinery
C. Viruses cannot replicate in artificial media because they require complex carbohydrate substrates not available in culture media
D. Viruses are too small to be grown in standard laboratory flasks
E. Viruses are sensitive to the antibiotics routinely added to bacterial culture media
mockBank.explanation: Viruses are obligate intracellular parasites — they possess no ribosomes, no metabolic enzymes (no Krebs cycle, no electron transport chain), and no capacity for independent protein synthesis or energy production. Every aspect of viral replication depends on the host cell's machinery. Therefore viruses can only replicate inside living, metabolically active host cells. Viral culture in the laboratory requires cell lines (e.g., MRC-5 human fibroblasts for CMV, Vero cells for HSV) or embryonated eggs. Viruses replicate at 37°C in cell culture (A is wrong). The size of viruses (D) is not the limiting factor — cell culture flasks can accommodate cells of all sizes. Some viruses are actually inactivated by certain antibiotics (E) but the main reason is the lack of independent metabolism (B). Carbohydrate substrates (C) are not the issue.

mockBank.question: Clindamycin is bacteriostatic against most organisms. Which ONE of the following BEST describes its mechanism of action?
options: A. Clindamycin binds to the 30S ribosomal subunit, causing misreading of mRNA codons
B. Clindamycin binds to the 50S ribosomal subunit, blocking the translocation step of protein synthesis
C. Clindamycin inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins
D. Clindamycin inhibits DNA gyrase, preventing DNA supercoiling relaxation
E. Clindamycin acts as a competitive inhibitor of dihydrofolate reductase, blocking folate synthesis
mockBank.explanation: Clindamycin is a lincosamide antibiotic that binds to the 23S rRNA of the 50S ribosomal subunit at the same site as macrolides (erythromycin, clarithromycin) and chloramphenicol. This blocks translocation — the movement of the ribosome along the mRNA from the A site to the P site — halting protein synthesis. Because the binding site overlaps with macrolides, macrolide-resistant strains (MLSB resistance) can also be resistant to clindamycin (inducible or constitutive). This cross-resistance is important clinically: macrolide-resistant Streptococcus pyogenes may also be clindamycin-resistant. Clindamycin is particularly active against anaerobes and Gram-positive cocci — making it ideal for dental infections in penicillin-allergic patients. 30S binding (A) = aminoglycosides, tetracyclines. PBP binding (C) = beta-lactams. DNA gyrase (D) = fluoroquinolones. DHFR (E) = trimethoprim.

mockBank.question: A patient with chronic HBV infection (HBsAg positive >6 months, HBeAg positive, ALT elevated, liver biopsy showing active hepatitis) is being considered for antiviral therapy. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate first-line treatment?
options: A. Interferon-alpha 3 MU subcutaneously three times per week for 6 months
B. Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate (TDF) or entecavir — oral nucleoside/nucleotide analogues with low resistance rates
C. Ribavirin 800mg daily for 48 weeks
D. Sofosbuvir-based direct acting antiviral therapy (as used for HCV)
E. Lamivudine monotherapy — the most established treatment with lowest resistance risk
mockBank.explanation: Current guidelines (EASL, NICE) recommend tenofovir disoproxil fumarate (TDF) or entecavir as first-line oral antiviral therapy for chronic HBV. Both are nucleoside/nucleotide analogues that inhibit HBV DNA polymerase (reverse transcriptase) and have: (1) High potency (rapid viral suppression); (2) Excellent safety profiles; (3) Very low resistance rates (unlike lamivudine which has >70% resistance after 5 years). Treatment goals: sustained viral suppression (HBV DNA undetectable), HBeAg seroconversion, normalisation of ALT, prevention of cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma. Interferon-alpha (A) is an alternative for selected patients (younger, high ALT, genotype A) but is less used now due to side effects. Ribavirin (C) has no role in HBV treatment. Sofosbuvir (D) targets HCV, not HBV polymerase. Lamivudine (E) is avoided first-line due to high resistance rates.

mockBank.question: A patient on HAART (ritonavir-boosted regimen) requires a prescription for midazolam-based IV sedation. Which ONE of the following is the MOST important consideration?
options: A. Ritonavir has no effect on benzodiazepine metabolism — sedation can proceed as normal
B. Ritonavir is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor — it dramatically increases midazolam plasma levels, risking profound and prolonged sedation or respiratory arrest
C. Ritonavir accelerates midazolam metabolism via CYP3A4 induction — a higher dose of midazolam will be needed
D. Ritonavir interacts with midazolam only when given intravenously — oral midazolam premedication is safe
E. The interaction is clinically insignificant — ritonavir only affects protease inhibitor metabolism
mockBank.explanation: Ritonavir (a protease inhibitor used as a pharmacokinetic booster in HAART — e.g., in lopinavir/ritonavir, darunavir/ritonavir) is one of the most potent CYP3A4 inhibitors in clinical use. Midazolam is metabolised almost entirely by CYP3A4. Co-administration causes massively elevated midazolam levels → excessive and prolonged sedation, respiratory depression, apnoea. This combination is potentially FATAL. Oral midazolam is actually MORE dangerous in this context because it undergoes extensive first-pass CYP3A4 metabolism — ritonavir's inhibition of first-pass increases bioavailability 10–20 fold. For patients on ritonavir requiring sedation: use a minimal dose of IV midazolam with extreme caution, or consider alternatives (e.g., diazepam, nitrous oxide) after specialist consultation. Ritonavir also inhibits metabolism of many other dental-relevant drugs: clarithromycin, alfentanil, pethidine.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the current Sepsis-3 definition of sepsis?
options: A. Sepsis is any bacteraemia (bacteria in the bloodstream) regardless of systemic inflammatory response
B. Sepsis is a life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection
C. Sepsis is defined by the presence of fever >38°C, tachycardia >100 bpm, tachypnoea >20 breaths/min, and white cell count >12,000 (SIRS criteria)
D. Sepsis is bacterial infection that has spread from a primary focus to the bloodstream
E. Sepsis is defined by blood culture positivity with clinical signs of infection
mockBank.explanation: The Sepsis-3 definition (2016): 'Sepsis is life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection.' It replaced the older SIRS (systemic inflammatory response syndrome) criteria because SIRS is too non-specific (many non-infectious conditions fulfil SIRS criteria) and too sensitive (misses some severe sepsis). Organ dysfunction is assessed by the Sequential Organ Failure Assessment (SOFA) score. Septic shock: sepsis + vasopressor requirement to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg + lactate >2mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation. Dental significance: dental infections can cause sepsis — Ludwig's angina, parapharyngeal spread, mediastinitis. Any patient with signs of systemic toxicity from a dental infection requires urgent hospital referral. Bacteraemia alone (A) is not sepsis. SIRS criteria (C) define the old sepsis-1/2 definition. Bloodstream spread (D) is bacteraemia/septicaemia. Positive blood cultures (E) are not required — many sepsis cases have negative cultures.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the role of Treponema denticola in periodontal disease?
options: A. T. denticola is a Gram-positive aerobic spirochaete that produces toxins causing gingival hyperplasia
B. T. denticola is a Gram-negative anaerobic spirochaete in the 'red complex' of periodontitis-associated bacteria, producing proteases that degrade collagen and evade immune responses
C. T. denticola causes acute necrotising ulcerative gingivitis (ANUG) exclusively without involvement of other bacteria
D. T. denticola is part of the early coloniser group of supragingival plaque formation
E. T. denticola is primarily a bloodstream pathogen that secondarily infects the periodontium from the blood
mockBank.explanation: Treponema denticola is a Gram-negative anaerobic spirochaete that, along with Porphyromonas gingivalis and Tannerella forsythia, forms the 'red complex' — the most strongly associated group of bacteria with chronic periodontitis. T. denticola virulence: (1) Chymotrypsin-like protease (CTLP) — degrades collagen, fibronectin, and complement factors; (2) Major outer sheath protein (MOSP) — impairs host cell function and immune response; (3) Trypsin-like protease — promotes bleeding and tissue destruction; (4) Motility in viscous gel — penetrates periodontal tissues. ANUG (C) involves T. denticola but also Fusobacterium and Prevotella species — it is not caused exclusively by T. denticola. T. denticola is a late coloniser of subgingival plaque (D), not an early coloniser. It is not primarily a bloodstream pathogen (E).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the mechanism of action of nystatin and its clinical application in dentistry?
options: A. Nystatin inhibits fungal cell wall synthesis by blocking glucan synthase
B. Nystatin is a polyene antifungal that binds ergosterol in the fungal cell membrane, creating pores that cause potassium and other ion leakage and cell death
C. Nystatin inhibits fungal DNA synthesis by acting as a nucleoside analogue
D. Nystatin inhibits lanosterol 14-alpha-demethylase (CYP51), depleting ergosterol from the fungal cell membrane
E. Nystatin is a bacteriostatic antifungal that inhibits fungal protein synthesis at the 50S ribosomal subunit
mockBank.explanation: Nystatin (named after New York State) is a polyene antifungal antibiotic that binds specifically to ergosterol (the sterol in fungal cell membranes, analogous to cholesterol in mammalian membranes). This binding creates membrane pores → leakage of intracellular potassium, magnesium, and small molecules → osmotic imbalance → fungal cell death. Clinical applications: pseudomembranous oral candidiasis (nystatin suspension 100,000 units/mL QDS after food), denture stomatitis (nystatin suspension applied to denture fitting surface), angular cheilitis (nystatin ointment). Key advantage: minimal systemic absorption → safe in pregnancy, liver disease, and with warfarin (no drug interactions). Glucan synthase inhibition (A) = echinocandins (caspofungin). Nucleoside analogue (C) = flucytosine. CYP51 inhibition (D) = azoles (fluconazole, miconazole, ketoconazole). Polyenes are fungicidal, not bacteriostatic (E), and act on the cell membrane, not ribosomes.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST explains the mechanism of fever during infection?
options: A. Bacterial toxins directly stimulate the hypothalamic thermostat to increase body temperature
B. Pyrogens (e.g., IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α, prostaglandin E2) released by macrophages act on the hypothalamic thermoregulatory centre to elevate the set-point temperature
C. Fever results from increased metabolic activity of immune cells generating excess heat
D. Fever is caused by bacterial LPS directly damaging the hypothalamic blood-brain barrier
E. Fever is solely mediated by IL-12 produced by dendritic cells during antigen presentation
mockBank.explanation: Fever is initiated by endogenous pyrogens. Macrophages and other innate immune cells activated by PAMPs (LPS, flagellin, viral RNA) release pro-inflammatory cytokines: IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α. These act on the hypothalamus to induce cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) expression → prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) production → PGE2 acts on the hypothalamic thermoregulatory centre (preoptic area) → raises the temperature set-point → peripheral vasoconstriction (reduces heat loss) + shivering (generates heat). The new set-point temperature is fever. This mechanism explains why antipyretics (aspirin, ibuprofen, paracetamol) reduce fever by inhibiting COX enzymes and prostaglandin synthesis. LPS (A) is an exogenous pyrogen that triggers endogenous pyrogen release — it does not directly act on the hypothalamus. Metabolic heat (C) is a minor contributor. LPS does not directly damage the BBB (D). IL-12 (E) drives Th1 responses, not fever primarily.

mockBank.question: A pregnant patient at 12 weeks gestation reports contact with a child with chickenpox. She has no history of chickenpox and anti-VZV IgG is negative. Which ONE of the following describes the risk to the fetus and appropriate management?
options: A. No fetal risk — chickenpox does not cross the placenta in the first trimester
B. High fetal risk — congenital varicella syndrome (CVS) can cause skin scarring, limb hypoplasia, neurological damage, and eye abnormalities; VZIG should be given within 96 hours
C. Fetal risk is highest in the third trimester — VZIG is only indicated after 28 weeks
D. The live attenuated varicella vaccine should be given immediately to protect the fetus
E. Oral aciclovir is safe in pregnancy and should be prescribed as prophylaxis
mockBank.explanation: Primary VZV infection during pregnancy carries risks at different stages: (1) First and second trimester (especially weeks 8–20): congenital varicella syndrome (CVS) — occurs in ~2% of cases — features: skin scarring in dermatomal distribution, limb hypoplasia (limb underdevelopment), CNS abnormalities (cortical atrophy, microcephaly), ocular defects (chorioretinitis, cataracts), IUGR. (2) Around delivery (5 days before to 2 days after): neonatal varicella — severe, potentially fatal (30% mortality if untreated) as the neonate has no maternal IgG yet. Management for non-immune pregnant contact: VZIG within 96 hours of exposure — reduces severity but may not prevent infection. Live varicella vaccine (D) is ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy. Aciclovir (E) may be used in hospitalised cases with serious VZV disease but not as routine prophylaxis. Fetal risk IS highest in weeks 8–20 (C is wrong about third trimester being highest risk period).

mockBank.question: A dentist recommends chlorhexidine gluconate 0.2% mouthwash to a patient with periodontal disease. Which ONE of the following BEST describes its mechanism of action and main side effect?
options: A. Chlorhexidine inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis and has no significant side effects

B. Chlorhexidine is a cationic bisbiguanide that binds to negatively charged bacterial cell membranes, disrupting them and causing cytoplasmic leakage; main side effects are tooth staining and taste alteration
C. Chlorhexidine acts as a bacteriostatic antiseptic by chelating calcium ions essential for bacterial metabolism
D. Chlorhexidine is a bactericidal antibiotic with broad-spectrum activity including antiviral and antifungal effects
E. Chlorhexidine inhibits fungal glucan synthase — making it the treatment of choice for oral candidiasis
mockBank.explanation: Chlorhexidine gluconate is a cationic bisbiguanide antiseptic with excellent substantivity (adheres to oral tissues and is released slowly over hours). Mechanism: cationic charge attracts it to negatively charged bacterial cell membranes → disruption of membrane integrity → leakage of cytoplasmic contents → bacterial cell death. Active against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, some fungi (Candida) and enveloped viruses (HBV, HCV, HIV). Main side effects: brown tooth and tongue staining (aesthetic concern), bitter taste alteration, and occasional mucosal desquamation with prolonged use. Uses in dentistry: pre-op mouthrinse, periodontal disease adjunct, post-extraction care, surgical site preparation, infection control. Chlorhexidine does not inhibit cell wall synthesis (A). It is not a calcium chelator (C). It is classified as an antiseptic, not an antibiotic (D). It does not specifically inhibit glucan synthase (E) — echinocandins do.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following is the MOST important intervention to prevent mother-to-child transmission (MTCT) of HIV during pregnancy and delivery?
options: A. Elective Caesarean section is the single most effective intervention, regardless of maternal viral load
B. Effective HAART during pregnancy to achieve and maintain an undetectable viral load — which reduces MTCT risk from ~25% to <1%
C. Breastfeeding avoidance alone reduces MTCT to zero in all cases
D. Zidovudine prophylaxis for the neonate only — maternal therapy is not required
E. HIV vaccination for the neonate immediately after birth
mockBank.explanation: The cornerstone of preventing MTCT of HIV is achieving an undetectable viral load in the mother through effective HAART during pregnancy. With undetectable viral load: (1) Vaginal delivery is as safe as Caesarean section; (2) MTCT rate <1% (down from 25% untreated); (3) Breastfeeding may be considered in some settings (though bottle feeding is recommended in high-income countries). The MTCT transmission routes: antepartum (transplacental), intrapartum (most common — exposure during delivery), postpartum (breastfeeding). Elective C-section (A) is recommended if viral load >50 copies/mL at 36 weeks but is NOT the primary intervention — maternal viral load suppression is. Breastfeeding avoidance (C) alone does not reduce antenatal/intrapartum risk. Neonatal prophylaxis (D) is given for 4 weeks post-delivery but is adjunctive to maternal therapy. There is no HIV vaccine (E).

mockBank.question: A new rapid antigen test for HBsAg has sensitivity 85% and specificity 98% when used in a high-prevalence population. Which ONE of the following BEST interprets a POSITIVE result from this test?
options: A. The patient definitely has chronic HBV infection — sensitivity 85% is sufficient for confirmation
B. A positive result in a high-prevalence population is likely a true positive but should be confirmed with a laboratory ELISA test
C. Specificity of 98% means 2% of ALL patients will test false positive — confirmatory testing is not needed
D. A positive result rules out HBV as sensitivity <90% makes the test unreliable
E. The test is only reliable for ruling in disease — it cannot be used to rule out HBV
mockBank.explanation: Understanding test characteristics is essential for interpreting clinical results. A positive test result in a high-prevalence population is likely a true positive (high positive predictive value, PPV), but given the 2% false positive rate (100% - 98% specificity), confirmatory testing is required before initiating treatment. PPV is higher when disease prevalence is high. In a low-prevalence population, the same test would have a much higher false positive rate (lower PPV). The 85% sensitivity (A) means 15% of truly infected patients will test negative (false negative) — inadequate for a standalone confirmatory test. A 2% false positive rate (C) is clinically significant for a serious diagnosis like HBV — confirmatory ELISA or NAAT is required. Low sensitivity means the test misses some true cases — a negative result cannot reliably rule OUT disease (E is correct about one limitation, but B is the best overall answer). Sensitivity determines rule-out power; specificity determines rule-in power.

mockBank.question: Fluconazole and miconazole are both azole antifungals but have very different indications and interaction profiles. Which ONE of the following CORRECTLY distinguishes them?
options: A. Fluconazole is topical only; miconazole is systemic — therefore miconazole is safer with warfarin

B. Fluconazole is a systemic oral/IV triazole with fewer CYP interactions; miconazole gel is significantly absorbed topically and is an extremely potent CYP2C9 inhibitor — absolutely contraindicated with warfarin
C. Both fluconazole and miconazole are equally contraindicated with warfarin — neither can be used safely
D. Fluconazole gel is safe for use with warfarin as it is topical — only systemic fluconazole interacts
E. Miconazole is safer than fluconazole in pregnancy as it is topical
mockBank.explanation: This is one of the highest-yield prescribing distinctions in dental pharmacology. Miconazole oral gel (24mg/g): applied topically in the mouth but is significantly absorbed through the oral mucosa (15–50% systemic absorption) — enough to potently inhibit CYP2C9 and CYP3A4. The CYP2C9 inhibition raises S-warfarin levels dramatically, with documented fatal haemorrhages. Therefore: miconazole gel is ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED in any patient on warfarin. Fluconazole (oral/IV, triazole): also inhibits CYP2C9 and raises warfarin levels, but the interaction is clinically manageable with dose adjustment and INR monitoring (not absolutely contraindicated, just requires monitoring). Nystatin is the safe topical antifungal for warfarin patients. Fluconazole is not topical (A). Both can interact with warfarin but not equally and not absolutely for fluconazole (C). Miconazole in pregnancy should actually be used cautiously (E) — topical use is generally accepted but systemic absorption is a concern.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST explains why influenza vaccines require annual reformulation?
options: A. Influenza virions are extremely fragile and vaccine antigens degrade within one year
B. Antigenic drift — gradual point mutations in influenza haemagglutinin (HA) and neuraminidase (NA) accumulate over time, changing the viral surface antigens so prior immunity is less protective
C. Influenza replicates so rapidly that the global viral population completely changes every 12 months
D. Antigenic shift — influenza regularly exchanges entire genome segments with avian influenza viruses
E. Annual reformulation is required because human immune memory for influenza wanes completely within one year
mockBank.explanation: Antigenic drift is the reason for annual influenza vaccine updates. Influenza RNA polymerase lacks proofreading → high mutation rate → point mutations accumulate in HA (haemagglutinin — mediates cell attachment) and NA (neuraminidase — mediates viral release from infected cells). New variants emerge with slightly altered HA/NA epitopes that are less well-recognised by antibodies from prior vaccination or natural infection → seasonal epidemic flu. WHO monitors circulating strains globally via the GISRS network and updates the vaccine formulation annually. Antigenic shift (D) — the exchange of entire gene segments between influenza A strains (e.g., human and avian strains) — causes PANDEMICS (1918, 1968, 2009), not seasonal epidemics requiring annual reformulation. Memory waning (E) occurs slowly over years, not annually. Vaccine stability (A) is not the primary reason. Complete viral population change (C) is an overstatement — drift occurs in existing strains.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following represents an important route of Hepatitis E transmission in the UK that differs from the classic faecal-oral route seen in developing countries?
options: A. Sexual transmission between men who have sex with men — similar to HBV
B. Blood transfusion — HEV RNA is detectable in a proportion of blood donations in endemic areas
C. Zoonotic transmission via consumption of undercooked pork or wild boar products — genotype 3 HEV is endemic in European pigs
D. Vertical transmission from HEV-infected mother to neonate during delivery
E. Nosocomial transmission in dialysis units — HEV spreads on contaminated equipment
mockBank.explanation: In the UK and Western Europe, HEV is predominantly caused by genotype 3 (G3) — a zoonotic strain endemic in domestic pigs, wild boar, and deer. Transmission occurs via consumption of undercooked pork products (pork sausages, liver sausage, offal) or game meat. This is distinct from genotype 1/2 HEV in developing countries which is waterborne/faecal-oral. UK HEV cases have tripled in recent years — many are autochthonous (domestically acquired) without travel history. HEV blood transfusion risk (B) is a recognised concern — blood services in some countries screen for HEV RNA. This is a genuine route but less common than zoonotic food-borne transmission in the UK. Sexual transmission (A) is not a recognised HEV route. Vertical transmission (D) is rare but can occur causing severe neonatal HEV. Dialysis unit nosocomial spread (E) is occasionally reported but not a major transmission route.

mockBank.question: A baby receives HBV immunoglobulin at birth because the mother is HBsAg positive. Which ONE of the following describes the type of immunity conferred and its duration?
options: A. Active natural immunity — provides lifelong protection similar to natural infection
B. Active artificial immunity — lasts 10–15 years
C. Passive artificial immunity — provides immediate but temporary protection (weeks to months) without immunological memory
D. Passive natural immunity — provides permanent immunity via maternal IgG transfer
E. Passive artificial immunity — the baby develops its own anti-HBs antibodies within 2 weeks
mockBank.explanation: Types of immunity: (1) Active natural — immunity acquired through natural infection (e.g., post-COVID antibodies); (2) Active artificial — immunity from vaccination (e.g., HBV vaccine stimulates own antibody production); (3) Passive natural — transfer of pre-formed maternal antibodies via placenta (IgG) or breast milk (sIgA); (4) Passive artificial — injection of pre-formed antibodies (immunoglobulin preparations). HBIG (hepatitis B immunoglobulin) contains pre-formed anti-HBs antibodies — it is passive artificial immunity. It provides immediate protection (hours) but antibodies are catabolised over weeks to months — no immunological memory is formed. The baby must also receive the HBV vaccine course concurrently for long-term active protection. Passive immunity does not lead to the baby producing its own antibodies (E) — that is active immunity. Passive immunity is always temporary (D is wrong to say permanent).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following antiseptics is MOST appropriate for skin preparation before venepuncture or intraoral injection site preparation before local anaesthesia?
options: A. Povidone-iodine 10% solution — the most effective broad-spectrum antiseptic
B. Chlorhexidine gluconate 0.5% in 70% isopropyl alcohol — combines rapid bacterial kill with sustained antibacterial activity
C. Hydrogen peroxide 3% — effective against all oral pathogens
D. Sodium hypochlorite 5% — hospital-grade surface disinfectant appropriate for skin use
E. Cetrimide 15% — the gold standard for pre-injection skin preparation
mockBank.explanation: Chlorhexidine 0.5% in 70% isopropyl alcohol is the recommended pre-procedure skin antiseptic. The combination provides: (1) Isopropyl alcohol — rapid immediate bactericidal action (denatures proteins, dissolves cell membranes); (2) Chlorhexidine — substantive residual bactericidal activity that persists for hours after application (binds to skin proteins and continues releasing). This combination is used for: venepuncture/cannula insertion, surgical site preparation, and pre-injection skin/mucosal preparation. Chlorhexidine alone (without alcohol) is slower-acting; alcohol alone has no residual activity. Povidone-iodine (A) is effective but has poor substantivity and is deactivated by organic matter. Hydrogen peroxide (C) is used for wound irrigation and cavity decontamination, not routine pre-injection prep. Sodium hypochlorite 5% (D) is a corrosive surface disinfectant — absolutely NOT for skin use. Cetrimide (E) is a quaternary ammonium compound used in wound cleaning but is not the gold standard for pre-injection prep.

mockBank.question: Dolutegravir is an integrase strand transfer inhibitor (INSTI) increasingly used in first-line HIV treatment. Which ONE of the following BEST describes its mechanism of action?
options: A. Dolutegravir blocks HIV protease from cleaving the Gag-Pol polyprotein into functional HIV proteins
B. Dolutegravir inhibits HIV integrase, blocking the strand transfer step that inserts viral DNA into the host chromosome
C. Dolutegravir is a nucleoside analogue that inhibits reverse transcriptase by chain termination
D. Dolutegravir binds the CCR5 co-receptor, blocking HIV entry into macrophages
E. Dolutegravir inhibits the HIV protease by binding allosterically rather than at the active site
mockBank.explanation: HIV integrase catalyses two reactions: (1) 3' processing — removes two nucleotides from each end of the viral DNA to create recessed 3' hydroxyl groups; (2) Strand transfer — the viral DNA with the 3' hydroxyl groups attacks the host chromosomal DNA, inserting the viral DNA. Dolutegravir (and other INSTIs — raltegravir, elvitegravir, bictegravir) specifically inhibit the strand transfer step by chelating the two Mg²⁺ ions in the integrase active site that are essential for the catalytic mechanism. Without integration, the provirus cannot be established and HIV cannot replicate. INSTIs have a high genetic barrier to resistance (especially dolutegravir) — multiple mutations are needed for clinical resistance. Protease inhibition (A) = darunavir, ritonavir. Chain termination NRTI (C) = tenofovir, zidovudine, emtricitabine. CCR5 antagonist (D) = maraviroc. Allosteric protease inhibition (E) is not the INSTI mechanism.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST differentiates acute osteomyelitis from chronic osteomyelitis of the jaws?
options: A. Acute osteomyelitis always requires surgical debridement; chronic osteomyelitis is managed with antibiotics alone

B. Acute osteomyelitis presents with fever, severe pain, and elevated inflammatory markers within days to weeks of the precipitating cause; chronic osteomyelitis persists >4 weeks, often with sequestra, sinus tracts, and may have a subacute presentation
C. Acute osteomyelitis affects the maxilla predominantly; chronic osteomyelitis predominantly affects the mandible
D. Chronic osteomyelitis is always caused by Staphylococcus aureus; acute osteomyelitis is polymicrobial
E. Acute osteomyelitis shows classic radiographic changes within the first 24 hours of onset
mockBank.explanation: Acute osteomyelitis: rapid onset (days–weeks), severe pain, fever, elevated WBC and CRP, localised jaw tenderness, regional lymphadenopathy, loosening of teeth.
