Radiograph: minimal early changes (cortical destruction is a late finding — requires 30–50% bone mineral loss to be visible on plain films). Chronic osteomyelitis: persistence >4 weeks, often subacute presentation, dead bone (sequestrum = avascular bone fragment), involucrum (new bone forming around sequestrum), sinus tracts (cloacae) discharging pus. Causes: inadequately treated acute OM, radiation-related, bisphosphonate-related. Mandible >> maxilla for both types (the mandible has a more limited blood supply and dense cortical bone compared to the porous maxilla with its excellent vascular supply) — C is wrong. Both can be polymicrobial (D). Radiographic changes in acute OM require 10–21 days to appear (E is wrong).

mockBank.question: Ganciclovir is the first-line treatment for serious CMV infections. Which ONE of the following BEST describes its mechanism and the key difference from aciclovir?
options: A. Ganciclovir is activated by viral thymidine kinase — the same enzyme that activates aciclovir
B. Ganciclovir is phosphorylated by the CMV-encoded UL97 kinase (not thymidine kinase), converted to ganciclovir triphosphate which inhibits CMV DNA polymerase and acts as a chain terminator
C. Ganciclovir is a reverse transcriptase inhibitor used for CMV in immunocompromised patients
D. Ganciclovir directly binds CMV surface glycoproteins, preventing viral entry
E. Ganciclovir and aciclovir have identical mechanisms — the choice between them is based on patient tolerance only
mockBank.explanation: Ganciclovir is a guanosine nucleoside analogue. Unlike aciclovir (activated by viral thymidine kinase), ganciclovir requires phosphorylation by the CMV-specific UL97 protein kinase (pUL97). UL97 converts ganciclovir to ganciclovir monophosphate; host cellular kinases then convert it to the active ganciclovir triphosphate (GCV-TP). GCV-TP inhibits CMV DNA polymerase (UL54) and acts as a chain terminator. Clinical uses: CMV retinitis in AIDS (CD4 <50), CMV pneumonitis in transplant recipients, CMV prophylaxis in high-risk transplant patients. Key toxicities: myelosuppression (neutropenia, thrombocytopenia) — more toxic than aciclovir. Resistance: UL97 mutations → ganciclovir resistance → switch to foscarnet or cidofovir. Aciclovir is NOT activated by UL97 (it uses HSV/VZV TK) and has poor CMV activity — the difference in kinase specificity (A, E are wrong) is the fundamental distinction.

mockBank.question: A patient with chronic HBV infection (HBsAg positive, on entecavir) is found to have superimposed HDV infection on routine testing. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate management for the HDV component of the infection?
options: A. Stop entecavir immediately — it accelerates HDV replication
B. Continue entecavir and add pegylated interferon-alpha (Peg-IFN-α) for 48 weeks — the only treatment with proven efficacy against HDV
C. Switch from entecavir to tenofovir — this is active against both HBV and HDV
D. Add ribavirin 1000mg daily for 24 weeks — ribavirin is effective against all hepatitis viruses
E. No additional treatment is required — HDV clears spontaneously in most chronic cases
mockBank.explanation: HDV treatment is challenging. Nucleoside/nucleotide analogues (entecavir, tenofovir) suppress HBV DNA but do NOT effectively suppress HDV RNA — because HDV replicates its own RNA independently once inside HBV-coinfected cells (only requiring HBsAg for packaging). Pegylated interferon-alpha (Peg-IFN-α) is the only currently approved treatment for chronic HDV: it inhibits HDV replication through immune modulation. Treatment for 48 weeks achieves SVR (sustained virological response) in only ~25–30% of patients. Entecavir should continue to suppress HBV (A is wrong). Tenofovir (C) also does not significantly suppress HDV. Ribavirin (D) has no proven efficacy against HDV. Chronic HDV coinfection does NOT resolve spontaneously (E) — it significantly accelerates cirrhosis compared to HBV alone. New agents (Bulevirtide — an entry inhibitor blocking NTCP receptor) are emerging as treatments.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes how autoimmune diseases arise from a breakdown in self-tolerance?
options: A. Autoimmune diseases occur exclusively due to genetic mutations in MHC Class II genes

B. Autoimmune diseases result from a failure of central tolerance (clonal deletion of autoreactive T cells in thymus) and/or peripheral tolerance mechanisms, leading to activation of self-reactive lymphocytes
C. Autoimmune diseases are caused by viral infections that destroy all T regulatory cells
D. Autoimmune diseases develop only in patients with complete absence of regulatory T cells (Tregs)
E. Autoimmune diseases result from overproduction of IgM antibodies that cross-react with self antigens
mockBank.explanation: Self-tolerance is maintained by two mechanisms: (1) Central tolerance — in the thymus, T cells with high-affinity receptors for self-antigens undergo clonal deletion (negative selection) or become regulatory T cells. B cells undergo similar editing in bone marrow. (2) Peripheral tolerance — autoreactive cells that escape central deletion are controlled by: T regulatory cells (Tregs, Foxp3+) that suppress self-reactive lymphocytes, anergy (unresponsiveness of T cells that encounter antigen without co-stimulation), and activation-induced cell death. When these mechanisms break down — due to genetic susceptibility (HLA alleles are risk factors, A is incomplete not exclusive), environmental triggers (infection via molecular mimicry, E is different mechanism), or Treg deficiency — autoreactive lymphocytes become activated and attack self-tissues. Viral infections can trigger but rarely cause complete Treg destruction (C). Autoimmunity can occur with reduced (not absent) Tregs (D). IgG (not IgM) antibodies are more commonly pathogenic in autoimmunity (E).

mockBank.question: A patient requiring a root canal treatment has a known penicillin allergy (rash with amoxicillin) and also takes warfarin. The dentist considers prescribing metronidazole alone for an associated abscess. Which ONE of the following BEST describes the most critical consideration?
options: A. Metronidazole is contraindicated with penicillin allergy — an alternative must be found
B. Metronidazole inhibits CYP2C9, significantly raising warfarin levels — INR monitoring within 3–5 days is essential; warn patient about bleeding signs
C. Metronidazole is absolutely contraindicated with warfarin — choose clindamycin instead
D. Metronidazole has no interaction with warfarin — it can be prescribed without any monitoring
E. Metronidazole and warfarin combination is safe if the patient avoids alcohol during treatment
mockBank.explanation: Metronidazole has no connection to penicillin allergy — the two are entirely unrelated drug classes with no cross-reactivity (A is wrong). Metronidazole can be used in penicillin-allergic patients. However, the critical consideration here is the warfarin interaction: metronidazole inhibits CYP2C9 (and possibly CYP3A4), reducing warfarin metabolism and significantly raising INR. Management: prescribe metronidazole at the normal therapeutic dose (400mg TDS), inform the patient to watch for unusual bleeding or bruising, and arrange an INR check within 3–5 days to guide potential warfarin dose adjustment. Metronidazole is NOT absolutely contraindicated with warfarin (C) — unlike miconazole, which is absolutely contraindicated. The interaction IS clinically significant and requires monitoring (D is dangerous). Avoiding alcohol reduces the disulfiram reaction risk but does NOT eliminate the CYP-mediated warfarin interaction (E).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes why dry socket (alveolar osteitis) is NOT an infection and should not be treated with antibiotics?
options: A. Dry socket involves Staphylococcus aureus infection of the alveolar bone — antibiotics target the correct pathogen

B. Dry socket is a disturbance of normal wound healing characterised by loss or non-formation of a blood clot with exposure of bare bone, causing fibrinolysis — it is an inflammatory, not infective, process and is treated with local lavage and sedative dressings
C. Dry socket is caused by Actinomyces species and requires prolonged penicillin therapy
D. Dry socket and osteomyelitis are the same condition — both require antibiotics and surgical debridement
E. Dry socket is always infected — swab culture is mandatory before any treatment can be initiated
mockBank.explanation: Dry socket (localised alveolar osteitis) is a wound healing complication, NOT an infection. It occurs when the blood clot in the extraction socket is lost or fails to organise (due to smoking, oral contraceptive-related fibrinolysis, traumatic extraction, or irrigation). The exposed alveolar bone is extremely painful and sensitive — characteristic throbbing pain radiating to the ear, foul taste/odour from clot debris. Histologically: bare bone with inflammatory cells but NO bacterial invasion of bone (distinguishes from osteomyelitis). Management: (1) Gentle irrigation to remove debris; (2) Sedative dressings (Alvogyl — contains Butamben, eugenol, and iodoform; provides pain relief and promotes healing); (3) Analgesics; (4) Review and redress as needed. Antibiotics (A, C, E) are NOT indicated — they do not improve outcomes. Dry socket and osteomyelitis (D) are distinctly different conditions.

mockBank.question: A patient who is HBsAg negative, anti-HBc positive, anti-HBs negative is about to start rituximab therapy for non-Hodgkin's lymphoma. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate management?
options: A. No action required — HBsAg negative status confirms the patient has cleared HBV
B. Test HBV DNA to confirm viral load; prophylactic antiviral therapy (entecavir or tenofovir) is recommended before and during rituximab to prevent HBV reactivation
C. Vaccinate against HBV before starting rituximab — this will prevent reactivation
D. Monitor ALT levels monthly — if elevated, then start antiviral therapy
E. Prescribe lamivudine as it has the best safety profile for HBV prophylaxis in this context
mockBank.explanation: The serological pattern HBsAg(-), anti-HBc(+), anti-HBs(-) indicates past HBV infection that has resolved. However, occult HBV infection persists — covalently closed circular DNA (cccDNA) remains in hepatocytes indefinitely even after HBsAg clearance. Rituximab (an anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody that depletes B cells) causes profound immunosuppression — particularly of the humoral immune arm. This can lead to HBV reactivation (re-emergence of HBsAg and high-level viral replication) in patients with past HBV exposure, with potentially fulminant hepatitis and hepatic failure. UK guidelines (BSH, NHSE): all patients with anti-HBc positivity should receive prophylactic entecavir or tenofovir before, during, and for 12 months after immunosuppressive therapy. HBsAg negative does not mean HBV is eradicated (A). Vaccination (C) is ineffective once HBV cccDNA is present. Reactive monitoring (D) risks missing reactivation. Lamivudine (E) is not recommended due to high resistance rates in prolonged prophylaxis.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST explains the mechanism by which tetracyclines cause permanent tooth staining in children?
options: A. Tetracyclines are incorporated into the surface enamel as the tooth erupts, causing surface discolouration

B. Tetracyclines chelate calcium ions and are deposited in mineralising dentine and enamel during active tooth formation, forming a yellow-coloured tetracycline-calcium orthophosphate complex that oxidises to grey-brown over time
C. Tetracyclines inhibit ameloblasts, causing hypocalcification of enamel
D. Tetracyclines are bacteriostatic and prevent the oral bacteria responsible for pigment production from being cleared
E. Tetracyclines cause direct toxic damage to pulp tissue, leading to pulp necrosis and associated internal discolouration
mockBank.explanation: Tetracyclines have high affinity for calcium ions. During active tooth mineralisation (primary dentition: in utero and up to 6 months after birth; permanent dentition: up to approximately 7–8 years of age), tetracyclines are incorporated into the mineralising dentine matrix by chelating calcium at the calcification front. The resulting tetracycline-calcium complex is yellow initially. Upon exposure to light (after tooth eruption), the complex undergoes photo-oxidation, transforming from yellow to grey-brown — this is permanent and cannot be reversed by bleaching. Severity depends on: dose, duration of exposure, and stage of tooth development during exposure. The staining affects dentine predominantly (not surface enamel — A is wrong). Tetracyclines do not inhibit ameloblasts specifically (C). Oral bacteria are not the cause of staining (D). Pulp toxicity (E) is not the mechanism — the teeth are vital.

mockBank.question: A patient with chronic lymphocytic leukaemia is about to start ibrutinib therapy. She has a past history of VZV shingles. Which ONE of the following prophylactic measures is MOST appropriate?
options: A. VZV vaccination with the live attenuated vaccine before starting ibrutinib
B. Aciclovir 400mg twice daily (or valaciclovir 500mg once daily) for VZV prophylaxis throughout immunosuppressive treatment
C. No prophylaxis required — shingles is mild and VZV reactivation risk is not significantly increased with ibrutinib
D. Famciclovir 500mg twice daily — the only antiviral licensed for VZV prophylaxis in immunocompromised patients
E. VZIG at the start of ibrutinib therapy to prevent VZV reactivation
mockBank.explanation: Ibrutinib (a BTK inhibitor) and other targeted immunotherapies for haematological malignancies significantly increase the risk of VZV reactivation (shingles). Standard practice for immunocompromised patients on such regimens: aciclovir 400mg twice daily or valaciclovir 500mg once daily as VZV prophylaxis throughout treatment and for a period after completion. Live attenuated VZV vaccine (A) is absolutely contraindicated in immunocompromised patients — it could cause disseminated vaccine-strain VZV infection. VZV reactivation risk IS significantly elevated with ibrutinib (C is wrong). Famciclovir (D) is an effective antiviral but aciclovir/valaciclovir are the standard prophylactic agents — famciclovir does not have a superior evidence base for this indication. VZIG (E) is passive post-exposure prophylaxis for non-immune individuals exposed to VZV — not indicated as pre-emptive prophylaxis in a patient with prior VZV infection (who already has memory immunity, just inadequate to prevent reactivation).

mockBank.question: The basic reproduction number (R₀) of an infectious disease represents which ONE of the following?
options: A. The average number of secondary infections caused by one infectious individual in a fully susceptible population
B. The proportion of the population that must be vaccinated to achieve herd immunity
C. The case fatality rate per 100 cases of a given infection
D. The average duration of the infectious period in days
E. The number of contacts a patient has with the healthcare system during an infectious illness
mockBank.explanation: R₀ (basic reproduction number) is a fundamental epidemiological measure representing the average number of secondary cases generated by one infectious individual when introduced into a completely susceptible population. R₀ > 1: epidemic spreads; R₀ < 1: epidemic dies out; R₀ = 1: endemic equilibrium. Herd immunity threshold: 1 - 1/R₀. Examples: Measles R₀ ≈ 12–18 (herd immunity requires ~92–95% vaccination coverage); SARS-CoV-2 original R₀ ≈ 2–3; Influenza R₀ ≈ 2–3; Ebola R₀ ≈ 1.5–2. Herd immunity threshold (B) is derived from R₀ (= 1 - 1/R₀) but is not the definition of R₀ itself. Case fatality rate (C) is a separate measure. Infectious period duration (D) is an input that affects R₀ but is not its definition. Healthcare contacts (E) are irrelevant to R₀.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following Candida species is INTRINSICALLY resistant to fluconazole, making speciation clinically important?
options: A. Candida albicans — the most common oral Candida, intrinsically resistant to fluconazole
B. Candida glabrata — intrinsically resistant to fluconazole
C. Candida krusei — intrinsically resistant to fluconazole through reduced CYP51 affinity
D. Candida tropicalis — intrinsically resistant to all antifungals including nystatin
E. Candida parapsilosis — resistant to echinocandins but sensitive to all azoles
mockBank.explanation: Candida krusei is intrinsically resistant to fluconazole — this resistance is innate (not acquired) and is caused by reduced affinity of its CYP51 (14-alpha-demethylase) enzyme for fluconazole, combined with reduced intracellular drug accumulation. If oral candidiasis fails to respond to fluconazole in an immunocompromised patient, speciation and sensitivity testing are essential as C. krusei (now reclassified as Pichia kudriavzevii) requires alternative therapy (voriconazole or caspofungin). Candida albicans (A) is typically sensitive to fluconazole (though acquired resistance can develop with long-term azole exposure). Candida glabrata (B) has dose-dependent sensitivity (reduced sensitivity, not intrinsic resistance) — requires higher fluconazole doses; intrinsic echinocandin susceptibility with acquired resistance possible. Candida tropicalis (D) is generally sensitive to fluconazole. Candida parapsilosis (E) has FKS-mediated echinocandin reduced susceptibility in some strains, not all.

mockBank.question: A 28-year-old patient suffers from recurrent herpes labialis (cold sores) with 8 episodes per year, each lasting 10 days and causing significant distress. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate management?
options: A. Topical aciclovir cream 5% at first sign of prodrome — to be used for episodic attacks
B. Oral valaciclovir 500mg twice daily continuously as suppressive therapy to reduce frequency and severity of recurrences
C. Oral aciclovir 200mg five times daily for each episode only — suppressive therapy is not available for labialis
D. Intravenous aciclovir 10mg/kg per 8 hours — the gold standard for herpes labialis
E. Advise sun protection only — pharmacological treatment of recurrent herpes labialis is not evidence-based
mockBank.explanation: Patients with frequent, severe, or psychosocially distressing recurrent herpes labialis (typically defined as ≥6 episodes per year) are candidates for suppressive (prophylactic) antiviral therapy. Valaciclovir 500mg twice daily (the prodrug of aciclovir with much better oral bioavailability) taken continuously reduces recurrence frequency by ~50% and severity of breakthrough episodes. Alternatively, aciclovir 400mg twice daily is used. Suppressive therapy continues for 6–12 months, then reassessed. Topical aciclovir cream (A) is only marginally effective and must be applied immediately at prodrome — inadequate for 8 episodes/year causing significant distress. Episodic oral aciclovir (C) is appropriate for mild-moderate recurrences but not for this frequency and impact. IV aciclovir (D) is reserved for severe primary infection, encephalitis, or neonatal HSV. Sun protection (E) is a useful adjunct (UV light is a trigger) but not sufficient as sole management.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following oral conditions has a KNOWN association with Hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection?
options: A. Acute necrotising ulcerative gingivitis (ANUG)
B. Lichen planus — HCV infection is found in up to 30% of oral lichen planus patients in certain populations
C. Peripheral giant cell granuloma
D. Oral hairy leukoplakia
E. Pyogenic granuloma of pregnancy
mockBank.explanation: Oral lichen planus (OLP) has a well-documented association with HCV infection, particularly in Mediterranean populations (Italy, Spain, Japan) where the prevalence of HCV in OLP patients is 30–40%. The mechanism may involve HCV-induced immune dysregulation leading to T cell-mediated attack on oral epithelium, or molecular mimicry between HCV antigens and keratinocyte proteins. Clinical implication: patients presenting with erosive/symptomatic OLP may warrant HCV testing, particularly if they have risk factors (IV drug use, blood transfusions before 1992, tattoos). Conversely, chronic HCV patients should be examined for OLP. ANUG (A) is associated with fusobacterium/spirochaete overgrowth, stress, and immunosuppression — not HCV. Peripheral giant cell granuloma (C) is a reactive lesion related to local trauma. OHL (D) is associated with EBV. Pyogenic granuloma (E) is related to hormonal changes and local irritation.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following scenarios represents the MOST appropriate indication for co-amoxiclav (amoxicillin + clavulanic acid) in dental practice?
options: A. First-line treatment for all dental infections regardless of severity
B. A patient who has failed to respond to amoxicillin after 48–72 hours for a dental abscess with systemic features
C. As prophylaxis before all dental extractions in immunocompetent patients
D. Treatment of dental infections in patients with documented penicillin allergy
E. Replacement for metronidazole as co-amoxiclav has superior anaerobic coverage
mockBank.explanation: Co-amoxiclav (625mg = amoxicillin 500mg + clavulanic acid 125mg, TDS) is a second-line antibiotic in UK dental prescribing guidelines. It is indicated when: (1) First-line amoxicillin (with or without metronidazole) has failed after 48–72 hours; (2) The infection is known/suspected to involve beta-lactamase-producing organisms. Clavulanic acid inhibits beta-lactamase enzymes, restoring amoxicillin's activity against resistant organisms. Co-amoxiclav is NOT first-line (A) — this represents poor antibiotic stewardship. It is not indicated as prophylaxis in immunocompetent patients (C). Co-amoxiclav is contraindicated in true penicillin allergy (D) — it contains amoxicillin. While it has good anaerobic activity, it does not entirely replace metronidazole in combination regimens (E) and has higher rates of C. difficile than amoxicillin alone. Note: co-amoxiclav should be used with caution in liver disease (cholestatic jaundice risk) and requires food to be taken with it to minimise GI side effects.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the current UK recommendation for HIV testing in dental practice?
options: A. HIV testing is not appropriate in dental settings — it must be performed by GUM clinics only

B. HIV testing is recommended for patients presenting with oral conditions strongly associated with HIV (oral candidiasis, hairy leukoplakia, Kaposi's sarcoma) — dentists should offer/facilitate testing and can signpost patients
C. Routine HIV testing of all dental patients is mandatory in the UK
D. Dentists may test for HIV only if they hold a special licence from Public Health England
E. HIV testing is only appropriate for patients who disclose injection drug use
mockBank.explanation: UK guidelines (BHIVA/BASHH, NHS England) support expanded HIV testing, including in dental settings. Dentists are well-placed to identify oral manifestations of HIV that may indicate undiagnosed HIV: persistent oral candidiasis without obvious cause, oral hairy leukoplakia, Kaposi's sarcoma lesions on the hard palate, and recurrent/severe herpes zoster in a young patient. In these situations, dentists should: (1) Discuss with the patient that their oral findings may be related to HIV; (2) Recommend HIV testing; (3) Facilitate referral to GUM/sexual health or provide information about testing services. Point-of-care HIV tests are available — rapid finger-prick tests with results in 15 minutes. This is not mandatory for all patients (C) and does not require a special licence (D). Testing should not be limited to drug users (E).

mockBank.question: A patient presents 3 days after a lower molar extraction with a dry, malodorous socket and severe throbbing pain. Temperature is 36.8°C, no lymphadenopathy, no trismus. Which ONE of the following represents the MOST appropriate management?
options: A. Prescribe amoxicillin 500mg TDS for 5 days and metronidazole 400mg TDS for 5 days
B. Irrigate the socket with saline, place an alvogyl sedative dressing, prescribe analgesics, and review in 2–3 days — no antibiotics indicated
C. Re-extract the socket under local anaesthesia to allow fresh clot formation
D. Prescribe co-amoxiclav 625mg TDS for 7 days
E. Refer immediately to oral and maxillofacial surgery for curettage under general anaesthesia
mockBank.explanation: Dry socket (alveolar osteitis) is an inflammatory wound healing complication, not an infection. Systemic features are absent (afebrile, no lymphadenopathy). Antibiotics are not indicated — they do not speed healing of dry socket and contribute to antimicrobial resistance. Management: gentle irrigation, sedative dressing (alvogyl contains butamben, eugenol, iodoform), analgesics, and review. Re-extraction (C) is not indicated — this would cause additional trauma without benefit. Hospital referral (E) is unnecessary for uncomplicated dry socket.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following statements about lysozyme in saliva is MOST accurate?
options: A. Lysozyme is an antibody produced by B cells in the submandibular glands
B. Lysozyme is an enzyme that cleaves the beta-1,4 glycosidic bond in bacterial peptidoglycan, disrupting Gram-positive bacterial cell walls
C. Lysozyme is active exclusively against Gram-negative bacteria by disrupting lipopolysaccharide
D. Lysozyme in saliva prevents all oral infections — its absence causes total loss of oral immunity
E. Lysozyme activates the complement membrane attack complex against oral bacteria
mockBank.explanation: Lysozyme (muramidase) is an antimicrobial enzyme found in saliva, tears, nasal secretions, and neutrophil granules. It cleaves the beta-1,4 glycosidic bond between N-acetylmuramic acid (NAM) and N-acetylglucosamine (NAG) in bacterial peptidoglycan. This disrupts the structural integrity of the cell wall, particularly of Gram-positive bacteria (which have thick exposed peptidoglycan). Gram-negative bacteria have their peptidoglycan protected by an outer membrane, making them less susceptible. Lysozyme is an enzyme, not an antibody (A). It is not a complement activator (E).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST explains why non-enveloped (naked) viruses are MORE resistant to environmental inactivation than enveloped viruses?
options: A. Non-enveloped viruses replicate faster and replace damaged particles more quickly

B. Non-enveloped viruses have only a protein capsid, which is resistant to desiccation, detergents, and many disinfectants — enveloped viruses depend on a fragile lipid envelope that is easily disrupted
C. Non-enveloped viruses are smaller and therefore harder to inactivate
D. Non-enveloped viruses produce more copies per cell, increasing environmental viral load
E. Non-enveloped viruses encode DNA repair enzymes that fix environmental damage to the genome
mockBank.explanation: The lipid envelope of enveloped viruses (HSV, HBV, HCV, HIV, influenza) is derived from host cell membranes. Lipids are easily disrupted by: alcohol-based disinfectants, detergents (soap), drying, UV radiation, and heat. Non-enveloped viruses (norovirus, adenovirus, HAV, poliovirus) have only a protective protein capsid — highly resistant to desiccation, alcohol-based hand rubs, and many disinfectants. This explains why: soap and water (disrupts lipids AND physically removes particles) is essential for norovirus decontamination where alcohol hand rub is insufficient; HAV and norovirus can survive on surfaces for days; enveloped viruses like HIV are rapidly inactivated outside the host.

mockBank.question: A patient's blood test shows: HBsAg positive, HBeAg negative, anti-HBe positive, HBV DNA 500 IU/mL, ALT mildly elevated. Which ONE of the following BEST describes this patient's status?
options: A. Acute HBV infection in the window period
B. Inactive HBV carrier — low replication, low infectivity, unlikely to need antiviral treatment
C. Highly infectious HBV carrier with active viral replication
D. Resolved HBV infection with residual surface antigen
E. HBeAg-negative chronic hepatitis B — requires antiviral therapy despite low viral load
mockBank.explanation: HBsAg(+), HBeAg(-), anti-HBe(+), HBV DNA <2000 IU/mL, normal or mildly elevated ALT = inactive HBV carrier state. HBeAg seroconversion (HBeAg → anti-HBe) indicates suppression of high-level viral replication. These patients have low infectivity, low risk of progression to cirrhosis, and generally do not require antiviral treatment — but require monitoring every 6–12 months as reactivation can occur. Note: HBeAg-negative chronic hepatitis B (E) exists — it has HBeAg(-), anti-HBe(+) but HBV DNA >2000 IU/mL with elevated ALT, indicating active disease despite HBeAg negativity — this pattern DOES require treatment. The key distinguisher here is the DNA level: 500 IU/mL is below the treatment threshold.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following microbiology techniques is MOST useful for identifying the species of an organism isolated from a pus sample when standard biochemical tests have been inconclusive?
options: A. Gram staining — provides definitive species identification based on cell morphology
B. Kirby-Bauer disc diffusion — determines antibiotic sensitivity
C. MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry — rapidly identifies bacteria and fungi to species level by analysing their unique protein mass fingerprint
D. Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) — the only method that can identify organisms from clinical samples
E. Ziehl-Neelsen staining — identifies all bacteria that are Gram-negative
mockBank.explanation: MALDI-TOF (Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionisation Time-Of-Flight) mass spectrometry has revolutionised clinical microbiology. It identifies bacteria and fungi in minutes by generating a unique mass spectrum fingerprint of cellular proteins, which is matched against a reference database. It can identify organisms to species level even when biochemical profiles are atypical. Gram staining (A) provides morphological clues but cannot definitively identify species. Kirby-Bauer (B) tests antibiotic sensitivity, not species identity. PCR (D) is highly sensitive but requires specific primers — MALDI-TOF does not require prior knowledge of target organism. Ziehl-Neelsen (E) identifies acid-fast bacilli (mycobacteria), not all Gram-negatives.

mockBank.question: A mother has active herpes labialis at the time of delivery. The neonate is at risk of which ONE of the following most serious complications?
options: A. Neonatal herpes simplex — can cause disseminated disease, encephalitis, or skin/eye/mouth infection with significant mortality and morbidity
B. Congenital varicella syndrome — limb hypoplasia and skin scarring
C. Neonatal cytomegalovirus — causing sensorineural hearing loss
D. Neonatal rubella — causing cardiac defects and cataracts
E. No risk — HSV-1 is oral and cannot infect neonates
mockBank.explanation: Neonatal herpes simplex is a serious complication occurring in approximately 1 in 3500 live births. HSV-1 (oral) and HSV-2 (genital) can both cause neonatal infection — primary genital HSV poses the highest risk. Transmission: 85% intrapartum (direct contact with viral shedding in birth canal or from HSV labialis during delivery/kissing), 10% postnatal (contact from carers with active lesions), 5% in utero. Neonatal HSV presents in 3 forms: (1) SEM: skin, eye, mouth only (least severe but can progress); (2) CNS: encephalitis; (3) Disseminated: involving liver, lungs, brain (50% mortality untreated). All forms require IV aciclovir. Active maternal HSV at delivery is a consideration for Caesarean section. HSV-1 absolutely CAN infect neonates (E is wrong).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following mechanisms allows bacteria to acquire antibiotic resistance genes from other bacteria through direct cell-to-cell contact?
options: A. Transformation — uptake of free DNA from the environment
B. Transduction — transfer of bacterial DNA via bacteriophage
C. Conjugation — transfer of plasmid DNA through a sex pilus connecting two bacteria
D. Transposition — movement of resistance genes between different locations within the same bacterial chromosome
E. Mutation — spontaneous changes in bacterial DNA leading to antibiotic resistance
mockBank.explanation: Conjugation is horizontal gene transfer via direct cell-to-cell contact through a sex pilus (F-pilus). Plasmids containing resistance genes (R-plasmids) are transferred from donor to recipient bacteria. This is the most clinically significant mechanism for spreading antibiotic resistance (including multi-drug resistance) between bacteria — even across species barriers (e.g., E. coli → Klebsiella, MRSA resistance genes spreading between Staphylococcal species). Transformation (A): uptake of naked DNA from lysed bacteria — requires 'competent' recipient cells. Transduction (B): bacteriophage carries bacterial DNA between cells — important for toxin gene transfer (cholera toxin, TSST-1). Transposition (D): movement of resistance genes within or between DNA elements in the same cell — not between different bacteria. Mutation (E): affects individual cells, not horizontal transfer.

mockBank.question: An HIV-positive patient with CD4 count of 95 cells/μL presents with extensive pseudomembranous candidiasis covering the entire palate and tongue that has not responded to nystatin after 14 days. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate next step?
options: A. Continue nystatin for a further 14 days — treatment failure at 14 days is premature
B. Prescribe miconazole gel if the patient is not on warfarin — second-line topical therapy
C. Prescribe systemic fluconazole 100mg daily for 14 days and review antiretroviral therapy optimisation with the HIV team
D. Perform oral biopsy — treatment-resistant candidiasis requires histological confirmation
E. Prescribe amphotericin B lozenges as first-line escalation for all oral candidiasis
mockBank.explanation: In a severely immunocompromised HIV patient (CD4 <100) with extensive oral candidiasis unresponsive to topical therapy, systemic antifungal treatment with fluconazole is indicated. Fluconazole 100–200mg daily for 14 days achieves excellent mucosal tissue levels. Additionally, this presentation signals urgent need for antiretroviral therapy review — CD4 of 95 should be on HAART, and persistent candidiasis may indicate virological failure or non-adherence. Continuing nystatin (A) is inappropriate when topical therapy has failed. Miconazole (B) is a reasonable second-line topical but inadequate for extensive disease in severe immunocompromise. Biopsy (D) is not needed to confirm pseudomembranous candidiasis — diagnosis is clinical. Amphotericin B lozenges (E) are an alternative but fluconazole is preferred for systemic escalation.

mockBank.question: A patient with chronic granulomatous disease (CGD) has recurrent severe bacterial and fungal infections despite normal neutrophil counts. Which ONE of the following BEST explains this susceptibility?
options: A. CGD patients have absent neutrophils — all infections are due to impaired neutrophil production
B. CGD patients have defective NADPH oxidase, preventing the oxidative burst — neutrophils can phagocytose but cannot kill catalase-positive organisms (S. aureus, Aspergillus)
C. CGD patients cannot produce IgG — bacteria are not opsonised for phagocytosis
D. CGD patients have dysfunctional complement, preventing C3b opsonisation of bacteria
E. CGD is a T cell deficiency — impaired cytokine production prevents macrophage activation
mockBank.explanation: Chronic granulomatous disease is caused by mutations in components of the NADPH oxidase complex (most commonly gp91phox, X-linked). NADPH oxidase generates the superoxide radical (O₂⁻) that initiates the respiratory burst — producing reactive oxygen species (H₂O₂, hypochlorous acid via myeloperoxidase) that are essential for killing catalase-positive organisms. Catalase-positive organisms (S. aureus, Aspergillus, Burkholderia cepacia, Serratia) produce catalase which breaks down the small amounts of H₂O₂ they generate — without the oxidative burst to supplement this, neutrophils cannot kill them. Neutrophil counts are NORMAL (A is wrong). IgG (C) and complement (D) are unaffected in CGD. CGD is an innate immune (phagocyte) defect, not a T cell deficiency (E).

mockBank.question: A patient with Child-Pugh Class C cirrhosis (severe) requires an urgent dental extraction. Which ONE of the following analgesics is MOST appropriate for post-operative pain control?
options: A. Ibuprofen 400mg TDS — for its anti-inflammatory benefit
B. Naproxen 500mg BD — long-acting anti-inflammatory
C. Paracetamol 500mg (maximum 2g/day) — safest analgesic in liver disease at reduced dose
D. Dihydrocodeine 30mg QDS — opioids bypass hepatic metabolism
E. Aspirin 600mg QDS — well-established analgesic
mockBank.explanation: All NSAIDs (A, B, E) are contraindicated in severe liver disease: they reduce prostaglandin-mediated renal blood flow (precipitating hepatorenal syndrome), impair platelet function (worsening the coagulopathy already present in cirrhosis), and can cause GI bleeding. Opioids (D) are hepatically metabolised and accumulate in cirrhosis, risking encephalopathy and respiratory depression — use with extreme caution if at all, at very low doses. Paracetamol at reduced dose (max 2g/day in severe hepatic impairment, normally 4g/day) is the safest option. The hepatotoxicity risk of paracetamol is with overdose/chronic alcohol use, not at low therapeutic doses in well-monitored patients.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following drug combinations is SAFE to prescribe together in a dental patient without requiring specific monitoring or dose adjustment?
options: A. Metronidazole + warfarin
B. Miconazole gel + warfarin
C. Clarithromycin + simvastatin
D. Amoxicillin + metronidazole (in a patient with no warfarin or other interacting drugs)
E. Erythromycin + carbamazepine
mockBank.explanation: Amoxicillin + metronidazole is a standard, safe, evidence-based combination for polymicrobial dental infections in patients without interacting co-medications. Amoxicillin covers aerobic streptococci; metronidazole covers anaerobes — together they provide comprehensive coverage of the mixed flora in odontogenic infections. Metronidazole + warfarin (A) requires INR monitoring — significant CYP2C9 interaction raises INR. Miconazole + warfarin (B) is absolutely contraindicated — risk of catastrophic INR elevation. Clarithromycin + simvastatin (C) risks rhabdomyolysis via CYP3A4 inhibition. Erythromycin + carbamazepine (E) risks carbamazepine toxicity (ataxia, diplopia, confusion) via CYP3A4 inhibition.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following antivirals works by inhibiting neuraminidase, thereby preventing release of newly formed influenza virions from infected cells?
options: A. Aciclovir
B. Zanamivir (Relenza) and oseltamivir (Tamiflu)
C. Amantadine
D. Ribavirin
E. Interferon-alpha
mockBank.explanation: Neuraminidase (NA) is an influenza surface glycoprotein that cleaves sialic acid residues on host cells, allowing newly assembled virions to be released and spread to adjacent cells. Zanamivir (inhaled) and oseltamivir (oral) are neuraminidase inhibitors — they competitively inhibit NA, trapping viral particles on the surface of infected cells and preventing spread. Must be given within 48 hours of symptom onset to have meaningful clinical benefit. Aciclovir (A) targets herpesvirus DNA polymerase. Amantadine (C) blocks the influenza A M2 ion channel, preventing viral uncoating — now rarely used due to resistance. Ribavirin (D) is a nucleoside analogue with broad antiviral activity (used in HCV, RSV). Interferon-alpha (E) broadly upregulates antiviral state in neighbouring cells.

mockBank.question: A microbiology report from a dental abscess swab identifies Streptococcus anginosus (milleri group), Prevotella melaninogenica, and Fusobacterium nucleatum. Sensitivity testing shows all organisms are sensitive to metronidazole. Which ONE of the following antibiotic strategies BEST explains why amoxicillin should also be prescribed?
options: A. Amoxicillin broadens coverage to include Gram-negative aerobes that metronidazole misses
B. Metronidazole covers anaerobes (Prevotella, Fusobacterium) but has poor activity against aerobic/microaerophilic streptococci (S. anginosus) — amoxicillin provides the streptococcal cover
C. Amoxicillin enhances metronidazole's penetration into abscess cavities through synergistic membrane disruption
D. Amoxicillin prevents metronidazole resistance by competitive inhibition of the metronidazole efflux pump
E. Both antibiotics are needed to ensure coverage of MRSA which is present in all dental abscesses
mockBank.explanation: This question illustrates the pharmacological rationale for the standard amoxicillin + metronidazole combination. Metronidazole is active exclusively against anaerobic and microaerophilic organisms (it requires reduction of its nitro group by bacterial pyruvate:ferredoxin oxidoreductase, which only functions in anaerobic conditions). Streptococcus anginosus (milleri group) — the pus-forming streptococcus most associated with deep space dental infections and abscesses — is an aerobic/microaerophilic organism not covered by metronidazole. Amoxicillin covers streptococci. Together they cover the full polymicrobial spectrum. The combination does not provide MRSA cover (E) — and MRSA is not typical in odontogenic infections.

mockBank.question: An HIV-positive patient discloses their status to their dentist before treatment. The dentist refuses to treat the patient. Which ONE of the following BEST describes the legal and ethical position?
options: A. The dentist is justified — HIV is a blood-borne virus and refusal is a reasonable precaution
B. Refusal to treat an HIV-positive patient using standard infection control precautions constitutes unlawful discrimination under the Equality Act 2010 and violates GDC professional standards
C. The dentist must refer to a specialist centre — all HIV patients require specialist dental care
D. Refusal is acceptable if the patient has a detectable viral load above 200 copies/mL
E. The GDC permits refusal of HIV-positive patients as a patient safety measure
mockBank.explanation: Under the Equality Act 2010 (England, Wales, Scotland), HIV infection is a protected disability. Refusing dental treatment to an HIV-positive patient who can be safely managed with standard infection control precautions constitutes unlawful discrimination. The GDC's 'Standards for the Dental Team' explicitly states that dental professionals must not discriminate against patients based on any protected characteristic. Well-controlled HIV with undetectable viral load poses negligible transmission risk; standard universal infection control (gloves, mask, eye protection, autoclave) is adequate. All HIV patients can receive routine dental care in primary care settings (C is wrong). There is no viral load threshold that justifies refusal (D). The GDC absolutely does not permit such refusal (E).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the role of mast cells in innate immunity and inflammation?
options: A. Mast cells are the primary cells responsible for phagocytosis of bacteria at infection sites

B. Mast cells are tissue-resident cells that degranulate in response to IgE cross-linking (allergy) or complement anaphylatoxins (C3a, C5a), releasing histamine, prostaglandins, and TNF-α to promote inflammation and recruit immune cells
C. Mast cells produce IgE antibodies in response to parasitic infections
D. Mast cells present antigens to CD4+ T cells via MHC Class II molecules
E. Mast cells are exclusively involved in allergy — they have no role in innate immunity against pathogens
mockBank.explanation: Mast cells are long-lived, tissue-resident innate immune cells (found in skin, mucosae, submucosa) that express IgE receptors (FcεRI) and complement receptors. They are activated by: (1) IgE cross-linking by allergen → immediate hypersensitivity (allergy, anaphylaxis); (2) Complement anaphylatoxins C3a and C5a → innate immune activation; (3) Bacterial products, neuropeptides, and physical stimuli. Degranulation releases: preformed mediators (histamine — vasodilation, vascular permeability), newly synthesised mediators (prostaglandins, leukotrienes — bronchoconstriction, inflammation), and cytokines (TNF-α — recruiting neutrophils). Mast cells are sentinel cells connecting innate immunity, allergy, and neurogenic inflammation. They do not phagocytose (A), produce IgE (C — IgE is produced by B cells), present antigen (D — they lack professional APC function), or function exclusively in allergy (E).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly describes the principle of Standard (Universal) Precautions in dental infection control?
options: A. Standard precautions apply only to patients known to be blood-borne virus positive — other patients require basic hygiene only
B. Standard precautions treat the blood, body fluids, and secretions of ALL patients as potentially infectious, applying the same protective measures regardless of known or suspected infection status
C. Standard precautions require full personal protective equipment (gown, gloves, mask, visor) for all procedures including routine examination
D. Standard precautions include HIV testing of all patients before invasive procedures
E. Standard precautions are only needed during aerosol-generating dental procedures
mockBank.explanation: Standard (Universal) Precautions, developed following the HIV epidemic, are based on the principle that the infection status of any patient cannot be reliably determined from history, examination, or routine testing alone. Therefore, ALL blood and potentially infectious body fluids from ALL patients are treated as potentially containing blood-borne viruses (HBV, HCV, HIV). The precautions are applied universally — not just to known positive patients (A is wrong). The specific PPE required varies by procedure risk level (C is too absolute — routine examination requires gloves and mask but not necessarily full gown and visor). Standard precautions do NOT include routine patient HIV testing (D). They apply to all clinical contact, not only aerosol procedures (E).

mockBank.question: A 40-year-old Southeast Asian male presents with a persistent posterior nasal space mass, unilateral cervical lymphadenopathy, and unilateral serous otitis media. Which ONE of the following is the MOST likely diagnosis and its viral aetiology?
options: A. Oral squamous cell carcinoma — HPV 16/18
B. Nasopharyngeal carcinoma — EBV (nearly 100% association in endemic regions)
C. Hodgkin's lymphoma — CMV reactivation
D. Burkitt's lymphoma — endemic form associated with malaria co-infection and EBV
E. Salivary gland carcinoma — HHV-8
mockBank.explanation: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC) is strongly associated with EBV in endemic regions (southern China, Southeast Asia, North Africa). EBV DNA and EBER transcripts are detectable in virtually 100% of NPC tumour cells in these regions. Presentation: painless posterior nasal space mass, unilateral cervical lymphadenopathy (trotter's syndrome = ipsilateral conductive hearing loss + soft palate palsy + trigeminal pain), and serous otitis media from eustachian tube obstruction. Plasma EBV DNA is used as a tumour marker for staging and monitoring treatment response. HPV 16/18 (A) causes oropharyngeal SCC (tonsillar, base of tongue) — distinct from NPC. CMV (C) does not cause Hodgkin's — EBV is the correct aetiology for Hodgkin's. Burkitt's (D) is also EBV-associated but presents as a jaw/abdominal mass in children, not a nasopharyngeal mass in adults. HHV-8 (E) causes Kaposi's sarcoma, not salivary gland carcinoma.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST explains why a patient with a deficiency in the mannose-binding lectin (MBL) pathway of complement is more susceptible to recurrent infections in early childhood?
options: A. MBL deficiency prevents B cells from producing IgM antibodies against bacteria

B. MBL is a pattern recognition molecule that activates complement via the lectin pathway when binding mannose on bacterial surfaces — its deficiency impairs opsonisation and complement-mediated killing in the pre-immune period before specific antibodies develop
C. MBL deficiency is only significant in adults — children are protected by maternal IgG
D. MBL directly kills bacteria by forming pores in bacterial membranes
E. MBL activates CD8+ T cells to kill bacteria before IgG antibodies are produced
mockBank.explanation: The lectin pathway of complement: mannose-binding lectin (MBL), a pattern recognition receptor, binds mannose-rich surface glycans on bacteria, fungi, and viruses (these differ from human glycans, which are rich in sialic acid and galactose). MBL binding activates MASP-1 and MASP-2 (serine proteases) → cleavage of C4 and C2 → C3 convertase formation → C3b deposition (opsonisation) and MAC formation. This pathway is crucial in early childhood and in naïve hosts before antigen-specific IgG antibodies have developed (IgG opsonisation requires prior exposure). MBL deficiency is therefore most clinically significant in young children (before 6 months, when maternal IgG is waning) and during new infections. It does not involve B cells or T cells directly (A, E). MBL does not form pores — that is the MAC (D).

mockBank.question: A patient on long-term zoledronic acid (IV bisphosphonate) for metastatic breast cancer requires extraction of a non-restorable lower molar. Which ONE of the following is the MOST important consideration?
options: A. Standard extraction can proceed — bisphosphonates do not affect dental healing
B. Antibiotic prophylaxis is mandatory before extraction to prevent bacteraemia
C. The patient should be managed by oral and maxillofacial surgery in a specialist setting — IV bisphosphonates carry high risk of medication-related osteonecrosis of the jaw (MRONJ)
D. The extraction should be deferred for 6 months after stopping bisphosphonate therapy
E. Prescribe fluoride trays for 3 months before extraction to strengthen the alveolar bone
mockBank.explanation: IV bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid, pamidronate) carry a high risk of medication-related osteonecrosis of the jaw (MRONJ) — exposed, necrotic jawbone that fails to heal. Risk: ~1% with IV bisphosphonates for oncological indications (vs. 0.001–0.01% with oral bisphosphonates for osteoporosis). Extraction is the commonest trigger. Patients on IV bisphosphonates should be referred to a specialist oral surgery/OMFS team for any dentoalveolar surgical procedure. Management principles: optimise oral health before bisphosphonate therapy, avoid extractions where possible, and when unavoidable perform with meticulous surgical technique and antibiotic prophylaxis in specialist settings. Stopping bisphosphonates (D) is not evidence-based for reducing MRONJ risk (long half-life in bone). Fluoride (E) is irrelevant to MRONJ prevention.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following behaviours carries the HIGHEST risk of hepatitis C transmission?
options: A. Unprotected sexual intercourse between heterosexual partners (one HCV positive)
B. Sharing contaminated needles among IV drug users
C. Dental treatment in a modern dental surgery following standard infection control
D. Tattooing in a registered studio using single-use needles
E. Sharing household items such as towels with an HCV-positive person
mockBank.explanation: Sharing needles/syringes among injecting drug users (IDU) is the highest-risk route for HCV transmission — per-use transmission risk ~3%, with cumulative risk approaching 80% over years of needle sharing. Blood from a high-viraemia HCV-positive individual is directly inoculated into the bloodstream. Sexual transmission (A) does occur but at much lower efficiency (~0.07% per sexual contact in heterosexual monogamous relationships) — men who have sex with men have higher risk. Dental treatment (C) with proper infection control carries negligible risk — occupational transmission risk is ~0.5% per needlestick from HCV-positive patient. Registered tattoo studios with single-use needles (D) carry minimal risk. Household contact (E) is not a significant route — HCV is not transmitted by casual contact, sharing utensils, or towels.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following antibiotics has activity against BOTH aerobic Gram-positive cocci AND anaerobes, making it useful as a single agent for dental infections in penicillin-allergic patients?
options: A. Metronidazole — covers all oral pathogens including streptococci
B. Ciprofloxacin — broad-spectrum including dental anaerobes
C. Clindamycin — active against aerobic Gram-positive cocci (streptococci, staphylococci) AND anaerobes
D. Azithromycin — the only macrolide with anaerobic coverage
E. Doxycycline — adequate monotherapy for all dental infections
mockBank.explanation: Clindamycin has broad activity against: Gram-positive aerobic cocci (Streptococcus species — viridans streptococci, S. pyogenes, S. pneumoniae), anaerobes (Bacteroides, Prevotella, Fusobacterium, Peptostreptococcus — the dominant bacteria in odontogenic infections), and Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA). This dual coverage makes it effective as a single agent for dental infections in penicillin-allergic patients — replacing the amoxicillin + metronidazole combination. Metronidazole (A) has only anaerobic/microaerophilic activity — it does not cover aerobic streptococci (the key organisms in dental infections). Ciprofloxacin (B) covers Gram-negatives well but has poor anaerobic activity and poor streptococcal activity. Azithromycin (D) has limited anaerobic activity. Doxycycline (E) has high resistance rates in UK oral streptococci — not recommended as monotherapy.

mockBank.question: Mumps virus causes parotitis, orchitis, oophoritis, pancreatitis, and meningitis. Which ONE of the following BEST explains this broad tissue tropism?
options: A. Mumps virus uses different surface glycoproteins to bind different receptor types on each tissue

B. Mumps virus spreads haematogenously after initial respiratory tract infection, and its attachment protein (haemagglutinin-neuraminidase) binds ubiquitous sialic acid receptors found on many cell types
C. Mumps virus integrates into host chromosomes in glandular tissue, causing latent infection that reactivates in each organ
D. Mumps causes autoimmune destruction of multiple glands by molecular mimicry
E. Mumps virus is transmitted blood-borne, allowing direct infection of each organ
mockBank.explanation: Mumps virus (paramyxovirus, negative-sense ssRNA) initially infects the upper respiratory tract via inhalation of respiratory droplets. The virus then spreads viraemically to multiple organs. The haemagglutinin-neuraminidase (HN) surface protein binds sialic acid receptors, which are present on the surface of many cell types throughout the body — parotid and salivary gland epithelium, testicular cells (Sertoli and Leydig cells), ovarian cells, pancreatic acinar cells, and meningeal cells. Post-infectious orchitis occurs in ~25% of post-pubertal males and can cause testicular atrophy and subfertility. Mumps does not use different glycoproteins for different tissues (A). It does not establish latency (C). Autoimmune destruction (D) is not the mechanism. Mumps is respiratory droplet-transmitted (E).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly describes the process of antibody class switching (isotype switching) and what drives it?
options: A. Class switching is when a single B cell changes its antigen-binding site to recognise a different antigen

B. Class switching is the process by which a B cell changes the constant region of its antibody (from IgM to IgG, IgA, or IgE) in response to T cell help (CD40L-CD40 interaction) and specific cytokines, without changing antigen specificity
C. Class switching occurs only in the thymus and produces IgG from IgM-producing T cells
D. Class switching is driven exclusively by IL-2 from T regulatory cells
E. Class switching occurs automatically after 7 days — all antibody responses transition from IgM to IgG without cytokine signals
mockBank.explanation: Class switching (isotype switching) is a key adaptive immune mechanism. Initially activated B cells produce IgM (pentameric, good at complement activation). In germinal centres, T follicular helper cells (Tfh) interact with B cells via CD40L (on T cell) binding CD40 (on B cell) — this 'help' signal plus cytokines drives class switching: IL-4 → IgG1, IgE; IL-10 → IgG1, IgG3; TGF-β → IgA, IgG2; IFN-γ → IgG1, IgG3. Only the constant region (Fc) changes — the variable region (antigen-binding specificity) remains identical. This explains why memory IgG antibodies bind the same antigen as the original IgM response but with different effector functions. The antigen-binding site does NOT change (A). Switching occurs in secondary lymphoid organs (B cells, not T cells — C). IL-2 drives T cell proliferation (D). Cytokine signals are required (E).

mockBank.question: A dental nurse sustains a needlestick injury while resheathing a local anaesthetic needle after an inferior alveolar nerve block. Which ONE of the following represents the single most important preventive measure that would have prevented this injury?
options: A. Wearing double gloves during all injections
B. Never resheathing needles using the two-handed technique — use a one-handed scoop technique or a needle-resheathing device
C. Using sharper needles that penetrate tissue more easily and reduce force required
D. Administering the injection more quickly to reduce procedure time
E. Asking the patient to hold the needle guard while resheathing
mockBank.explanation: Resheathing needles is the single highest-risk activity for needlestick injuries in dental practice — bringing a sharp contaminated needle close to the fingers holding the sheath. The key preventive measure: NEVER use the two-handed resheathing technique. Instead: one-handed scoop technique (place sheath on flat surface, scoop with needle using one hand) or use safety-engineered needles/resheathing devices (clips, guards). Additional prevention: never pass sharps hand-to-hand — use a neutral zone/tray; dispose of used needles directly into a sharps bin. Double gloves (A) reduce but do not prevent needlestick injuries. Sharper needles (C) are irrelevant to resheathing injury. Speed (D) is irrelevant. The patient should never touch sharps (E) — both impractical and dangerous.

mockBank.question: In the UK, which ONE of the following groups currently has the HIGHEST incidence of new HIV diagnoses?
options: A. IV drug users sharing needles
B. Blood transfusion recipients
C. Men who have sex with men (MSM)
D. Healthcare workers after needlestick injuries
E. Heterosexual women in the 60–70 age group
mockBank.explanation: In the UK, men who have sex with men (MSM) continue to account for the largest proportion of new HIV diagnoses, although the number has declined significantly since PrEP (pre-exposure prophylaxis) became available through NHS England in 2020. Key UK epidemiology: MSM ~40% of new diagnoses; heterosexual transmission (predominantly acquired abroad) ~40%; PWID (people who inject drugs) ~2%. Blood transfusion recipients (B) are at negligible risk in the UK — all donations screened since 1985. Healthcare workers (D) have very low occupational risk (<0.3% per needlestick). Heterosexual women over 60 (E) are not a high-incidence group. Understanding epidemiology guides targeted testing — MSM should be tested at least annually if sexually active.

mockBank.question: A patient with an absolute neutrophil count of 0.3 × 10⁹/L (severe neutropenia) due to chemotherapy requires emergency extraction. Which ONE of the following best describes the modified management approach?
options: A. No modification required — local anaesthesia prevents systemic complications
B. Defer all extractions until neutrophil count recovers above 1.0 × 10⁹/L

C. Antibiotic prophylaxis (amoxicillin/clindamycin) before the procedure, meticulous haemostasis, and ideally coordinate with haematology/oncology — proceed only if urgent and after risk-benefit discussion
D. Use IV antibiotics (IV co-amoxiclav) for all extractions regardless of severity
E. Use topical antibiotic gel in the socket only — systemic antibiotics are contraindicated in neutropenia
mockBank.explanation: Neutropenic patients (ANC <0.5 × 10⁹/L = severe neutropenia) have severely impaired innate immune defence — dental procedures can cause bacteraemia that the patient cannot clear, leading to neutropenic sepsis (a haematological emergency with ~10% mortality). Management: (1) Coordinate with the haematology/oncology team — timing relative to chemotherapy cycle matters (ANC nadir is 10–14 days post-chemotherapy); (2) Antibiotic prophylaxis is recommended (amoxicillin 3g or clindamycin 600mg pre-procedure); (3) Meticulous haemostasis; (4) Monitor post-procedure for signs of infection. Deferral (B) is ideal but not always possible for acute infections. Blanket IV antibiotics (D) are not routinely indicated. Topical antibiotics alone (E) are inadequate.

mockBank.question: A patient on aciclovir for herpes zoster develops severe nausea, confusion, and reduced urine output. Which ONE of the following adverse effects of aciclovir is MOST likely responsible?
options: A. Hepatotoxicity — aciclovir commonly causes liver failure
B. Aciclovir crystallises in renal tubules causing nephrotoxicity (crystalline nephropathy), particularly when administered rapidly IV or without adequate hydration
C. Aciclovir causes bone marrow suppression with anaemia and neutropenia
D. Aciclovir interacts with all NSAIDs causing acute tubular necrosis
E. Aciclovir causes ototoxicity at therapeutic doses
mockBank.explanation: Aciclovir nephrotoxicity (crystalline nephropathy) is the most important adverse effect, particularly with IV aciclovir. Aciclovir has low aqueous solubility and precipitates as crystals in the renal tubules when urinary concentration is high — causing obstruction, tubular damage, and acute kidney injury. Risk factors: rapid IV infusion (give over at least 60 minutes), dehydration, pre-existing renal impairment, high doses (e.g., encephalitis dosing). Prevention: adequate hydration before and during IV aciclovir, slow infusion rate, dose adjustment in renal failure. Confusion and reduced urine output indicate acute kidney injury from crystalluria. Oral aciclovir at standard doses has much lower nephrotoxicity risk. Hepatotoxicity (A), myelosuppression (C), and ototoxicity (E) are not significant aciclovir effects. NSAID interaction (D) is not a specific aciclovir mechanism.

mockBank.question: Can Hepatitis C virus be transmitted via dental treatment? Which ONE of the following BEST describes the current evidence?
options: A. Yes — dental treatment is a high-risk route for HCV transmission, responsible for 40% of UK infections

B. Transmission is theoretically possible via contaminated instruments or needlestick injuries, but the risk with modern infection control is negligible — individual occupational risk per needlestick is approximately 0.5%
C. No — HCV cannot survive outside the human body and cannot be transmitted via dental instruments
D. Yes — HCV is transmitted via salivary contamination during routine examination
E. Transmission is only possible when the source patient has HCV RNA > 1,000,000 IU/mL
mockBank.explanation: HCV transmission in dental settings: historically, inadequate instrument sterilisation has been linked to HCV outbreaks in dental settings (especially in Eastern Europe and developing countries where sterilisation was poor). With modern infection control (autoclave sterilisation, single-use needles, universal precautions), the risk to patients from a dental procedure is negligible. Occupational risk: 0.5% per needlestick from an HCV-positive source (compared to 30% for HBV from HBeAg-positive source, 0.3% for HIV). HCV can survive on environmental surfaces for up to 16 hours at room temperature (C is wrong). HCV is blood-borne, not saliva-borne in routine contact (D). The risk is not eliminated at lower viral loads — even low-level viraemia can transmit (E).

mockBank.question: Trimethoprim is sometimes used for urinary tract infections. Which ONE of the following BEST describes its mechanism of action and why it is not a first-line dental antibiotic?
options: A. Trimethoprim inhibits bacterial RNA polymerase — it has poor activity against oral streptococci
B. Trimethoprim inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, blocking bacterial folate synthesis — it has limited activity against the anaerobes predominant in dental infections
C. Trimethoprim is a beta-lactam antibiotic with activity restricted to urinary tract pathogens
D. Trimethoprim inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis — oral streptococci are intrinsically resistant
E. Trimethoprim is nephrotoxic — it cannot be used in any oral dose
mockBank.explanation: Trimethoprim inhibits dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR) — the enzyme that converts dihydrofolate to tetrahydrofolate, an essential cofactor for DNA synthesis (nucleotide production). Bacteria cannot use exogenous folate (unlike humans who absorb dietary folate), so they are selectively affected. When combined with sulfamethoxazole (co-trimoxazole), it provides synergistic blockade of folate synthesis at two sequential steps. Dental relevance: trimethoprim has poor activity against oral anaerobes (key dental pathogens) and is not recommended for odontogenic infections. Its spectrum covers Gram-negative aerobes (E. coli, Proteus) — appropriate for UTIs but not dental infections. It does not inhibit RNA polymerase (A) — that is rifampicin. It is not a beta-lactam (C) and does not inhibit cell wall synthesis (D). It is not nephrotoxic at standard doses (E).

mockBank.question: A patient has undergone splenectomy following trauma. Which ONE of the following infections poses the GREATEST risk and why?
options: A. HIV — the spleen produces CD4+ T cells essential for HIV immunity

B. Encapsulated bacteria (Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, Neisseria meningitidis) — the spleen is the primary site for opsonisation and phagocytosis of encapsulated bacteria via IgM antibodies
C. Herpes simplex — the spleen produces interferon-alpha against herpesviruses
D. Candida albicans — the spleen contains all antifungal neutrophils
E. Mycobacterium tuberculosis — the spleen coordinates granuloma formation
mockBank.explanation: Post-splenectomy patients are at lifelong risk of overwhelming post-splenectomy infection (OPSI) — most commonly from encapsulated bacteria: Streptococcus pneumoniae (most common, most dangerous), Haemophilus influenzae type b, Neisseria meningitidis, and Capnocytophaga canimorsus (from dog bites). The spleen is crucial for: (1) Phagocytosis of opsonised bacteria by splenic macrophages (especially IgM-opsonised encapsulated bacteria in primary infection); (2) Production of IgM antibodies against T-independent polysaccharide antigens (on bacterial capsules); (3) Complement activation. Without a spleen, encapsulated bacteria cannot be efficiently cleared. Prevention: pneumococcal, meningococcal, and Hib vaccines; lifelong prophylactic penicillin V (or amoxicillin); patient education about seeking urgent care for fever. Dental relevance: inform the asplenic patient, ensure antibiotic awareness — they should have amoxicillin available.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following oral bacteria is correctly classified as Gram-negative, anaerobic, and strongly associated with aggressive periodontitis?
options: A. Streptococcus mutans — Gram-positive, facultative anaerobe, caries-associated
B. Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitans (Aa) — Gram-negative, capnophilic (CO₂-requiring), strongly associated with localised aggressive periodontitis
C. Staphylococcus aureus — Gram-positive, aerobic, associated with suppurative parotitis
D. Actinomyces israelii — Gram-positive, anaerobic, associated with cervicofacial actinomycosis
E. Treponema pallidum — Gram-negative, anaerobic, causes primary syphilis
mockBank.explanation: Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitans (Aa, formerly Actinobacillus) is a Gram-negative, non-motile, capnophilic (grows optimally in 5–10% CO₂) coccobacillus. It is the key pathogen in localised aggressive periodontitis (LAP), particularly in adolescents of African heritage. Virulence factors: leukotoxin (kills neutrophils and macrophages), cytolethal distending toxin, collagenase, epitheliotoxin. Aa is the defining organism in LAP, which presents as severe bone loss around first molars and incisors in young patients with minimal plaque. Treatment: scaling + root planing + systemic amoxicillin + metronidazole (the only combination shown to suppress Aa). Treponema pallidum (E) causes syphilis — while it is Gram-negative and anaerobic, it is not an oral pathogen associated with periodontitis. The other options are correctly described in the question.

mockBank.question: Approximately 1% of Northern Europeans carry a homozygous deletion in the CCR5 gene (CCR5Δ32). Which ONE of the following BEST describes the clinical significance of this mutation?
options: A. CCR5Δ32 homozygotes have no functional CCR5 and are almost completely resistant to HIV-1 infection via R5-tropic strains
B. CCR5Δ32 homozygotes are resistant to all HIV strains including CXCR4-tropic variants
C. CCR5Δ32 causes severe immunodeficiency as CCR5 is essential for normal macrophage function
D. CCR5Δ32 carriers are resistant to HIV but highly susceptible to dengue fever
E. CCR5Δ32 only provides partial resistance — homozygotes can still be infected by standard sexual transmission
mockBank.explanation: CCR5 is the co-receptor used by the predominant sexually transmitted HIV-1 R5 (macrophage-tropic) strains. CCR5Δ32 is a 32-base pair deletion that causes a frameshift and truncated, non-functional CCR5 protein that is retained in the endoplasmic reticulum. Homozygotes (two copies of Δ32) lack cell-surface CCR5 → R5-tropic HIV cannot enter cells → near-complete protection against HIV infection. This is the basis for the 'Berlin patient' cure (Timothy Ray Brown) — an HIV-positive patient received a bone marrow transplant from a CCR5Δ32 homozygous donor. Heterozygotes (one copy) have partial protection. CXCR4-tropic X4 strains can still infect CCR5Δ32 homozygotes (B is wrong — they can be infected by X4 strains, though this is less common sexually). CCR5Δ32 homozygotes are immunologically normal (C). The dengue association (D) is a myth.

mockBank.question: A patient with previous infective endocarditis is scheduled for an upper molar extraction. According to current NICE guidelines (NG178), which ONE of the following is correct?
options: A. Amoxicillin 3g one hour before the procedure is mandatory for patients with previous IE
B. Antibiotic prophylaxis is NOT recommended — optimal oral hygiene and regular dental review are more important
C. Clindamycin 600mg is indicated for all high-risk cardiac patients regardless of penicillin allergy status
D. IV teicoplanin is the only acceptable prophylaxis for patients with previous IE
E. Prophylaxis is required only if the INR is >2.5 at time of extraction
mockBank.explanation: NICE NG178 explicitly states that antibiotic prophylaxis against infective endocarditis is NOT recommended for any dental procedure, even for high-risk cardiac patients including: those with previous IE, prosthetic heart valves, complex congenital heart disease, or previous cardiac surgery. The rationale: (1) No RCT evidence that prophylaxis prevents IE; (2) Daily oral hygiene causes more cumulative bacteraemia than dental procedures; (3) Antibiotic resistance concerns outweigh unproven benefit. Focus: optimal oral health, regular dental review, patient education about oral hygiene, avoiding unnecessary dental infections. Note: some European countries still recommend prophylaxis — NICE NG178 is UK-specific guidance. This guideline change (from previous guidance recommending prophylaxis) is a high-yield examination point.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following is the MOST serious rare complication of measles infection occurring years after the initial infection?
options: A. Post-measles encephalitis occurring within 2 weeks of the rash
B. Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE) — a progressive fatal encephalitis caused by persistent measles virus in the CNS, occurring 7–10 years after primary infection
C. Measles-associated aplastic anaemia in under-fives
D. Reactivation of latent measles virus causing a varicella-like rash in adults
E. Measles-induced autoimmune thyroiditis developing 1–2 years post-infection
mockBank.explanation: Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE) is a devastating late complication of measles. A defective, persistently replicating measles virus establishes itself in CNS neurons and oligodendrocytes — remaining latent for 7–10 years before causing progressive neurodegeneration. SSPE presentation: behavioural changes → cognitive decline → myoclonic jerks → seizures → vegetative state → death (usually within 1–3 years). There is no effective treatment. Risk is higher in children who had measles before age 2. The MMR vaccine has dramatically reduced SSPE incidence. Post-measles acute encephalitis (A) does occur (1/1000 cases), within weeks of rash — distinct from SSPE. Aplastic anaemia (C) is not a recognised measles complication. Measles does not establish lifelong latency in ganglia like herpesvirus (D). Autoimmune thyroiditis (E) is associated with other viruses (Coxsackievirus) but not specifically measles.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the function of chemokines in infection?
options: A. Chemokines are antibodies that bind to bacterial surface proteins to direct phagocytes
B. Chemokines are small signalling proteins that create concentration gradients directing the migration of immune cells (neutrophils, monocytes, lymphocytes) to sites of infection
C. Chemokines directly kill bacteria by inserting into bacterial cell membranes
D. Chemokines are produced exclusively by mast cells during allergic reactions
E. Chemokines activate complement via the alternative pathway
mockBank.explanation: Chemokines (chemoattractant cytokines) are small (8–12 kDa) secreted proteins that create chemotactic gradients — directing immune cell migration. They bind G-protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) on leukocytes. During infection: macrophages at the infection site produce chemokines (IL-8/CXCL8 — attracts neutrophils; MCP-1/CCL2 — attracts monocytes/macrophages; RANTES/CCL5 — attracts T cells and NK cells). These chemokines create a concentration gradient from high (infection site) to low (blood vessel), attracting immune cells directionally through a process called chemotaxis. HIV exploits chemokine receptors (CCR5, CXCR4) as co-receptors for cell entry. Chemokines are not antibodies (A), membrane-lytic (C), produced exclusively by mast cells (D), or complement activators (E).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes lipopolysaccharide (LPS) and its clinical significance in Gram-negative infections?
options: A. LPS is a Gram-positive cell wall component that activates complement via the classical pathway

B. LPS is the lipid A component of the Gram-negative outer membrane that is recognised by TLR4 on macrophages, triggering massive cytokine release — responsible for the pathophysiology of Gram-negative septic shock
C. LPS is an exotoxin secreted by Gram-negative bacteria that directly kills host cells
D. LPS activates B cells directly to produce IgM antibodies without T cell help
E. LPS is a Gram-negative virulence factor that inhibits complement C3 cleavage
mockBank.explanation: Lipopolysaccharide (LPS/endotoxin) is the major structural component of the Gram-negative outer membrane. It consists of: lipid A (the bioactive toxic moiety) embedded in the outer membrane + core polysaccharide + O-antigen (variable outer polysaccharide). When Gram-negative bacteria lyse (from antibiotics or immune killing), LPS is released and binds TLR4 (in complex with MD-2 and CD14) on macrophages and dendritic cells. This triggers massive NF-κB activation → TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6, IL-12 release → systemic inflammatory response. Excessive LPS in septicaemia = septic shock: vasodilation, capillary leak, multi-organ failure. LPS is a Gram-NEGATIVE component (A). LPS is an endotoxin (part of the cell wall, not secreted) — not an exotoxin (C). LPS does activate B cells as a T-independent antigen (D is partially correct — LPS is a TI-2 antigen, not TI-1, and does not need T cells for IgM production in this context, but the primary significance is macrophage activation). LPS activates complement, not inhibits C3 (E).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes when combining two antibiotics is clinically justified in dental practice?
options: A. Always — combination therapy is always superior to monotherapy for dental infections
B. Never — combination antibiotic therapy increases resistance without clinical benefit

C. When infection involves both aerobic and anaerobic organisms (e.g., amoxicillin + metronidazole), or when treating severe spreading infections, or when a high-risk patient's infection fails to respond to monotherapy
D. Only when the patient requests it — combination therapy is entirely patient preference
E. For any dental extraction in a patient over 60 — older patients always require combination therapy
mockBank.explanation: Combination antibiotic therapy in dentistry is justified in specific clinical scenarios: (1) Polymicrobial infections: dental abscesses involve both aerobic streptococci and anaerobes — amoxicillin covers streptococci, metronidazole covers anaerobes; their combination is synergistic and covers the full spectrum; (2) Failure to respond: if adequate monotherapy has not controlled a spreading infection within 48–72 hours; (3) Severe or immunocompromised: host factors that increase the risk of clinical failure with monotherapy. Combination therapy is NOT always superior (A) — for simple infections, targeted monotherapy avoids unnecessary antibiotic exposure and resistance pressure. It is NOT never justified (B). Patient preference (D) is not an indication. Age alone (E) is not a criterion — clinical presentation determines the need.

mockBank.question: A 55-year-old HIV-positive man with CD4 count of 45 cells/μL presents with purple nodular lesions on the hard palate. Biopsy shows a vascular spindle cell tumour. Which ONE of the following describes the MOST appropriate initial management?
options: A. Topical corticosteroids — the palatal lesions are likely lichen planus
B. Initiation/optimisation of antiretroviral therapy to reconstitute CD4 count, referral to oncology for assessment of disease extent — antiretrovirals can cause tumour regression
C. Immediate surgical excision of all visible lesions under general anaesthesia
D. Intralesional bleomycin only — systemic therapy is not needed for palatal KS
E. Radiotherapy to the hard palate with concurrent co-trimoxazole prophylaxis
mockBank.explanation: Purple nodular palatal lesions in an AIDS patient (CD4 45) = oral Kaposi's sarcoma (caused by HHV-8).
