Management: (1) Optimise/initiate HAART — immune reconstitution is the cornerstone; CD4 count improvement reduces HHV-8 replication and often causes tumour regression; (2) Staging (CT chest/abdomen — assess visceral KS extent); (3) For localised oral KS: intralesional vinblastine, local radiotherapy, or cryotherapy; (4) For systemic/extensive KS: liposomal doxorubicin (systemic chemotherapy). Corticosteroids (A) are contraindicated — they suppress immunity and may accelerate KS. Surgical excision (C) is not the primary approach — multifocal disease requires systemic treatment. Intralesional bleomycin (D) alone without antiretroviral optimisation misses the fundamental treatment. Radiotherapy alone (E) without HAART is suboptimal.

mockBank.question: A 35-year-old man acquired HBV perinatally (vertically from his mother). He is now HBsAg positive. Which ONE of the following BEST describes his likely clinical course compared to an adult who acquires HBV?
options: A. Perinatal HBV acquisition is less likely to cause chronic infection than adult acquisition
B. Perinatal HBV acquisition leads to chronic infection in >90% of cases (vs. ~5% in immunocompetent adults) because neonatal immune systems are immature and establish immunological tolerance to HBV
C. The clinical course is identical — route of acquisition does not affect chronicity risk
D. Perinatal HBV causes more severe acute illness but lower chronicity rates
E. Adults who acquire HBV have a 50% risk of chronicity — similar to perinatal acquisition
mockBank.explanation: The risk of chronic HBV infection is strongly age-dependent at time of infection: Perinatal: >90% chronicity; <5 years: 25–50%; Immunocompetent adult: 5–10%. This dramatic difference reflects neonatal immune immaturity — the developing immune system encounters HBV antigens during a critical window of immune tolerance induction, establishing HBV-specific immune tolerance rather than clearance. This is why HBIG + HBV vaccine within 12 hours of birth is crucial for babies of HBsAg-positive mothers — preventing infection before this tolerance window. This patient, having acquired HBV perinatally with >90% chronicity risk, has had the virus for 35 years and faces significant risk of cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma (surveillance is essential). Adult acquisition (immunocompetent) has 5–10% chronicity risk, not 50% (E).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following antibiotics requires therapeutic drug monitoring (measuring blood plasma levels) to ensure safety and efficacy during treatment?
options: A. Amoxicillin — requires weekly monitoring of plasma levels
B. Metronidazole — requires daily plasma level monitoring in all patients
C. Gentamicin (aminoglycoside) — requires peak and trough level monitoring due to narrow therapeutic index and risk of nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity
D. Clindamycin — requires hepatic function tests every 48 hours
E. Clarithromycin — requires cardiac monitoring for QT prolongation in all patients
mockBank.explanation: Gentamicin (and other aminoglycosides — tobramycin, amikacin) have a narrow therapeutic index — the therapeutic concentration range is close to the toxic range. Peak levels correlate with efficacy (bactericidal); trough levels correlate with toxicity (nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity). Therefore: peak levels (measured 1 hour after IV dose) must be high enough for efficacy; trough levels (measured just before next dose) must be low enough to allow nephrotoxic metabolite clearance. Extended interval dosing (once-daily high-dose gentamicin) uses different monitoring parameters (Hartford nomogram or similar). Amoxicillin (A) has a wide therapeutic index — no monitoring required. Metronidazole (B) does not require routine plasma monitoring. Clindamycin (D) does not require hepatic function monitoring routinely. Clarithromycin (E) requires caution with QT-prolonging drugs but not cardiac monitoring for all patients.

mockBank.question: Dendritic cells are described as 'professional antigen presenting cells'. Which ONE of the following BEST describes why they are the MOST efficient activators of naïve T cells?
options: A. Dendritic cells produce more IgG than other antigen-presenting cells
B. Dendritic cells are the only cells in the body that express MHC Class II molecules
C. Dendritic cells express high levels of MHC Class I and Class II, co-stimulatory molecules (CD80/CD86), and cytokines — providing all three signals needed to activate naïve T cells
D. Dendritic cells can directly kill bacteria without T cell activation
E. Dendritic cells migrate to the bone marrow to activate B cells rather than T cells
mockBank.explanation: Naïve T cell activation requires three signals: (1) Signal 1: antigen-specific — TCR binds peptide-MHC complex on APC; (2) Signal 2: co-stimulatory — CD28 on T cell binds CD80/CD86 (B7 molecules) on APC (without this signal, T cell becomes anergic/tolerised); (3) Signal 3: cytokines — IL-12 drives Th1; IL-4 drives Th2; TGF-β + IL-6 drives Th17. Dendritic cells are uniquely positioned as the only APCs that constitutively express high levels of all three signals. In peripheral tissues, immature DCs sample antigens (phagocytosis, macropinocytosis, pattern recognition via TLRs). After antigen capture, DCs mature, upregulate MHC, CD80/CD86, and CCR7 (lymph node homing receptor), and migrate to draining lymph nodes to activate naïve T cells. B cells and macrophages also express MHC Class II (C is right that DCs are most efficient, but B cells and macrophages also express MHC II — B is wrong).

mockBank.question: A 24-year-old patient presents with an acute pharyngitis that does not respond to a standard course of penicillin. Throat swab identifies Neisseria gonorrhoeae. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate management?
options: A. Extend the penicillin course to 14 days — gonorrhoea requires prolonged beta-lactam therapy
B. Ceftriaxone 1g IM single dose (or 500mg if weight <150kg) — dual therapy guidance from UK STI guidelines, plus test-of-cure at 2 weeks
C. Oral doxycycline 100mg twice daily for 14 days
D. Metronidazole 400mg three times daily for 5 days
E. Refer to GUM clinic for diagnosis only — treatment is not within the dental scope
mockBank.explanation: Pharyngeal gonorrhoea can present to dentists as a pharyngitis not responding to standard antibiotics. Gonorrhoea is a notifiable disease. Penicillin resistance is now widespread in N. gonorrhoeae (beta-lactamase-producing strains, chromosomal resistance) — penicillin is no longer effective. UK BASHH guidelines: ceftriaxone 1g IM single dose for all gonococcal infections including pharyngeal (1g used for pharyngeal sites as harder to eradicate than genital). Test-of-cure at 2 weeks is recommended for pharyngeal gonorrhoea (because treatment success is lower at this site). Partner notification is essential. Doxycycline (C) is used for chlamydia, not as primary gonorrhoea treatment. Metronidazole (D) has no activity against N. gonorrhoeae. Referral to GUM (E) is appropriate but the urgent diagnosis should be made and patient signposted immediately.

mockBank.question: A liver transplant recipient on ciclosporin and prednisolone is CMV seronegative (R-) but received a liver from a CMV seropositive donor (D+). Which ONE of the following describes the MOST appropriate CMV management strategy?
options: A. No prophylaxis required — CMV is mild in transplant recipients
B. Valganciclovir (oral prodrug of ganciclovir) prophylaxis for 3–6 months post-transplant — the highest risk group (D+/R-) requires the longest prophylaxis duration
C. Aciclovir at standard dose — effective against CMV as well as herpesviruses
D. CMV-specific immunoglobulin only — antivirals are too toxic in transplant recipients
E. Monitor CMV viral load monthly — treat only if symptomatic disease develops
mockBank.explanation: CMV management in solid organ transplant is risk-stratified by donor/recipient serostatus: D+/R- (highest risk): donor-derived primary CMV infection in a seronegative recipient — risk of serious CMV disease is highest (no pre-existing immunity). D+/R+ or D-/R+: reactivation risk — moderate. D-/R- (lowest risk). High-risk patients receive antiviral prophylaxis: valganciclovir 900mg daily for 3–6 months (oral bioavailability is excellent — converted to ganciclovir in the intestinal wall and liver). CMV disease in transplant recipients can be severe: pneumonitis, colitis, retinitis, encephalitis, and allograft rejection. Aciclovir (C) has poor anti-CMV activity — ganciclovir/valganciclovir are required (CMV lacks thymidine kinase; aciclovir cannot be activated). Immunoglobulin alone (D) is insufficient. Pre-emptive therapy (E — treat when viral load rises above a threshold, before symptoms) is an alternative strategy but not for the highest-risk D+/R- combination.

mockBank.question: When administering local anaesthesia to a patient with severe liver disease (Child-Pugh Class C), which ONE of the following is the MOST important consideration?
options: A. Avoid articaine — it is hepatotoxic in liver disease
B. Lidocaine is metabolised by the liver — accumulation can occur in severe liver disease; use the minimum effective dose and avoid repeat injections
C. Prilocaine is the safest local anaesthetic in liver disease as it is renally excreted
D. Adrenaline-containing cartridges are absolutely contraindicated in all liver disease
E. Maximum dose calculations do not apply in liver disease — titrate only to pain response
mockBank.explanation: Lidocaine (and most amide local anaesthetics — articaine, mepivacaine, bupivacaine) are hepatically metabolised by CYP enzymes. In severe liver disease, reduced first-pass metabolism and reduced plasma protein binding (due to low albumin) can lead to accumulation of free lidocaine → systemic local anaesthetic toxicity (CNS: seizures; cardiovascular: arrhythmias). Management: use the minimum effective volume, avoid repeat injections, consider the cumulative dose over the appointment. Prilocaine (C) is also hepatically metabolised — it is not safer than lidocaine in liver disease. Adrenaline (D) is not absolutely contraindicated in liver disease (it is contraindicated in severe cardiovascular disease, not liver disease per se — though caution is warranted if the patient has portal hypertension or varices). Dose limits always apply (E).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following pharmacokinetic parameters MOST determines the duration of antibiotic action rather than its peak concentration effect?
options: A. Maximum plasma concentration (Cmax) — determines bactericidal activity for all antibiotics
B. Area under the concentration-time curve (AUC) — relevant for concentration-dependent antibiotics
C. Half-life (t½) — determines the dosing interval needed to maintain effective concentrations above MIC
D. Volume of distribution (Vd) — determines the total amount of antibiotic in the body
E. Protein binding — all highly protein-bound antibiotics act for shorter periods
mockBank.explanation: Antibiotic pharmacodynamics: (1) Time-dependent killing (beta-lactams, macrolides): efficacy depends on the time plasma concentration remains above the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) — half-life determines how long the drug stays above MIC, dictating dosing interval. Beta-lactams must be given regularly (TDS, QDS) to maintain concentrations above MIC for >40–50% of the dosing interval. (2) Concentration-dependent killing (aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones): efficacy depends on Cmax/MIC ratio — higher peaks kill more efficiently, allowing once-daily dosing. (3) AUC-dependent (vancomycin, azithromycin): efficacy depends on AUC/MIC ratio. Half-life (C) is the key parameter for time-dependent antibiotics — it determines the minimum dosing frequency to maintain therapeutic concentrations. Cmax (A) is most relevant for concentration-dependent antibiotics. AUC (B) is relevant for specific drugs. Volume of distribution (D) affects total amount. Protein binding (E) affects free drug fraction but is not the primary determinant of duration.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes how poliovirus causes paralytic poliomyelitis despite infecting millions of people, the vast majority of whom recover without neurological complications?
options: A. All poliovirus infections cause paralysis — the paralysis is transient in most patients

B. Poliovirus enters via the faecal-oral route, replicates in the GI tract and lymphoid tissue, and in ~1% of cases accesses the bloodstream and invades anterior horn motor neurons — causing irreversible motor neuron destruction and flaccid paralysis
C. Poliovirus causes paralysis by triggering autoimmune destruction of myelin in the spinal cord
D. Paralytic polio occurs only in malnourished individuals — nutritional status determines outcome
E. Poliovirus causes paralysis by producing a neurotoxin that blocks acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction
mockBank.explanation: Poliovirus (enterovirus, positive-sense ssRNA) pathogenesis: (1) Faecal-oral transmission; (2) Primary replication in the oropharynx and GI tract lymphoid tissue (Peyer's patches); (3) Minor viraemia → spread to other lymphoid organs; (4) In 1% of cases: major viraemia → CNS invasion, preferentially infecting anterior horn cells (motor neurons) of the spinal cord and brainstem via the bloodstream or retrograde axonal transport; (5) Lytic viral replication → irreversible motor neuron destruction → flaccid (lower motor neuron) paralysis — asymmetric, typically affecting one limb. Only ~1% develop paralysis; 90% have subclinical or mild 'minor illness' (fever, sore throat); 1–5% develop aseptic meningitis. The irreversibility is due to inability of destroyed motor neurons to regenerate. Autoimmune demyelination (C) describes multiple sclerosis. Neurotoxin (E) describes botulinum toxin or tetanus toxin mechanisms.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the function of regulatory T cells (Tregs) in immune homeostasis?
options: A. Tregs produce large amounts of IgG antibodies to neutralise circulating pathogens

B. Tregs (Foxp3+ CD4+ CD25+ T cells) suppress excessive immune responses and maintain self-tolerance by producing IL-10 and TGF-β and by direct cell-to-cell inhibitory interactions with effector T cells
C. Tregs are activated by IL-12 and drive Th1 responses against intracellular pathogens
D. Tregs are the primary cell type responsible for antibody class switching in germinal centres
E. Tregs produce perforin and granzymes to directly kill autoreactive B cells
mockBank.explanation: Regulatory T cells (Tregs) are defined by expression of the Foxp3 transcription factor, and co-expression of CD4, CD25 (IL-2 receptor), and CTLA-4. They are the master regulators of peripheral tolerance, preventing autoimmunity and excessive inflammation. Mechanisms of suppression: (1) Cytokine-mediated — secretion of IL-10 (anti-inflammatory), TGF-β (suppressive), and IL-35; (2) Direct cell contact — CTLA-4 on Tregs competes with CD28 on effector T cells for CD80/CD86 binding on APCs, reducing co-stimulation; (3) Cytokine deprivation — consumption of IL-2 (essential for effector T cell survival). Treg deficiency or dysfunction → autoimmunity (IPEX syndrome from FOXP3 mutations). Tregs do not produce IgG (A), drive Th1 responses (C), mediate class switching (D), or use perforin/granzymes (E).

mockBank.question: A patient presents with a 3-day history of severe sore throat, fever of 39°C, tonsillar exudate, anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, and no cough. The Centor score is 4. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate management?
options: A. Prescribe amoxicillin 500mg TDS for 10 days immediately
B. Send a throat swab for culture, await results, and prescribe antibiotics only if Streptococcus pyogenes is confirmed
C. Perform a rapid antigen test for Group A Streptococcus or take a throat swab; prescribe phenoxymethylpenicillin (penicillin V) 500mg QDS for 10 days if confirmed
D. Prescribe amoxicillin 500mg TDS immediately — EBV monospot is not needed
E. Prescribe prednisolone 40mg once daily to reduce tonsillar inflammation
mockBank.explanation: A Centor score of 4 (fever >38°C, tonsillar exudate, anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, no cough) gives a 50–60% probability of Group A Streptococcus (GAS = S. pyogenes). UK NICE guidance (NG84): for Centor 3–4, offer rapid antigen test (if available) or throat swab + phenoxymethylpenicillin if positive. CRITICAL: never prescribe amoxicillin/ampicillin empirically for tonsillitis before EBV is excluded — in EBV infectious mononucleosis (which can present identically), amoxicillin causes a characteristic widespread maculopapular rash in 80–90% of cases. Phenoxymethylpenicillin (penicillin V — narrow spectrum) is the preferred antibiotic for confirmed GAS pharyngitis. Awaiting culture (B) delays treatment by 48 hours. Prednisolone (E) may reduce tonsillar oedema in severe cases but is not first-line — and is inappropriate without excluding infection.

mockBank.question: A patient on fluconazole for oropophageal candidiasis is also taking phenytoin for epilepsy. Which ONE of the following is the MOST important interaction to be aware of?
options: A. Phenytoin accelerates fluconazole metabolism via CYP induction — fluconazole dose may need increasing
B. Fluconazole inhibits phenytoin metabolism (CYP2C9), causing phenytoin accumulation and toxicity (nystagmus, ataxia, diplopia)
C. The combination is safe — there are no significant interactions between azoles and anticonvulsants
D. Fluconazole is contraindicated with all anticonvulsants — use nystatin instead
E. Phenytoin inhibits fluconazole absorption — give the drugs 4 hours apart
mockBank.explanation: Fluconazole inhibits CYP2C9 and CYP3A4. Phenytoin is metabolised by CYP2C9 — inhibition of this enzyme causes phenytoin accumulation. Phenytoin has a narrow therapeutic index — small increases in plasma levels cause toxicity: nystagmus (most sensitive sign), ataxia, diplopia, slurred speech, drowsiness, and at very high levels, paradoxical seizure worsening and cardiac arrhythmias. Management: close monitoring of phenytoin levels when starting or stopping fluconazole, consider phenytoin dose reduction, warn the patient about signs of toxicity. Phenytoin IS a CYP inducer (A is partially correct about phenytoin inducing some enzymes) but in this case the dominant effect is fluconazole inhibiting phenytoin clearance. The interaction is significant (C is wrong). Fluconazole is not absolutely contraindicated with all anticonvulsants (D). The interaction is metabolic, not absorption-based (E).

mockBank.question: A dental surgeon is found to be HBsAg positive and HBeAg positive following routine occupational health testing. Which ONE of the following BEST describes the appropriate course of action in the UK?
options: A. The surgeon can continue all clinical work including exposure-prone procedures with no restrictions

B. The surgeon must immediately cease all exposure-prone procedures (EPPs) and be referred to the Occupational Health team and a specialist hepatologist — EPPs can only resume after antiviral treatment achieves HBV DNA <200 IU/mL
C. The surgeon must permanently retire from dental practice
D. The surgeon should wear double gloves for all procedures — no further action is required
E. The surgeon is allowed to continue EPPs if the patient gives informed consent
mockBank.explanation: UK guidance (UKHSA/PHE, 2022): healthcare workers who are HBsAg positive and HBeAg positive (high infectivity) MUST NOT perform exposure-prone procedures (EPPs — procedures where the worker's hands are inside a body cavity where they may be injured by sharp instruments, teeth, or bone, and where HBV could be transmitted to the patient). This applies to all dental surgical procedures. The surgeon: must be referred to occupational health and hepatology; must commence antiviral therapy (tenofovir or entecavir); may return to EPPs only when HBV DNA is proven to be <200 IU/mL on consecutive measurements. Permanent retirement (C) is not required — antiviral therapy can suppress viral load sufficiently. Double gloves (D) reduce but do not eliminate transmission risk to patients. Patient consent (E) does not override the surgeon's legal and professional obligation to protect patients.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly matches an immunoglobulin class with its primary function?
options: A. IgM — the main antibody in secondary immune responses, crosses the placenta
B. IgE — mediates allergic responses and helminth immunity by sensitising mast cells and basophils
C. IgA — the primary opsonin for phagocytosis in blood
D. IgG — the first antibody produced in a primary immune response
E. IgD — secreted in tears and saliva to prevent mucosal infection
mockBank.explanation: IgE functions: binds to high-affinity FcεRI receptors on mast cells and basophils — when allergen cross-links IgE → degranulation → histamine, prostaglandins, leukotrienes → allergic symptoms. IgE also mediates immunity to helminths (worms) via eosinophil activation. IgM (A) is the FIRST antibody produced in primary responses (IgG dominates secondary responses); IgG crosses the placenta (not IgM). IgA (C) is the mucosal antibody (secretory IgA in tears/saliva); IgG is the primary opsonin in blood. IgG (D) is the main secondary response antibody; IgM is produced first. IgD (E) is expressed on naive B cell surfaces as a co-receptor alongside IgM — it is not secreted in tears/saliva (that is secretory IgA).

mockBank.question: A patient on rifampicin for tuberculosis treatment also takes the combined oral contraceptive pill (COCP). Which ONE of the following is the MOST important counselling point?
options: A. Rifampicin has no interaction with oral contraceptives — the COCP is safe to continue

B. Rifampicin is a potent CYP inducer — it accelerates oestrogen and progestogen metabolism, rendering the COCP unreliable; additional contraception (barrier methods) must be used during treatment and for 4 weeks after
C. Rifampicin causes breakthrough bleeding only — the contraceptive effect is maintained
D. The COCP should be replaced with an intrauterine device for all patients on rifampicin
E. Doubling the COCP dose overcomes the rifampicin interaction
mockBank.explanation: Rifampicin is one of the most potent CYP3A4 inducers in clinical use. It dramatically accelerates hepatic metabolism of oestrogen and progestogen in the COCP, reducing plasma hormone levels by up to 70%. This can lead to contraceptive failure — unexpected pregnancy — even if pills are taken correctly. This is a high-yield drug interaction. Management: use additional barrier contraception throughout rifampicin treatment and for at least 4 weeks after the last dose. Long-term rifampicin users (TB, leprosy) may prefer a non-hormonally metabolised method (copper IUD). Rifampicin also reduces the efficacy of: warfarin, ciclosporin, antiretrovirals (critical in HIV-TB co-infection), digoxin, phenytoin, methadone. This enzyme induction accounts for an important class of drug-drug interactions relevant to dental prescribers.

mockBank.question: SARS-CoV-2 (the COVID-19 virus) is a coronavirus. Which ONE of the following BEST describes its key biological characteristics relevant to its transmission and pathogenicity?
options: A. SARS-CoV-2 is a double-stranded DNA virus transmitted by the faecal-oral route
B. SARS-CoV-2 is a positive-sense single-stranded RNA coronavirus with a spike (S) protein that binds ACE2 receptors on respiratory epithelium, transmitted by respiratory droplets and aerosols
C. SARS-CoV-2 establishes lifelong latency in respiratory epithelial cells similar to HSV in neurons
D. SARS-CoV-2 primarily infects CD4+ T cells, causing immunodeficiency similar to HIV
E. SARS-CoV-2 is a non-enveloped virus resistant to standard alcohol hand rubs
mockBank.explanation: SARS-CoV-2 biological characteristics: positive-sense ssRNA genome (~30kb, the largest RNA virus genome); enveloped (lipid bilayer — inactivated by alcohol, detergents); spike (S) protein binds angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2) receptors — highly expressed in respiratory epithelium, type II pneumocytes, GI tract, oral mucosa (dentally relevant); transmitted primarily via respiratory droplets and aerosols (short and long range); non-sterilising immunity (reinfection possible); high mutation rate (variants of concern: Alpha, Delta, Omicron). Dental relevance: aerosol-generating dental procedures significantly increase transmission risk — led to enhanced PPE requirements during pandemic. Not DNA (A). Does not establish latency (C). Primarily infects respiratory cells, not CD4+ T cells (D) — though some lymphocytopaenia occurs in severe COVID. SARS-CoV-2 IS enveloped — it IS sensitive to alcohol hand rubs (E is wrong).

mockBank.question: A previously healthy 28-year-old presents with 2 weeks of jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, and anorexia. ALT is 2400 U/L (normal <40), bilirubin 145 μmol/L. HBsAg is positive, anti-HBc IgM is positive. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate initial management?
options: A. Immediate antiviral therapy with entecavir to clear the virus
B. Supportive management — most acute HBV infections in immunocompetent adults resolve spontaneously; monitor for signs of acute liver failure
C. Prescribe prednisolone to suppress the immune-mediated liver injury
D. Refer immediately for liver transplantation — ALT of 2400 indicates irreversible hepatocyte death
E. Prescribe metronidazole and amoxicillin to treat any superinfection
mockBank.explanation: Acute HBV infection (HBsAg+, anti-HBc IgM+) in immunocompetent adults is managed supportively in most cases — 95% spontaneously clear the virus within 6 months with appropriate immune response. Supportive care: rest, adequate hydration, avoid alcohol and hepatotoxic drugs, monitor for acute liver failure (INR >1.5, encephalopathy, ALT not improving). Antiviral therapy (A) is considered only for: severe acute hepatitis (INR >1.5, encephalopathy, or ALT > 10× ULN in older/immunocompromised patients at risk of fulminant disease). ALT of 2400 (60× ULN) is common in acute HBV but alone is not an indication for antivirals without other markers of acute liver failure. Corticosteroids (C) are harmful in viral hepatitis — they suppress immunity needed to clear the virus. ALT of 2400 does not mean irreversible liver failure (D) — fulminant hepatic failure is defined by encephalopathy + coagulopathy. Antibiotics (E) are not indicated.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST differentiates recurrent herpes labialis (cold sores) from recurrent aphthous stomatitis (mouth ulcers) to guide correct management?
options: A. Herpes labialis occurs on the attached gingiva and hard palate; aphthous ulcers occur on the vermilion border

B. Herpes labialis begins as grouped vesicles on the vermilion border/perioral skin (keratinised epithelium), typically preceded by a prodrome; aphthous ulcers occur on non-keratinised moveable mucosa (buccal/labial mucosa, soft palate, ventral tongue), never on skin
C. Herpes labialis is bacterial; aphthous ulcers are viral — antivirals work for aphthous ulcers
D. Both conditions exclusively affect immunocompromised patients
E. Aphthous ulcers respond to aciclovir; herpes labialis does not respond to any treatment
mockBank.explanation: This distinction is critical for correct diagnosis and treatment. Recurrent herpes labialis (HSV-1 reactivation): affects the vermilion border (lip-skin junction) and perioral skin — keratinised epithelium with tight attachment to underlying tissue. Prodrome: burning/tingling/itching for 24–48 hours before vesicles appear. Lesions: groups of small vesicles → ulcerate → crust → heal in 7–10 days. Treatment: topical aciclovir at first prodrome, or oral antivirals for frequent/severe cases. Recurrent aphthous stomatitis: occurs on non-keratinised, moveable oral mucosa only (buccal mucosa, labial mucosa, soft palate, floor of mouth, ventral tongue) — NEVER on vermilion border, gingiva, or hard palate. No viral aetiology. Treatment: topical corticosteroids (not antivirals). The anatomical site distinction (keratinised vs non-keratinised) is the key differentiator.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following best describes 'empirical' antibiotic therapy as opposed to 'definitive' antibiotic therapy?
options: A. Empirical therapy is based on culture results; definitive therapy is prescribed before culture results return

B. Empirical therapy is prescribed based on the most likely causative organisms and local resistance patterns before culture results are available; definitive therapy is adjusted based on confirmed organism identity and sensitivity results
C. Empirical therapy always uses broad-spectrum antibiotics; definitive therapy uses narrow-spectrum
D. Empirical therapy is used only in immunocompromised patients; definitive therapy is for all others
E. Empirical and definitive therapy are identical — the distinction is only academic
mockBank.explanation: Empirical therapy (or empiric therapy) is the initial antibiotic treatment prescribed based on clinical presentation, known microbiology of the most likely organisms, and local antibiogram data — before microbiological culture results are available. For dental infections: empirical choice = amoxicillin (covers viridans streptococci) ± metronidazole (covers anaerobes) because these are the known polymicrobial organisms in odontogenic infections. Definitive therapy: once culture and sensitivity results return (48–72 hours), therapy can be narrowed to target the specific confirmed organism — this is antibiotic de-escalation, a key stewardship principle. A is backwards (B corrects this). Empirical therapy is not always broad-spectrum — in dental infections, the empirical choice is often relatively narrow. Empirical therapy applies to all patient groups (D). The distinction is clinically important for stewardship (E).

mockBank.question: A patient on HAART for 3 years develops rising viral load despite good adherence. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate next investigation?
options: A. CD4 count only — if CD4 is stable, no action is needed
B. HIV resistance genotyping — identifies specific mutations in viral reverse transcriptase, protease, and integrase that confer resistance to specific antiretrovirals
C. HBV viral load — rising HIV viral load may indicate HBV co-infection
D. LFTs only — rising viral load is caused by hepatotoxicity of HAART
E. Repeat HIV antibody test — rising viral load may indicate re-infection with a different HIV strain
mockBank.explanation: Virological failure on HAART (HIV RNA rising above 200 copies/mL on two consecutive measurements despite good adherence) requires HIV genotypic resistance testing. Resistance genotyping: viral RNA is sequenced and analysed for mutations known to confer reduced susceptibility to specific antiretrovirals. Common resistance mutations: M184V/I (lamivudine/emtricitabine resistance), K65R (tenofovir), K103N (efavirenz/nevirapine), M46I/I50V (protease inhibitors). The results guide the virologist and HIV physician to design a new rescue regimen using drugs to which the virus remains susceptible. CD4 alone (A) may not detect early failure. HBV co-infection (C) is possible but does not directly cause HIV viral load rise. LFTs (D) detect hepatotoxicity, not resistance. HIV re-infection (E) is possible but the primary investigation is resistance testing — and re-infection would typically still be detected by existing tests.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following antibiotics is BACTERICIDAL (kills bacteria) rather than bacteriostatic (inhibits growth)?
options: A. Tetracyclines — bactericidal via ribosomal inhibition
B. Macrolides — bactericidal at high concentrations
C. Clindamycin — bactericidal against all oral pathogens
D. Amoxicillin — bactericidal by irreversibly binding PBPs and causing cell lysis
E. Trimethoprim — bactericidal via folate pathway inhibition
mockBank.explanation: Bactericidal vs bacteriostatic: Beta-lactams (amoxicillin, flucloxacillin, co-amoxiclav) are bactericidal — they covalently bind PBPs, preventing cell wall cross-linking. The resulting weak cell wall cannot withstand osmotic pressure → cell lysis and death. Aminoglycosides and fluoroquinolones are also bactericidal. Bacteriostatic antibiotics: tetracyclines (A), macrolides (B — erythromycin, clarithromycin), clindamycin (C), trimethoprim (E) — they halt bacterial replication but the immune system must clear the remaining bacteria. This distinction matters in: immunocompromised patients (may need bactericidal drugs as the immune system cannot clear static bacteria); infective endocarditis (bactericidal drugs required — endocarditis organisms are protected within vegetations where static drugs fail to clear the infection).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following CORRECTLY describes the three pathways of complement activation and their initiating triggers?
options: A. Classical: activated by IgM or IgG antibody-antigen complexes; Lectin: activated by mannose-binding lectin on bacterial surfaces; Alternative: activated spontaneously on foreign surfaces
B. Classical: activated by IgE antibodies; Lectin: activated by IgA at mucosal surfaces; Alternative: activated by LPS only
C. All three pathways are initiated by C1q binding to bacterial cell walls
D. Classical: triggered by IgA; Alternative: triggered by IgG; Lectin: triggered by carbohydrate structures on host cells
E. The classical pathway is the most important for innate immunity; the alternative pathway is the most important in adaptive immunity
mockBank.explanation: The three complement activation pathways all converge at C3 cleavage but have different initiating mechanisms: (1) Classical pathway: C1q binds the Fc region of IgM or IgG antibodies bound to antigen → C1r and C1s activation → C4 and C2 cleavage → C3 convertase. Requires adaptive immune antibodies — bridges innate and adaptive immunity; (2) Lectin pathway: Mannose-binding lectin (MBL) or ficolins bind carbohydrate patterns (mannose) on pathogen surfaces → MASP-1/2 activation → C4 and C2 cleavage → C3 convertase. Purely innate — no antibodies needed; (3) Alternative pathway: spontaneous hydrolysis of C3 in plasma → C3b binds foreign surfaces and activates Factor B → alternative C3 convertase. Provides constant low-level complement surveillance; amplified on foreign surfaces (not suppressed by host regulators like factor H). The classical pathway is NOT the most important in innate immunity — the alternative and lectin pathways are the primary innate complement mechanisms (E is wrong).

mockBank.question: A patient with a liver transplant on tacrolimus and prednisolone requires extraction of a symptomatic upper molar. Which ONE of the following represents the MOST important consideration?
options: A. Tacrolimus has no effect on clotting or infection risk — proceed as normal

B. Tacrolimus causes significant immunosuppression — increased infection risk; antibiotic prophylaxis may be considered after discussion with the transplant team; dental treatment is best coordinated with the hepatology team
C. Prednisolone causes hyperglycaemia only — this is the sole concern in this patient
D. All extractions in transplant patients must be performed under general anaesthesia in hospital
E. The patient will require anticoagulation reversal before any dental procedure
mockBank.explanation: Liver transplant recipients on tacrolimus (calcineurin inhibitor) and prednisolone have multiple dental considerations: (1) Immunosuppression — significantly impaired immune response → infection risk after dental procedures; antibiotic prophylaxis considered on a case-by-case basis with the transplant team; (2) Steroid cover — if on significant prednisolone doses (>10mg/day long-term), may require supplemental steroid cover for surgical procedures to prevent adrenal crisis; (3) Drug interactions — tacrolimus has narrow therapeutic index and many drug interactions (clarithromycin dramatically raises tacrolimus levels via CYP3A4 inhibition — avoid; metronidazole, azole antifungals also interact); (4) Coagulopathy — assess liver function, clotting (transplanted liver should be functioning, but early post-transplant patients may have residual coagulopathy). Not all transplant patients need GA (D). Tacrolimus IS relevant to infection risk (A). Prednisolone has multiple effects beyond hyperglycaemia (C).

mockBank.question: A 3-year-old child presents with painful oral ulcers, vesicular lesions on the palms and soles, and low-grade fever. Which ONE of the following is the MOST likely diagnosis?
options: A. Primary herpetic gingivostomatitis — HSV-1
B. Hand, foot, and mouth disease — Coxsackievirus A16 or Enterovirus 71
C. Chicken pox — VZV
D. Herpangina — Coxsackievirus A confined to the posterior oropharynx
E. Stevens-Johnson syndrome — drug-induced mucocutaneous eruption
mockBank.explanation: Hand, foot, and mouth disease (HFMD) is a common viral exanthem in children under 10, caused predominantly by Coxsackievirus A16 and Enterovirus 71. Classic triad: (1) Oral ulcers — shallow painful ulcers on buccal mucosa, tongue, hard palate; (2) Vesicular lesions on palms and soles (palmoplantar distribution — distinguishes from varicella which has a centripetal distribution); (3) Low-grade fever, malaise. Self-limiting (7–10 days), supportive treatment. Primary herpetic gingivostomatitis (A) causes extensive gingivitis + perioral skin vesicles — palms and soles are not affected. Varicella (C) causes centripetal vesicular rash (trunk > extremities, not specifically palms/soles). Herpangina (D) is also caused by Coxsackievirus but causes only posterior oropharyngeal lesions (uvula, soft palate, tonsillar pillars) — no skin lesions. Stevens-Johnson (E) is drug-induced — mucosal involvement + extensive skin blistering/detachment.

mockBank.question: A patient takes amoxicillin 500mg with a full meal. Which ONE of the following BEST describes the effect of food on amoxicillin absorption?
options: A. Food significantly reduces amoxicillin absorption — it must always be taken on an empty stomach
B. Food has minimal effect on amoxicillin absorption — it can be taken with or without food, and taking with food reduces GI side effects
C. Food doubles amoxicillin bioavailability via chelation with dietary calcium
D. Amoxicillin must be taken with food — absorption is almost zero on an empty stomach
E. Food delays amoxicillin peak plasma levels by 4 hours but this is clinically significant
mockBank.explanation: Amoxicillin has good oral bioavailability (~90%) and absorption is minimally affected by the presence of food. This distinguishes it from some other antibiotics: doxycycline absorption is significantly reduced by dairy products, antacids, and iron supplements (chelation); ciprofloxacin absorption is reduced by dairy and antacids. Amoxicillin can be taken with or without food — advising patients to take it with food can improve tolerance of GI side effects (nausea, diarrhoea) without compromising efficacy. In contrast, co-amoxiclav SHOULD be taken with or just before food — taking at the start of a meal reduces the incidence of GI side effects from the clavulanic acid component. Metronidazole can also be taken with food to reduce nausea.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the two signals required for B cell activation and antibody production?
options: A. Signal 1: TLR binding pathogen-associated molecular patterns; Signal 2: CD28-CD80 co-stimulation
B. Signal 1: antigen binding to the B cell receptor (BCR); Signal 2: T cell help via CD40L-CD40 interaction and IL-4 (for T-dependent antigens)
C. Signal 1: IgM secretion; Signal 2: complement C3d binding to CR2
D. Signal 1: MHC Class II presenting antigen to CD8+ T cells; Signal 2: B cell receptor cross-linking
E. Both signals come from antigen — B cells do not require T cell help for any antibody response
mockBank.explanation: B cell activation for most protein antigens (T-dependent antigens) requires two signals: (1) Signal 1: antigen binds specific B cell receptor (BCR = membrane-bound IgM) → BCR cross-linking and signal transduction via Ig-α/Ig-β heterodimer. (2) Signal 2: T cell help — CD40L on T follicular helper cell (Tfh) binds CD40 on B cell + cytokines (IL-4, IL-21). Without T cell help: B cells become anergic or undergo apoptosis (tolerance). T-independent (TI) antigens (LPS, polysaccharides, flagellin) can activate B cells without T cell help — they cross-link multiple BCRs simultaneously — but responses are weaker (IgM only, no memory). Signal 1 in B cells involves BCR, not TLR (A — TLR is innate). CD28-CD80 is T cell co-stimulation. IgM secretion is the outcome, not the signal (C). MHC Class II presents to CD4+ T cells, not CD8+ (D). T cell help IS required for most antibody responses (E).

mockBank.question: Porphyromonas gingivalis is a key periodontal pathogen. Which ONE of the following virulence mechanisms is MOST unique to P. gingivalis and clinically significant?
options: A. Production of glucosyltransferases that form insoluble glucan biofilm matrix
B. Gingipains — cysteine proteases that degrade host proteins (IgG, complement C3, fibronectin) and activate virulence factors
C. Production of cytotoxic exotoxin that directly kills gingival epithelial cells
D. Expression of flagellin that activates TLR5 to cause excessive bone resorption
E. Production of hydrogen sulphide that bleaches dental enamel
mockBank.explanation: P. gingivalis (Gram-negative, anaerobic, black-pigmented) is the primary keystone pathogen of chronic periodontitis. Its key virulence factor: gingipains (Arg-gingipain and Lys-gingipain) — potent cysteine protease enzymes that: (1) Degrade host defence proteins: IgG (reducing opsonisation), complement C3 (impairing complement-mediated killing), fibronectin; (2) Activate protease-activated receptors on host cells → cytokine production → bone resorption; (3) Cleave C5 to generate C5a (dysregulating complement in P. gingivalis's favour); (4) Provide nutrients (amino acids from protein degradation). P. gingivalis is a 'keystone pathogen' — it subverts host immunity at low abundance to enable a dysbiotic community to thrive. Glucosyltransferases (A) are characteristic of S. mutans (caries). Exotoxin production (C) is not a key P. gingivalis feature. Flagellin/TLR5 (D) — P. gingivalis has unusual flagellin that evades TLR5. Hydrogen sulphide (E) is produced by some oral bacteria but does not bleach enamel.

mockBank.question: Why is HCV genotype testing performed before initiating direct-acting antiviral (DAA) therapy, and which genotype is MOST prevalent in the UK?
options: A. Genotype determines disease severity — genotype 1 causes cirrhosis; genotype 3 causes hepatitis only

B. Genotype testing is needed because older regimens (interferon-based) had varying efficacy by genotype; modern pangenotypic DAAs work across all genotypes but genotype guides duration in some regimens
C. Genotype 1 is present only in IV drug users; other genotypes affect blood transfusion recipients only
D. HCV has only 2 genotypes — genotyping distinguishes between them for treatment selection
E. Genotype testing is no longer required — all UK patients receive the same treatment regardless of genotype
mockBank.explanation: HCV has 6 major genotypes (GT1–6) with multiple subtypes. In the UK: GT1 is most prevalent (~40–50%), followed by GT3 (~30%). Historically, interferon-based regimens had dramatically different SVR (cure) rates by genotype (GT1 was hardest to treat with interferon). Modern pangenotypic DAAs (sofosbuvir/velpatasvir; glecaprevir/pibrentasvir) achieve >95% SVR across all genotypes, making genotype less critical for treatment selection. However, genotype may still influence: treatment duration (some regimens 8 weeks for GT1, 12 weeks for GT3 with cirrhosis) and drug selection for specific resistant variants. UK NICE guidance now permits empirical pangenotypic therapy without mandating genotyping in all cases. Genotype does NOT reliably predict disease severity (A). Different genotypes are not exclusively linked to specific transmission routes (C). There are 6 major genotypes (D is wrong). Genotype may still inform some treatment decisions (E is an oversimplification).

mockBank.question: Dental handpieces (air turbines and slow-speed drills) can retract oral fluids into their internal mechanisms. Which ONE of the following BEST describes the correct infection control management?
options: A. Handpieces can be wiped with alcohol wipes between patients — this is sufficient decontamination
B. All dental handpieces must be cleaned, lubricated, and steam autoclaved (sterilised) between patients — they cannot be reliably decontaminated by surface wiping or chemical disinfection alone
C. Handpieces should be disposed of after each patient — they are single-use items
D. Handpieces require soaking in 2% glutaraldehyde for 20 minutes between patients
E. The dental nurse can clean handpieces by running them for 60 seconds before each patient — internal flushing is sufficient
mockBank.explanation: Dental handpieces are classified as semi-critical items (contact with mucous membranes but not sterile tissue — in most definitions) or critical (when they may penetrate tissue). UK guidance (HTM 01-05): all dental handpieces that enter the mouth must be sterilised (autoclaved) between patients — surface disinfection alone is inadequate as internal fluid pathways can retain contaminated material. Correct process: (1) Run handpiece to expel coolant water; (2) Remove from chair unit; (3) Clean externally and internally (following manufacturer's instructions); (4) Lubricate; (5) Bag and autoclave. Alcohol wipes (A) are for environmental surfaces only — inadequate for handpiece sterilisation. Handpieces are reusable, not single-use (C). Glutaraldehyde (D) is a chemical sterilant/high-level disinfectant used for heat-sensitive equipment — not the standard for heat-tolerant handpieces. Internal flushing (E) is a step in decontamination but does not achieve sterilisation.

mockBank.question: A patient newly diagnosed with HIV is starting HAART. The regimen includes tenofovir alafenamide (TAF) + emtricitabine (FTC) + dolutegravir (DTG). Which ONE of the following correctly identifies the drug classes in this regimen?
options: A. Two NRTIs + one NNRTI
B. Two NRTIs + one integrase strand transfer inhibitor (INSTI)
C. One NRTI + one NNRTI + one protease inhibitor
D. One NRTI + two INSTIs
E. Three protease inhibitors with ritonavir boosting
mockBank.explanation: TAF + FTC + DTG: TAF (tenofovir alafenamide) is an NRTI (nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitor — specifically a prodrug that delivers tenofovir diphosphate intracellularly); FTC (emtricitabine) is an NRTI (cytidine analogue); DTG (dolutegravir) is an INSTI (integrase strand transfer inhibitor — inhibits the strand transfer step of viral DNA integration). This combination (Biktarvy = bictegravir+FTC+TAF; or Triumeq = abacavir+3TC+DTG) represents current preferred first-line HIV therapy: 2 NRTIs provide the 'backbone' inhibiting reverse transcription; the INSTI provides the 'third agent' blocking integration. Advantages: once daily, high barrier to resistance (especially dolutegravir), excellent tolerability. NNRTIs (A, C) include efavirenz, rilpivirine, doravirine — these are not in this specific regimen. INSTIs include raltegravir, elvitegravir, bictegravir, dolutegravir (D is wrong — only one INSTI here).

mockBank.question: A patient with a history of recurrent herpes labialis has no visible lesions but reports a tingling sensation on their lip (prodrome). Which ONE of the following BEST describes their infectivity status?
options: A. They are not infectious — HSV can only be transmitted when visible vesicles are present
B. They are infectious — HSV shedding occurs during the prodrome before visible lesions appear, and asymptomatic viral shedding also occurs between episodes
C. They are only infectious if they have immunosuppression
D. They are infectious only if their salivary IgA is below normal range
E. The prodrome is caused by a different virus — HSV is not active during the prodrome phase
mockBank.explanation: HSV-1 shedding patterns: (1) Prodromal shedding: virus is actively replicating and being shed from the skin/mucosa BEFORE visible lesions appear — this is the peak infectious period (24–48 hours before vesicles). Antiviral topical therapy is MOST effective if started at this stage. (2) Active lesion phase: highly infectious (vesicles contain high-titre virus). (3) Crusting phase: lower infectivity but still some shedding. (4) Asymptomatic shedding: HSV-1 is detectable in saliva on ~20% of days in seropositive individuals without any lesions — this accounts for most HSV transmission (because people don't modify behaviour during asymptomatic periods). Dental relevance: PPE should be used for all patients, as serostatus may be unknown and asymptomatic shedding occurs. The prodrome IS caused by HSV reactivation — the tingling is from active viral replication in the nerve root (E is wrong).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following dental procedures is MOST likely to produce a significant bacteraemia (bacteria entering the bloodstream)?
options: A. Taking a dental radiograph
B. Fitting and adjusting a dental bridge
C. Dental extraction of a non-infected tooth
D. Applying topical fluoride varnish
E. Taking an alginate impression
mockBank.explanation: Bacteraemia can occur with any procedure that disrupts the gingival or mucosal barrier. Frequency and magnitude varies: High bacteraemia risk: tooth extractions (40–96% incidence), periodontal surgery, scaling and root planing, endodontic treatment beyond the apex. Moderate risk: subgingival probing, placement of orthodontic bands. Low risk: simple restorations, supragingival scaling, taking radiographs (A), impressions (E), fluoride application (D), bridge fitting (B) if no gingival manipulation. The clinically significant bacteraemia lasts only 15–30 minutes and is cleared by the healthy immune system. This is the context in which IE prophylaxis was historically recommended — though NICE now recommends against routine prophylaxis even for high-risk procedures.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following strategies is MOST effective at disrupting established oral biofilm (dental plaque)?
options: A. Systemic antibiotic therapy — antibiotics penetrate all biofilm layers equally
B. Mechanical disruption — toothbrushing, interdental cleaning, and professional scaling physically removing the biofilm structure, supplemented by antimicrobial agents
C. Chlorhexidine mouthwash alone — it penetrates all biofilm without mechanical disruption
D. Fluoride application — fluoride kills all biofilm bacteria at low concentrations
E. Increased saliva production — saliva dissolves biofilm matrix proteins
mockBank.explanation: Oral biofilm control requires mechanical disruption as the foundation — the extracellular polymeric matrix protects bacteria from antimicrobial penetration and immune attack. Effective strategies: (1) Mechanical: toothbrushing (disrupts supragingival plaque), interdental brushes/floss (interproximal plaque), professional scaling/root planing (subgingival debridement); (2) Chemical adjuncts: chlorhexidine (substantive antibiofilm effect), essential oil mouthwashes, fluoride (bacteriostatic at high concentrations — not kills all bacteria). Systemic antibiotics (A) do not penetrate established biofilm effectively — they may suppress planktonic bacteria but cannot clear the biofilm structural matrix. Chlorhexidine alone (C) without mechanical removal cannot disrupt established plaque. Fluoride (D) has antimicrobial properties but primary mechanism is remineralisation, not biofilm dissolution. Saliva (E) contains protective factors but cannot dissolve established biofilm.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following CORRECTLY matches an innate immune cell with its primary function?
options: A. Eosinophils — primary phagocytes for bacterial killing via oxidative burst
B. Basophils — the primary cell type for antigen presentation to naïve T cells
C. Neutrophils — the first responders to bacterial infection, rapidly recruited to infection sites where they phagocytose and kill bacteria
D. Natural killer cells — produce large amounts of IgM against viral pathogens
E. Mast cells — the main effectors of cell-mediated cytotoxicity against tumour cells
mockBank.explanation: Innate immune cells and their primary roles: Neutrophils: first responders to bacterial infection — recruited within hours via chemokine gradients (IL-8/CXCL8); phagocytose opsonised bacteria; kill via oxidative burst (NADPH oxidase → superoxide) and degranulation (elastase, MPO, defensins). Eosinophils (A): involved in allergic inflammation and antiparasitic immunity (degranulate major basic protein, eosinophil cationic protein onto helminth surfaces) — not primary phagocytes for bacteria. Basophils (B): circulating cells in allergy (IgE-mediated); not professional APCs (dendritic cells are the primary APCs). NK cells (D): innate lymphocytes that kill virus-infected and tumour cells — they do NOT produce antibodies (IgM is produced by B cells). Mast cells (E): tissue-resident cells in allergy and early innate responses — not the primary cell for cell-mediated cytotoxicity (that is CD8+ cytotoxic T cells and NK cells).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following drugs commonly prescribed in dentistry is MOST likely to cause hepatotoxicity with long-term use?
options: A. Amoxicillin — the most hepatotoxic dental antibiotic
B. Metronidazole — causes hepatic failure in most patients after 5 days
C. Co-amoxiclav (amoxicillin-clavulanate) — associated with cholestatic hepatitis, particularly in older men, appearing days to weeks after completing the course
D. Chlorhexidine mouthwash — systemic absorption causes liver damage
E. Sodium fluoride — accumulates in the liver causing fluorosis-related hepatitis
mockBank.explanation: Co-amoxiclav (amoxicillin + clavulanic acid) is the most hepatotoxic commonly prescribed dental antibiotic. It causes cholestatic hepatitis — inflammation + bile stasis — presenting as jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, and elevated bilirubin/ALP/GGT. Important features: (1) Delayed onset — symptoms appear 1–6 weeks after starting or even after completing the course; (2) Male sex and older age are risk factors; (3) Usually self-limiting but can be severe; (4) Mechanism: clavulanic acid (not amoxicillin) is the primary hepatotoxic component — important as amoxicillin alone is much less hepatotoxic. Management: stop co-amoxiclav, supportive care; most resolve within weeks to months. Amoxicillin alone (A) has much lower hepatotoxicity than co-amoxiclav. Metronidazole (B) rarely causes severe hepatotoxicity at standard dental doses. Chlorhexidine (D) and fluoride (E) are not hepatotoxic at therapeutic concentrations.

mockBank.question: Valaciclovir is preferred over oral aciclovir for HSV and VZV treatment. Which ONE of the following BEST explains this preference?
options: A. Valaciclovir has a different mechanism of action from aciclovir — it inhibits viral protease

B. Valaciclovir is the L-valyl ester prodrug of aciclovir — it is better absorbed orally (bioavailability 55% vs 15–30% for aciclovir) and converted to aciclovir in the intestinal wall and liver, requiring less frequent dosing
C. Valaciclovir is less toxic than aciclovir — it does not cause nephrotoxicity
D. Valaciclovir requires viral thymidine kinase for activation, while aciclovir does not
E. Valaciclovir is effective against CMV while aciclovir has no CMV activity
mockBank.explanation: Valaciclovir is the L-valyl ester prodrug of aciclovir. After oral administration, intestinal esterases cleave the valine ester, releasing aciclovir. Oral bioavailability of valaciclovir → aciclovir: ~55% (vs 15–30% for oral aciclovir). This means: (1) Higher plasma aciclovir levels are achieved; (2) Less frequent dosing — herpes labialis: valaciclovir 2g twice daily for 1 day vs aciclovir 200mg 5×/day for 5 days; shingles: valaciclovir 1g three times daily vs aciclovir 800mg 5×/day. Valaciclovir and aciclovir have identical mechanisms — valaciclovir IS converted to aciclovir and activated by viral TK (B includes correct conversion mechanism; A and D are wrong). Nephrotoxicity risk exists for both drugs (C). Valaciclovir has poor CMV activity (like aciclovir) — CMV requires ganciclovir/valganciclovir (E).

mockBank.question: A patient reports anaphylaxis to penicillin. The dentist considers prescribing a cephalosporin antibiotic. Which ONE of the following BEST describes the risk of cross-reactivity between penicillins and cephalosporins?
options: A. Cross-reactivity rate is 50% — cephalosporins are equally dangerous in penicillin allergy

B. Historical estimates of 10% cross-reactivity were overestimated — true IgE-mediated cross-reactivity is approximately 1–2%, primarily between older cephalosporins and penicillins sharing similar R1 side chains
C. There is no cross-reactivity — cephalosporins are completely safe in anaphylactic penicillin allergy
D. Cross-reactivity depends on the route of administration — IV cephalosporins cross-react but oral ones do not
E. All beta-lactams cross-react equally — carbapenems are also absolutely contraindicated in penicillin allergy
mockBank.explanation: Penicillin-cephalosporin cross-reactivity is clinically important but often overestimated. Historical 10% cross-reactivity rates were derived from skin test studies using poorly characterised reagents. Modern evidence: true IgE-mediated cross-reactivity between penicillins and cephalosporins is ~1–2%, and primarily occurs between specific drug pairs sharing similar R1 side-chain structures (e.g., amoxicillin and cefalexin have identical R1 groups — higher cross-reactivity than between benzylpenicillin and cefuroxime). For UK dental practice: cephalosporins are generally AVOIDED in confirmed immediate penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis/angioedema/urticaria within 60 minutes) due to this residual risk. Non-immediate reactions (delayed rashes >72 hours after penicillin) carry much lower cross-reactivity risk. Carbapenem cross-reactivity (E) is very low (<1%) — not equally contraindicated to cephalosporins.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST explains why a second exposure to the same antigen produces a faster, stronger, and more durable antibody response than the first exposure?
options: A. The innate immune system learns from previous infections and mounts a faster non-specific response
B. Long-lived plasma cells and memory B cells generated during the primary response allow rapid high-affinity antibody production upon re-exposure — the secondary (anamnestic) response
C. More antigen is present in the second exposure, amplifying the immune response
D. The complement system is pre-activated from the first exposure and immediately opsonises antigen
E. Regulatory T cells suppress the primary response, allowing a stronger unrestricted secondary response
mockBank.explanation: Immunological memory is the foundation of vaccination. Primary immune response: naïve B cells activated by antigen → clonal expansion → differentiation into: (1) Plasma cells (short-lived antibody factories, mainly IgM initially) and (2) Memory B cells (long-lived, persist for decades in bone marrow and secondary lymphoid organs). Primary response: IgM appears at 5–7 days; IgG peaks at 3–4 weeks; antibody titres decline. Secondary (anamnestic) response: upon re-exposure, memory B cells — already selected for antigen specificity, class-switched to IgG, and hypermutated for higher affinity — rapidly differentiate into plasma cells. Response is: faster (antibody appears within 1–3 days), higher peak titre (10–100× higher IgG), predominantly IgG (switched, high affinity), more durable. This is why booster vaccine doses generate much stronger, longer-lasting immunity than a single dose.

mockBank.question: A 30-year-old woman presents with fatigue, elevated ALT (500 U/L), and hypergammaglobulinaemia. HBsAg, anti-HBc, anti-HCV, and HAV IgM are all negative. ANA and anti-smooth muscle antibodies are strongly positive. Which ONE of the following is the MOST likely diagnosis and appropriate management?
options: A. Acute HBV infection — treat with entecavir
B. Autoimmune hepatitis — treat with prednisolone (and azathioprine for maintenance)
C. HCV infection — treat with direct-acting antivirals
D. Primary biliary cholangitis — treat with ursodeoxycholic acid
E. Wilson's disease — treat with D-penicillamine to chelate copper
mockBank.explanation: Autoimmune hepatitis (AIH) diagnosis: typically young to middle-aged women; elevated transaminases; hypergammaglobulinaemia (especially IgG); positive autoantibodies — ANA and ASMA (anti-smooth muscle antibodies) in Type 1 AIH (most common); liver biopsy shows interface hepatitis with plasma cell infiltrate; negative viral hepatitis serology. Treatment: prednisolone 30–40mg daily (induction) → taper + azathioprine (maintenance) — achieves biochemical remission in 80%. Untreated AIH progresses to cirrhosis. The negative viral serology and positive autoantibodies rule out viral hepatitis (A, C). Primary biliary cholangitis (D) causes a predominantly cholestatic picture (elevated ALP/bilirubin > ALT) and has anti-mitochondrial antibodies (AMA), not ANA. Wilson's disease (E) presents in younger patients with neuropsychiatric features + Kayser-Fleischer rings + low caeruloplasmin — diagnosed with serum copper, caeruloplasmin, and 24-hour urine copper.

mockBank.question: In the management of chronic periodontitis, which ONE of the following local antimicrobial agents is placed directly into periodontal pockets as an adjunct to scaling and root planing?
options: A. Systemic amoxicillin — the most evidence-based adjunct for chronic periodontitis
B. Local minocycline microspheres (Arestin) or chlorhexidine chip (PerioChip) — placed into pockets >5mm after scaling to sustain antimicrobial levels at the site
C. Topical nystatin gel — covers the anaerobic organisms in subgingival plaque
D. Local metronidazole gel for short-term use — it dissolves within 30 minutes
E. Subgingival hydrogen peroxide irrigation — kills all anaerobic periodontal pathogens
mockBank.explanation: Local delivery antimicrobials (LDA) are used as adjuncts to mechanical debridement for periodontal pockets ≥5mm that do not respond to conventional SRP alone. Products available: (1) Minocycline microspheres (Arestin) — sustained-release tetracycline antibiotic; (2) Chlorhexidine gluconate chip (PerioChip) — biodegradable chip releasing CHX over 7–10 days; (3) Doxycycline gel (Atridox) — subgingival sustained-release. Advantages: high local concentration without systemic toxicity, treating the site directly. They are NOT substitutes for SRP. Systemic antibiotics (A) have evidence in aggressive periodontitis (amoxicillin + metronidazole) but not routine chronic periodontitis. Nystatin (C) covers fungi, not periodontal anaerobes. Metronidazole gel (D) as described does not have the sustained-release properties of Atridox. H₂O₂ irrigation (E) has limited evidence and does not have sustained antibacterial activity.

mockBank.question: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) is the most common cause of bronchiolitis in infants. Which ONE of the following BEST describes its relevance to dentistry?
options: A. RSV causes parotitis in infants — dental teams may see swollen salivary glands
B. RSV is shed in respiratory secretions — dental aerosols could potentially transmit RSV to young children in waiting rooms; hand hygiene and appropriate scheduling of unwell children are important
C. RSV causes recurrent oral ulcers in children under 2 years
D. RSV causes immune suppression leading to secondary candidiasis in all affected children
E. RSV is a blood-borne virus requiring dental teams to take specific additional precautions beyond standard PPE
mockBank.explanation: RSV (paramyxovirus, negative-sense ssRNA) is the leading cause of viral lower respiratory tract infection in infants (bronchiolitis, peak age <6 months). It is highly contagious via respiratory droplets and direct contact with contaminated secretions. Dental relevance: (1) Aerosol-generating dental procedures produce respiratory droplets — a waiting room with a symptomatic RSV-infected infant could expose other vulnerable infants; (2) Hand hygiene is essential — RSV survives on surfaces for hours; (3) Unwell children with active RSV should have non-urgent dental appointments deferred; (4) Palivizumab (monoclonal antibody prophylaxis) is used in high-risk infants (premature, congenital heart disease) — dentists should be aware of the patient's vulnerability. RSV does not cause parotitis (A), oral ulcers (C), candidiasis (D), or blood-borne transmission (E).

mockBank.question: Fusobacterium nucleatum is found in the 'orange complex' of periodontal pathogens. Which ONE of the following roles does it play in periodontal biofilm ecology?
options: A. F. nucleatum is an early coloniser that attaches to pellicle before other bacteria

B. F. nucleatum acts as a bridge organism — it coaggregates with early colonisers (streptococci) and late colonisers (red complex species including P. gingivalis), allowing the development of a pathogenic subgingival community
C. F. nucleatum produces hydrogen peroxide, creating an oxidative environment that kills anaerobic pathogens
D. F. nucleatum is exclusively pathogenic — it is never found in healthy periodontal sites
E. F. nucleatum is Gram-positive and is the primary target for amoxicillin in periodontitis treatment
mockBank.explanation: Fusobacterium nucleatum's ecological role in subgingival biofilm is as a 'bridging organism' or 'coaggregation bridge'. Early colonisers (S. gordonii, S. mitis, S. oralis) adhere to the tooth-associated pellicle. Late colonisers (P. gingivalis, T. denticola — red complex pathogens) cannot directly adhere to early colonisers but CAN coaggregate with F. nucleatum. F. nucleatum coaggregates with both early colonisers AND late colonisers — physically bridging them and allowing pathogenic species to join the maturing biofilm. This makes F. nucleatum essential for the development of a mature, disease-associated polymicrobial community. F. nucleatum is NOT an early coloniser (A) — it appears in intermediate biofilm development. It produces butyrate, not H₂O₂ (C). F. nucleatum IS found in health at low levels (D). F. nucleatum is Gram-NEGATIVE (E) — a Gram-negative rod.

mockBank.question: The measles vaccine requires approximately 95% population vaccination coverage to maintain herd immunity. Which ONE of the following BEST explains why this threshold is higher than for many other vaccine-preventable diseases?
options: A. The measles vaccine is less immunogenic than other vaccines — 95% vaccination is needed to compensate for poor immune responses

B. Measles has an exceptionally high basic reproduction number (R₀ ≈ 12–18), meaning a highly infectious individual infects many susceptibles — therefore a very high proportion of the population must be immune to interrupt transmission
C. 95% coverage is required by law — this is a regulatory threshold not based on scientific calculation
D. The measles virus mutates rapidly, requiring near-universal vaccination to prevent vaccine escape variants
E. Herd immunity thresholds are identical for all respiratory viruses — 95% applies to influenza and COVID-19 too
mockBank.explanation: Herd immunity threshold (HIT) = 1 − 1/R₀. Measles R₀ ≈ 12–18 (one of the highest of any infectious disease). Applying the formula: HIT = 1 − 1/15 ≈ 93%. In practice, 95%+ coverage is recommended to account for: imperfect vaccine efficacy (~97% effective after 2 doses), geographic clustering of unvaccinated individuals (pockets of susceptibility), and waning immunity in some individuals. Compare: polio R₀ ≈ 5–7 → HIT ~80–86%; SARS-CoV-2 original R₀ ≈ 2–3 → HIT ~50–67%; COVID-19 Omicron R₀ ≈ 10–15 → HIT ~90%. The measles vaccine is highly immunogenic (A is wrong). The 95% threshold is evidence-based, not regulatory (C). Measles does not mutate rapidly (D) — measles virus is antigenically stable (unlike influenza). HITs vary by disease R₀ (E).

mockBank.question: Caspofungin is an echinocandin antifungal used for invasive Candida infections. Which ONE of the following correctly describes its mechanism of action?
options: A. Caspofungin binds ergosterol in the fungal cell membrane, creating pores
B. Caspofungin inhibits CYP51 (lanosterol 14-alpha-demethylase), depleting ergosterol
C. Caspofungin inhibits beta-1,3-D-glucan synthase, preventing synthesis of fungal cell wall glucan — a target absent from mammalian cells
D. Caspofungin inhibits fungal DNA gyrase, preventing DNA replication
E. Caspofungin acts as a nucleoside analogue, blocking fungal RNA synthesis
mockBank.explanation: Echinocandins (caspofungin, micafungin, anidulafungin) have a unique mechanism targeting the fungal cell wall. Beta-1,3-D-glucan is a major structural polysaccharide in the fungal cell wall — absent from mammalian cells, making glucan synthase an ideal antifungal target with minimal host toxicity. Inhibition of glucan synthase → impaired cell wall synthesis → osmotic lysis. Echinocandins are fungicidal against Candida species and fungistatic against Aspergillus. They are used for: invasive candidiasis, candidaemia, aspergillosis (if azole-resistant). Ergosterol binding (A) = polyenes (nystatin, amphotericin B). CYP51 inhibition (B) = azoles (fluconazole, miconazole). DNA gyrase (D) is a bacterial target (fluoroquinolones). RNA synthesis inhibition (E) = nucleoside analogues (flucytosine).

mockBank.question: PrEP (pre-exposure prophylaxis) for HIV prevention became available on the NHS in 2020. Which ONE of the following BEST describes the mechanism and efficacy of PrEP?
options: A. PrEP contains HIV antibodies that neutralise the virus before it can infect cells

B. PrEP uses tenofovir alafenamide + emtricitabine (NRTIs) taken before exposure — they inhibit reverse transcriptase so that if HIV enters the body, it cannot establish an infection; >99% effective when taken correctly
C. PrEP is a live attenuated HIV vaccine taken orally once per year
D. PrEP contains integrase inhibitors that prevent HIV DNA from integrating into host cells
E. PrEP efficacy is approximately 50% — it is used in combination with intensive barrier protection
mockBank.explanation: PrEP for HIV: the UK regimen is tenofovir disoproxil fumarate (TDF) + emtricitabine (FTC) — also called Truvada or generic equivalent. Both are NRTIs. They are taken: daily (most effective) or event-based (2-4-2 dosing for anal sex). Mechanism: if HIV enters the body during exposure, the pre-existing intracellular triphosphate metabolites of TDF and FTC inhibit HIV reverse transcriptase → viral RNA cannot be converted to DNA → proviral integration cannot occur → infection is aborted. Efficacy: >99% when taken correctly (daily). Side effects: renal (monitor creatinine, eGFR — TDF is nephrotoxic in some patients) and bone density changes. Note: PrEP does not protect against other STIs (gonorrhoea, chlamydia, syphilis). No HIV vaccine exists (A, C). Integrase inhibitors (D) are not in current PrEP regimens. Efficacy is >99%, not 50% (E).

mockBank.question: A patient has a fluctuant, pointing dental abscess with skin fixation in the submandibular region. The dentist elects to drain the abscess. Which ONE of the following BEST describes the correct approach for incision and drainage?
options: A. Intraoral approach only — extraoral incisions always leave permanent scars

B. Aspirate with a needle first, then incise at the point of maximum fluctuance, insert a haemostat to break up loculi, place a corrugated drain, and prescribe systemic antibiotics if systemic features are present
C. Incise boldly over the most prominent area and allow to drain without drain placement
D. Refer immediately to A&E without any attempt at drainage — all extraoral abscesses require hospital management
E. Prescribe antibiotics for 5 days — if no improvement then consider drainage
mockBank.explanation: Surgical drainage of a pointing submandibular abscess with skin fixation: (1) Aspiration to confirm pus (and culture); (2) Local anaesthesia — may require nerve blocks as local infiltration into infected tissue is less effective; (3) Incision at point of maximum fluctuance, preferably in a skin crease to minimise scar visibility; (4) Blunt dissection with haemostat to break loculations; (5) Copious saline irrigation; (6) Corrugated rubber drain sutured in place to prevent premature closure and allow continued drainage; (7) Antibiotics if systemic features present (fever, trismus, spreading cellulitis); (8) Review in 24–48 hours to reassess drain. Intraoral-only approach (A) is appropriate for intraoral pointing abscesses but not for submandibular abscesses with skin fixation. Antibiotics alone (E) do not replace surgical drainage — antibiotics cannot penetrate avascular abscess cavity.

mockBank.question: A 72-year-old patient had herpes zoster (shingles) affecting the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve 6 months ago. The rash has resolved but she has persistent severe burning pain in the same distribution. Which ONE of the following BEST describes this condition and its management?
options: A. This is a recurrence of shingles — prescribe aciclovir 800mg five times daily

B. Post-herpetic neuralgia (PHN) — neuropathic pain persisting >3 months after shingles resolution; treated with gabapentinoids (gabapentin/pregabalin), tricyclic antidepressants, or topical lidocaine/capsaicin patches
C. This is herpes simplex neuralgia — prescribe valaciclovir for HSV reactivation
D. This is normal healing pain — reassure and prescribe simple analgesics (ibuprofen 400mg TDS)
E. Urgent referral for MRI — persistent facial pain after shingles indicates cerebral vasculitis
mockBank.explanation: Post-herpetic neuralgia (PHN) is the most common and debilitating complication of herpes zoster. It is defined as pain persisting for >30–90 days (definitions vary) after the acute rash. Incidence increases with age — up to 40% in patients over 70. Pathophysiology: VZV causes axonal injury and sensitisation of peripheral nociceptors during the acute episode — PHN results from lasting neural damage and central sensitisation. Management: (1) Gabapentinoids — gabapentin or pregabalin (first-line); (2) Tricyclic antidepressants — amitriptyline (also first-line, especially with sleep disturbance); (3) Topical therapies — lidocaine 5% patches or capsaicin 8% patch (specialist-applied); (4) Opioids — only if above insufficient. Simple analgesics (D) are ineffective for neuropathic pain. Antivirals (A, C) do not treat established PHN — they must be used acutely to prevent it. MRI (E) is not routinely indicated.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following cells forms the MOST important bridge between innate and adaptive immunity?
options: A. Neutrophils — they present antigens to T cells in lymph nodes
B. Natural killer cells — they activate B cells to produce antibodies
C. Dendritic cells — they sample antigens in peripheral tissues (innate), then migrate to lymph nodes and present antigens to naïve T cells (adaptive) via MHC Class I and II
D. Mast cells — they produce IL-12 that activates naïve CD8+ T cells
E. Eosinophils — they ingest antigen and present to CD4+ T cells in the gut
mockBank.explanation: Dendritic cells (DCs) are the bridge between innate and adaptive immunity. In peripheral tissues: immature DCs patrol and sample antigens via phagocytosis, macropinocytosis, and pattern recognition receptors (TLRs) — this is innate sensing. Upon pathogen detection and activation, DCs mature and upregulate: MHC Class I and II (for antigen presentation), CD80/CD86 (co-stimulatory molecules), CCR7 (chemokine receptor for lymph node homing). Mature DCs migrate via afferent lymphatics to the T cell zones of draining lymph nodes where they present antigen to naïve CD4+ (via MHC II) and CD8+ T cells (via MHC I cross-presentation) — initiating the adaptive immune response. Neutrophils (A) are primarily phagocytes — not professional APCs. NK cells (B) do not activate B cells directly. Mast cells (D) produce TNF-α and histamine, not primarily IL-12. Eosinophils (E) are not professional APCs.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following patient groups should be ROUTINELY offered hepatitis B and C testing in UK dental practice?
options: A. All patients over 65 — age is the main risk factor for viral hepatitis

B. All patients presenting with significant hepatic risk factors: IV drug use history, blood transfusion before 1992, sex work, prisoner status, migration from high-prevalence countries, or presenting with oral/systemic features of liver disease
C. Testing is not within the dental scope — only GP practices offer hepatitis testing
D. All patients with more than 5 dental fillings — amalgam contains mercury which activates hepatitis viruses
E. All patients who present with toothache — dental pain is an early indicator of hepatitis
mockBank.explanation: UK guidance supports opportunistic hepatitis B and C testing in various healthcare settings including dentistry. Risk factors that should prompt consideration of testing: (1) History of IV drug use (highest risk for HCV, HBV); (2) Blood transfusion or blood products before systematic screening (pre-1992 for HCV in the UK); (3) Sex work or unprotected sex with multiple partners; (4) Country of origin from high-prevalence regions (sub-Saharan Africa for HBV; Central/Eastern Europe, Egypt for HCV); (5) Imprisonment; (6) Unexplained elevated liver enzymes; (7) Oral manifestations suggesting liver disease (jaundice, oral lichen planus for HCV). Dentists are well-placed to identify these risk factors during history-taking and refer for testing. Age (A) is not the primary risk factor. Dental testing IS within scope when appropriately trained (C). Mercury amalgam (D) has no role in hepatitis. Toothache (E) is not related.

mockBank.question: Iodine-containing antiseptics (povidone-iodine) are used for wound irrigation and surgical site preparation. Which ONE of the following BEST describes the mechanism by which iodine kills microorganisms?
options: A. Iodine chelates calcium ions essential for bacterial cell wall stability
B. Iodine oxidises and precipitates proteins, disrupts cell membrane lipids, and inactivates nucleic acids — acting on multiple simultaneous targets
C. Iodine inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase, preventing DNA replication
D. Iodine is a competitive inhibitor of bacterial thymidine kinase
E. Iodine creates pores in fungal cell membranes by binding ergosterol
mockBank.explanation: Iodine's antimicrobial mechanism is broad and multi-target — this is why resistance does not develop (unlike antibiotic resistance). Molecular iodine (I₂) and hypoiodous acid (HOI) generated from povidone-iodine: (1) Oxidise and precipitate proteins, including enzymes (oxidises sulfhydryl groups in cysteine residues); (2) Disrupt cell membrane lipids by reacting with fatty acid double bonds; (3) Inactivate nucleic acids by oxidising purine/pyrimidine bases. This simultaneous multi-target attack makes resistance essentially impossible — no single mutation can overcome all mechanisms simultaneously.
