Applications in dentistry: povidone-iodine irrigation of infected sockets, pre-surgical mucosal preparation, treatment of ANUG. Limitations: deactivated by organic matter (pus, blood), so surfaces must be cleaned first. Calcium chelation (A) = EDTA in root canal irrigation. DNA gyrase inhibition (C) = fluoroquinolones. Thymidine kinase inhibition (D) = antivirals. Ergosterol binding (E) = polyene antifungals.

mockBank.question: A 48-year-old man presents with new diagnosis of HIV with CD4 count of 45 cells/μL. He is also found to have CMV retinitis and oral thrush. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate initial management sequence?
options: A. Start HAART immediately alongside treatment for all opportunistic infections simultaneously
B. Treat CMV retinitis with IV ganciclovir first, then start HAART when CD4 >100

C. Treat active opportunistic infections first (prioritise CMV retinitis with ganciclovir and candidiasis with fluconazole), then start HAART within 2 weeks — avoid immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) complications
D. Defer all treatment — the patient's prognosis is poor with CD4 of 45
E. Start HAART only — antiretrovirals will control all opportunistic infections without specific treatment
mockBank.explanation: Management of advanced HIV with AIDS-defining illnesses: (1) Treat active opportunistic infections first to stabilise the patient — CMV retinitis: IV ganciclovir (to prevent blindness); oral candidiasis: fluconazole; PCP (if present): co-trimoxazole. (2) Start HAART within 2 weeks of starting OI treatment — delayed HAART initiation beyond 2 weeks increases mortality. Exception: CMV retinitis — some evidence supports starting HAART within 1–2 weeks even for CMV retinitis. (3) Monitor for IRIS (immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome) — as CD4 recovers on HAART, the reconstituted immune system can paradoxically worsen OI symptoms (e.g., worsening CMV retinitis, cryptococcal meningitis deterioration). IRIS is more common when HAART is started immediately alongside OI treatment (A) or when CD4 is very low. Deferring treatment (D) is inappropriate — mortality is high untreated. HAART alone (E) will not resolve CMV retinitis — specific therapy is required.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes noma (cancrum oris) and its causative factors?
options: A. Noma is a fungal infection of the jaw bone caused by Mucor species in immunocompromised patients

B. Noma is a rapidly progressive, necrotising orofacial infection primarily affecting malnourished children in sub-Saharan Africa, associated with fusospirochaetal bacteria (Fusobacterium, Treponema) and predisposed by malnutrition, measles, and immunosuppression
C. Noma is a chronic granulomatous infection caused by Actinomyces israelii
D. Noma is a term for Ludwig's angina — bilateral submandibular space infection
E. Noma is a viral disease caused by measles virus directly infecting orofacial tissues
mockBank.explanation: Noma (from Greek 'noma' = to devour; cancrum oris = eating mouth) is a rapidly progressive gangrenous stomatitis affecting malnourished children (2–6 years) in sub-Saharan Africa, parts of Asia and South America. It begins as necrotising ulcerative stomatitis and rapidly spreads through all layers of the face — skin, muscle, and bone — causing horrific facial disfigurement within days. WHO-recognised neglected tropical disease. Causative organisms: polymicrobial, predominantly Fusobacterium necrophorum and Prevotella intermedia (combined with Treponema species). Predisposing factors: severe malnutrition (kwashiorkor), recent measles or other viral illness, HIV, poor oral hygiene, contaminated water. Untreated mortality: up to 90%. Survivors require reconstructive surgery. Mucormycosis (A) causes rhinocerebral infection in diabetic ketoacidosis. Actinomycosis (C) is chronic. Ludwig's angina (D) is a different condition. Measles (E) predisposes but does not directly cause noma.

mockBank.question: A returning traveller from West Africa presents with fever, myalgia, and haemorrhagic manifestations. Ebola virus disease is suspected. What is the dental team's MOST important role in this clinical scenario?
options: A. Perform a full dental examination to rule out oral haemorrhage as the source of bleeding

B. Immediately alert the infection control team, isolate the patient following HPS (high consequence infectious disease) protocols, and do NOT perform any dental examination — HCID management takes priority
C. Prescribe ciprofloxacin empirically to cover bacterial causes of haemorrhagic fever
D. Take blood samples for viral culture to confirm Ebola
E. Reassure the patient — Ebola cannot survive in UK climate and poses no risk in dental settings
mockBank.explanation: Ebola virus disease (EVD) is classified as a High Consequence Infectious Disease (HCID) in the UK. If a patient presents to a dental setting with features of suspected Ebola (travel to endemic region + fever + haemorrhagic manifestations): (1) Immediately isolate the patient in a single room; (2) Do NOT remove PPE hastily — apply HCID-level PPE before any contact; (3) Immediately contact the Infection Control team and Public Health England HCID network; (4) Do NOT perform invasive procedures or dental examination — any invasive contact with blood or body fluids carries extreme transmission risk; (5) The patient must be transferred to a High Level Isolation Unit (HLIU). EVD transmission: direct contact with blood/body fluids of symptomatic patients — extremely high infectivity. Oral examination (A) is contraindicated. Antibiotics (C) have no role. Blood culture (D) must be done in specialist facilities only. Ebola CAN survive in clinical settings and poses extreme risk (E is dangerously wrong).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following dental prescribing behaviours MOST contributes to global antimicrobial resistance?
options: A. Prescribing amoxicillin for acute dentoalveolar abscesses with systemic features (fever, trismus)
B. Prescribing antibiotics for every patient with dental pain 'just in case' without performing source control
C. Using clindamycin for penicillin-allergic patients with spreading dental infections
D. Prescribing metronidazole for acute necrotising ulcerative gingivitis (ANUG)
E. Limiting antibiotic courses to 3–5 days for most dental infections
mockBank.explanation: Prescribing antibiotics 'just in case' — without a clear clinical indication and without performing source control (extraction, drainage) — is the most common inappropriate antibiotic prescribing behaviour in dentistry and the greatest contributor to antimicrobial resistance. Every unnecessary antibiotic course: (1) Selects for resistant organisms in the patient's commensal flora; (2) Disrupts the gut microbiome, increasing C. difficile risk; (3) Exposes the patient to adverse effects without clinical benefit; (4) Contributes to the global resistance gene pool. UK audits show dentistry accounts for ~10% of all community antibiotic prescriptions in England — with significant overuse. Options A, C, D, and E represent APPROPRIATE prescribing. Amoxicillin for systemic features (A) = correct indication. Clindamycin for penicillin allergy + spreading infection (C) = correct. Metronidazole for ANUG (D) = correct. Short courses (E) = good stewardship.

mockBank.question: A dental nurse develops urticaria and facial oedema within 15 minutes of putting on latex gloves. Which ONE of the following BEST describes the type of hypersensitivity reaction and its mechanism?
options: A. Type II hypersensitivity — IgG antibodies against latex proteins on the glove surface cause complement activation
B. Type I (immediate) hypersensitivity — IgE antibodies against latex allergens on mast cells degranulate upon latex exposure, releasing histamine and causing urticaria and angioedema
C. Type IV (delayed) hypersensitivity — T cells sensitised to latex cause a delayed dermatitis 24–48 hours after exposure
D. Type III hypersensitivity — immune complexes of anti-latex antibodies and latex antigen deposit in small vessels
E. This is a toxic reaction — latex chemicals are directly cytotoxic without immune involvement
mockBank.explanation: Latex allergy: Type I (IgE-mediated, immediate) hypersensitivity to natural rubber latex (NRL) proteins (Hev b 1–13). Pathophysiology: prior sensitisation → IgE antibodies produced against latex proteins → IgE binds mast cell FcεRI receptors. On re-exposure → latex proteins cross-link mast cell-bound IgE → degranulation → histamine, prostaglandins, leukotrienes → urticaria (hives), angioedema, rhinitis, bronchospasm, anaphylaxis. Occurs within minutes of exposure. Management: switch to latex-free gloves (vinyl, nitrile), carry adrenaline auto-injector (EpiPen) for anaphylaxis risk. Dental relevance: latex allergy affects up to 10% of healthcare workers. All dental practices should have latex-free options. Type IV (C) is the correct type for allergic contact dermatitis — a delayed (24–72 hours) reaction to chemicals in gloves (e.g., thiurams), not NRL proteins — this is a separate condition. Type II (A) and III (D) are not the mechanisms of latex allergy. Cytotoxic irritant contact dermatitis (E) is different.

mockBank.question: A patient with alcoholic liver disease (ALD) requires dental treatment. His most recent bloods show: ALT 85 U/L, GGT 420 U/L (markedly elevated), bilirubin normal, INR 1.1, platelets 140. Which ONE of the following is the MOST accurate assessment of his dental management risk?
options: A. No increased risk — all bloods are within clinically acceptable ranges for dentistry
B. High risk of haemorrhage — INR >1.5 and thrombocytopenia indicate uncontrolled cirrhosis

C. Moderate risk — raised GGT and ALT suggest significant alcohol-related liver damage; assess for drug interactions (many drugs metabolised by CYP enzymes altered by alcohol); avoid hepatotoxic drugs (NSAIDs, high-dose co-amoxiclav); check current alcohol intake and advise cessation
D. No risk — GGT elevation is only caused by gallbladder disease and does not indicate liver dysfunction
E. High risk of infection only — elevated GGT specifically impairs neutrophil function
mockBank.explanation: Interpreting this LFT profile: ALT 85 (mildly elevated, ×2 ULN) indicates hepatocellular inflammation; GGT 420 (markedly elevated, >10× ULN) is highly sensitive for alcohol use and hepatobiliary disease — GGT is induced by alcohol and many medications; INR 1.1 (normal) indicates adequate hepatic synthetic function; platelets 140 (low normal, borderline) suggests early portal hypertension or bone marrow effect. Risk assessment: this patient has active alcohol-related liver injury but without coagulopathy or thrombocytopaenia sufficient to preclude routine dentistry. Key considerations: (1) Drug interactions — alcohol and liver disease alter drug metabolism; (2) Avoid NSAIDs (GI bleeding risk, renal effects), high-dose co-amoxiclav (hepatotoxicity risk); (3) Paracetamol in moderation (2–3g/day) is acceptable — NOT contraindicated at therapeutic doses in ALD without cirrhosis; (4) Counsel on alcohol cessation. This is a moderate-risk patient, not high-risk from a coagulation perspective.

mockBank.question: The shingles vaccine (Shingrix) is recommended for all adults over 70 in the UK. Which ONE of the following BEST describes the type of vaccine and how it differs from the older Zostavax?
options: A. Shingrix and Zostavax are both live attenuated vaccines — Shingrix simply contains a higher dose

B. Shingrix is a recombinant subunit vaccine (VZV glycoprotein E + AS01B adjuvant) — it is NOT a live vaccine; it is safe in immunocompromised patients and more effective (>90% efficacy vs ~50% for Zostavax, which was live attenuated)
C. Shingrix is a DNA vaccine encoding VZV surface proteins
D. Both vaccines are given as single doses — the only difference is the route of administration
E. Shingrix is contraindicated in adults over 80 due to excessive immune activation
mockBank.explanation: Shingrix (recombinant zoster vaccine, RZV): contains VZV glycoprotein E (gE) antigen combined with AS01B adjuvant system (MPL + QS-21 — Toll-like receptor agonists that strongly boost cell-mediated immunity). It is a non-live, recombinant subunit vaccine — unlike the older Zostavax (live attenuated, similar to varicella vaccine). Advantages of Shingrix: (1) Efficacy >90% against shingles (vs ~50% for Zostavax, which declines with age); (2) Safe in immunocompromised patients — live vaccines are contraindicated; (3) Given as 2 doses 2–6 months apart (vs Zostavax: single dose). UK JCVI introduced Shingrix into the routine immunisation schedule for adults aged 70–79 and immunosuppressed individuals ≥50. AS01B adjuvant causes significant local reactions (injection site pain, fatigue) — patients should be warned. No contraindication in adults over 80 (E).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST explains why Clostridioides difficile is so difficult to eradicate from hospital environments compared to non-spore-forming bacteria?
options: A. C. difficile is intrinsically resistant to all antibiotics including vancomycin

B. C. difficile forms heat-resistant, desiccation-resistant endospores that survive on environmental surfaces for months and are resistant to alcohol-based hand rubs — hypochlorite (bleach) disinfection and soap-and-water handwashing are required
C. C. difficile forms a thick capsule that protects it from all disinfectants
D. C. difficile is a Gram-positive aerobe that thrives in oxygenated hospital environments
E. C. difficile is transmitted by airborne droplets rather than contact — standard precautions cannot prevent its spread
mockBank.explanation: Clostridioides difficile (formerly Clostridium difficile) forms endospores — highly resistant dormant structures with a multi-layered spore coat. C. difficile spores: (1) Survive on environmental surfaces (bed rails, commodes, floors) for months; (2) Are resistant to alcohol-based hand rubs (alcohol does NOT inactivate C. difficile spores — a key clinical point); (3) Require hypochlorite (bleach) solutions (1000–10,000 ppm available chlorine) for environmental decontamination; (4) Require soap and water handwashing to physically remove spores from hands (not alcohol gel). Dental relevance: C. difficile-associated diarrhoea is the most important adverse effect of clindamycin — dentists prescribing clindamycin should warn patients to report diarrhoea promptly. C. difficile IS sensitive to vancomycin (A — it is a primary treatment). A capsule (C) is different from endospores. C. difficile is a Gram-positive ANAEROBE (D). It is contact-transmitted, not airborne (E).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly matches a penicillin subclass with its spectrum of activity and a clinical indication?
options: A. Flucloxacillin — broad-spectrum penicillin used for dental infections and respiratory infections
B. Flucloxacillin — narrow-spectrum penicillinase-resistant penicillin, used specifically for MSSA skin and soft tissue infections
C. Amoxicillin — narrow-spectrum penicillin, active only against Streptococcus species
D. Co-amoxiclav — antipseudomonal penicillin with activity against Pseudomonas aeruginosa
E. Phenoxymethylpenicillin (penicillin V) — broad-spectrum penicillin effective against all oral bacteria including anaerobes
mockBank.explanation: Penicillin subclasses and spectra: (1) Natural penicillins (penicillin G, V): narrow spectrum, Gram-positive cocci, some spirochaetes — NOT Staphylococcus (penicillinase-producing); (2) Aminopenicillins (amoxicillin, ampicillin): broader spectrum, adds activity against some Gram-negative rods (E. coli, H. influenzae) but NOT Pseudomonas, destroyed by penicillinase; (3) Penicillinase-resistant penicillins (flucloxacillin, methicillin): narrow spectrum specifically designed to resist staphylococcal penicillinase — used for MSSA infections (skin, bone, endocarditis); (4) Antipseudomonal penicillins (piperacillin, ticarcillin): extend to Pseudomonas aeruginosa; (5) Beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combinations (co-amoxiclav): amoxicillin spectrum + beta-lactamase inhibitor. Flucloxacillin (A) is narrow-spectrum, not broad-spectrum. Amoxicillin (C) covers more than just Streptococcus. Co-amoxiclav (D) does NOT cover Pseudomonas. Penicillin V (E) is narrow-spectrum with poor anaerobic activity.

mockBank.question: Parvovirus B19 causes fifth disease (erythema infectiosum) in children and aplastic crisis in patients with haemolytic anaemias. Which ONE of the following is the MOST important consideration for a pregnant healthcare worker exposed to a child with active parvovirus B19 infection?
options: A. No risk — parvovirus B19 does not cross the placenta

B. Risk of fetal hydrops fetalis — parvovirus B19 infects fetal erythroid precursors via P antigen, causing fetal anaemia, high-output cardiac failure, and hydrops; serology testing and obstetric referral are recommended
C. Risk to the healthcare worker only — the fetus is protected by placental maternal IgG
D. Prescribe aciclovir prophylactically — parvovirus is a herpesvirus sensitive to aciclovir
E. Administer live attenuated parvovirus vaccine immediately
mockBank.explanation: Parvovirus B19 (DNA virus, non-enveloped) is teratogenic. It infects erythroid progenitor cells via the P antigen (globoside) receptor — causing temporary suppression of erythropoiesis (leading to aplastic crisis in adults with haemolytic anaemia). In pregnancy: parvovirus B19 crosses the placenta and infects fetal erythroid precursors (which express high levels of P antigen). The fetal marrow cannot compensate → severe fetal anaemia → high-output cardiac failure → fetal hydrops fetalis (generalised oedema, ascites, pleural effusion). Risk of fetal death: ~10% if infected in first 20 weeks. Management for pregnant healthcare worker: serology (test IgG — if already immune, reassure; if non-immune, monitor with serial fetal ultrasound). No specific antiviral treatment. No vaccine available (E). Maternal IgG does transfer (C) but is insufficient if the fetus is infected before adequate maternal antibody develops. Parvovirus is NOT a herpesvirus (D).

mockBank.question: In the context of normal wound healing after a dental extraction, which ONE of the following correctly describes the sequential role of immune cells?
options: A. B cells → neutrophils → macrophages → T cells
B. Neutrophils (immediate phase) → macrophages (days 2–4) → T cells and fibroblast activation → resolution
C. T cells → NK cells → mast cells → neutrophils
D. Macrophages are present first — neutrophils arrive after 5 days
E. The immune response is absent in normal extraction healing — only fibroblasts participate
mockBank.explanation: Wound healing phases and immune cell sequence: (1) Haemostatic phase (0–minutes): platelet activation, fibrin clot formation. (2) Inflammatory phase (hours–days 4): Neutrophils: first responders (within 6–12 hours) — phagocytose bacteria, debris, and necrotic tissue via oxidative burst; peak at 24–48 hours, then apoptose. Macrophages: replace neutrophils from day 2–4 — phagocytose apoptotic neutrophils (efferocytosis), orchestrate transition to repair by releasing growth factors (VEGF, PDGF, TGF-β). (3) Proliferative phase (days 4–14): fibroblasts produce collagen (under macrophage direction); angiogenesis; epithelial migration. (4) Remodelling phase (weeks–months): collagen organisation. T cells also participate in regulating the inflammatory response. B cells (A) are not key wound healing cells. Macrophages (D) arrive after neutrophils. The immune response IS crucial for normal healing (E).

mockBank.question: An HBsAg-positive patient has been in the 'immune tolerant phase' of chronic HBV infection for 20 years: HBV DNA 10⁸ IU/mL, HBeAg positive, ALT normal, liver biopsy shows minimal inflammation. Which ONE of the following BEST explains why his immune system tolerates such high viral levels?
options: A. HBV is not recognised by the immune system — it lacks surface antigens
B. HBV establishes tolerance by infecting regulatory T cells and causing them to suppress anti-HBV responses

C. During perinatal or early childhood infection, HBV antigens are encountered during a window of immature immune function — leading to central and peripheral tolerance of HBV-specific T cells, preventing viral clearance
D. HBV produces a protein that blocks all MHC Class I expression, preventing CD8+ T cell recognition
E. The liver is an immunoprivileged site — no immune responses can occur there
mockBank.explanation: The immune tolerant phase of chronic HBV (high viral load, HBeAg+, normal ALT, minimal liver damage) is most common in patients with perinatal or early childhood HBV acquisition. The mechanism of immune tolerance: (1) Central tolerance: neonatal immune system exposed to HBV antigens during thymic T cell education → HBV-specific T cells deleted (clonal deletion) or converted to Tregs; (2) Peripheral tolerance: remaining HBV-specific T cells become exhausted or anergic in the presence of extremely high antigen loads (10⁸ IU/mL viral antigen); (3) HBV decoy mechanism: HBsAg is produced in massive excess over infectious virions, saturating anti-HBV B cell responses. This explains the paradox: high virus, normal liver enzymes = tolerant immune system, not actively fighting. HBV does have surface antigens (A). HBV does not infect Tregs specifically (B). HBV downregulates but not abolishes MHC Class I (D). The liver is NOT immunoprivileged (E) — it can mount immune responses.

mockBank.question: Summarising UK guidelines, which ONE of the following correctly describes a scenario where antibiotic prophylaxis IS currently recommended before a dental procedure?
options: A. Patient with a mechanical heart valve — prophylaxis before any invasive dental procedure
B. Patient with a hip replacement — prophylaxis before extractions only
C. Patient with previous infective endocarditis — prophylaxis before extraction, scaling, and periodontal treatment
D. Patient about to receive jaw radiotherapy who requires extraction before the treatment begins — prophylaxis with amoxicillin to reduce the risk of osteoradionecrosis
E. Immunocompetent patient with controlled diabetes — prophylaxis before all dental extractions
mockBank.explanation: This question tests the ONE scenario where dental antibiotic prophylaxis IS recommended under current UK guidance. Pre-radiotherapy dental extraction with antibiotic prophylaxis: Patients about to undergo therapeutic radiation to the jaws for head and neck cancer require dental assessment and any necessary extractions BEFORE radiotherapy begins. If extraction after radiation is unavoidable, antibiotic prophylaxis is recommended to reduce osteoradionecrosis (ORN) risk — amoxicillin 3g (or clindamycin 600mg) 1 hour pre-operatively. Mechanical heart valves (A): NICE NG178 = NO IE prophylaxis for any dental procedure. Hip replacement (B): current UK guidance (NICE, BSOS) = NO prophylaxis needed. Previous IE (C): NICE NG178 = still NO routine prophylaxis. Controlled diabetes (E): not an indication for prophylaxis in immunocompetent patients. The pre-radiotherapy/post-radiotherapy extraction is the key scenario where dental antibiotic prophylaxis has current evidence-based UK recommendations.
